- **Collective Primitives:** Operations involving a group of processes (e.g., `MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Reduce`, `MPI_Gather`, `MPI_Scatter`, `MPI_Allgather`, `MPI_Alltoall`).
- **Synchronization Primitives:** Operations that establish ordering or barriers across processes (e.g., `MPI_Barrier`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A "primitive" in distributed computing is a *guaranteed-to-be-atomic* communication action. It cannot be decomposed by the user into smaller meaningful communication actions using the same API. This atomicity guarantees consistency of distributed state.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **post office** (the distributed system) where multiple clerks (processes) sit at different counters (nodes), each working on a piece of a giant puzzle (parallel computation).

A **primitive for distributed communication** is like a **standardized postal form or telephone protocol**:

- **Sending a Letter (`MPI_Send`):** The clerk writes a message, seals it in an envelope, and hands it to the postal service. The postal service *promises* it will reach the destination, but the clerk has no guarantee about *when*.
- **Making a Phone Call (`MPI_Recv`):** A clerk dials another counter and waits for the other person to answer. Until they answer, the calling clerk is *blocked* (cannot do other work).
- **Conference Call Broadcast (`MPI_Bcast`):** One clerk announces something, and the postal service simultaneously delivers that exact message to every other clerk in the office.
- **Group Survey Collection (`MPI_Reduce`):** Every clerk writes down a number, and the postal service collects all numbers and computes the *sum* (or *max*, *min*, etc.), returning the single combined answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are "Primitives" so important?**
> Without these standardized primitives, every distributed program would have to manually implement packet construction, routing, error handling, and synchronization from scratch — an engineering impossibility for large systems. Primitives provide a *reusable vocabulary* for distributed coordination.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics in Distributed Communication

The two fundamental performance metrics used to evaluate every communication primitive are:

- **Latency ($\alpha$ or $T_s$):** The fixed time overhead to *initiate* a communication, measured in **microseconds (µs)**. This is the "start-up cost" — the time before the first byte is even on the wire.
- **Bandwidth ($\beta$ or $1/T_w$):** The rate at which bulk data can be transmitted once the channel is established, measured in **Megabytes per second (MB/s)** or **Gigabits per second (Gbps)**.
- **Message Size ($n$):** The size of the data payload, measured in **bytes (B)**.

> [!TIP]
> A high-performing primitive is one that minimizes $\alpha$ and maximizes $\beta$. The KTU 2024 exam frequently tests the derivation of communication time using the **Hockney Model** $T(n) = T_s + n \cdot T_w$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Distributed Communication Primitives

The primitives are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

#### Axis A: Number of Processes Involved

- **Point-to-Point (PTP) Primitives:** Involve exactly **two** processes — one sender, one receiver. Example: `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv`.
- **Collective Primitives:** Involve a **group** (communicator) of $p$ processes. Example: `MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Reduce`, `MPI_Gather`.

#### Axis B: Blocking Behavior

- **Blocking Primitives:** The calling process is *suspended* (paused) until the communication operation is locally complete.
  - A *send* is locally complete when the send buffer can be safely reused (either the data is in the network or already received).
  - A *receive* is locally complete when the data has arrived in the receive buffer.
- **Non-Blocking Primitives:** The call returns *immediately*, allowing the process to do useful work while communication happens in the background. The user must later test or wait on a request handle. Example: `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`.

#### Axis C: Synchronization Guarantees (Send Modes only)

- **Standard Mode:** Implementation-dependent. The send may or may not synchronize with the receiver.
- **Buffered Mode:** The send always copies the data into a local user/system buffer and returns immediately.
- **Synchronous Mode:** The send blocks until a matching receive has *started* (rendezvous protocol).
- **Ready Mode:** The send assumes the matching receive is *already posted*. Failure to do so is undefined behavior.

### 2.2 Detailed Logic of Each Major Primitive

#### 2.2.1 `MPI_Send(buf, count, datatype, dest, tag, comm)`
- **How it works:** The sender serializes the data from `buf` and initiates transmission to process `dest`. The `tag` allows the receiver to filter specific messages. The `comm` (communicator) defines the process group.
- **Why it matters:** This is the *workhorse* of point-to-point communication; every MPI program uses it.

#### 2.2.2 `MPI_Recv(buf, count, datatype, source, tag, comm, status)`
- **How it works:** The receiver waits for a message matching `source` and `tag`. Wildcards `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` and `MPI_ANY_TAG` are allowed.
- **Why it matters:** Provides the synchronization point. Without a matching receive, the message is "in limbo" or buffered.

#### 2.2.3 `MPI_Bcast(buffer, count, datatype, root, comm)`
- **How it works:** One process (`root`) sends an identical copy of a message to *all* other processes in the communicator.
- **Why it matters:** Used for distributing configuration data, polynomial coefficients, and initial problem parameters.

#### 2.2.4 `MPI_Scatter(sendbuf, sendcount, sendtype, recvbuf, recvcount, recvtype, root, comm)`
- **How it works:** The root process partitions its data into $p$ equal chunks and sends the $i$-th chunk to process $i$. **Each process receives a different piece.**
- **Why it matters:** Used for distributing distinct work units (e.g., rows of a matrix) to workers.

#### 2.2.5 `MPI_Gather(sendbuf, sendcnt, sendtype, recvbuf, recvcount, recvtype, root, comm)`
- **How it works:** The reverse of Scatter. Every process sends its piece of data to the root, which concatenates them in rank order.
- **Why it matters:** Used to collect partial results from workers.

#### 2.2.6 `MPI_Reduce(sendbuf, recvbuf, count, datatype, op, root, comm)`
- **How it works:** Each process contributes a value; the predefined reduction operator `op` (e.g., `MPI_SUM`, `MPI_MAX`, `MPI_MIN`, `MPI_PROD`) is applied across all contributions, and the result is delivered **only to the root**.
- **Why it matters:** Foundation of distributed aggregations (sum, max, dot product).

#### 2.2.7 `MPI_Allgather` and `MPI_Allreduce`
- **How it works:** Same as Gather and Reduce, but the result is delivered to *every* process, not just the root.
- **Why it matters:** Required when all processes need a copy of the aggregated value (e.g., global error norm in iterative solvers).

#### 2.2.8 `MPI_Barrier(comm)`
- **How it works:** Every process in `comm` must call the barrier; none proceeds until *all* have arrived. It performs no data transfer.
- **Why it matters:** Global synchronization point to ensure all stages are complete.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to denote absolute value inside table cells, per KTU 2024 markdown rendering rules.

| **Primitive** | **Category** | **Time Complexity (Hockney Model)** | **Communication Volume** | **Synchronization Provided** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv` | Point-to-Point | $T(n) = T_s + n \cdot T_w$ | $n$ bytes | Possible (Sync mode) |
| `MPI_Bcast` | Collective | $T(n) = \left(\lceil \log_2 p \rceil\right) \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_w$ | $(p-1) \cdot n$ bytes | Implicit |
| `MPI_Scatter` | Collective | $T(n) = \left(\lceil \log_2 p \rceil\right) \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_w$ | $(p-1) \cdot n/p$ bytes per proc. | Implicit |
| `MPI_Gather` | Collective | $T(n) = \left(\lceil \log_2 p \rceil\right) \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_w$ | $(p-1) \cdot n/p$ bytes per proc. | Implicit |
| `MPI_Reduce` | Collective | $T(n) = \left(\lceil \log_2 p \rceil\right) \cdot T_s + 2n \cdot T_w$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \log p)$ | Implicit |
| `MPI_Allreduce` | Collective | $T(n) = 2 \cdot \left(\lceil \log_2 p \rceil\right) \cdot T_s + 2n \cdot T_w$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \log p)$ | Full |
| `MPI_Barrier` | Synchronization | $T = \left(\lceil \log_2 p \rceil\right) \cdot T_s$ | 0 bytes | Full |
| `MPI_Alltoall` | Collective | $T(n) = \left(\lceil \log_2 p \rceil\right) \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_w \cdot (p-1)$ | $n \cdot p$ total | Implicit |

**Where:**
- $T_s$ = startup latency (seconds)
- $T_w$ = transfer time per byte (seconds/byte), where $T_w = 1/\beta$
- $n$ = message size in bytes
- $p$ = number of processes in the communicator
- $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$ = depth of communication tree (e.g., for $p=8$, $\log_2 8 = 3$)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These primitives power the most demanding distributed workloads on Earth:

- **Weather Forecasting (WRF Model):** The atmospheric grid is *scattered* across thousands of nodes. Each node computes local physics; results are *reduced* to form the global state.
- **Large Language Model Training (GPT-class):** `MPI_Allreduce` synchronizes gradients across thousands of GPUs.
- **Search Engines:** Inverted indexes are built using `MPI_Gather` after parallel map phases.
- **Database Sharding:** Cross-shard aggregations are direct applications of `MPI_Reduce` semantics.
- **Scientific Simulations (CFD, Molecular Dynamics):** Halo exchanges use point-to-point `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv` between neighbor subdomains.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Communication Time of `MPI_Bcast` Using a Tree-Based Algorithm

**Goal:** Derive the time $T_{bcast}(n, p)$ to broadcast an $n$-byte message to $p$ processes using a balanced binary tree, assuming a log-step parallel pipeline.

**Given:**
- $T_s$ = startup latency per message
- $T_w$ = time to transfer one byte
- $p$ = number of processes (assumed to be a power of 2: $p = 2^d$)
- $n$ = total message size in bytes

**Step 1: Determine the number of levels in the broadcast tree.**

The binary tree has a depth of $d = \log_2 p$ levels. At level 0, the root has 1 node. At level $d$, there are $p$ leaves.

**Step 2: Identify what happens at each level.**

At each level $k$ (where $k$ ranges from $0$ to $d-1$):
- Each process that has the message at level $k$ sends it to 2 new children at level $k+1$.
- The number of senders at level $k$ is $2^k$.
- Each sender transmits the **same full $n$-byte message**.

**Step 3: Compute the time per level.**

The critical path runs through one send per level. The time for a single send of $n$ bytes is given by the Hockney point-to-point formula:
$$T_{send}(n) = T_s + n \cdot T_w$$

**Step 4: Multiply by the number of levels (sequential along the critical path).**

The total time is the sum over all levels:
$$T_{bcast}(n, p) = d \cdot T_{send}(n) = (\log_2 p) \cdot (T_s + n \cdot T_w)$$

**Step 5: Express final answer.**

$$T_{bcast}(n, p) = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot n \cdot T_w$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Compare this with naive unicast-loop broadcasting, which takes $(p-1) \cdot (T_s + n \cdot T_w)$. For $p=1024$ and large $n$, the tree-based approach is **100× faster**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Communication Time of `MPI_Reduce` for the `MPI_SUM` Operator

**Goal:** Derive $T_{reduce}(n, p)$ using a binary tree reduction (similar to tournament bracket).

**Step 1: Pair processes in a tournament structure.**

At level 0, $p$ processes are paired into $p/2$ pairs. Each pair sends its $n$-byte value to a partner; the partner computes the sum. After this level, $p/2$ processes hold partial sums.

**Step 2: Recurse for $\log_2 p$ levels.**

After $d = \log_2 p$ levels, only 1 process (the root) holds the final result.

**Step 3: Time per level.**

At each level, two operations occur:
- One point-to-point send of $n$ bytes: cost $T_s + n \cdot T_w$.
- One local addition: cost $t_{add} \cdot n$ (computation cost, often ignored for simplicity).

**Step 4: Add a final sequential cost on root.**

The root receives $\log_2 p$ partial sums and must add them serially (or with a small tree), incurring an extra $n \cdot T_w$ term in the worst case.

**Step 5: Total reduction time.**

$$T_{reduce}(n, p) = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_w \cdot (\lceil \log_2 p \rceil + 1)$$

For large $p$, this is often simplified to:
$$T_{reduce}(n, p) \approx 2 \cdot n \cdot T_w \cdot \lceil \log_2 p \rceil$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost Comparison: `MPI_Reduce` vs `MPI_Allreduce`

**`MPI_Allreduce`** is logically equivalent to `MPI_Reduce` followed by `MPI_Bcast` of the result. Therefore:

$$T_{allreduce}(n, p) = T_{reduce}(n, p) + T_{bcast}(n, p)$$

$$T_{allreduce}(n, p) = \left[ \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_w \cdot (\lceil \log_2 p \rceil + 1) \right] + \left[ \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot n \cdot T_w \right]$$

Simplifying:
$$T_{allreduce}(n, p) = 2 \cdot \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + (2 \cdot \lceil \log_2 p \rceil + 1) \cdot n \cdot T_w$$

> [!TIP]
> **Smart implementations** of `MPI_Allreduce` (e.g., the Rabenseifner algorithm) overlap the reduce and bcast phases in a single tree, achieving the same complexity with better constant factors.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A distributed application uses 8 processes to broadcast a 4 KB (4096 bytes) message. Given $T_s = 10 \, \mu s$ and $T_w = 0.001 \, \mu s/byte$, compute the time taken by the broadcast.

**Solution:**

$$\log_2 8 = 3 \text{ levels}$$

$$T_{bcast} = 3 \cdot T_s + 3 \cdot n \cdot T_w$$

$$T_{bcast} = 3 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6}) + 3 \cdot (4096) \cdot (0.001 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$T_{bcast} = 30 \times 10^{-6} + 3 \cdot 4096 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$T_{bcast} = 30 \, \mu s + 12.288 \, \mu s = 42.288 \, \mu s$$

**For comparison, naive loop broadcast** would take:
$$T_{naive} = (p-1) \cdot (T_s + n \cdot T_w) = 7 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6} + 4.096 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$T_{naive} = 7 \cdot 14.096 \, \mu s = 98.672 \, \mu s$$

**Speedup:**
$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{98.672}{42.288} \approx 2.33\times$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Primitive

| **Scenario** | **Recommended Primitive** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Distribute identical data (e.g., coefficients) to all | `MPI_Bcast` | Single communication, log-time |
| Distribute distinct rows of a matrix | `MPI_Scatter` | Sends unique data to each process |
| Collect partial sums to a master | `MPI_Gather` + `MPI_Reduce` | Two-step aggregation |
| Global error norm every iteration | `MPI_Allreduce` | Every process needs the result |
| Boundary exchange in 2D grid | `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv` (4 neighbors) | Localized, point-to-point |
| Synchronize phases | `MPI_Barrier` | No data, only timing |
| Cross-shard histogram merge | `MPI_Alltoall` | Every process gives to every other |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Classification of Communication Primitives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Distributed Communication Primitives"]
    rootA --> catA["Point to Point Primitives"]
    rootA --> catB["Collective Primitives"]
    rootA --> catC["Synchronization Primitives"]
    catA --> ptp1["Blocking MPI_Send MPI_Recv"]
    catA --> ptp2["Non Blocking MPI_Isend MPI_Irecv"]
    catA --> ptp3["Synchronous Mode MPI_Ssend"]
    catA --> ptp4["Buffered Mode MPI_Bsend"]
    catA --> ptp5["Ready Mode MPI_Rsend"]
    catB --> col1["One to All MPI_Bcast"]
    catB --> col2["All to One MPI_Reduce MPI_Gather"]
    catB --> col3["All to All MPI_Allreduce MPI_Allgather MPI_Alltoall"]
    catB --> col4["Personalized Distribution MPI_Scatter MPI_Gather"]
    catC --> sync1["MPI_Barrier"]
    sync1 --> noteA["No data transfer Ensures all processes reach the same point before proceeding"]
    col1 --> noteB["Root sends identical copy to every other process in group"]
    col2 --> noteC["All processes contribute Root receives combined result"]
    col3 --> noteD["Every process receives the combined result"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture: Pipeline of a Typical MPI Program

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0["Process 0 Root Rank"] -->|MPI_Bcast coefficients| p1["Process 1"]
    p0 -->|MPI_Bcast coefficients| p2["Process 2"]
    p0 -->|MPI_Bcast coefficients| p3["Process 3"]
    p0 -->|MPI_Scatter data chunks| p1
    p0 -->|MPI_Scatter data chunks| p2
    p0 -->|MPI_Scatter data chunks| p3
    p1 -->|Local Computation| work1["Compute local result"]
    p2 -->|Local Computation| work2["Compute local result"]
    p3 -->|Local Computation| work3["Compute local result"]
    work1 -->|MPI_Reduce to root| p0
    work2 -->|MPI_Reduce to root| p0
    work3 -->|MPI_Reduce to root| p0
    p0 --> barrier["MPI_Barrier synchronize before next phase"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Tree-Based Broadcast Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    level0["Level 0: Root Process 0"]
    level1a["Level 1: Process 1"]
    level1b["Level 1: Process 2"]
    level2a["Level 2: Process 3"]
    level2b["Level 2: Process 4"]
    level2c["Level 2: Process 5"]
    level2d["Level 2: Process 6"]
    level3a["Level 3: Process 7"]
    level3b["Level 3: Process 8"]
    level0 -->|Step 1| level1a
    level0 -->|Step 1| level1b
    level1a -->|Step 2| level2a
    level1a -->|Step 2| level2b
    level1b -->|Step 2| level2c
    level1b -->|Step 2| level2d
    level2a -->|Step 3| level3a
    level2b -->|Step 3| level3b
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above tree diagram illustrates that for $p = 8$ processes, a binary tree broadcast completes in **3 sequential steps** (log base 2 of 8 = 3). Each arrow represents one `MPI_Send` operation.

### 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram: Blocking vs Non-Blocking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bA["Blocking Primitive Call"]
    bA --> bB["Process Suspended"]
    bB --> bC["Communication Completes Locally"]
    bC --> bD["Process Resumes Execution"]
    nA["Non Blocking Primitive Call"]
    nA --> nB["Request Handle Returned"]
    nB --> nC["Process Continues Useful Work"]
    nC --> nD["Process Calls MPI_Wait or MPI_Test"]
    nD --> nE["Communication Finally Completed"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**
**Define communication primitives in distributed programming. Differentiate between point-to-point and collective communication primitives with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Communication primitives are atomic, system-level operations that allow distributed processes to exchange data and synchronize. They form the vocabulary of distributed programming.

**Point-to-Point Primitives:** Involve exactly one sender and one receiver.
- *Example:* `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv`.

**Collective Primitives:** Involve a group of processes and are called by *all* members of a communicator.
- *Example:* `MPI_Bcast` (one-to-all broadcast), `MPI_Reduce` (all-to-one reduction), `MPI_Gather` (all-to-one collection).

**Key Difference:** Point-to-point has explicit source/destination tagging and is the building block; collective primitives are higher-level and provide implicit synchronization but require all members of the group to participate.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2023]:**
**What is the difference between blocking and non-blocking communication primitives? Why are non-blocking primitives preferred for high-performance applications?**

**Model Answer:**

A **blocking primitive** suspends the calling process until the communication operation is *locally complete*. For a receive, this means the data has been delivered; for a send, the send buffer can be safely reused.

A **non-blocking primitive** (e.g., `MPI_Isend`/`MPI_Irecv`) returns *immediately* with a request handle. The process can do other work and check completion later using `MPI_Wait` or `MPI_Test`.

**Why non-blocking is preferred for HPC:**
1. **Latency hiding:** Computation can overlap with communication in pipelined fashion.
2. **Avoiding deadlocks:** Since the call returns immediately, cyclic dependencies do not cause hangs.
3. **Better resource utilization:** Processors do not idle while data is in transit.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**KTU 2024 Note:** Per KTU regulations, Part B questions carry internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain the classification of MPI communication primitives with a clear diagram. Discuss **blocking vs non-blocking** and **synchronous vs asynchronous** semantics in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the total time to perform an `MPI_Bcast` of an $n$-byte message across $p$ processes using a balanced binary tree, given startup latency $T_s$ and per-byte transfer time $T_w$. For $p=16$, $n=1024$ bytes, $T_s=20 \, \mu s$, $T_w=0.005 \, \mu s/byte$, compute the broadcast time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of MPI Communication Primitives (7 marks):**

MPI primitives are classified as follows:

1. **Point-to-Point Primitives:** Used for one-to-one communication.
   - Blocking: `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`.
   - Non-blocking: `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`.
   - Synchronous send: `MPI_Ssend` (rendezvous).
   - Buffered send: `MPI_Bsend` (always copies to local buffer).
   - Ready send: `MPI_Rsend` (assumes receive is posted).

2. **Collective Primitives:** Used for group communication.
   - **Data Movement:** `MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Scatter`, `MPI_Gather`, `MPI_Allgather`, `MPI_Alltoall`.
   - **Reduction:** `MPI_Reduce`, `MPI_Allreduce`, `MPI_Reduce_scatter`.

3. **Synchronization Primitives:** `MPI_Barrier`.

**Blocking vs Non-Blocking:** A blocking call (e.g., `MPI_Send`) suspends the caller until the local operation is complete. A non-blocking call (e.g., `MPI_Isend`) returns immediately with a request handle, and the caller must later use `MPI_Wait` to ensure completion. Non-blocking enables overlap of communication with computation.

**Synchronous vs Asynchronous Semantics:** In a synchronous send (`MPI_Ssend`), the sender blocks until the matching receive has *started*. In asynchronous send (standard mode `MPI_Send`), the message is buffered or sent at the convenience of the implementation; the sender does not wait.

**Diagram (3 marks for the diagram component):**
```
   [MPI Primitives]
        |
   ------------------
   |       |        |
 PTP   Collective  Sync
   |       |        |
   |   --------     |
   |   |   |   |   Barrier
 Bcast Reduce Gather
 Scatter  Allreduce
 Allgather Alltoall
```

**(b) Derivation of Broadcast Time (7 marks):**

Given a binary tree broadcast with $p$ processes:
- Number of levels $d = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil$.
- At each level, one send of $n$ bytes occurs along the critical path.
- Cost per send: $T_s + n \cdot T_w$.

$$\boxed{T_{bcast}(n, p) = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot n \cdot T_w}$$

**Numerical computation for $p=16$, $n=1024$ bytes, $T_s=20 \, \mu s$, $T_w=0.005 \, \mu s/byte$:**

$$\log_2 16 = 4$$

$$T_{bcast} = 4 \cdot 20 + 4 \cdot 1024 \cdot 0.005$$

$$T_{bcast} = 80 \, \mu s + 20.48 \, \mu s$$

$$\boxed{T_{bcast} = 100.48 \, \mu s}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]

**Q. (a)** Explain the following collective primitives in detail: `MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Scatter`, `MPI_Gather`, and `MPI_Reduce`. Provide a real-world example where each is used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the communication time of `MPI_Reduce` and `MPI_Allreduce` for $p$ processes. Show that `MPI_Allreduce` can be implemented as `MPI_Reduce` followed by `MPI_Bcast` and derive the time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Collective Primitives Explained (7 marks):**

1. **`MPI_Bcast` (One-to-All Broadcast):** Root process sends an identical copy of a message to all other processes.
   - *Example:* Distributing polynomial coefficients to all workers in a parallel root-finding algorithm.

2. **`MPI_Scatter` (One-to-All Personalized):** Root process partitions its buffer into $p$ chunks and sends chunk $i$ to process $i$. Each process receives **different** data.
   - *Example:* Distributing distinct rows of a matrix to worker processes for parallel matrix-vector multiplication.

3. **`MPI_Gather` (All-to-One):** Each process sends its data to the root, which concatenates them in rank order.
   - *Example:* Collecting per-process histogram bins to the master for a final image histogram.

4. **`MPI_Reduce` (All-to-One Aggregation):** Each process contributes a value; a reduction operator (`MPI_SUM`, `MPI_MAX`, etc.) is applied; result is delivered to root.
   - *Example:* Computing the global sum of partial integrals in Monte Carlo simulation.

**(b) Reduce vs Allreduce Time Complexity (7 marks):**

`MPI_Reduce` using a binary reduction tree:
$$T_{reduce}(n, p) = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_w \cdot (\lceil \log_2 p \rceil + 1)$$

`MPI_Allreduce` = `MPI_Reduce` + `MPI_Bcast` (semantically):
$$T_{allreduce}(n, p) = T_{reduce}(n, p) + T_{bcast}(n, p)$$

$$T_{allreduce}(n, p) = 2 \cdot \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_w \cdot (2 \cdot \lceil \log_2 p \rceil + 1)$$

**Verification for $p=4$, $n=2$ KB, $T_s=10 \, \mu s$, $T_w=0.002 \, \mu s/byte$:**

$\log_2 4 = 2$, $n=2048$ bytes.

$$T_{reduce} = 2 \cdot 10 + 2048 \cdot 0.002 \cdot 3 = 20 + 12.288 = 32.288 \, \mu s$$

$$T_{allreduce} = 2 \cdot 20 + 2048 \cdot 0.002 \cdot 5 = 40 + 20.48 = 60.48 \, \mu s$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Confusing "communication volume" with "per-process volume":** Many students write that `MPI_Bcast` transmits $n$ bytes total. **WRONG.** It transmits $(p-1) \cdot n$ bytes. The model transmits from root to each of $(p-1)$ destinations.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\log_2 p$ factor:** Collective primitives have a logarithmic number of sequential steps, not $p$ steps. If you write $T_{bcast} = T_s + n \cdot T_w$ (same as a point-to-point send), **you lose 4 marks** because the question is testing whether you understand tree-based algorithms.
> 3. **Mixing up `MPI_Scatter` and `MPI_Bcast`:** Scatter sends *different* data; Bcast sends *same* data. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Ignoring tag and communicator parameters:** A definition that omits `comm` (communicator) and `tag` parameters is incomplete.
> 5. **Writing $T_s$ as the total time:** $T_s$ is *startup latency*, not total communication time. Students confuse $T_s$ (single send) with $T_{total}$.
> 6. **Not stating the unit:** Always write the answer with units ($\mu s$ or $ms$).
> 7. **Drawing bare boxes without labels in flowcharts:** The KTU valuation penalizes unlabeled arrows and ambiguous diagrams in Part B.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Primitives for Distributed Communication":**
> - Primitives are **atomic, system-level communication operations** that abstract the network.
> - **Three major classes:** Point-to-Point (`MPI_Send`/`MPI_Recv`), Collective (`MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Reduce`, etc.), and Synchronization (`MPI_Barrier`).
> - **Blocking calls** suspend the process; **non-blocking calls** return immediately with a request handle.
> - **Four send modes:** Standard, Buffered (`MPI_Bsend`), Synchronous (`MPI_Ssend`), Ready (`MPI_Rsend`).
> - `MPI_Bcast` = one-to-all **identical** data; `MPI_Scatter` = one-to-all **distinct** data.
> - `MPI_Gather` = all-to-one **collection**; `MPI_Reduce` = all-to-one **aggregation**.
> - `MPI_Allreduce` = Reduce + Bcast combined (every process gets the result).
> - `MPI_Barrier` = synchronization only, no data transfer.
> - **Hockney Model for one send:** $T(n) = T_s + n \cdot T_w$.
> - **Tree-based collective cost:** $T_{collective} = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot T_s + f(n,p) \cdot T_w$.
> - **Speedup of tree over naive loop** = up to $p / \log_2 p$ for large $p$.
> - `MPI_Bcast` communication volume = $(p-1) \cdot n$ bytes; `MPI_Scatter` volume = $(p-1) \cdot n/p$ per process.
> - **Non-blocking preferred** for latency hiding and deadlock avoidance in HPC codes.
> - All collective primitives provide **implicit synchronization**; explicit barriers are needed only when data ordering is critical.
> - Real-world uses: weather forecasting (scatter/reduce), LLM training (`Allreduce`), database sharding (`Reduce`), CFD halo exchange (`Send`/`Recv`).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Parallel and Distributed Programming**, *Design Issues* refer to the fundamental architectural, algorithmic, and systemic challenges that a programmer or system architect must resolve when transforming a sequential problem statement into a parallel/distributed execution model. These issues govern **how work is partitioned**, **how cooperating processes exchange information**, **how they coordinate access to shared resources**, and **how the overall system scales** with increasing problem size or hardware resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 - Design Issues):**
> Design issues encompass communication, synchronization, data dependencies, load balancing, granularity, scalability, heterogeneity, and the identification of bottlenecks such as those described by **Amdahl's Law** and **Gustafson's Law**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen**
>
> Imagine a single chef (sequential program) trying to cook a 10-course meal alone. He chops, boils, fries, and plates — slowly. Now imagine **6 chefs in a parallel kitchen** (multi-core system). For the team to succeed, they must decide:
>
> - **Communication** – How does Chef A tell Chef B "the sauce is ready"?
> - **Synchronization** – Only one chef can use the single stove burner at a time.
> - **Load Balancing** – Don't let Chef A stand idle while Chef D is buried in work.
> - **Data Dependency** – The garnish cannot be plated before the main course is cooked.
> - **Granularity** – Should a chef cook an entire course, or should each chef handle just one dish?
> - **Scalability** – Will adding an 8th chef actually speed things up, or just create traffic jams in the kitchen hallway?
>
> Each of these is a *Design Issue*. The same issues appear when CPUs, GPUs, and distributed nodes cooperate.

### 1.3 The Seven Pillars of Parallel & Distributed Design Issues

The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes design issues into the following core dimensions, each with a **standard metric** for evaluation:

| # | Design Issue | Core Question | Standard Metric |
|---|--------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Communication | How do tasks exchange data? | **Latency (μs)**, **Bandwidth (GB/s)** |
| 2 | Synchronization | How do tasks coordinate? | **Barrier wait time** |
| 3 | Data Dependency | What must finish first? | **Dependency graph depth** |
| 4 | Load Balancing | Is work evenly distributed? | **Load Imbalance Factor** |
| 5 | Granularity | Fine vs. coarse tasks? | **Task size (Tcomp / Tcomm)** |
| 6 | Scalability | Does it grow with resources? | **Iso-efficiency metric** |
| 7 | Heterogeneity | Can it handle mixed hardware? | **Performance variance ratio** |

> [!NOTE]
> All metrics listed in **bold** above are **frequently asked in KTU university examinations**, especially in 3-mark Part A questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Communication

Communication is the mechanism by which cooperating processes exchange data. Two fundamental paradigms exist:

- **Shared Memory Communication** – Processes read/write to a common address space (e.g., OpenMP, Pthreads). Communication is implicit via reads/writes.
- **Message Passing Communication** – Processes explicitly send/receive messages (e.g., MPI). Communication is explicit.

**Key Design Trade-off:**
- Shared memory is **fast** but **not scalable** beyond a single node.
- Message passing is **slower per byte** but **scales to thousands of nodes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The choice of communication model directly impacts the **Communication-to-Computation Ratio (CCR)**, denoted as:

$$CCR = \frac{T_{comm}}{T_{comp}}$$

where $T_{comm}$ is total time spent in communication and $T_{comp}$ is total time spent in computation. **Lower CCR = Better parallel efficiency.**

### 2.2 Synchronization

Synchronization enforces ordering constraints on concurrent operations. Common primitives include:

- **Locks/Mutexes** – Mutual exclusion for critical sections.
- **Barriers** – All processes must arrive before any can proceed.
- **Semaphores** – Counting-based signaling.
- **Condition Variables** – Wait for state changes.

> [!NOTE]
> Over-synchronization causes **contention** and **deadlock**. Under-synchronization causes **race conditions**. The art of design lies in *just enough* synchronization.

### 2.3 Data Dependency

Three classical types of dependencies that block parallel execution:

1. **Flow (True) Dependency** – $S_2$ reads a value produced by $S_1$ (RAW – Read After Write).
2. **Anti-Dependency** – $S_2$ writes a location read by $S_1$ (WAR – Write After Read).
3. **Output Dependency** – Both $S_1$ and $S_2$ write the same location (WAW – Write After Write).

### 2.4 Load Balancing

Two strategies:

- **Static Load Balancing** – Work assigned *a priori* based on estimation. Best for predictable workloads.
- **Dynamic Load Balancing** – Work assigned at runtime via work-stealing/work-sharing. Best for unpredictable workloads.

### 2.5 Granularity

Granularity describes the ratio of computation to communication per task.

- **Fine-grained parallelism** – Many small tasks, high communication overhead. Suitable for **heterogeneous** systems.
- **Coarse-grained parallelism** – Few large tasks, minimal communication. Suitable for **distributed memory** systems.

### 2.6 Scalability

A parallel system is *scalable* if increasing the number of processors $p$ produces a proportional speedup. Measured by:

- **Iso-efficiency** – How much problem size must grow to maintain a fixed efficiency as $p$ increases?

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Variables | KTU Use |
|---------|---------|-----------|---------|
| $S(p) = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$ | Speedup with $p$ processors | $T_s$ = sequential time, $T_p$ = parallel time | Module 1, 2 |
| $E(p) = \frac{S(p)}{p} = \frac{T_s}{p \cdot T_p}$ | Efficiency | $0 < E \le 1$ | Module 1, 2 |
| $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{f}$ | Amdahl's Bound | $f$ = parallel fraction | Module 1 (High-Yield) |
| $S(p) = p - (p-1)f$ | Amdahl's Law (exact) | $f$ = serial fraction | Module 1 (High-Yield) |
| $S(p) = f + p(1-f)$ | Same as above (alternate form) | - | Module 1 |
| $S(p) = \frac{p}{1 + (p-1) \cdot \text{overhead}}$ | Generalized Speedup | overhead $= T_o / T_s$ | Module 1 |
| $CCR = \frac{T_{comm}}{T_{comp}}$ | Communication-Computation Ratio | $T_{comm}, T_{comp}$ | Module 1 |
| $\text{Avg\_Load} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} L_i}{p}$ | Average Load | $L_i$ = load on processor $i$ | Module 1 |
| $\text{Imbalance} = \frac{\max(L_i)}{\text{Avg\_Load}}$ | Load Imbalance Factor | - | Module 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in:
> - **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** cluster design (weather modeling, genomics).
> - **Data Center** capacity planning (Google, AWS).
> - **GPU programming** occupancy tuning (NVIDIA CUDA, OpenCL).
> - **Real-time embedded systems** (automotive ECUs, avionics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (KTU Most-Frequently Asked)

**Problem Setup (KTU 2024 Style):**
> Given a program where fraction $f$ of the execution time is *strictly serial* (cannot be parallelized) and the remaining $(1-f)$ can be perfectly parallelized, derive the maximum achievable speedup with $p$ processors.

**Step 1: Identify the Time Components**

The sequential execution time $T_s$ is split into two parts:

- Serial portion: $T_{serial} = f \cdot T_s$
- Parallel portion: $T_{parallel} = (1-f) \cdot T_s$

**Step 2: Apply Parallelization to the Parallel Portion Only**

The serial portion cannot be sped up — it remains $f \cdot T_s$ regardless of $p$.

The parallel portion, when distributed over $p$ processors, takes:

$$T_{parallel}^{p} = \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Parallel Execution Time**

$$T_p = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

**Step 4: Compute Speedup**

$$S(p) = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{T_s}{f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}}$$

**Step 5: Simplify (cancel $T_s$ in numerator and denominator)**

$$S(p) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$

**Step 6: Multiply numerator and denominator by $p$ to obtain the standard form**

$$S(p) = \frac{p}{pf + (1-f)} = \frac{p}{1 + f(p-1)}$$

This is the **standard Amdahl's Law** form used in KTU exams.

**Step 7: Derive the Asymptotic Bound**

As $p \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S(p) = \lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{p}{1 + f(p-1)} = \lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{p}{fp} = \frac{1}{f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic of Each Step:**
> - Step 1 decomposes the problem — this is the *partitioning* phase of design.
> - Step 2 applies the *mapping* phase.
> - Step 4 evaluates the *speedup* metric.
> - Step 7 shows the *fundamental limit of parallelization* — even infinite processors cannot overcome a serial bottleneck.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem:** A program has 20% serial code ($f = 0.2$). Calculate the speedup with $p = 4$, $p = 16$, and $p = \infty$.

**Solution for $p = 4$:**

$$S(4) = \frac{4}{1 + 0.2 \times (4-1)} = \frac{4}{1 + 0.6} = \frac{4}{1.6} = 2.5$$

**Solution for $p = 16$:**

$$S(16) = \frac{16}{1 + 0.2 \times 15} = \frac{16}{1 + 3.0} = \frac{16}{4.0} = 4.0$$

**Solution for $p = \infty$:**

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5$$

**Efficiency for $p = 16$:**

$$E(16) = \frac{S(16)}{16} = \frac{4.0}{16} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Doubling processors from 4 to 16 improved speedup from 2.5× to 4.0×, but the maximum possible is 5×. The 20% serial portion is the *bottleneck*. This is the central message of Amdahl's Law.

### 3.3 Derivation of Efficiency

Starting from $S(p) = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$:

$$E(p) = \frac{S(p)}{p} = \frac{T_s}{p \cdot T_p}$$

Substituting $T_p = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f)T_s}{p}$:

$$E(p) = \frac{T_s}{p \left( fT_s + \frac{(1-f)T_s}{p} \right)} = \frac{1}{pf + (1-f)}$$

This is the **efficiency formula** frequently tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Problem Classification by Design Issue

| KTU Question Pattern | Design Issue Tested | Formula to Apply | Common Pitfall |
|----------------------|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| "Calculate speedup for $f$% serial code" | Amdahl's Law | $S(p) = \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p}$ | Forgetting to use *fraction*, not *percentage* |
| "Justify if the system is scalable" | Scalability | Iso-efficiency analysis | Confusing strong vs. weak scaling |
| "Suggest a load balancing scheme" | Load Balancing | $\text{Imbalance Factor} = \frac{\max L_i}{\text{avg} L_i}$ | Confusing static vs. dynamic |
| "Compare fine vs. coarse grain" | Granularity | $CCR$ analysis | Ignoring $T_{comm}$ in CCR |
| "What is the dependency type?" | Data Dependency | RAW/WAR/WAW classification | Misidentifying output dependency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Interrelationship of Design Issues

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    issueA[Communication]
    issueB[Synchronization]
    issueC[Data Dependency]
    issueD[Load Balancing]
    issueE[Granularity]
    issueF[Scalability]
    issueG[Amdahl Law Limit]
    issueH[Performance Bottleneck]
    issueI[CCR Metric]
    issueJ[Efficiency Metric]
    issueK[Iso-Efficiency Metric]

    issueA --> issueI
    issueA --> issueE
    issueB --> issueH
    issueC --> issueB
    issueC --> issueA
    issueD --> issueH
    issueE --> issueI
    issueF --> issueK
    issueF --> issueJ
    issueG --> issueH
    issueI --> issueJ
    issueJ --> issueF
    issueK --> issueF
    issueH --> issueG

    classDef core fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef metric fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef problem fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class issueA,issueB,issueC,issueD,issueE,issueF core
    class issueI,issueJ,issueK metric
    class issueG,issueH problem
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Amdahl's Law Speedup Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pointA[Sequential: S=1, p=1]
    pointB[Small Parallel: S=2, p=4]
    pointC[Mid Parallel: S=4, p=16]
    pointD[Large Parallel: S=4.8, p=64]
    pointE[Asymptote: S=5, p=Infinity]

    pointA --> pointB
    pointB --> pointC
    pointC --> pointD
    pointD --> pointE

    classDef seq fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#000
    classDef par fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000
    classDef asym fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000

    class pointA seq
    class pointB,pointC,pointD par
    class pointE asym
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Design Issue Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputBlock[Sequential Problem Statement]
    analyzeBlock[Analyze Design Issues]
    partitionBlock[Partition Work]
    commBlock[Choose Communication Model]
    syncBlock[Insert Synchronization]
    balanceBlock[Apply Load Balancing]
    measureBlock[Measure Speedup and Efficiency]
    validateBlock[Validate Scalability]
    outputBlock[Parallel Program]

    inputBlock --> analyzeBlock
    analyzeBlock --> partitionBlock
    partitionBlock --> commBlock
    commBlock --> syncBlock
    syncBlock --> balanceBlock
    balanceBlock --> measureBlock
    measureBlock --> validateBlock
    validateBlock --> outputBlock

    validateBlock -.Feedback Loop.-> analyzeBlock

    classDef step fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#000
    class inputBlock,outputBlock step
    class analyzeBlock,partitionBlock,commBlock,syncBlock,balanceBlock,measureBlock,validateBlock step
    class validateBlock feedback
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows how all 7 design issues are interconnected and feed into the central metrics (CCR, Efficiency, Iso-efficiency).
> - The second diagram visualizes the diminishing returns of Amdahl's Law — speedup approaches a horizontal asymptote.
> - The third diagram is the **block-level architecture** a designer follows, with a feedback loop from validation back to analysis (iterative refinement).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1. Define Amdahl's Law. What does it fundamentally limit in parallel computing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Amdahl's Law, formulated by Gene Amdahl in 1967, expresses the theoretical maximum speedup $S(p)$ of a program when a fraction $f$ of its execution is serial and $(1-f)$ is parallelizable, executed on $p$ processors:

$$S(p) = \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p} = \frac{p}{1 + f(p-1)}$$

It fundamentally limits the achievable speedup by the **serial portion** of the code. As $p \to \infty$, $S(p) \to 1/f$, meaning even with infinite processors, a non-zero serial fraction creates an absolute ceiling on performance. This forces designers to **minimize serial code paths** and use techniques like **algorithmic restructuring** to push $f \to 0$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between static and dynamic load balancing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Load Balancing | Dynamic Load Balancing |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Decision time | Compile time | Runtime |
| Overhead | Low | High (scheduler cost) |
| Best for | Predictable workloads | Unpredictable/irregular workloads |
| Examples | Block-cyclic distribution | Work-stealing, work-sharing |
| Scalability | Limited under heterogeneity | Adapts to runtime conditions |

Static balancing assigns tasks to processors *a priori* based on estimation, while dynamic balancing redistributes work *during execution* via queues or stealing mechanisms. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**
>
> **Part B - Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Derive Amdahl's Law for speedup. Clearly define all variables. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A parallel program has 15% serial code. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable with (i) $p = 8$ and (ii) $p = 64$ processors. Also compute the efficiency in each case. Comment on your observations. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation of Amdahl's Law (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Let $T_s$ be the total sequential execution time of the program.

**Step 2:** Partition $T_s$ into serial and parallel fractions:
- Serial time: $T_{serial} = f \cdot T_s$
- Parallel time: $T_{parallel} = (1-f) \cdot T_s$

**Step 3:** On $p$ processors, the serial part remains unchanged at $f \cdot T_s$, but the parallel part is divided:

$$T_{parallel}^{(p)} = \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

**Step 4:** Total parallel execution time:

$$T_p = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

**Step 5:** Speedup is defined as $S(p) = T_s / T_p$:

$$S(p) = \frac{T_s}{fT_s + (1-f)T_s / p} = \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p}$$

**Step 6:** Multiplying numerator and denominator by $p$:

$$S(p) = \frac{p}{pf + (1-f)} = \frac{p}{1 + f(p-1)}$$

**Step 7:** As $p \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S(p) = \frac{1}{f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Key (7 marks):** Step 1 (1 mark) + Step 2 (1 mark) + Steps 3–4 (2 marks) + Step 5 (1 mark) + Step 6 (1 mark) + Step 7 + discussion (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 0.15$, so $(1-f) = 0.85$.

**Case (i): $p = 8$**

$$S(8) = \frac{8}{1 + 0.15 \times 7} = \frac{8}{1 + 1.05} = \frac{8}{2.05} \approx 3.90$$

$$E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{8} = \frac{3.90}{8} \approx 0.488 = 48.8\%$$

**Case (ii): $p = 64$**

$$S(64) = \frac{64}{1 + 0.15 \times 63} = \frac{64}{1 + 9.45} = \frac{64}{10.45} \approx 6.12$$

$$E(64) = \frac{6.12}{64} \approx 0.0956 = 9.56\%$$

**Observations (1 mark):**
- Speedup increased from 3.90× to 6.12× when processors increased 8×.
- Efficiency dropped drastically from 48.8% to 9.56% — classic diminishing returns.
- The maximum possible speedup is $1/f = 1/0.15 \approx 6.67$.
- Adding more processors beyond $p = 64$ yields negligible improvement due to the 15% serial bottleneck.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write $f = 15$ instead of $f = 0.15$. This gives a *speedup less than 1* and loses 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to compute efficiency — only speedup is asked, but KTU often bundles both. Lose 1 mark if efficiency is missing.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not commenting on observations. The 1-mark "comment" is mandatory; it tests conceptual understanding of Amdahl's bottleneck.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using $S(p) = p \cdot (1-f)$ instead of the correct formula. This is a wrong simplification that loses full marks.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 - Alternative Choice]**
>
> **Part B - Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain the design issues in parallel programming: communication, synchronization, and load balancing. Provide a real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider a parallel system with 4 processors. The workload consists of 4 independent tasks with execution times: $T_1 = 10$s, $T_2 = 20$s, $T_3 = 15$s, $T_4 = 5$s. Calculate the average load, load imbalance factor, and propose a load balancing strategy. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Explanation of Design Issues (7 marks):**

**1. Communication (2.5 marks):**
Communication is the mechanism by which cooperating processes exchange data. It exists in two forms:
- *Shared Memory:* Implicit communication via shared variables. Fast but limited scalability. Example: In a multi-core CPU, threads update a shared array.
- *Message Passing:* Explicit send/receive between processes. Example: In MPI, `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv` exchange data between cluster nodes.

**Real-world example:** A team of doctors collaborating on a patient's diagnosis — they exchange test results (data) through emails, calls, or shared hospital records (communication channels).

**2. Synchronization (2.5 marks):**
Synchronization enforces ordering between concurrent operations to prevent race conditions. Common primitives are locks, barriers, and semaphores.

**Real-world example:** A traffic signal at an intersection — it synchronizes the flow of vehicles from different directions to prevent collisions.

**3. Load Balancing (2 marks):**
Load balancing distributes work evenly across processors to minimize idle time. Strategies include static (compile-time) and dynamic (runtime, e.g., work-stealing).

**Real-world example:** A call center routing incoming calls to available agents — dynamically balanced to avoid one agent being overwhelmed while another is idle.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $p = 4$, $T_1 = 10$s, $T_2 = 20$s, $T_3 = 15$s, $T_4 = 5$s.

**Step 1: Average Load (2 marks)**

$$\text{Avg\_Load} = \frac{10 + 20 + 15 + 5}{4} = \frac{50}{4} = 12.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Load (1 mark)**

$$T_{\max} = \max(10, 20, 15, 5) = 20 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Load Imbalance Factor (2 marks)**

$$\text{Imbalance} = \frac{T_{\max}}{\text{Avg\_Load}} = \frac{20}{12.5} = 1.6$$

This means the slowest processor is 1.6× more loaded than the average — significant imbalance.

**Step 4: Propose Load Balancing Strategy (2 marks):**
- Use **dynamic work-stealing**: The processor handling $T_2 = 20$s is the bottleneck. Idle processors (after finishing $T_4 = 5$s and $T_1 = 10$s) can steal subtasks from $T_2$.
- Alternatively, **repartition** $T_2$ into two sub-tasks of 10s each and assign one to the processor that finishes early.
- After rebalancing: New loads become [10, 10, 15, 10] (approx) with $\text{Avg\_Load} = 11.25$s and imbalance factor $\approx 1.33$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting units in the imbalance factor (it's dimensionless but students write "1.6 seconds" — wrong, lose 1 mark).
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Recommending *static* balancing for an irregular workload. The KTU key explicitly prefers *dynamic* strategies for non-uniform tasks. Lose 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to mention the *bottleneck processor* ($T_2 = 20$s). The key requires explicit identification.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Skipping the "real-world example" in part (a). KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing example.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> - **Amdahl's Law (MOST HIGH-YIELD):** Memorize the standard form $S(p) = \frac{p}{1 + f(p-1)}$ and the asymptotic limit $S_{\max} = 1/f$.
> - **Serial Fraction $f$:** Always a *decimal* (e.g., 20% → $f = 0.2$, NOT 20).
> - **Efficiency Formula:** $E(p) = \frac{1}{1 + f(p-1)}$ — *always less than 1*.
> - **Three Types of Data Dependency:** RAW (flow), WAR (anti), WAW (output). RAW is the only *true* dependency.
> - **Communication Models:** Shared memory (implicit, fast, unscalable) vs. Message passing (explicit, scalable, overhead).
> - **Synchronization Primitives:** Lock, Barrier, Semaphore, Condition Variable. Over-synchronization = contention/deadlock. Under-synchronization = race condition.
> - **Load Balancing:** Static (compile-time, low overhead) vs. Dynamic (runtime, work-stealing). Choose dynamic for irregular workloads.
> - **Granularity Rule of Thumb:** Fine-grained for shared memory; coarse-grained for distributed memory.
> - **Scalability Metric:** Iso-efficiency — how much problem size must grow to maintain constant efficiency as $p$ increases.
> - **CCR Rule:** $CCR < 1$ → computation-bound (good); $CCR > 1$ → communication-bound (bad).
> - **Amdahl vs. Gustafson:** Amdahl = *fixed problem size* (strong scaling). Gustafson = *scaled problem size* (weak scaling). Both may appear in KTU.
> - **Real-World Examples (often asked for 2 marks):** Restaurant kitchen, hospital staff, traffic signals, call center — always have one ready.
> - **Always Comment:** KTU 14-mark answers without a final "observation/comment" lose 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Challenges and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Challenges and Applications # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Challenges in Parallel & Distributed Programming — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** > *Challenges in Parallel and Distributed Programming* refer to the set of architectural, algorithmic, and operational obstacles that emerge when computational workloads are decomposed, distributed, coordinated, and synchronized across multiple processing units (CPUs, cores, or networked nodes). These challenges arise from fundamental constraints such as finite bandwidth, non-uniform memory access (NUMA), communication latency, contention, and the need to maintain data consistency, determinism, and fault tolerance in a concurrent execution environment. In simple KTU-board terminology, challenges are the **"problems you inherit the moment you stop running your program on a single machine."** ## 1.2 Applications of Parallel & Distributed Programming — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** > *Applications* of Parallel and Distributed Programming are computational problem domains where the workload is inherently decomposable into independent or loosely-coupled sub-tasks. The motivation is to exploit **concurrency** for performance (speedup), **scalability** (handling growth), **availability** (fault tolerance), or **geographic distribution** (resource sharing across networks). Typical application classes include scientific simulation, data-intensive analytics, machine learning, web services, and embedded/real-time systems. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy (Intuition):** > Imagine a single chef cooking 100 dishes one after another — that is **sequential programming**. Now imagine 10 chefs cooking together. Sounds faster, right? But immediately new problems appear: > - Who gets the only oven? (**Resource Contention**) > - Two chefs need the same chopped onion at the same time (**Race Condition**) > - The head chef must shout instructions to all, slowing them down (**Communication Overhead**) > - If one chef quits, the order must still be served (**Fault Tolerance**) > - Some dishes (omelets) cook fast, others (biryani) take long — equal division is hard (**Load Balancing**) > > **The Challenges section lists every "headache" the head chef faces. The Applications section lists all the kitchens (domains) where this multi-chef setup is profitable.** ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Board-Favorite Constants) - **Speedup (S)**: ratio of sequential time to parallel time. Industry target: **S → N** (linear) where N = number of processors. - **Efficiency (E)**: Speedup per processor. KTU benchmark value: **E = 1** is ideal. - **Amdahl's Law ceiling**: maximum speedup is bounded by the serial fraction. - **Famous architectural constant**: **π ≈ 3.14159** (used in HPC benchmarks), **speed of light ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s** (sets physical latency floor for inter-node communication). - **Standard network metrics**: Latency in **microseconds (μs)**, Bandwidth in **Gigabits per second (Gbps)**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Eight Pillars of Challenges (Module 1 High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU 2024 ESE, students are expected to enumerate and briefly explain **at least 6 of the following 8 challenges**. Memorize the one-line definitions below. ### Pillar 1 — **Communication Overhead** - **Why it exists:** Processors must exchange data, but every message has a fixed cost (latency + serialization). - **How it hurts:** As more processors are added, inter-process communication traffic often grows super-linearly. - **Symptom:** Speedup saturates or even drops beyond a certain N. ### Pillar 2 — **Synchronization** - **Why it exists:** Concurrent tasks must coordinate access to shared resources (locks, barriers, semaphores). - **How it hurts:** Idle wait time; contention on lock variables. - **Tools:** Barriers (`MPI_Barrier`), mutexes, monitors, condition variables. ### Pillar 3 — **Load Balancing** - **Why it exists:** Workload may be unevenly distributed; some nodes finish early and sit idle. - **How it hurts:** Total wall-clock time = time of the *slowest* node. - **Strategies:** Static partitioning vs Dynamic scheduling (work-stealing, master-worker). ### Pillar 4 — **Data Partitioning & Decomposition** - **Why it exists:** Algorithm must be split into tasks and data into chunks — non-trivial for irregular problems. - **Types:** Domain decomposition, functional decomposition, dataflow decomposition. ### Pillar 5 — **Scalability Limits** - **Why it exists:** Memory bandwidth, cache coherence traffic, OS limits, Amdahl's serial fraction all cap N. - **Two flavors:** Strong scaling (fixed problem size) vs Weak scaling (problem grows with N). ### Pillar 6 — **Fault Tolerance & Reliability** - **Why it exists:** In distributed systems, nodes crash, networks partition, messages get lost. - **How it hurts:** Without replication/checkpointing, one failure kills the whole computation. - **Patterns:** Checkpoint-restart, active replication, Raft/Paxos consensus. ### Pillar 7 — **Memory Coherency & Consistency** - **Why it exists:** Each processor has local cache; a value updated in one cache must be visible to others. - **How it hurts:** Cache invalidation storms, false sharing. - **Protocols:** MESI, MOESI. ### Pillar 8 — **Debugging, Testing & Determinism** - **Why it exists:** Non-deterministic interleaving makes bugs (Heisenbugs) hard to reproduce. - **How it hurts:** Tools like Intel Thread Checker, ReplayEngine become necessary. --- ## 2.2 The Major Application Domains (Module 1 High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > For KTU 2024, examiners expect students to **map each application to a parallel programming class** (Embarrassingly Parallel, Data Parallel, Task Parallel, or Distributed Service). | # | Application Domain | Parallelism Class | Why Parallel? | |---|-------------------|-------------------|---------------| | 1 | **Scientific Simulation** (climate, fluid dynamics) | Data + Task | Petascale/Eascale compute needed | | 2 | **Big Data Analytics** (Hadoop, Spark) | Data Parallel | Datasets in TB-PB range | | 3 | **Machine Learning / Deep Learning** | Data + Model | Massive matrix multiplications | | 4 | **Web Servers & Cloud Services** | Distributed Service | Millions of concurrent requests | | 5 | **Bioinformatics** (gene sequencing) | Embarrassingly Parallel | Independent alignments | | 6 | **Graphics & Gaming (GPUs)** | Data Parallel | Pixel-level SIMD operations | | 7 | **Financial Modeling** (Monte Carlo) | Embarrassingly Parallel | Independent random walks | | 8 | **Cryptography & Blockchain** | Task Parallel | Brute-force key search, hashing | | 9 | **Real-time Embedded Systems** | Task Parallel | Sensor fusion, control loops | | 10 | **Database Systems** | Data Parallel | Parallel query evaluation | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (No Pipes Inside Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > Master every row. The KTU 2024 ESE paper will mix these formulas with derivations and numerical problems. | Symbol | Name | Formula | Meaning / Use | |--------|------|---------|---------------| | $S$ | Speedup | $S(N) = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p(N)}$ | Time saved by N processors | | $E$ | Efficiency | $E(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N}$ | Quality of parallelization | | $f$ | Serial Fraction | $f \in [0,1]$ | Portion that cannot be parallelized | | $A$ | Amdahl's Bound | $S(N) \le \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}}$ | Hard ceiling on S | | $G$ | Gustafson (Scaled) | $S(N) = N - (N-1) \cdot f$ | Scaled speedup when workload grows | | $K$ | Karp-Flatt Metric | $e = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S(N)} - \dfrac{1}{N}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{N}}$ | Experimentally finds serial fraction | | $\Phi_0$ | Isoefficiency | $\Phi_0 = \dfrac{T_s}{T_o}$ | Ratio of serial time to overhead | | $C$ | Cost | $C(N) = N \cdot T_p(N)$ | Processor-time product | | $R$ | Redundancy | $R = \dfrac{\text{extra resources}}{\text{minimum required}}$ | Fault-tolerance overhead | | $L$ | Latency | $L = T_{prop} + T_{trans}$ | Total message delay | > **Real-world utility:** Amdahl's Law is used by Intel/AMD to justify multi-core chip design limits. Karp-Flatt is the metric used in HPC papers at Supercomputing Conference (SC). Isoefficiency guides algorithm selection for clusters of 1,000+ nodes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amdahl's Law (From First Principles) > [!NOTE] > **Board Expectation:** Show all five steps. Marks are awarded for every algebraic transition. **Step 1 — Start with the timing decomposition.** Let the total sequential execution time of a program be $T_s$. We split $T_s$ into two parts: a serial portion (cannot be parallelized) and a parallel portion. $$T_s = T_{serial} + T_{parallel}$$ **Step 2 — Introduce the serial fraction $f$.** Define $f$ as the fraction of $T_s$ that is inherently serial. Hence: $$T_{serial} = f \cdot T_s \qquad \text{and} \qquad T_{parallel} = (1-f) \cdot T_s$$ **Step 3 — Apply N processors to the parallel part.** On $N$ processors, the parallel portion ideally divides perfectly, while the serial portion remains unchanged. $$T_p(N) = f \cdot T_s + \dfrac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{N}$$ **Step 4 — Form the speedup ratio $S(N)$.** $$\begin{aligned} S(N) &= \dfrac{T_s}{T_p(N)} \\ &= \dfrac{T_s}{f \cdot T_s + \dfrac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{N}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by $T_s$.** $$S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}}$$ This is **Amdahl's Law** — the most frequently tested formula in KTU Module 1. **Step 6 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$ to get the maximum possible speedup.** $$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f + 0} = \dfrac{1}{f}$$ **Numerical example (frequently asked):** If $f = 0.05$ (5% serial), then maximum speedup = $1 / 0.05 = 20\times$ even with infinite processors. This is the famous "5% serial rule" used in HPC lectures. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gustafson's Scaled Speedup **Step 1 — Acknowledge Amdahl's assumption (fixed problem size).** Amdahl assumes the *problem* stays the same and we throw more processors. Gustafson argues this is unrealistic for scientific computing — bigger machines are used for *bigger* problems. **Step 2 — Re-define parallel time in scaled form.** With N processors, problem size grows so that the *parallel* portion takes equal wall-clock time: $$T_p(N) = f \cdot T_s + (1-f) \cdot T_s = T_s \quad \text{(unchanged wall-clock)}$$ **Step 3 — Express the *equivalent sequential* time on the scaled workload.** $$T_s^{\prime}(N) = N \cdot f \cdot T_s + (1-f) \cdot T_s \cdot N \cdot \text{(re-scaled)}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the standard scaling model.** With weak scaling, sequential time grows linearly with the parallel portion: $$T_s^{\prime}(N) = (1-f) \cdot N \cdot T_s + f \cdot T_s$$ **Step 5 — Form the speedup.** $$\begin{aligned} S_{\text{Gustafson}}(N) &= \dfrac{T_s^{\prime}(N)}{T_p(N)} \\ &= \dfrac{(1-f) \cdot N \cdot T_s + f \cdot T_s}{T_s} \\ &= N - (N-1) \cdot f \end{aligned}$$ > **Key Insight (KTU favorite):** For $f = 0.05$ and $N = 1000$, Amdahl gives ~20× but Gustafson gives $1000 - 999(0.05) = 950.05\times$. This is why HPC centers (like PARAM Siddhi) report Gustafson numbers. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Karp-Flatt Metric (Experimental Serial Fraction) **Step 1 — Start from observed speedup.** $$S_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}}$$ **Step 2 — Invert both sides.** $$\dfrac{1}{S_{\text{obs}}} = f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}$$ **Step 3 — Expand the right-hand side.** $$\dfrac{1}{S_{\text{obs}}} = f + \dfrac{1}{N} - \dfrac{f}{N} = f \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right) + \dfrac{1}{N}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $f$.** $$f \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N}\right) = \dfrac{1}{S_{\text{obs}}} - \dfrac{1}{N}$$ $$\boxed{f = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S_{\text{obs}}} - \dfrac{1}{N}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{N}}}$$ This is the **experimentally measured serial fraction** — it includes *all* hidden overheads (communication, synchronization, load imbalance), unlike Amdahl's theoretical $f$. **Numerical example:** Suppose $N = 8$ processors give $S_{\text{obs}} = 5.0$. Then: $$f = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{5} - \dfrac{1}{8}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{8}} = \dfrac{0.200 - 0.125}{0.875} = \dfrac{0.075}{0.875} \approx 0.0857$$ So the *effective* serial fraction is ~8.6%, larger than the theoretical 5%, indicating hidden overheads of ~3.6%. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Matching Challenge to Solution > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a KTU 2024 high-yield table for short-answer and Part A questions. | # | Challenge | Quantitative Symptom | Standard Solution | |---|-----------|---------------------|-------------------| | 1 | Communication Overhead | Message volume $V$ grows with $N$ | Latency hiding (prefetch, async send), topology-aware mapping | | 2 | Synchronization Delay | Barrier wait time $T_b$ | Tree barriers, non-blocking sync, lock-free data structures | | 3 | Load Imbalance | $\max(T_i) - \bar{T}$ | Work-stealing, dynamic scheduling, master-worker | | 4 | Memory Contention | Cache miss rate increases | Privatization, padding to avoid false sharing | | 5 | Faults | MTTF drops with $N$ | Checkpoint/restart, replication, gossip protocols | | 6 | Cache Coherence | Invalidation traffic | Directory-based protocols, region-based coherence | | 7 | Scalability | $S(N)$ plateau | Algorithmic redesign, weak scaling (Gustafson) | | 8 | Debugging | Reproducibility index low | Deterministic replay tools, model checking | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The "Challenge Pyramid" — Hierarchy of Obstacles > [!NOTE] > This block-level architecture flow shows how the 8 challenges stack on top of one another in a real HPC system. Each layer depends on the layer below it. ```mermaid graph TD A0[APPLICATION LAYER
Web Services ML Climate Simulation] B0[ALGORITHM LAYER
Parallel Algorithms Decomposition Mapping] C0[CHALLENGE LAYER
Communication Sync Load Balance] D0[ARCHITECTURE LAYER
Multi Core NUMA Cluster] E0[PHYSICAL LAYER
Network Memory Cache] A0 --> B0 B0 --> C0 C0 --> D0 D0 --> E0 C1[Ch1 Comm Overhead] --> C0 C2[Ch2 Synchronization] --> C0 C3[Ch3 Load Balancing] --> C0 C4[Ch4 Data Partitioning] --> C0 C5[Ch5 Scalability] --> C0 C6[Ch6 Fault Tolerance] --> C0 C7[Ch7 Memory Coherency] --> C0 C8[Ch8 Debug Determinism] --> C0 ``` ## 4.2 Application-Class Taxonomy Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR P[Parallel and Distributed Applications] P --> EP[Embarrassingly Parallel] P --> DP[Data Parallel] P --> TP[Task Parallel] P --> DS[Distributed Service] EP --> EP1[Monte Carlo] EP --> EP2[Bioinformatics] EP --> EP3[Cryptographic Brute Force] DP --> DP1[Matrix Multiply] DP --> DP2[Image Processing] DP --> DP3[MapReduce Analytics] TP --> TP1[Pipeline Stages] TP --> TP2[Producer Consumer] TP --> TP3[Workflow DAG] DS --> DS1[Web Farms] DS --> DS2[Cloud Microservices] DS --> DS3[CDN Edge Networks] ``` ## 4.3 Speedup Curve Behavior — Block Comparison ```mermaid graph TD L[Linear Ideal Speedup S equals N] A[Amdahl Bound Plateaus at 1 over f] G[Gustafson Scaled Speedup Grows with N] K[Karp Flatt Real Hardware Drops from Linear] L --> Compare A --> Compare G --> Compare K --> Compare Compare[Compare on Realistic N Axis] ``` > **Read this diagram (left-to-right intuition):** > - **Linear** = perfect world, impossible in practice. > - **Amdahl** = fixed problem, real serial fraction caps it. > - **Gustafson** = scaled problem, speed keeps rising. > - **Karp-Flatt** = real hardware, drops below Amdahl because of hidden overheads. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark to 3-Mark Items) > Each question below is worth **3 marks**. Provide precise, 3–4 sentence model answers as expected by the KTU board examiner. --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **List and briefly explain any four major challenges in parallel and distributed programming.** **Model Answer (3 marks — full-marks answer):** 1. **Communication Overhead:** Processors must exchange messages, which incurs latency and bandwidth costs. As N grows, message traffic may exceed useful computation, capping speedup. 2. **Synchronization:** Concurrent tasks must coordinate via barriers, locks, or semaphores. Poor synchronization causes idle wait time and serialization bottlenecks. 3. **Load Balancing:** Work may be unevenly distributed, leaving some processors idle while others are overloaded. Total runtime is determined by the slowest node. 4. **Scalability Limits:** Hardware (memory bandwidth, network) and software (Amdahl's serial fraction) impose hard ceilings on how much N can be increased productively. *(Any 4 of the 8 pillars are accepted. Award 0.75 marks per correct challenge.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **Explain any three major applications of parallel and distributed programming with examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Scientific Simulation (e.g., Weather Forecasting):** Requires petascale computation for fluid dynamics equations — implemented using MPI on HPC clusters. 2. **Big Data Analytics (e.g., Hadoop/Spark):** Massive datasets in the order of TB–PB are processed via data-parallel MapReduce across commodity clusters. 3. **Machine Learning (e.g., Deep Learning Training):** Matrix multiplications in neural networks are accelerated using GPU data-parallelism (CUDA, cuDNN). 4. **Web/Cloud Services (e.g., Google Search):** Distributed service architecture handles millions of concurrent user requests with replication and load balancing. *(Any 3 are accepted. Award 1 mark per application with a valid example.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14-Mark Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **ONE full question** of their choice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Challenges Deep-Dive** > **[KTU University Exam — Expected Pattern 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks] With suitable diagrams and derivations, explain Amdahl's Law. Why does it impose a fundamental limit on parallel speedup?** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Amdahl's Law, formulated by Gene Amdahl in 1967, states that the maximum speedup $S(N)$ achievable by parallelizing a program on $N$ processors is limited by the fraction $f$ of the program that is inherently serial. **Derivation:** $$T_p(N) = f \cdot T_s + \dfrac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{N}$$ $$S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}}$$ **Limit Analysis:** $$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f}$$ **Numerical illustration (table the student should draw):** | N | f = 0.05 | f = 0.10 | f = 0.20 | |---|----------|----------|----------| | 10 | 6.89 | 5.26 | 3.57 | | 100 | 16.80 | 9.17 | 4.81 | | 1000 | 19.62 | 9.91 | 4.98 | | ∞ | 20.00 | 10.00 | 5.00 | **Why it's a fundamental limit:** Even with infinite processors, the serial fraction $f$ executes on a single processor, creating an unbreakable bottleneck. In practice, $f$ is rarely zero — it includes I/O, memory accesses, and unavoidable dependencies. **Real-world impact:** This is why CPU designers moved from frequency scaling (which hit a power wall) to multi-core: a 3 GHz, 8-core chip is more energy-efficient than a 24 GHz single core, but the *serial* portion of the workload still bounds performance. --- **(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the challenges of synchronization and load balancing in distributed systems. Mention suitable solutions.** **Model Solution:** **Synchronization Challenge:** - Multiple processes must coordinate access to shared resources (e.g., variables, files, devices). - **Symptoms:** Race conditions, deadlocks, priority inversion, convoying. - **Synchronization primitives:** Mutexes, semaphores, monitors, condition variables, barriers. - **Solutions:** - **Non-blocking synchronization** (compare-and-swap, lock-free queues) - **Hierarchical barriers** to reduce wake-up latency - **Transactional memory** for fine-grained atomicity **Load Balancing Challenge:** - Work distribution may be uneven, causing some nodes to idle while others are overloaded. - **Symptoms:** $\max(T_i) \gg \bar{T}$, low efficiency $E$. - **Strategies:** - **Static:** Round-robin, block-cyclic distribution (works for regular data) - **Dynamic:** Work-stealing (Cilk, TBB), master-worker pools (better for irregular) - **Adaptive:** Run-time profiling of node speeds with task migration **Trade-off:** Fine-grained dynamic balancing adds synchronization overhead. The optimal point is where load-imbalance cost equals migration cost. **Case study:** Google's MapReduce uses a *speculative execution* of straggler tasks — if a node is slow, the master launches a duplicate task on another node, and the first to finish wins. This converts a load-imbalance problem into a redundancy one. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Applications + Gustafson)** > **[KTU University Exam — Expected Pattern 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks] Enumerate at least six major application areas of parallel and distributed programming. Classify each as Embarrassingly Parallel, Data Parallel, Task Parallel, or Distributed Service.** **Model Solution:** | # | Application | Class | Justification | |---|-------------|-------|---------------| | 1 | Monte Carlo Option Pricing | Embarrassingly Parallel | Each random walk is independent | | 2 | Hadoop MapReduce Word Count | Data Parallel | Same `map` applied to each shard | | 3 | Deep Learning Training (CNN) | Data Parallel | Mini-batches split across GPUs | | 4 | Web Server Farm (nginx) | Distributed Service | Independent HTTP requests | | 5 | Pipeline Video Encoder | Task Parallel | Stages (decode → filter → encode) | | 6 | Climate Simulation (WRF) | Data + Task Parallel | Domain decomposition + I/O staging | | 7 | Blockchain Mining | Embarrassingly Parallel | Independent nonce search | | 8 | Database Query (parallel scan) | Data Parallel | Partitioned tables scanned in parallel | **Key observation:** Most real-world applications are *hybrid* — combining multiple classes. ML training uses data parallelism within a node and model parallelism across nodes. --- **(b) [7 Marks] Derive Gustafson's Law. Show that it overcomes Amdahl's pessimism in scaled computing environments.** **Model Solution:** **Premise:** Amdahl assumes fixed problem size. Gustafson (1988) observed that HPC users scale the *problem size* with N. **Derivation (repeat with explanation):** $$S_G(N) = N - (N-1) \cdot f$$ **Numerical comparison table (must be drawn):** | N | Amdahl S (f=0.05) | Gustafson S (f=0.05) | |---|-------------------|----------------------| | 10 | 6.89 | 9.55 | | 100 | 16.80 | 95.05 | | 1000 | 19.62 | 950.05 | | 10000 | 19.96 | 9500.05 | **Conclusion:** Gustafson's Law shows that as problem size grows proportionally with N, near-linear speedup is achievable because the serial portion is amortized over a much larger parallel workload. **Real-world relevance:** India's **PARAM Siddhi-AI** (AI-focused supercomputer) and **PRATYUSH** (CRAY XC40) use Gustafson-style weak scaling to handle ever-larger climate and AI workloads. **Critique of Gustafson:** It assumes the user always wants a *bigger* result, which is true for scientific computing but not for fixed-size transactional workloads (e.g., ATM networks). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in this Topic (Read Carefully):** > 1. **Writing $S(N) = N$ instead of $S(N) = N - (N-1)f$ in Gustafson questions** — Costs 2–3 marks. Remember: Gustafson has a *linear* dependence on N, not equality. > 2. **Forgetting the limit $\lim_{N \to \infty} S = 1/f$ in Amdahl derivations** — Examiners expect this line. Skipping it loses 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing Karp-Flatt $f$ with Amdahl's $f$** — Karp-Flatt's $f$ is *empirical* (includes all overheads); Amdahl's $f$ is *theoretical* (only true serial code). This is a classic 2-mark trap. > 4. **Listing challenges without examples** — Always pair each challenge with a *symptom* (e.g., "Load Imbalance → some processors idle while others overloaded"). Generic lists get only 50% marks. > 5. **Mixing up "embarrassingly parallel" with "distributed"** — Embarrassingly parallel is a *property of the algorithm* (minimal communication); distributed is a *property of the architecture* (nodes connected by network). The two are orthogonal. > 6. **Not labeling the axes of a speedup graph** — In Amdahl/Gustafson graphs, label X = N, Y = S(N), and mark the curve name.
