<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the Ring Algorithm for leader election in distributed systems. List any two assumptions made by the algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Ring Algorithm is a **leader election protocol** used in distributed systems where processes are organized in a logical unidirectional ring. When a process detects the failure of the current coordinator, it initiates an election by sending an election message containing its ID around the ring. Each process appends its ID and forwards the message. When the message returns to the initiator, the process with the **highest ID** is elected as the new coordinator.

**Two Key Assumptions:**
1. Processes are arranged in a **logical ring** with each process knowing its successor.
2. **Unique IDs** are assigned to all processes, and message delivery is **reliable** (no loss or corruption).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: What is the message complexity of the Ring Algorithm? Derive the total number of messages exchanged for $N$ processes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Ring Algorithm has a **message complexity of $2N - 1$** messages in the worst case.

**Derivation:**
- **Election Phase:** The election message traverses the entire ring, requiring $N - 1$ message transmissions (initiator sends once, then $N - 2$ forwards, then one return).
- **Announcement Phase:** The coordinator message must circulate the entire ring, requiring $N$ transmissions.

$$
M_{total} = (N - 1) + N = 2N - 1
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *election* and *announcement* message counts. The election phase sends **N−1** messages, NOT N. Also, do not forget to mention that message complexity is **deterministic** — it does not depend on which process initiated the election or where the failure occurred.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the Ring Algorithm for leader election in a distributed system. Consider 6 processes with IDs 22, 8, 14, 31, 17, and 5, arranged in a ring in that order. Process $P_3$ (ID=14) detects the coordinator failure. Trace the complete algorithm showing the message flow and identify the new coordinator. Also compute the total message complexity.**

#### **Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation with Diagram [7 Marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Ring Algorithm is a deterministic leader election protocol where processes are arranged in a logical unidirectional ring. The goal is to elect a unique coordinator following a coordinator failure.

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Detection:** Process $P_k$ detects that the coordinator is not responding (timeout).
2. **Initiation:** $P_k$ creates an *election message* containing its own ID and sends it to its successor.
3. **Forwarding:** On receiving an election message, each process $P_i$:
   - Appends its ID to the message.
   - Forwards the message to its successor.
4. **Termination:** The message returns to $P_k$ after traversing all $N$ processes.
5. **Selection:** $P_k$ selects the process with the **maximum ID** as the new coordinator.
6. **Announcement:** $P_k$ sends a *coordinator message* around the ring to inform all processes.

**Diagram (2 marks):** [Refer to Figure 4.1 above for the logical ring structure with 6 processes]

---

#### **Part (b) — Trace and Complexity Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given:** 6 processes with IDs: $P_1=22$, $P_2=8$, $P_3=14$, $P_4=31$, $P_5=17$, $P_6=5$. Initiator: $P_3$.

**Message Flow Table (4 marks):**

| Step | Sender | Receiver | Message Content |
|------|--------|----------|-----------------|
| 1 | $P_3$ | $P_4$ | $\{14\}$ |
| 2 | $P_4$ | $P_5$ | $\{14, 31\}$ |
| 3 | $P_5$ | $P_6$ | $\{14, 31, 17\}$ |
| 4 | $P_6$ | $P_1$ | $\{14, 31, 17, 5\}$ |
| 5 | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $\{14, 31, 17, 5, 22\}$ |
| 6 | $P_2$ | $P_3$ | $\{14, 31, 17, 5, 22, 8\}$ |

**Coordinator Selection (1 mark):**
$$
\text{New Coordinator} = \max\{14, 31, 17, 5, 22, 8\} = 31 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_4
$$

**Message Complexity (2 marks):**
- Election phase messages: $N - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5$
- Announcement phase messages: $N = 6$
- Total: $M_{total} = 2N - 1 = 2(6) - 1 = 11$ messages

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Compare the Ring Algorithm with the Bully Algorithm for leader election in distributed systems. Explain the Ring Algorithm in detail and state its advantages and disadvantages.**

#### **Part (a) — Detailed Ring Algorithm Explanation [7 Marks]**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The Ring Algorithm (LCR, 1977) organizes processes in a logical unidirectional ring for electing a coordinator.

**Detailed Working (4 marks):**

- When a process $P_i$ notices the coordinator is down, it creates an **election message** with its own ID and passes it to its successor.
- Each receiving process **adds its ID** to the message and forwards it.
- The message circulates through all processes and returns to the initiator with a complete list of IDs.
- The initiator identifies the **highest ID** and broadcasts a coordinator message.

**Properties (2 marks):**
- **Message complexity:** $2N - 1$
- **Time complexity:** $O(N)$ rounds
- **Deterministic:** Same message count regardless of initiator.

---

#### **Part (b) — Comparison with Bully Algorithm [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | Ring Algorithm | Bully Algorithm |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Topology** | Logical ring | No specific structure |
| **Worst-Case Messages** | $2N - 1$ | $O(N^2)$ |
| **Best-Case Messages** | $2N - 1$ | $N - 1$ |
| **Coordinator** | Highest ID | Highest ID |
| **Knowledge Required** | Local (successor) | Global (all processes) |
| **Election Speed** | Slower (sequential ring) | Faster (parallel OK messages) |
| **Robustness** | Sensitive to initiator failure | Self-recovering |
| **Synchronization** | Not required | Required (timeouts) |

**Advantages of Ring Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Lower worst-case message complexity: $2N - 1$ vs. $O(N^2)$.
2. No global knowledge needed; only successor information.
3. Works in asynchronous systems.

**Disadvantages of Ring Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. If the initiator fails mid-election, the algorithm blocks.
2. Slower than Bully in best-case scenarios.
3. Assumes a static ring topology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- ✅ **Full Name:** Ring Algorithm / LCR Algorithm (LeLann, Chang & Roberts, 1977).
- ✅ **Purpose:** Leader election in distributed systems with ring topology.
- ✅ **Election Messages:** Exactly $N - 1$ messages.
- ✅ **Coordinator Messages:** Exactly $N$ messages.
- ✅ **Total Message Complexity:** $\boxed{2N - 1}$ messages (deterministic).
- ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(N)$ rounds.
- ✅ **Coordinator Selection Rule:** Process with the **maximum unique ID** wins.
- ✅ **Topology Requirement:** Unidirectional logical ring.
- ✅ **Key Difference from Bully:** Lower worst-case messages ($2N-1$ vs. $O(N^2)$).
- ✅ **Limitation:** If the initiator crashes after sending the message but before the message returns, the election halts — requires additional fault-tolerance mechanisms.
- ✅ **Phases:** Two phases — *Election* and *Coordinator Announcement*.
- ✅ **Required Assumption:** No message loss; reliable FIFO channels.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always draw the ring diagram and provide a step-by-step message trace table for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Termination Detection - Spanning Tree-Based Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Termination Detection - Spanning Tree-Based Algorithm # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Termination Detection Problem **Termination Detection** is a fundamental problem in distributed systems: determining *globally* whether all processes in a distributed computation have finished their work, given that each process has only a *local* view of its own state. A process is **passive (idle)** if it has no pending internal computation and no outstanding messages to send; it is **active (engaged)** otherwise. The system is **terminated** when every process is passive and no application messages are in transit on any communication channel. The difficulty arises because: - No single process has a complete global snapshot of the system. - Messages may still be in transit on channels even after a sender has become passive. - Local idleness of every process does **not** guarantee global termination. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 1)**: Termination detection is the problem of designing a distributed algorithm that runs concurrently with the underlying computation and correctly decides (with guarantees of no false positives and eventual detection) when the entire distributed computation has terminated. ## 1.2 The Spanning Tree-Based Approach — Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Spanning Tree-Based Termination Detection (Dijkstra–Scholten, 1980)** is the canonical algorithm used for **diffusing computations** — computations that begin at a single process (the *initiator/root*) and spread outward as messages are exchanged. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Project Manager and the Team" Imagine a project manager (the **Root**) sitting at the top of an organizational tree. Each subordinate (an internal node) has some junior workers (children). A worker can only become *officially free* after both: 1. They themselves have finished their own pending work, **AND** 2. All the workers they delegated tasks to (their children in the tree) have reported back that they are also free. The workers send **acknowledgment signals (ACKs)** upward. The manager knows the entire project is complete *only when* every subordinate in the entire reporting tree has signaled back. This is precisely the spanning tree-based termination detection algorithm. ### Key Entities | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Root / Initiator** | The single process that starts the diffusing computation | | **Engaged Process** | A process that is part of the spanning tree and has not yet sent its ACK upward | | **Passive Process** | A process not in the tree (never received a message from an engaged process) | | **Signal / ACK** | A control message sent from a child to its parent indicating it is idle | | **Application Message** | The actual computational message of the user program | > [!NOTE] > The spanning tree dynamically grows as new processes are reached by messages and shrinks back as signals propagate up to the root. The tree is **always** a subset of the engaged processes. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Model and Assumptions The algorithm assumes a **diffusing computation** with the following properties: 1. Computation begins at exactly one process, called the **Root (R)**. 2. Only an engaged process may send a message to a non-engaged process; doing so causes the receiver to become a child of the sender. 3. Communication channels are **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) — this is essential for correctness. 4. Message delivery is **reliable** (no message loss). ## 2.2 The Two Types of Messages - **Application Messages (M)**: The actual computational traffic — these cause the tree to grow. - **Signal / ACK Messages (S)**: Control messages — these cause the tree to shrink. > [!IMPORTANT] > The algorithm piggybacks a boolean flag **`engage_ack_pending`** on each application message. When a process P sends an application message to Q: > - If Q is **not yet engaged**, then P becomes Q's parent, Q becomes engaged, and `Q.engage_ack_pending = true`. > - If Q is **already engaged**, no new tree edge is created (a cycle would otherwise form in a tree). ## 2.3 Detailed Algorithmic Rules Let each engaged process `P` maintain: - `parent(P)` — the process from which `P` first received an engagement message. - `children(P)` — the set of processes for which `P` is the parent. - `acks_received(P)` — count of signals received from children so far. - `idle(P)` — true if `P` has no pending computation and has no outstanding unsent messages. ### Rule Set (Operational Logic) **Rule 1 — Engagement of a New Process:** When an engaged process `P` sends an application message to a non-engaged process `Q`: 1. `parent(Q) ← P` 2. `children(P) ← children(P) ∪ {Q}` 3. `Q` becomes engaged with `acks_received(Q) ← 0`. **Rule 2 — Idle Process with Children:** When engaged process `P` becomes idle (i.e., has no pending local work and no application messages to send): - If `children(P) = ∅` (P is a leaf): immediately send a **Signal** to `parent(P)`. - If `children(P) ≠ ∅`: **wait** until `acks_received(P) = |children(P)|`, then send a Signal to `parent(P)`. **Rule 3 — Receiving a Signal:** When engaged process `P` receives a Signal from one of its children `C`: 1. `acks_received(P) ← acks_received(P) + 1` 2. If `idle(P) = true` AND `acks_received(P) = |children(P)|`: send Signal to `parent(P)`. **Rule 4 — Root Detection of Termination:** When the **Root R** becomes idle AND `acks_received(R) = |children(R)|`: **TERMINATION IS DECLARED**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Quantity / Property | Formula / Bound | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Number of Signals | $N - 1$ | One signal per non-root engaged process | | Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(D)$ | $D$ = depth of the spanning tree (max signal path) | | Message Complexity | $M_{\text{app}} + (N - 1)$ | Application messages + control signals | | Space per Process | $\mathcal{O}(\vert children(P) \vert)$ | Stores list of children + counters | | Termination Condition | $idle(R) \wedge acks\_received(R) = \vert children(R) \vert$ | Root decides termination | | Tree Invariant | $E = N - 1$ | Tree on $N$ engaged nodes has exactly $N-1$ edges | | No False Detection | Guaranteed by FIFO + ACK rules | Proof uses the *cut* property of the tree | | Eventual Detection | Guaranteed if app. computation terminates | Signals travel upward in finite time | > [!NOTE] > In the KTU Formula Sheet, $\vert x \vert$ denotes the cardinality (size) of a set or the absolute value of a numeric quantity. It is rendered here as `\vert x \vert` to preserve LaTeX validity inside the markdown table. ## 2.5 Why FIFO Channels Are Mandatory The correctness relies on a key invariant: > **The Signal from a child can only arrive at the parent after the last application message that child sent has been delivered and acknowledged by its own children.** If channels were not FIFO, a Signal could overtake an application message, causing the parent to believe the child is idle when, in fact, that child had already sent further work outward. This is a **false termination** — the algorithm forbids it. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility Spanning tree-based termination detection is used in: - **Parallel runtime systems** (e.g., MPI collectives in diffusive patterns). - **MapReduce-style frameworks** where the master is the root. - **Workflow orchestration engines** (e.g., Airflow, Luigi). - **Garbage collection in distributed object stores** (e.g., the tree-of-objects scan). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Traces, and Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Correctness Sketch — Step-by-Step Proof We prove two properties: **(P1) No false termination** and **(P2) Eventual detection**. ### Property P1 — No False Termination (Safety) We must show: if the Root declares termination, then the computation has truly terminated. **Step 1.** Suppose Root R declares termination. By **Rule 4**, both conditions hold: $$idle(R) = \text{true} \quad \wedge \quad acks\_received(R) = \vert children(R) \vert$$ This means R has received one signal from every process in `children(R)`. **Step 2.** Consider any process $C \in children(R)$. By the algorithm, $C$ sent a Signal to R only after: $$idle(C) = \text{true} \quad \wedge \quad acks\_received(C) = \vert children(C) \vert$$ So $C$ itself has received signals from all of its children. **Step 3.** By **induction on the depth** $d$ of any process in the tree, every engaged process $P$ at depth $d$ has all its descendants idle and itself idle. Since the tree is finite and the depth is bounded, this extends to all leaves. **Step 4.** Consider any edge in the tree $(P, Q)$ where $Q$ is a child of $P$. The last application message from $Q$ (if any) was sent **before** $Q$ became idle. Because channels are FIFO, by the time $Q$'s signal reaches $P$, this last message has been delivered. Hence no descendant of $P$ can be active when $P$ sends its own signal. **Step 5.** Since the tree edges cover exactly the set of engaged processes, and every engaged process is idle, and no application messages are in transit, the computation is terminated. $\blacksquare$ ### Property P2 — Eventual Detection (Liveness) **Step 1.** Suppose the underlying computation terminates at time $t_0$. After $t_0$, no process sends any new application message. **Step 2.** A leaf process (a process with no children) has no application messages to send. So it transitions to idle and immediately sends a Signal to its parent within finite time $t_0 + \delta_{\text{net}}$. **Step 3.** Internal processes wait for signals from children. Since each child eventually sends one (by induction on the depth from leaves to root), and FIFO channels guarantee ordered arrival, each internal process becomes "ready to signal" within finite time. **Step 4.** The Root receives the last signal no later than: $$t_{\text{detect}} \leq t_0 + D \cdot \delta_{\text{net}}$$ where $D$ is the tree depth. The Root then declares termination. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Trace of a 5-Process System Consider processes $P_1$ (Root), $P_2, P_3, P_4, P_5$. ### Initial Spanning Tree Setup Suppose $P_1$ sends application messages to $P_2$ and $P_3$. Then $P_2$ sends to $P_4$, and $P_3$ sends to $P_5$. The tree is: $$P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_4, \quad P_1 \rightarrow P_3 \rightarrow P_5$$ So: - $children(P_1) = \{P_2, P_3\}$, $children(P_2) = \{P_4\}$, $children(P_3) = \{P_5\}$, $children(P_4) = children(P_5) = \emptyset$. ### Step-by-Step Trace | Step | Event | Tree State | Signals in Flight | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | $P_1$ initiates; sends to $P_2, P_3$ | $P_2, P_3$ engaged | 0 | | 1 | $P_2$ sends to $P_4$ | $P_4$ engaged as child of $P_2$ | 0 | | 2 | $P_3$ sends to $P_5$ | $P_5$ engaged as child of $P_3$ | 0 | | 3 | $P_4$ becomes idle (leaf) | $P_4 \rightarrow Signal \rightarrow P_2$ | 1 | | 4 | $P_5$ becomes idle (leaf) | $P_5 \rightarrow Signal \rightarrow P_3$ | 2 | | 5 | $P_2$ receives signal from $P_4$ | $P_2$ now idle; $acks\_received(P_2) = 1$ | 1 | | 6 | $P_2$ sends signal to $P_1$ | Tree edge $P_2 \rightarrow P_1$ consumed | 1 | | 7 | $P_3$ receives signal from $P_5$ | $P_3$ now idle; $acks\_received(P_3) = 1$ | 1 | | 8 | $P_3$ sends signal to $P_1$ | Tree edge $P_3 \rightarrow P_1$ consumed | 1 | | 9 | $P_1$ receives both signals | $acks\_received(P_1) = 2$ | 0 | | 10 | $P_1$ is idle and $acks\_received(P_1) = 2 = \vert children(P_1) \vert$ | **TERMINATION DECLARED** | 0 | ### Verification of the Detection Bound In this example, $N = 5$ engaged processes and $N - 1 = 4$ signals were sent — exactly matching the formula. The depth $D = 2$ (longest path from root to leaf), so detection occurred within $2 \cdot \delta_{\text{net}}$ of real termination. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Spanning Tree (Dijkstra-Scholten) | Credit-Based (Mattern) | Chandy-Lamport | |---|---|---|---| | Computation Type | Diffusing | General | General | | Channels Required | FIFO | No FIFO | FIFO | | Control Messages | $N - 1$ | $2E$ or $2N$ | $2E$ | | Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(D)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ snapshot | $\mathcal{O}(D)$ | | Space per Process | $\mathcal{O}(\vert children \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(E)$ | $\mathcal{O}(E)$ | | Initiator | Required | Not required | Not required | | Symmetric | No (asymmetric tree) | Yes | Yes | | False Positives | None | None | None (uses probe/echo) | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU exams, the comparison table is a frequent 7-mark sub-question. Memorize the **columns** as dimensions and **rows** as algorithms. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams and Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Algorithm Flow (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Process Initiated as Root"] --> B["Send Application Messages"] B --> C{"Receiver Already Engaged?"} C -- "No" --> D["Add Receiver as Child
Set Receiver as Engaged"] C -- "Yes" --> E["Message Delivered
No Tree Change"] D --> F["Process Executes Local Work"] E --> F F --> G{"Process Becomes Idle?"} G -- "No" --> F G -- "Yes" --> H{"Has Children?"} H -- "No" --> I["Send Signal to Parent
Immediately"] H -- "Yes" --> J["Wait for Signals from
All Children"] J --> K{"All Children Signaled
AND Process Idle?"} K -- "No" --> J K -- "Yes" --> L["Send Signal to Parent"] I --> M["Deallocate from Tree"] L --> N{"Is This the Root?"} N -- "No" --> M N -- "Yes" --> O["TERMINATION DETECTED
Announce Globally"] M --> P["Continue Local Work
or Wait"] P --> G ``` ## 4.2 Spanning Tree State Diagram (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INIT["INITIATION PHASE"] ROOT["ROOT
P1
Engaged"] end subgraph GROW["TREE GROWTH PHASE"] P2["P2
Engaged
Child of P1"] P3["P3
Engaged
Child of P1"] end subgraph DEEP["DEEPER ENGAGEMENT"] P4["P4
Engaged
Child of P2"] P5["P5
Engaged
Child of P3"] end subgraph SHRINK["SIGNAL PROPAGATION"] S4["Signal S4
P4 to P2"] S5["Signal S5
P5 to P3"] S2["Signal S2
P2 to P1"] S3["Signal S3
P3 to P1"] end subgraph TERM["TERMINATION"] DEC["DECISION
at Root P1"] end ROOT -- "App Msg" --> P2 ROOT -- "App Msg" --> P3 P2 -- "App Msg" --> P4 P3 -- "App Msg" --> P5 P4 -- "Signal" --> P2 P5 -- "Signal" --> P3 P2 -- "Signal" --> ROOT P3 -- "Signal" --> ROOT ROOT --> DEC ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of the Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PROC["ENGAGED PROCESS MODULE"] direction TB L1["1. Message Handler
Receives App Messages & Signals"] L2["2. State Manager
Idle / Active + Children Set"] L3["3. Tree Manager
Parent Pointer + ACK Counter"] L4["4. Signal Sender
Generates Control Signals"] L5["5. Local Computation Engine
Runs User Code"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L2 --> L5 L5 --> L1 end subgraph NET["FIFO NETWORK LAYER"] N1["Reliable Ordered Channels"] end subgraph GLB["GLOBAL OBSERVER"] G1["Root Termination Decision
(idle AND acks == children)"] end PROC <--> N1 N1 --> G1 L4 -- "Signal" --> N1 N1 -- "Signal/App Msg" --> L1 ``` ## 4.4 Detailed Mermaid Sequence Diagram of Signal Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant R as Root P1 participant A as P2 participant B as P3 participant C as P4 participant D as P5 R->>A: Application Message R->>B: Application Message A->>C: Application Message B->>D: Application Message Note over C,D: Leaves finish local work C->>A: Signal (leaf, idle) D->>B: Signal (leaf, idle) Note over A,B: Internal nodes wait for child signals A->>R: Signal (after all children ACKed) B->>R: Signal (after all children ACKed) Note over R: idle(R) = true
acks_received(R) = 2 R-->>R: TERMINATION DECLARED ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > Define the *Termination Detection* problem in distributed systems. Why is it non-trivial to solve? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Termination detection is the problem of determining, in a distributed system, whether an ongoing parallel computation has completed — i.e., all processes have finished their local tasks and no application messages are in transit. It is non-trivial because (1) **no process has a global view** of the system, (2) messages may be in flight on channels even after senders are idle, and (3) local idleness does not imply global termination due to the possibility of pending messages triggering further work. A correct algorithm must guarantee **no false termination** (safety) and **eventual detection** (liveness). *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons.)* ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Model]** > State any **three** key assumptions of the Spanning Tree-Based Termination Detection algorithm (Dijkstra-Scholten). **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. The underlying computation is a **diffusing computation** — it starts at one process (root) and propagates outward via messages. 2. **Communication channels are FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) — necessary so that signals cannot overtake application messages. 3. **Message delivery is reliable** — no message is lost in transit. 4. (Bonus) A process becomes engaged only upon receiving a message from another engaged process. *(1 mark per assumption, any three accepted.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B offers a choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** #### (a) Explain the Dijkstra-Scholten Spanning Tree-Based Termination Detection algorithm in detail. State its key rules. (7 Marks) **Model Solution (7 Marks):** The **Dijkstra-Scholten algorithm (1980)** is designed for **diffusing computations** in distributed systems. It dynamically constructs a spanning tree of engaged processes rooted at the initiator. **Data structures maintained by each engaged process P:** - `parent(P)` — its parent in the tree (undefined for root). - `children(P)` — the set of children. - `acks_received(P)` — number of signals received from children. - `idle(P)` — boolean flag. **Key Rules (Award 2 marks for listing, 5 marks for explanation):** **Rule 1 (Engagement):** When an engaged process P sends an application message to a passive (non-engaged) process Q, then P becomes Q's parent, Q becomes engaged, and `acks_received(Q) = 0`. *(1 mark)* **Rule 2 (Leaf Signaling):** When a leaf process P (i.e., `children(P) = ∅`) becomes idle, it immediately sends a **Signal (ACK)** to its parent. *(1 mark)* **Rule 3 (Internal Signaling):** When an internal process P becomes idle, it waits until it has received signals from **all** its children. Only then does P send a Signal to its own parent. *(2 marks)* **Rule 4 (Root Decision):** The Root R, when idle and having received signals from all its children, declares the computation **terminated**. *(1 mark)* **Correctness Highlights (Bonus, 1 mark):** - **No false termination** because FIFO channels guarantee signals arrive only after all descendant application messages. - **Eventual detection** because every leaf eventually signals, propagating upward in finite time. --- #### (b) Consider the following 6-process system. Process $P_1$ (root) sends application messages to $P_2, P_3$. Then $P_2$ sends to $P_4, P_5$ and $P_3$ sends to $P_6$. Trace the algorithm and determine when termination is detected. (7 Marks) **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 (Tree construction, 2 marks):** The spanning tree formed is: - $P_1$ (root) → $P_2, P_3$ - $P_2 \rightarrow P_4, P_5$ - $P_3 \rightarrow P_6$ So $\vert children(P_1) \vert = 2$, $\vert children(P_2) \vert = 2$, $\vert children(P_3) \vert = 1$, and $P_4, P_5, P_6$ are leaves. **Step 2 (Leaf signal emission, 2 marks):** After completing local work, the leaves signal their parents: - $P_4 \rightarrow Signal \rightarrow P_2$ - $P_5 \rightarrow Signal \rightarrow P_2$ - $P_6 \rightarrow Signal \rightarrow P_3$ **Step 3 (Internal node signaling, 2 marks):** - $P_2$ receives signals from $P_4$ and $P_5$ (acks = 2 = |children|). Assuming $P_2$ is also idle, it sends a signal to $P_1$. - $P_3$ receives signal from $P_6$ (acks = 1 = |children|). Assuming $P_3$ is also idle, it sends a signal to $P_1$. **Step 4 (Root decision, 1 mark):** - $P_1$ receives signals from both $P_2$ and $P_3$ (acks = 2 = |children|). - $P_1$ is idle → **TERMINATION DECLARED**. **Verification:** Number of signals sent $= N - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5$. Indeed we traced 5 signals: $P_4, P_5, P_6, P_2, P_3$. ✓ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** #### (a) Draw the block diagram of the Spanning Tree-Based Termination Detection algorithm and explain each component. Discuss the correctness proof. (7 Marks) **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ ENGAGED PROCESS MODULE │ │ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ Message │ │ State │ │ │ │ Handler │←→│ Manager │ │ │ └──────────────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ ▲ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ ┌──────┴──────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ │ │ │ Tree │ │ Signal │ │ Local │ │ │ │ Manager │←→│ Sender │ │ Compute │ │ │ └─────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └─────────────┘ │ └──────────────────────▲│▲────────────────────────────┘ ││ ┌──────────┘└──────────┐ │ FIFO NETWORK LAYER │ └──────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ GLOBAL OBSERVER │ │ (Root Termination) │ └──────────────────────┘ ``` **Component Description (2 marks):** - **Message Handler**: Receives both application messages and signals; routes them to the State Manager. - **State Manager**: Maintains `idle(P)`, `children(P)`, `acks_received(P)`. - **Tree Manager**: Manages `parent(P)`, child set updates, engagement state. - **Signal Sender**: Emits ACK to `parent(P)` when conditions are satisfied. - **Local Compute Engine**: Runs user-defined computation that may send application messages. - **Global Observer (at Root)**: Evaluates the termination predicate `idle(R) ∧ acks_received(R) = |children(R)|`. **Correctness Proof (2 marks):** - **Safety**: By induction on tree depth, every engaged process that has signaled is genuinely idle. The FIFO property ensures that no in-transit application messages exist when the parent receives the signal. Hence Root's declaration is valid. - **Liveness**: Every leaf eventually becomes idle and signals. This propagates upward in finite time along a finite tree. Therefore termination is detected within $D \cdot \delta_{\text{net}}$ of actual termination. --- #### (b) Compare the Spanning Tree-Based algorithm with the Credit-Recovery and Chandy-Lamport algorithms on at least **five** parameters. (7 Marks) **Model Solution (7 Marks):** | Parameter | Spanning Tree (Dijkstra-Scholten) | Credit-Recovery (Mattern) | Chandy-Lamport | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Computation Type | **Diffusing** only | General | General | | 2. Initiator Required | Yes (asymmetric) | No (symmetric) | No (symmetric) | | 3. Channel Assumption | **FIFO mandatory** | No FIFO needed | **FIFO mandatory** | | 4. Control Messages | $N - 1$ signals | $2E$ or $2N$ | $2E$ markers | | 5. Time Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(D)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per round | $\mathcal{O}(D)$ | | 6. Space per Process | $\mathcal{O}(\vert children(P) \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(E)$ | $\mathcal{O}(E)$ | | 7. Detect Snapshots | No (only termination) | Optional | **Yes (global snapshot)** | | 8. Practical Use | MPI collectives, MapReduce | Distributed debuggers | Distributed snapshots, checkpoints | *(1 mark per valid comparison, up to 7. Each row above is a valid parameter; presenting 5+ earns full marks.)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: > - **Don't confuse "termination" with "snapshot"** — Chandy-Lamport can capture snapshots of ongoing computations; Dijkstra-Scholten only detects when the computation is fully done. > - **Don't claim Spanning Tree is symmetric** — it is inherently asymmetric (root vs. leaves). > - **Don't omit the FIFO assumption** — losing this point costs a full mark on the 7-mark sub-question. > - **In trace questions, do not skip the "idle condition check"** — explicit verification that `idle(P) = true` is required before signaling. > - **Always state N − 1 signals** as a derived result, not as an assumption. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following 12 points before the KTU exam:** - [ ] **Termination detection** = globally knowing when all processes are idle AND no messages are in transit. - [ ] **Spanning tree-based algorithm** (Dijkstra-Scholten, 1980) is the **canonical** algorithm taught in Module 1. - [ ] It applies to **diffusing computations** — those starting at one root process. - [ ] The **root** is the initiator; **engaged processes** form a tree rooted at the root. - [ ] **Application messages** grow the tree (child engagement). - [ ] **Signals (ACKs)** shrink the tree (propagate upward). - [ ] A **leaf** signals its parent as soon as it becomes idle. - [ ] An **internal node** signals its parent only after receiving signals from **all** children AND being idle. - [ ] The **root declares termination** when it is idle and has received signals from all its children. - [ ] **FIFO channels** are mandatory for correctness (no false termination). - [ ] Total number of **control signals** = $N - 1$ (where $N$ = number of engaged processes). - [ ] **Time complexity** = $\mathcal{O}(D)$ where $D$ is the depth of the spanning tree. ### Bonus Memory Aid > **"Leaves report, internal nodes wait, root decides."** > *(Write this in the margin of your answer sheet if you tend to forget the directionality of signal flow.)* ### Common Confusions to Avoid | Confusion | Clarification | |---|---| | "Signals travel downward" | **No** — signals travel **upward** (child → parent → ... → root). | | "The root is a special case for tree management" | **No** — root follows the same rules, except it is the one declaring termination. | | "The algorithm works for any distributed computation" | **No** — only **diffusing** computations. For general computations, use Chandy-Lamport or credit-based. | | "Idle means no local work" | **No** — idle means **no local work AND no outstanding unsent application messages**. |

topics.title: Distributed Mutual Exclusion Algorithms - System Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Distributed Mutual Exclusion Algorithms - System Model # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Distributed Mutual Exclusion (DME) **Distributed Mutual Exclusion (DME)** is a fundamental concurrency-control problem in distributed systems in which a set of independent processes, executing on different machines connected by a network, must coordinate access to a *shared critical resource* such that the following two classical conditions hold simultaneously: 1. **Safety (Mutual Exclusion):** At any instant of time, *at most one* process is allowed to execute inside its Critical Section (CS). 2. **Liveness (Progress / No Deadlock / No Starvation):** Every request to enter the CS is eventually granted, and the algorithm makes forward progress without indefinite postponement of any process. Because there is *no shared memory* and *no common physical clock* in a distributed environment, mutual exclusion cannot be enforced using simple hardware `TEST-AND-SET` or software `Dekker/Peterson` algorithms. It must instead be enforced by exchanging **messages** over a communication network. ## 1.2 The "System Model" – Formal Definition The **System Model** is the *precise mathematical specification* of the distributed system within which DME algorithms are designed and validated. It formally declares the assumptions about: - The set of processes ($P_1, P_2, \dots, P_N$) - The communication channels (reliable / unreliable, FIFO / non-FIFO) - The failure behaviour of processes (crash-stop, omission, Byzantine) - The existence or absence of a global clock - The atomicity of message-broadcast primitives - The structure of the request queue at each site > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *“The System Model provides the contract. Any DME algorithm (Lamport, Ricart-Agrawala, Maekawa, Token-Ring, Raymond’s Tree) is only valid and provably correct under the assumptions of the System Model under which it is derived.”* ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a single key that allows only one person to enter a special conference room at any given time, in a building where each person is on a different floor, cannot see the others, has no intercom, and must communicate *only by writing letters*. - **Without the key:** two people might enter simultaneously → safety violation. - **With the key:** whoever holds the key is inside; others must wait → safety guaranteed. - **The post office (network):** how letters travel; sometimes they get lost (channel reliability). - **The "letter-protocol" (algorithm):** the rules each person follows when requesting/relinquishing the key. The *System Model* defines the rules of the post office, the letter-writing language, what happens if a person leaves the building mid-meeting (failure), and how letters are sequenced (FIFO/non-FIFO). All DME algorithms (Lamport, Ricart-Agrawala, etc.) are different "letter-protocols" written against this shared contract. ## 1.4 Key Terminology | Term | Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Critical Section (CS)** | The code segment of a process that accesses the shared resource. | | **Request Queue ($RQ_i$)** | A FIFO buffer at site $i$ that stores pending requests from other sites, ordered by timestamps. | | **Channel** | A logical communication link between two sites over which messages are exchanged. | | **Quorum** | A subset of sites whose collective permission is required to enter the CS. | | **Token** | A special control message whose unique possession grants entry into the CS. | | **FIFO Channel** | A channel that preserves the order in which messages are sent. | > [!NOTE] > **Quick Mnemonic:** *"No Shared Memory → Use Messages. No Common Clock → Use Logical Timestamps. No Guarantee → Explicitly State It in the System Model."* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of the System Model The DME System Model is composed of five tightly interlocked layers. The strength (and complexity) of any DME algorithm depends entirely on which layer assumptions are active. ### 2.1.1 Layer 1 – Process Layer - The system has a **fixed set** $\mathcal{P} = \{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_N\}$ of $N$ processes. - Each $P_i$ is *sequential* and executes the canonical cycle: **Non-CS → TRY → CS → EXIT → Non-CS**. - A process may *request* the CS only after leaving the previous one. ### 2.1.2 Layer 2 – Communication Layer - Processes communicate **only via messages** over point-to-point channels. - Two sub-models: * **Reliable channels:** Every sent message is eventually delivered exactly once. * **Unreliable channels:** Messages may be lost, duplicated, or reordered. - Ordering variants: * **FIFO (TCP-like):** Messages between $i$ and $j$ arrive in send order. * **Non-FIFO (UDP-like):** No ordering guarantee. ### 2.1.3 Layer 3 – Synchrony / Clock Layer - **Asynchronous model:** No bound on relative process speeds or message delays. - **Synchronous model:** Known upper bounds exist on message delivery time and process speed. - **Partially synchronous:** Holds only during stable intervals. - **Logical clocks (Lamport / Vector):** Provide causal ordering instead of physical time. ### 2.1.4 Layer 4 – Failure Layer | Failure Model | Behaviour | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **No Failures (Crash-Free)** | All processes are perfectly correct. | Classic teaching algorithms (Lamport, Ricart-Agrawala). | | **Crash-Stop (Fail-Stop)** | A process halts permanently and is detected. | Token-recovery variants. | | **Omission Failure** | A process intermittently skips send/receive. | Realistic networks. | | **Byzantine Failure** | Arbitrary malicious behaviour. | Blockchain, secure systems. | ### 2.1.5 Layer 5 – Broadcast Layer - **Best-effort broadcast:** Delivered to available recipients; no delivery guarantee. - **Reliable broadcast:** Either all correct processes deliver a message, or none do. - **Atomic broadcast:** Reliable broadcast + total order of delivery across all sites. ## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Requirements of DME For *every* algorithm evaluated against this system model: 1. **ME1 – Mutual Exclusion (Safety):** At any instant $\vert CS(t) \vert \le 1$. 2. **ME2 – No Starvation (Fairness):** If $P_i$ requests the CS at time $t_i$ and no other process holds it forever after, $P_i$ enters the CS within finite time. 3. **ME3 – No Deadlock (Liveness):** Two or more processes never wait on each other indefinitely. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Performance Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Memory Bank:** Memorise the four metrics below — they appear in virtually every Part-B question. | Metric | Definition | Formula / Symbol | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Message Complexity** | Number of messages exchanged per CS entry. | $M$ | messages | | **Synchronization Delay** | Time between *exit* of one CS and *entry* of the next. | $D_s$ | time units | | **Response Time** | Time from issue of REQUEST to entry into CS. | $R_t$ | time units | | **Throughput** | Rate of CS executions per unit time (under continuous load). | $\mathcal{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cs} + D_s}$ | entries / time | Where: - $T_{cs}$ = Average duration of one critical section execution. - $D_s$ = Synchronization delay. ### 2.3.1 Key Complexities of Standard Algorithms (under the System Model) | Algorithm | Messages per CS entry | Synchronization Delay | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Centralized (Server-based)** | $3$ | $2 \times T$ | | **Lamport (1978)** | $3(N-1)$ | $T$ | | **Ricart-Agrawala (1981)** | $2(N-1)$ | $T$ | | **Maekawa (1985)** | $O(\sqrt{N})$ | $2T$ | | **Token Ring** | $0$ to $\infty$ (avg $N/2$) | $0$ to $N \cdot T$ | | **Raymond’s Tree** | $O(\log N)$ | $\tfrac{T \cdot L_{req} + T \cdot L_{rel}}{2}$ | Where $T$ = maximum message-propagation delay and $N$ = number of sites. ### 2.3.2 Critical Boundary Condition > [!IMPORTANT] > Under **reliable FIFO channels** + **asynchronous clocks**, the *minimum* number of messages required per CS entry is **$2(N-1)$** (Ricart-Agrawala lower bound). Any algorithm claiming fewer requires either an atomic-broadcast primitive or a logical-token invariant. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | DME System Model Applied | | :--- | :--- | | **Distributed Databases** | Row-level locking across shards (Spanner, CockroachDB). | | **Cloud Microservices** | Leader election + lease-based mutex (etcd, ZooKeeper). | | **Operating Systems** | Extensions of monitors across NUMA nodes. | | **Industrial Control / IoT** | Time-bounded CS for safety-critical actuators. | | **Blockchain Consensus** | Byzantine-tolerant mutex layer (PBFT HotStuff). | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section develops the analytical backbone of the System Model — from the *safety proof structure* to the *performance-metric derivations* that frequently appear in **KTU Part-B derivations**. ## 3.1 Derivation of the Message-Complexity Lower Bound (Ricart-Agrawala) **Claim:** Under reliable, FIFO channels, without an atomic broadcast, any DME algorithm needs *at least* $2(N-1)$ messages per CS entry. ### Proof Outline (Step-by-Step) **Step 1 – Counting the receiver set.** Suppose process $P_k$ wants to enter the CS. To guarantee that no other process is simultaneously inside, $P_k$ must inform *every* other process. Otherwise, some silent process might itself enter the CS, violating **ME1**. $$ \text{Messages emitted} \ge N - 1 $$ **Step 2 – Counting the replies.** To *confirm* absence of contention, $P_k$ must receive an acknowledgment (or implicit permission) from each of the other $N - 1$ processes. $$ \text{Messages received} \ge N - 1 $$ **Step 3 – Total per entry.** Adding emission + reception: $$ M_{min} = (N - 1) + (N - 1) = 2(N - 1) $$ **Step 4 – Conclusion.** $$ \boxed{M_{min} \;=\; 2(N-1) \;\; \text{messages/CS-entry}} $$ Hence, Lamport’s $3(N-1)$ is *suboptimal*; Ricart-Agrawala attains the *exact* bound by piggy-backing a release message onto the reply, eliminating the dedicated RELEASE message. ## 3.2 Derivation of Synchronization Delay ($D_s$) **Definition:** Synchronization delay is the interval between the EXIT of one process and the ENTRY of the *next* process into the CS, under a continuous load. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 – Identify the events.** Let $T_{exit}$ = time the current CS-holder emits its EXIT/RELEASE message. Let $T_{next\_enter}$ = time the next process actually begins its CS. **Step 2 – Maximum time for the EXIT to traverse the network.** $$ \Delta_1 = T \; \text{(propagation to slowest site)} $$ **Step 3 – Maximum time for the chosen next process to receive the EXIT and reach the CS.** $$ \Delta_2 = T \; \text{(propagation back)} $$ **Step 4 – Add local processing slack (negligible vs. $T$ in async systems).** $$ D_s = \Delta_1 + \Delta_2 = 2T $$ However, when the *next* process has already been *primed* (already holding permissions, e.g., in token-ring with greedy token transfer), only the relay delay counts: $$ D_s^{greedy} = T $$ **Step 5 – Final consolidated formula.** $$ \boxed{D_s \;=\; \begin{cases} 2T & \text{for permission-based (Ricart/Lamport)} \\ T & \text{for token-ring with greedy handoff} \\ kT & \text{for hierarchical / tree-based schemes where } k = \log_{b} N \end{cases}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Steady-State Throughput ($\mathcal{T}$) **Step 1 – Consider the critical section as a service facility.** The CS behaves like a single-server queue with deterministic service time $T_{cs}$. **Step 2 – Inter-arrival spacing.** Under continuous load with synchronization delay $D_s$, the *minimum* time between two successive CS executions is: $$ T_{cycle} = T_{cs} + D_s $$ **Step 3 – Throughput (Little's Law at the server).** $$ \mathcal{T} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{T_{cs} + D_s} $$ **Step 4 – Substituting Ricart-Agrawala $D_s = T$:** $$ \boxed{\mathcal{T}_{RA} = \frac{1}{T_{cs} + T}} $$ **Step 5 – Substituting Lamport $D_s = T$:** $$ \mathcal{T}_{Lamport} = \frac{1}{T_{cs} + T} \quad \text{(same D_s, but } M \text{ is higher, so network congestion differs)} $$ > [!NOTE] > The throughput formula is identical for Lamport & Ricart-Agrawala *in isolation*; the practical difference emerges under heavy contention, where the extra $N-1$ messages of Lamport create network congestion, indirectly reducing $\mathcal{T}$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of System-Model Assumptions on Algorithm Suitability | System-Model Assumption | Lamport | Ricart-Agrawala | Maekawa | Token Ring | Raymond’s Tree | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Asynchronous clocks | ✅ (uses Lamport clocks) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | No failures | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Crash failures tolerated | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (token loss) | ✅ (via regeneration) | | Reliable FIFO channels | Required | Required | Required | Required | Required | | Message complexity | $3(N-1)$ | $2(N-1)$ | $\le 3\sqrt{N}$ | Avg $N/2$ | $4 \log_2 N$ | | Synchronization delay | $T$ | $T$ | $2T$ | $\le NT$ | $T \log_2 N$ | ## 3.5 Derivation of the Quorum-Intersection Property (Maekawa’s Foundation) Maekawa’s algorithm generalises from the *all-sites* quorum of Lamport to *minimal* quorums. The condition for mutual exclusion is: $$ \forall i, j \in \{1..N\}, \; Q_i \cap Q_j \ne \emptyset $$ That is, **the intersection of any two quorums is non-empty**, ensuring that no two processes can simultaneously receive permission from disjoint sets. For Maekawa’s projective-plane construction: $$ |Q_i| = K \quad \text{where } K = \sqrt{N} $$ and the total number of messages per entry is: $$ M = 2(K - 1) + K = 3K - 2 \approx 3\sqrt{N} $$ This is the mathematical justification for Maekawa’s $O(\sqrt{N})$ scaling. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram A – Distributed System Model Architecture (Logical View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph site1["SITE P1"] app1["Application Layer"] cs1["Critical Section Module"] rq1["Request Queue RQ1"] app1 --> cs1 cs1 --> rq1 end subgraph site2["SITE P2"] app2["Application Layer"] cs2["Critical Section Module"] rq2["Request Queue RQ2"] app2 --> cs2 cs2 --> rq2 end subgraph site3["SITE P3"] app3["Application Layer"] cs3["Critical Section Module"] rq3["Request Queue RQ3"] app3 --> cs3 cs3 --> rq3 end subgraph site4["SITE PN"] appN["Application Layer"] csN["Critical Section Module"] rqN["Request Queue RQN"] appN --> csN csN --> rqN end NET["Communication Network
Asynchronous FIFO Channel"] site1 <-- message passing --> NET site2 <-- message passing --> NET site3 <-- message passing --> NET site4 <-- message passing --> NET style NET fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px style cs1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d style cs2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d style cs3 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d style csN fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram enforces the **no-shared-memory** axiom: the only interaction between sites is the message-passing network. ## 4.2 Diagram B – Process State Machine (DME Lifecycle) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> stateIdle stateIdle: STATE IDLE
Non CS Code Running stateRequest: STATE REQUEST
Broadcast REQ with Timestamp stateWait: STATE WAIT
Awaiting REPLY from Quorum stateCS: STATE CS
Executing Critical Section stateRelease: STATE RELEASE
Send RELEASE to Queue stateIdle --> stateRequest : REQUEST CS stateRequest --> stateWait : REQ Sent stateWait --> stateCS : Quorum Replies Received stateCS --> stateRelease : Exit CS stateRelease --> stateIdle : Defer Pending REQs stateWait --> stateRequest : New REQ after Deflection ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Each state transition is *triggered by message arrival*, not by a clock — this is the *asynchronous* core of the System Model. ## 4.3 Diagram C – Message Exchange Sequence (Ricart-Agrawala Template) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant P1 as Process P1 participant P2 as Process P2 participant P3 as Process P3 P1->>P2: REQUEST ts1 P1->>P3: REQUEST ts1 P2-->>P1: REPLY (deferred if conflict) P3-->>P1: REPLY (deferred if conflict) Note over P1: All Replies Received P1->>P1: ENTER CRITICAL SECTION P1->>P2: RELEASE implicit in REPLY P1->>P3: RELEASE implicit in REPLY Note over P1,P3: Mutual Exclusion Maintained ``` ## 4.4 Diagram D – Block-Level Functional Architecture of DME ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Application Layer] --> B[Request Initiator] B --> C[Timestamp Generator
Lamport Logical Clock] C --> D[Message Composer] D --> E[Network Layer
Reliable FIFO Channel] E --> F[Receiver Queue Manager] F --> G[Conflict Resolver
Priority Logic] G --> H[Reply Generator] H --> I[Decision Engine
Grant or Defer] I --> J[Critical Section Entry] J --> K[Resource Access Module] K --> L[Exit and Release Handler] L --> E style C fill:#fdebd0 style G fill:#fadbd8 style I fill:#d5f5e3 style J fill:#aed6f1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define the System Model for Distributed Mutual Exclusion. List its key assumptions.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The *System Model* for DME specifies the assumptions under which distributed mutual-exclusion algorithms are designed. Its key assumptions are: 1. The system consists of $N$ independent processes $\{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_N\}$, each with a critical section. 2. Processes communicate **only by exchanging messages** — there is no shared memory. 3. There is **no common physical clock**; logical (Lamport) timestamps are used. 4. Communication channels are **reliable** and usually **FIFO**. 5. Processes may have a **request queue** $RQ_i$ to hold pending requests ordered by timestamp. 6. Optionally, processes may be subject to **crash-stop failures**. **(3 marks – full credit for listing 5–6 assumptions.)** --- ### **Question 2: What is the difference between synchronisation delay and response time in DME?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Synchronization Delay ($D_s$) | Response Time ($R_t$) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Time between *exit* of one CS and *entry* of the *next* CS. | Time from when a process *issues* its REQUEST to its *actual* CS entry. | | **Scope** | System-wide property. | Per-process, per-request property. | | **Depends on** | Network latency and algorithm class. | Request queue length + network latency + CS duration. | | **Ideal Value** | $T$ (one message delay). | $2T + T_{cs}$. | **(3 marks – credit for correct definitions and distinguishing scope.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks): Detailed Treatment of System Model** > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** #### (a) Explain the assumptions of the System Model used in distributed mutual exclusion algorithms. Discuss the impact of each assumption on algorithm correctness and complexity. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The System Model for DME is built upon the following assumptions, each of which has direct consequences on algorithm behaviour: **1. Distributed processes with no shared memory:** Processes reside on independent machines; the only synchronisation primitive is *message passing*. *Impact:* all DME algorithms must be designed around message-exchanges rather than atomic read/write of shared variables, fundamentally raising message complexity. **2. Reliable FIFO channels:** Every sent message is delivered exactly once, and in send-order. *Impact:* guarantees that Lamport timestamps preserve causal order, allowing simple comparison of $ts(req_i) < ts(req_j)$ to resolve conflicts deterministically. Without FIFO, vector clocks are needed. **3. Asynchronous system:** No bound on process speeds or message delays. *Impact:* algorithms must be *wait-free* and *tolerant* of arbitrary delays — only a *majority* or *quorum* argument guarantees safety. **4. No common clock:** Sites cannot read wall-clock time of other sites. *Impact:* all DME algorithms use *logical clocks* (Lamport scalar or vector clocks) to totally order events. **5. Failure-free process model (baseline):** All processes follow the protocol faithfully. *Impact:* simplifies proofs and is the assumed baseline of Lamport, Ricart-Agrawala, and Maekawa. Crash-tolerance extensions require recovery protocols (e.g., Raymond’s tree token regeneration). **6. Atomic broadcast (optional):** When available, it permits $O(1)$ messages per CS entry. *Impact:* sets the theoretical lower bound on message count. --- #### (b) With neat sketches, derive the message complexity and synchronisation delay for Lamport’s and Ricart-Agrawala’s DME algorithms. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Lamport’s Algorithm — Step-by-Step:** 1. Process $P_i$ sends `REQUEST(ts_i, i)` to **all** $N-1$ other sites → **$N-1$ messages**. 2. On receiving `REQUEST`, every other site $P_j$: - Adds the request to $RQ_j$ in timestamp order. - Sends a `REPLY` to $P_i$ only if $P_j$ is **not** in CS and has no earlier pending request → **$N-1$ REPLY messages**. 3. $P_i$ enters CS after receiving all $N-1$ REPLYs. 4. Upon exit, $P_i$ sends `RELEASE` to every site whose request was deferred → **$N-1$ RELEASE messages**. $$ \boxed{M_{Lamport} = 3(N - 1) \text{ messages per CS entry}} $$ Synchronization delay: only the *next* request must arrive and propagate its REPLY → $$ \boxed{D_{s,\,Lamport} = T} $$ **Ricart-Agrawala Algorithm — Step-by-Step:** 1. $P_i$ sends `REQUEST(ts_i, i)` to all other sites → **$N-1$ messages**. 2. Each $P_j$ sends `REPLY` if and only if: - $P_j$ is not requesting, OR - $P_j$ is requesting but $(ts_i, i) < (ts_j, j)$. Otherwise, $P_j$ *defers* the reply. 3. $P_i$ enters CS after receiving all $N-1$ REPLYs → **$N-1$ REPLYs received**. 4. Upon exit, $P_i$ simply sends the deferred REPLYs in timestamp order → **0 dedicated RELEASE messages**, folded into the next reply. $$ \boxed{M_{RA} = 2(N - 1) \text{ messages per CS entry}} $$ $$ \boxed{D_{s,\,RA} = T} $$ **Comparison Table:** | Metric | Lamport | Ricart-Agrawala | Improvement | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Messages / CS | $3(N-1)$ | $2(N-1)$ | $33\%$ saving | | Synchronization Delay | $T$ | $T$ | — | | Storage | $N$ queue slots | $N$ queue slots | — | Hence Ricart-Agrawala *optimises* Lamport by piggy-backing the release semantics onto the reply mechanism. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative — Focus on Performance Metrics** > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** #### (a) Define and derive the throughput metric for a DME algorithm under continuous load. Discuss how Lamport and Ricart-Agrawala compare. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Throughput ($\mathcal{T}$) is the number of *successful* critical-section entries per unit time when every process continuously demands entry. **Derivation:** Model the CS as a single-server FIFO queue. Let: - $T_{cs}$ = average CS execution duration. - $D_s$ = synchronization delay between consecutive CS entries. The minimum time between the start of one CS and the start of the next is: $$ T_{cycle} = T_{cs} + D_s $$ Therefore, by elementary queueing theory (single-server, deterministic service): $$ \mathcal{T} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{T_{cs} + D_s} $$ For Lamport and Ricart-Agrawala, $D_s = T$ (one message delay). Thus: $$ \mathcal{T}_{Lamport} = \mathcal{T}_{RA} = \frac{1}{T_{cs} + T} $$ **Practical Caveat:** Under *high contention*, the $33\%$ extra messages of Lamport saturate the network bandwidth, indirectly increasing *effective* $T$ and thereby *reducing* $\mathcal{T}_{Lamport}$ relative to Ricart-Agrawala. **Maekawa Comparison:** Maekawa’s $D_s = 2T$ yields: $$ \mathcal{T}_{Maekawa} = \frac{1}{T_{cs} + 2T} $$ which is *worse* than Lamport/RA in throughput, but it scales to large $N$ with fewer messages. --- #### (b) A distributed system has 10 sites. Compute the message complexity per CS entry for Lamport, Ricart-Agrawala, and Maekawa (assume projective plane construction with $K = \sqrt{N}$). Also state the synchronisation delay in terms of $T$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Given: $N = 10$. **Lamport:** $$ M_{Lamport} = 3(N-1) = 3 \times 9 = 27 \text{ messages} $$ $$ D_s = T $$ **Ricart-Agrawala:** $$ M_{RA} = 2(N-1) = 2 \times 9 = 18 \text{ messages} $$ $$ D_s = T $$ **Maekawa:** $$ K = \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 \implies K = 4 \text{ (rounded to nearest projective-plane integer)} $$ $$ M_{Maekawa} \approx 3K - 2 = 3 \times 4 - 2 = 10 \text{ messages} $$ $$ D_s = 2T $$ **Comparison Table:** | Algorithm | Messages / CS | $D_s$ | Notes | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Lamport | $27$ | $T$ | Highest overhead, simplest logic | | Ricart-Agrawala | $18$ | $T$ | Optimal under no-broadcast assumption | | Maekawa | $10$ | $2T$ | Best message count, slower hand-off | **Inference:** Maekawa achieves the lowest message count at the cost of slightly higher $D_s$. Ricart-Agrawala strikes the best balance for $N=10$, achieving both minimum-messages and minimum-delay. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Lamport and Ricart-Agrawala message counts:** Students frequently write $3(N-1)$ for RA — *this is wrong*. RA is $2(N-1)$. > 2. **Forgetting FIFO assumption:** When asked to justify timestamp ordering, always *explicitly mention* the FIFO channel assumption; without it, scalar Lamport clocks fail. > 3. **Ignoring the request queue:** A correct explanation of mutual exclusion must mention that deferred requests are *enqueued in $RQ_i$* in timestamp order, not forgotten. > 4. **Throughput formula missing $T_{cs}$:** Writing $\mathcal{T} = 1/D_s$ instead of $\mathcal{T} = 1/(T_{cs} + D_s)$ loses a mark. > 5. **Quorum intersection:** In Maekawa, students often state the property but do not write the formal $\forall i, j: Q_i \cap Q_j \ne \emptyset$ — KTU examiners award marks for the mathematical statement. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **System Model = the contract** under which every DME algorithm is proved correct. - **No shared memory, no common clock** → message passing + logical clocks. - **Reliable FIFO channels** is the most common baseline assumption. - **Three DME requirements:** ME1 (Safety), ME2 (No Starvation), ME3 (No Deadlock). - **Four performance metrics:** Message complexity $M$, Synchronisation delay $D_s$, Response time $R_t$, Throughput $\mathcal{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cs} + D_s}$. - **Lamport:** $3(N-1)$ messages, $D_s = T$ — simplest, foundation for all others. - **Ricart-Agrawala:** $2(N-1)$ messages, $D_s = T$ — optimal under no-broadcast. - **Maekawa:** $O(\sqrt{N})$ messages, $D_s = 2T$ — uses quorums with $Q_i \cap Q_j \ne \emptyset$. - **Token-Ring:** $0$ to $\infty$ messages (avg $N/2$), $D_s \le NT$ — simple but worst-case delay. - **Raymond’s Tree:** $O(\log N)$ messages — best for large $N$ and crash-tolerant extensions. - **Failure model:** Crash-stop is the *default* teaching assumption; Byzantine models need $3f+1$ replication. - **Throughput formula** must always include $T_{cs}$, not just $D_s$. - **Lower bound proof:** Always cite the *Ricart-Agrawala lower bound* of $2(N-1)$ messages.

topics.title: Requirements of Mutual Exclusion Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Requirements of Mutual Exclusion Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Distributed & Parallel Computing**, **Mutual Exclusion (ME)** is a fundamental synchronization primitive that ensures that at any given instant, **only one process (or thread) is allowed to execute within a designated *Critical Section (CS)*** — a code region that accesses shared, non-replicable resources (e.g., shared memory, distributed database record, shared printer, file lock).

A **Mutual Exclusion Algorithm** is the distributed protocol that *grants* and *revokes* access to this Critical Section among competing concurrent processes. To qualify as a *correct* and *robust* algorithm, the protocol must satisfy a specific set of formal properties known as the **Requirements of a Mutual Exclusion Algorithm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> The *Requirements of a Mutual Exclusion Algorithm* are the formal correctness, fairness, and liveness conditions that any mutual exclusion protocol (centralized, decentralized, token-based, or quorum-based) **must** satisfy to be considered correct in a distributed environment.

The five universally accepted requirements, as per the canonical distributed algorithms literature and KTU Module 2 syllabus, are:

| # | Requirement Name | Property Type |
|---|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Mutual Exclusion (Safety)** | Safety / Correctness |
| 2 | **No Deadlock (Liveness 1)** | Liveness |
| 3 | **No Starvation (Liveness 2 / Fairness)** | Fairness |
| 4 | **Freedom from Unnecessary Delays** | Efficiency |
| 5 | **Eventual Entry (No Lockout)** | Progress / Liveness |

Some advanced texts also include:
- **Ordered Entry (FIFO / FCFS)**
- **Fault Tolerance**
- **Symmetry / Fairness of Participation**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Single-Stall Restroom" Model

To understand these requirements intuitively, picture a **single-stall public restroom** in a busy railway station. Multiple travellers (concurrent processes) are waiting to use the exact same stall (Critical Section). A washroom attendant or a queue-token system acts as the **mutual exclusion algorithm**.

Now observe what *must* hold:

- 🚪 **Mutual Exclusion** — The door can allow **only one person** inside at a time. Two people in the same stall is unacceptable.
- 🧍 **No Deadlock** — It should never happen that a person is *forever* stuck just outside the door because the door mechanism has failed.
- 🍔 **No Starvation** — Every person in the queue must, eventually, get a turn. A "VIP jump-the-queue" forever would starve a regular traveller.
- ⚡ **No Unnecessary Delay** — If the stall is *empty* and someone is *waiting*, they should be allowed in **without any artificial waiting period**.
- 📜 **Eventual Entry** — The waiting time must be *bounded* (not infinite). Each requester must be served within a finite number of steps.

This analogy maps directly onto the KTU-defined algorithm requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Frequently Tested):**
> - **Deadlock** → *No one* can proceed. (System-wide halt)
> - **Starvation** → *At least one specific* process is perpetually denied entry, even though others progress.
> - **Unnecessary Delay** → A *runnable* process is *artificially* delayed by the protocol, even when the CS is free.

---

## 1.3 Why Are These Requirements Critical in Engineering?

| Real-World System | Shared Resource | Failure if Requirement is Violated |
|-------------------|-----------------|------------------------------------|
| ATM Network (Banking) | Shared Account Record | Double-withdrawal (No ME) |
| Railway Reservation | Limited Seat Inventory | Over-booking (No ME) |
| Operating System Kernels | Kernel Data Structures | Kernel panic / system hang (Deadlock) |
| Database Transactions | DB Row Lock | Indefinite transaction wait (Starvation) |
| Real-Time Control (Airbag ECU) | Sensor Buffer | Missed safety deadline (Unnecessary Delay) |
| Distributed Cloud File Systems | File Handle | Livelock (No Eventual Entry) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first requirement (Mutual Exclusion / Safety) is **non-negotiable** — violating it produces *incorrect* results. The remaining four are *liveness/efficiency* requirements — violating them produces a *correct but useless* algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Requirements — Exhaustive Logical Breakdown

Below is a structured, step-by-step analysis of each requirement as per KTU 2024 Module 2 expectations.

### 🔹 Requirement 1: Mutual Exclusion (Safety Property)

- **Statement:** At any instant of time, **at most one process** is allowed to be inside the Critical Section.
- **Formal Logic:** For all times $t$, $\mid P_{in}(t) \mid \leq 1$, where $P_{in}(t)$ is the set of processes executing inside the CS at time $t$.
- **Why it matters:** Violation leads to a **race condition** on shared data, producing non-deterministic and incorrect outputs.
- **How it is enforced:** Through locks, tokens, permission matrices, or quorum-based voting protocols.

### 🔹 Requirement 2: No Deadlock (Liveness Property)

- **Statement:** The system must never reach a global state where two or more processes are *waiting indefinitely* for each other to release the resource, and **no process can enter the CS**.
- **Formal Logic:** $\neg \exists$ a reachable state $S$ such that $\forall p \in S_{wait},\ \text{progress}(p) = \text{false}$ *and* the CS is free.
- **Why it matters:** A deadlock freezes the entire system — no further computation can proceed.
- **How it is enforced:** Using timeouts, lock ordering, token regeneration protocols, or by avoiding circular wait conditions.

### 🔹 Requirement 3: No Starvation (Fairness / Strong Liveness)

- **Statement:** Every process that *requests* entry to the CS must **eventually** be granted entry. No requester is left waiting forever.
- **Formal Logic:** $\forall p: \text{Request}(p) \leadsto \text{Grant}(p)$ (using linear temporal logic "leads-to" operator $\leadsto$).
- **Why it matters:** Even if the system is deadlock-free, one specific process may be perpetually bypassed. This is "unfair" and unacceptable.
- **How it is enforced:** FIFO queues, timestamps, or a logical ring of tokens.

### 🔹 Requirement 4: Freedom from Unnecessary Delays

- **Statement:** If a process *requests* entry and **no other process is currently inside the CS**, then the requesting process should be allowed in **without any idle/forced wait time** imposed by the protocol.
- **Formal Logic:** If $P_{in}(t) = \emptyset$ and process $p$ issues a request at time $t$, then $\exists \Delta \le k$ steps such that $p \in P_{in}(t + \Delta)$, where $k$ is a small constant (often $1$ or $2$ message delays).
- **Why it matters:** Without this, the system wastes CPU cycles and degrades throughput.
- **How it is enforced:** Immediate grant upon request when the resource is idle (e.g., Central Server algorithm achieves this in 2 messages).

### 🔹 Requirement 5: Eventual Entry (No Lockout)

- **Statement:** After a process has *expressed intent* to enter the CS, the wait time must be **bounded** by a function of the number of contending processes, not by the duration of system activity.
- **Formal Logic:** If $n$ processes are contending, the maximum wait for any process $p$ is bounded by $O(n)$ message exchanges or $O(n \times T_{cs})$ time, where $T_{cs}$ is the critical section execution time.
- **Why it matters:** Guarantees the system is *useful* in practice — infinite waits render the algorithm theoretical only.
- **How it is enforced:** Bounded request queues, age-based priority, or rotating token rings.

---

## 2.2 Optional / Advanced Requirements (Bonus Marks Territory)

| Requirement | Description | KTU Tag |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| **Ordered Entry (FIFO)** | Requests are granted in the order they were issued | Sometimes called "FCFS Mutual Exclusion" |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Algorithm continues to operate even if $f$ nodes crash | Crucial for cloud systems |
| **Symmetry** | All processes play identical roles; no process is special | Avoids single point of failure |
| **Scalability** | Message complexity must not blow up with $n$ | Decentralized requirement |
| **Bounded Waiting** | A process's wait is bounded by the time other processes spend in CS | Stronger than no-starvation |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for answering KTU exam questions on this topic. Memorize it!

| Property | Formal Statement | Notation / Formula | Violation Consequence | Achieved By |
|----------|------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| **Mutual Exclusion** | At most one process in CS at a time | $\vert P_{in}(t) \vert \leq 1$ | Race condition | Lock/Token/Permission |
| **No Deadlock** | No circular wait, CS not blocked forever | $\neg \exists S: P_{in}(S) = \emptyset \ \land\ \text{wait}(S) \neq \emptyset$ | System hang | Timeouts, ordering |
| **No Starvation** | Every request is eventually granted | $\forall p: R_p \leadsto G_p$ | Unfairness | FIFO queue |
| **No Unnecessary Delay** | Idle CS $\Rightarrow$ immediate entry | $P_{in}=\emptyset,\ R_p \Rightarrow G_p$ in $O(1)$ steps | Wasted CPU | Central coordinator |
| **Eventual Entry** | Bounded wait for all processes | $W_{max} = O(n \times T_{cs})$ | Indefinite wait | Rotating token |

### 2.4 Key Metrics Used in Mutual Exclusion Algorithms

| Metric | Definition | KTU Notation |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Message Complexity** | Number of messages exchanged per CS entry | $M$ (messages per entry) |
| **Synchronization Delay** | Time between one process exiting CS and next entering | $T_{sync}$ |
| **Response Time** | Time from request to entry | $T_{resp} = T_{req} + T_{sync}$ |
| **Throughput** | Number of CS entries per unit time | $\Theta = \dfrac{1}{T_{cs} + T_{sync}}$ |
| **Number of Contending Processes** | Active processes requesting CS access | $n$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These metrics are used directly in designing:
> - **DBMS lock managers** (MySQL InnoDB, Oracle RAC) — aim for $M = O(1)$ using latches.
> - **Distributed coordination services** (Apache ZooKeeper, etcd, Chubby) — use Paxos/Raft variants.
> - **Real-time OS kernels** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) — minimize $T_{sync}$ to meet deadlines.
> - **Multi-core CPUs** — use hardware atomic instructions (Test-and-Set, Compare-And-Swap) for $M = 0$.

### 2.5 Comparison Table: Which Algorithm Satisfies Which Requirement?

| Algorithm | ME | No Deadlock | No Starvation | No Unnecessary Delay | Eventual Entry |
|-----------|:--:|:-----------:|:-------------:|:--------------------:|:--------------:|
| **Central Server (Ricart-Agrawala's predecessor)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Ricart-Agrawala (Distributed)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Token Ring (Suzuki-Kasami)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (with FIFO) | ❌ (token may circulate pointlessly) | ✅ |
| **Maekawa (Quorum)** | ✅ | ✅ (modified) | ❌ (can starve) | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Raymond's Tree (Lock-based)** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
