# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Logical Derivation

This section is reserved in KTU's high-mark question (14-mark) answers for **logical proofs and step-by-step reasoning**. The structure of a typical 14-mark answer requires you to state, prove, and illustrate each requirement. Below is the **exhaustive analytical framework** you must reproduce in the exam hall.

## 3.1 Logical Proof Skeleton for Each Requirement

### 🔸 Proof of Mutual Exclusion (Safety Proof)

**Step 1 — State the invariant:**
Assume for contradiction that two processes $P_i$ and $P_j$ ($i \neq j$) are simultaneously inside the CS at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Trace the protocol condition:**
For $P_i$ to enter the CS, the algorithm requires a specific condition to be true — e.g., receipt of permission from all other processes (Ricart-Agrawala), or possession of the unique token (Token Ring).

**Step 3 — Show contradiction:**
If $P_j$ is also inside, then by the same protocol rule, $P_i$ must have either:
- (a) not yet satisfied the entry condition, or
- (b) violated the condition the algorithm assumes to be necessary.

In a correctly designed algorithm, case (a) holds because $P_j$'s prior entry prevented $P_i$ from satisfying it. Therefore, both cannot hold simultaneously. $\blacksquare$

### 🔸 Proof of No Deadlock (Liveness Proof)

**Step 1 — Identify resource acquisition graph:**
List all conditions required for a process to enter the CS — these become "resources" in the wait-for graph.

**Step 2 — Show no circular wait:**
Construct the directed wait-for graph $G = (V, E)$ where edge $P_i \to P_j$ means $P_i$ is waiting for $P_j$ to release a needed condition. The algorithm design ensures $G$ cannot contain a cycle, or if it can, a *recovery mechanism* (timeout, token regeneration) breaks the cycle.

**Step 3 — Conclude:**
Since no cycle can persist in $G$, there is always at least one process whose conditions are satisfied → it enters the CS. The other processes eventually follow. $\blacksquare$

### 🔸 Proof of No Starvation (Fairness Proof)

**Step 1 — Define a fairness measure:**
Assign a logical timestamp $T_i$ to each request from process $P_i$ (Lamport-style).

**Step 2 — Order requests globally:**
The protocol grants CS access in the order of these timestamps (lexicographic ordering of $\langle T_i, P_i \rangle$).

**Step 3 — Bound the wait:**
Each request from $P_i$ is granted within at most $(n-1)$ other entries, i.e., a bounded number of intervening CS executions. Since the CS is released in finite time by every process, $P_i$ is guaranteed entry in bounded time. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example — Verifying Requirements in Ricart-Agrawala

Consider **$n = 3$ processes** $\{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$. All three request CS at times $t_1 < t_2 < t_3$.

| Step | Event | Messages Exchanged | Process in CS |
|:----:|-------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P_1$ requests CS at $t_1$ | $P_1 \to P_2$, $P_1 \to P_3$ (2 REQ) | None |
| 2 | $P_2$ requests CS at $t_2$ | $P_2 \to P_1$, $P_2 \to P_3$ (2 REQ) | None |
| 3 | $P_3$ requests CS at $t_3$ | $P_3 \to P_1$, $P_3 \to P_2$ (2 REQ) | None |
| 4 | $P_1$ receives $P_2$'s REQ; $t_1 < t_2$ → $P_1$ defers reply to $P_2$ | None | None |
| 5 | $P_1$ receives $P_3$'s REQ; $t_1 < t_3$ → $P_1$ defers reply to $P_3$ | None | None |
| 6 | $P_3$ receives $P_1$'s REQ; $t_1 < t_3$ → $P_3$ sends REPLY to $P_1$ | $P_3 \to P_1$ | None |
| 7 | $P_2$ receives $P_1$'s REQ; $t_1 < t_2$ → $P_2$ defers reply to $P_1$ | None | None |
| 8 | $P_2$ receives $P_3$'s REQ; $t_2 < t_3$ → $P_2$ sends REPLY to $P_3$ | $P_2 \to P_3$ | None |
| 9 | $P_1$ collects REPLYs from $P_2$ and $P_3$ | — | $P_1$ enters CS |
| 10 | $P_1$ exits CS, sends deferred REPLYs | $P_1 \to P_2$, $P_1 \to P_3$ | None |
| 11 | $P_2$ collects REPLY from $P_1$ and (deferred) from $P_3$ (if pending) | — | $P_2$ enters CS |
| 12 | $P_2$ exits, releases replies | — | None |
| 13 | $P_3$ finally collects all replies | — | $P_3$ enters CS |

### Verification of Each Requirement

| Requirement | Observed in Trace? | Justification |
|-------------|:-----------------:|---------------|
| Mutual Exclusion | ✅ | Only $P_1$ entered at Step 9; $P_2$ and $P_3$ were still waiting. |
| No Deadlock | ✅ | Even when both $P_2$ and $P_3$ were waiting, the timestamp ordering guaranteed eventual progress. |
| No Starvation | ✅ | $P_3$ entered last but eventually did so (Step 13) — bounded by 3 CS entries. |
| No Unnecessary Delay | ✅ | If $P_1$ had been the only requester, it would have entered in **2 message delays** (sent 2 REQs + received 2 REPLYs) — no idle time. |
| Eventual Entry | ✅ | $P_3$ waited at most 2 CS executions, a bounded delay. |

### 3.3 Message Complexity Derivation

For a system of $n$ processes using the **Ricart-Agrawala Algorithm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Messages per CS entry} &= (n-1) \text{ REQ} + (n-1) \text{ REPLY} \\
&= 2(n-1) \\
\therefore M(n) &= 2(n-1) = O(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Token Ring Algorithm** (with token already circulating):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Messages per CS entry} &= 0 \text{ (steady state)} \\
&\text{to } 1 \text{ (token transfer to next node)} \\
\therefore M_{avg} &\approx 0 \text{ to } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Maekawa's Quorum Algorithm** (each process requests a quorum of $\sqrt{n}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quorum size} &= K = \sqrt{n} \\
\text{Messages per CS entry} &= K \text{ REQ} + K \text{ REPLY} + (K-1) \text{ RELEASE} \\
&= 3\sqrt{n} - 1 \\
\therefore M(n) &= 3\sqrt{n} - 1 = O(\sqrt{n})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Analysis of Maekawa's Quorum:**
> While Maekawa achieves $O(\sqrt{n})$ message complexity (better than $O(n)$), it has a **subtle starvation problem** — a process can be indefinitely bypassed if requests keep arriving in a specific order. KTU examiners often test this trade-off.

### 3.4 Throughput Analysis for Centralized Algorithm

For the **Central Server Algorithm** (one coordinator, $n$ clients):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sync} &= 2 \times T_{msg} \quad \text{(request + reply)} \\
\therefore \text{Throughput } \Theta &= \frac{1}{T_{cs} + T_{sync}} \\
&= \frac{1}{T_{cs} + 2T_{msg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $T_{cs}$ = time spent inside critical section
- $T_{msg}$ = one-way message delay between coordinator and a client

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Interpretation:** If $T_{cs} = 10$ ms and $T_{msg} = 1$ ms, then $\Theta \approx 83$ entries/second. In contrast, a token ring with $n = 100$ nodes and $T_{msg} = 1$ ms gives $\Theta \approx 9.9$ entries/second (token takes 100 ms to circulate even when CS is idle). This is why centralized algorithms are preferred when low contention is expected.

### 3.5 Starvation-Free Proof via Logical Timestamps

Define Lamport's logical clock as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LC_{new} &= \begin{cases}
LC_{current} + 1 & \text{if local event} \\
\max(LC_{current}, LC_{received}) + 1 & \text{if message received}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Each request $R_i$ is timestamped $T_i = LC_i$ at the moment of issue.

**Step 2:** The algorithm sorts pending requests by $\langle T_i, P_i \rangle$ lexicographically (smaller timestamp wins; ties broken by process ID).

**Step 3 — Wait Bound for $P_k$:**
Suppose $P_k$ issues request with timestamp $T_k$. The number of requests that can "jump ahead" of $P_k$ is bounded by the number of pending requests at time $T_k$, which is at most $n-1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{max}(P_k) &\leq (n-1) \times T_{cs} \\
\therefore W_{max} &= O(n \cdot T_{cs}) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Conceptual Flow of Mutual Exclusion Lifecycle

This diagram illustrates the state transitions a process undergoes when interacting with a mutual exclusion algorithm.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NonCritical : Process starts
    NonCritical --> Waiting : Issue Request (REQ)
    Waiting --> Waiting : Receive Higher-Priority REQ / Defer REPLY
    Waiting --> Critical : Receive All Required REPLYs
    Critical --> NonCritical : Exit CS / Send Deferred REPLYs
    Waiting --> Waiting : Timeout / Backoff (Deadlock Recovery)
    Critical --> [*] : Process terminates
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A process begins execution in the *Non-Critical* region. When it needs the shared resource, it issues a request and transitions to the *Waiting* state. It remains there until all protocol conditions (e.g., permission, token, quorum) are satisfied, at which point it enters the *Critical* section. On exit, it releases the resource and signals waiting peers, returning to the *Non-Critical* region.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: The Five Requirements as a Verification Layer Cake

This block-level functional architecture shows the layered relationship between the five canonical requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["Distributed System of n Processes<br/>Sharing a Common Resource"]
    L1["Requirement 1: Mutual Exclusion<br/>Safety Property - At most 1 in CS"]
    L2["Requirement 2: No Deadlock<br/>Liveness - System Progresses"]
    L3["Requirement 3: No Starvation<br/>Fairness - Every Request Served"]
    L4["Requirement 4: No Unnecessary Delay<br/>Efficiency - No Idle Wait"]
    L5["Requirement 5: Eventual Entry<br/>Liveness - Bounded Wait"]
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L1 -.Violates.-> X["Race Condition"]
    L2 -.Violates.-> Y["System Hang"]
    L3 -.Violates.-> Z["Unfairness / Lockout"]
    L4 -.Violates.-> W["Wasted Bandwidth"]
    L5 -.Violates.-> V["Indefinite Delay"]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Message Exchange Sequence for Ricart-Agrawala ($n = 3$)

This sequence diagram captures the temporal flow of messages among 3 processes during one critical section entry.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P1
    participant P2
    participant P3

    Note over P1: t1 - Request CS
    P1->>P2: REQ (ts=1)
    P1->>P3: REQ (ts=1)
    Note over P2: t2 - Request CS
    P2->>P1: REQ (ts=2)
    P2->>P3: REQ (ts=2)
    Note over P3: t3 - Request CS
    P3->>P1: REQ (ts=3)
    P3->>P2: REQ (ts=3)
    P3->>P1: REPLY (ts=1 < 3)
    P2->>P3: REPLY (ts=2 < 3)
    Note over P1: All REPLYs received
    P1->>P2: REPLY (deferred)
    P1->>P3: REPLY (deferred)
    P1->>P1: Enter Critical Section
    Note over P1: Exit CS
    P2->>P1: REPLY (deferred, ts=2>1)
    Note over P2: All REPLYs received
    P2->>P2: Enter Critical Section
    Note over P2: Exit CS
    P3->>P2: REPLY (deferred, ts=3>2)
    Note over P3: All REPLYs received
    P3->>P3: Enter Critical Section
    Note over P3: Exit CS
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Token Ring Algorithm — Logical Ring Topology

A schematic of the **Token Ring** approach, where a single *token* circulates around a logical ring of $n$ processes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["P1 (Holds Token)"] -- Token --> B["P2 (Idle)"]
    B -- Token --> C["P3 (Idle)"]
    C -- Token --> D["P4 (Wants CS)"]
    D -- Token --> E["P5 (Idle)"]
    E -- Token --> A
    D -.Enters CS.-> Dnote["P4 captures token,<br/>executes CS,<br/>releases token after exit"]
    style A fill:#90EE90
    style D fill:#FFD700
    style Dnote fill:#FFE4B5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** In the Token Ring algorithm, a process can enter the CS **only when it receives the token**. The token continuously circulates along the ring. If no process wants the CS, the token still travels the ring, consuming bandwidth — this is *unnecessary delay*.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Quorum Intersection Property (Maekawa's Algorithm)

The following illustrates how quorums of size $\sqrt{n}$ overlap, ensuring that any two requesting sets have a common member to act as a tie-breaker.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["Quorum for P1<br/>P1, P2, P3"]
    Q2["Quorum for P2<br/>P2, P4, P5"]
    Q3["Quorum for P3<br/>P3, P5, P1"]
    Q1 -.Intersects.-> Q2
    Q2 -.Intersects.-> Q3
    Q1 -.Intersects.-> Q3
    Q1 --> P2["P2 (Common Member)"]
    Q2 --> P2
    Q3 --> P5["P5 (Common Member)"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Intersection Matters:** Any two quorums share at least one process. This guarantees that **conflicting requests are detected and serialized** — the common process can serve as a tie-breaker. Maekawa's algorithm leverages this combinatorial property to achieve $O(\sqrt{n})$ message complexity.

---

## 5.2 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`
**Q: List any four requirements of a mutual exclusion algorithm in distributed systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four requirements of a mutual exclusion algorithm in distributed systems are:

1. **Mutual Exclusion (Safety):** At any instant, at most one process is allowed to execute in the critical section.
2. **No Deadlock (Liveness):** Two or more processes should not be indefinitely waiting for events that will never occur; the system must always make progress.
3. **No Starvation (Fairness):** Every process that requests entry to the critical section must eventually be granted access; no process should be bypassed indefinitely.
4. **Freedom from Unnecessary Delays:** If no process is currently executing in the critical section and some process wants to enter, it should be allowed in without any forced or artificial delay.

*(A fifth commonly cited requirement — "Eventual Entry" or "No Lockout" — can be added if students wish to provide a complete answer.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`
**Q: Differentiate between Deadlock and Starvation in the context of mutual exclusion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Deadlock | Starvation |
|-----------|----------|------------|
| **Scope** | System-wide; affects *all* processes waiting for the resource | Affects *one or more specific* processes; others may progress |
| **Cause** | Circular wait for resources; no process can enter CS | A process is perpetually bypassed; others repeatedly enter CS |
| **State of CS** | CS may remain *unoccupied* indefinitely | CS is *occupied* — by other processes, not the starved one |
| **Detection** | Wait-for graph contains a cycle | Wait-for graph has a path, but the target is never released for this requester |
| **Recovery** | Abort one or more processes; preempt resources | Use FIFO queue, aging, or rotating priority |

**Example:** If $P_1$ holds a lock needed by $P_2$, and $P_2$ holds a lock needed by $P_1$, **both are deadlocked**. If $P_3$ keeps arriving and the queue always prioritizes new arrivals, $P_1$ is **starved**.

---

## 5.3 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme requires internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** for every module. Both are provided below for full preparation.

---

### 📘 **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the requirements of Mutual Exclusion Algorithm in detail. Compare how the Centralized and Ricart-Agrawala algorithms satisfy these requirements.**

#### **Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of Requirements (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **mutual exclusion algorithm** governs concurrent access to a shared resource in a distributed system. To be considered correct, it must satisfy five canonical requirements:

**1. Mutual Exclusion (Safety Property):**
At any instant of time, **at most one process** is allowed to be executing inside the critical section (CS). Formally, $\vert P_{in}(t) \vert \leq 1$ for all time $t$. This is a *safety* property — its violation produces incorrect results (race conditions). The Centralized algorithm enforces this by routing all requests through a single coordinator that grants permission to only one process at a time. Ricart-Agrawala enforces it by requiring a process to collect REPLYs from **all** other processes before entering.

**2. No Deadlock:**
The system must never reach a state where two or more processes are **forever** waiting for each other, with the CS remaining permanently unoccupied. The Centralized algorithm avoids deadlock as the coordinator is the single arbiter. Ricart-Agrawala avoids deadlock by using logical timestamps — a process with the lowest timestamp is guaranteed entry.

**3. No Starvation (Fairness):**
Every process that requests entry to the CS must **eventually** be granted access, regardless of system load. In the Centralized algorithm, the coordinator maintains a FIFO queue, ensuring fairness. In Ricart-Agrawala, the timestamp-based ordering prevents any process from being indefinitely bypassed.

**4. Freedom from Unnecessary Delays:**
If no process is currently in the CS and a request arrives, the requesting process should enter **without any artificial waiting period**. The Centralized algorithm achieves this in **2 message delays** (1 REQ + 1 REPLY). Ricart-Agrawala requires $2(n-1)$ messages but still enters immediately when the requester has the lowest timestamp.

**5. Eventual Entry:**
The wait time for any process must be **bounded** by a function of the system parameters (number of contending processes and CS execution time). Both Centralized and Ricart-Agrawala satisfy this: a process waits at most $(n-1)$ other entries in Ricart-Agrawala, and the queue length in Centralized.

**Reference Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Process P_i"] -- "REQ" --> B["Coordinator C / All Peers"]
    B -- "REPLY" --> A
    A --> CS["Critical Section"]
    CS --> Exit["Exit / Release"]
    Exit --> B
```

#### **Part (b) — Comparison of Centralized vs. Ricart-Agrawala (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Requirement | Centralized Algorithm | Ricart-Agrawala Algorithm |
|-------------|:---------------------:|:-------------------------:|
| **Mutual Exclusion** | ✅ Satisfied (coordinator grants atomically) | ✅ Satisfied (all REPLYs required) |
| **No Deadlock** | ✅ (single arbiter, no circular wait) | ✅ (timestamp ordering breaks cycles) |
| **No Starvation** | ✅ (FIFO queue at coordinator) | ✅ (timestamp order) |
| **No Unnecessary Delay** | ✅✅ (best — 2 messages, $T_{sync} = 2T_{msg}$) | ✅ (2(n-1) messages, but no delay once REPLYs arrive) |
| **Eventual Entry** | ✅ (bounded by queue length) | ✅ (bounded by $n-1$ CS executions) |
| **Message Complexity** | $3(n-1)$ (REQ + REPLY + RELEASE) per entry | $2(n-1)$ (REQ + REPLY only) per entry |
| **Fault Tolerance** | ❌ Single point of failure (coordinator crash = system halt) | ✅ Fully distributed, no SPOF |
| **Bottleneck** | Coordinator (load concentration) | No bottleneck (symmetric) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (coordinator saturates) | Good (scales linearly in messages) |

**Conclusion:**
The **Centralized algorithm** is simpler, has lower synchronization delay, and provides strong fairness, but suffers from a single point of failure. The **Ricart-Agrawala algorithm** is fault-tolerant and scalable, but requires $2(n-1)$ messages per entry — a higher message cost. KTU examiners often ask students to recommend an algorithm for a given scenario; the choice depends on whether fault tolerance or message cost is more critical.

---

### 📗 **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Q: (a) Explain the five key requirements that a distributed mutual exclusion algorithm must satisfy. Illustrate each with a small example. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss, with proofs, how Ricart-Agrawala's algorithm ensures "No Starvation" and "Mutual Exclusion". Compare its message complexity with Maekawa's Quorum algorithm. (7 marks)**

#### **Part (a) — Five Requirements with Examples (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Requirement 1 — Mutual Exclusion:**
At any time, only one process may be inside the CS. **Example:** In a bank database, two ATMs accessing the same account must not both be allowed to debit it simultaneously; otherwise the balance becomes incorrect.

**Requirement 2 — No Deadlock:**
The system must not enter a state where processes are forever blocked. **Example:** In a ticketing system, if $P_1$ is waiting for $P_2$ to release a seat lock, and $P_2$ is waiting for $P_1$ to release a payment lock, no transaction proceeds — a deadlock.

**Requirement 3 — No Starvation:**
Every requester eventually gets served. **Example:** In a CPU scheduler, a low-priority process must still get CPU time eventually; otherwise it starves.

**Requirement 4 — No Unnecessary Delays:**
If the CS is free, the next requester should not be forced to wait. **Example:** A single cashier window with no customers should immediately serve the next arriving customer; no idle wait is imposed.

**Requirement 5 — Eventual Entry:**
The wait time is bounded. **Example:** In a fair queue at a hospital, each patient is given a token number and is served within a fixed maximum number of patients ahead — a *bounded* wait.

**Summary Table:**

| Requirement | Type | One-line Formal Statement |
|-------------|------|---------------------------|
| Mutual Exclusion | Safety | $\vert P_{in} \vert \leq 1$ |
| No Deadlock | Liveness | System never halts permanently |
| No Starvation | Fairness | Every request is granted in finite time |
| No Unnecessary Delay | Efficiency | Idle CS $\Rightarrow$ immediate entry |
| Eventual Entry | Liveness | Bounded wait for every requester |

#### **Part (b) — Proofs and Complexity Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Proof 1 — Ricart-Agrawala Ensures Mutual Exclusion:**

Assume for contradiction that $P_i$ and $P_j$ ($i \neq j$) are simultaneously in the CS. For $P_i$ to enter, it must have received a REPLY from **every** other process, including $P_j$. But for $P_j$ to have sent that REPLY, it must **not** have been in the CS or requesting it at the time of $P_i$'s request. This means $P_j$ entered the CS *after* $P_i$ — contradicting our assumption. Therefore, mutual exclusion holds. $\blacksquare$

**Proof 2 — Ricart-Agrawala Ensures No Starvation:**

Each request is tagged with a Lamport logical timestamp $T_i$. The algorithm grants the CS to the request with the **smallest** $\langle T_i, P_i \rangle$ pair. Consider any request from $P_k$ with timestamp $T_k$. The number of requests that can "jump ahead" of $P_k$ is at most $n-1$ (one per other process), and each such requester holds the CS for a *finite* time $T_{cs}$. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{max}(P_k) &\leq (n-1) \cdot T_{cs} \\
\therefore W_{max} &= O(n \cdot T_{cs}) \quad \text{(bounded)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Message Complexity Comparison:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{Ricart\text{-}Agrawala} &= 2(n - 1) \quad \text{(REQ + REPLY)} \\
M_{Maekawa} &= 3\sqrt{n} - 1 \quad \text{(REQ + REPLY + RELEASE, with quorum of size } \sqrt{n}\text{)} \\
M_{Centralized} &= 3(n - 1) \quad \text{(REQ + REPLY + RELEASE)} \\
M_{Token\text{ }Ring} &\approx 1 \text{ (steady state) to } n \text{ (worst case)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Metric | Ricart-Agrawala | Maekawa | Token Ring | Centralized |
|--------|:----------------:|:-------:|:----------:|:-----------:|
| **Messages per entry** | $2(n-1)$ | $3\sqrt{n} - 1$ | $1$ avg | $3(n-1)$ |
| **Asymptotic** | $O(n)$ | $O(\sqrt{n})$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **No Starvation?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ Possible | ✅ (with FIFO) | ✅ Yes |
| **No Deadlock?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Synchronization Delay** | $2T_{msg}$ | $2T_{msg}$ | $0$ to $nT_{msg}$ | $2T_{msg}$ |
| **Best Use Case** | Reliable LANs, moderate $n$ | Large-scale, contention-aware | Embedded real-time rings | Small clusters, low $n$ |

**Key Insight:** Maekawa achieves $O(\sqrt{n})$ messages but **sacrifices no-starvation** — a classic engineering trade-off between message cost and fairness.

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Mistakes (Avoid these!)**
>
> 1. **Confusing Deadlock with Starvation:** Examiners frequently deduct 2-3 marks for stating "no starvation" when the question asks for "no deadlock" and vice versa. Memorize the distinction: **Deadlock = nobody progresses; Starvation = one specific process doesn't progress**.
>
> 2. **Omitting the Formal Statement:** Always state the requirement in *both* plain English AND a formal sentence. Example: "At most one process executes in CS at any time (i.e., $\vert P_{in}(t) \vert \leq 1$)."
>
> 3. **Missing the Diagram:** A 14-mark question without a diagram loses at least 1-2 marks. Always include a state diagram, sequence diagram, or a flow chart.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Message Complexity Formulas:** KTU loves asking "How many messages per CS entry?" — remember:
>    - Centralized: $3(n-1)$
>    - Ricart-Agrawala: $2(n-1)$
>    - Maekawa: $3\sqrt{n} - 1$
>    - Token Ring: $1$ (steady state)
>
> 5. **Not Distinguishing Safety vs. Liveness:** "Mutual Exclusion" is a *safety* property; "No Deadlock" and "No Starvation" are *liveness* properties. Examiners test this explicitly.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Fault Tolerance:** For a 14-mark "discuss" question, mentioning that Centralized has a single point of failure and Ricart-Agrawala is fault-tolerant earns extra marks.
>
> 7. **Skipping a Comparison Table:** KTU valuation keys explicitly allocate 2-3 marks for a "comparison table" sub-part. Skipping it = direct loss.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Pointers — Glance at this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ The **5 core requirements** of a mutual exclusion algorithm are: **Mutual Exclusion, No Deadlock, No Starvation, No Unnecessary Delays, Eventual Entry**.
- ✅ **Mutual Exclusion** is a *safety* property; the remaining four are *liveness/fairness/efficiency* properties.
- ✅ **Deadlock** affects *all* waiting processes; **Starvation** affects a *specific* process.
- ✅ **Centralized Algorithm** is simplest (1 coordinator) but has a single point of failure and a bottleneck. Messages per entry = $3(n-1)$.
- ✅ **Ricart-Agrawala** is fully distributed, uses **Lamport timestamps**, and has $2(n-1)$ messages per entry. It satisfies all 5 requirements.
- ✅ **Maekawa's Quorum** achieves $O(\sqrt{n})$ messages with quorum size $\sqrt{n}$ but **can starve** processes.
- ✅ **Token Ring** has $O(1)$ average messages in steady state but suffers from unnecessary delay when no one wants the CS (token keeps circulating).
- ✅ Message complexity ordering (best to worst in best case): **Token Ring (1) < Maekawa ($\sqrt{n}$) < Ricart-Agrawala ($n$) ≈ Centralized ($n$)**.
- ✅ **Logical timestamps** are the standard tool for ordering requests in distributed mutual exclusion (Lamport's clock or vector clocks).
- ✅ **Quorum intersection property:** Any two quorums must share at least one process — this is what gives Maekawa its correctness.
- ✅ In the **exam**, always state: (1) the requirement's plain-language meaning, (2) its formal logical statement, (3) an example or algorithm that satisfies it, and (4) include a diagram.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, structure your response into clearly labeled sub-parts: (a) Detailed Explanation + (b) Comparison + Proofs/Complexity.
- ✅ **Synchronization Delay Formula:** $T_{sync} = 2T_{msg}$ (for non-token algorithms), and the throughput is $\Theta = \dfrac{1}{T_{cs} + T_{sync}}$.
- ✅ Always mention **fault tolerance** as a differentiator when comparing algorithms in exam answers.

---

**End of Notes — Module 2: Requirements of Mutual Exclusion Algorithm**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PECCT411 | Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10*

topics.title: Lamport's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lamport's Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lamport's Algorithm** is a foundational *distributed mutual exclusion* algorithm proposed by **Leslie Lamport (1978)** that allows a set of distributed processes competing for a single shared *Critical Section (CS)* to coordinate entry using **logical (scalar) clocks** and a **total ordering of events**, without relying on a centralized coordinator. It is formally classified as a **permission-based (contention-based, non-token-passing) algorithm** because every process must obtain explicit *permission* (acknowledgement) from all other processes before it can enter the critical section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECCT411 / Module 2):**
> *“Lamport's Algorithm – logical clocks, happens-before relation, distributed mutual exclusion, request-reply-release protocol, total ordering of events, correctness proofs (safety, liveness, fairness).”*

The algorithm operates on three core pillars:

1. **Logical Clocks (`C_i`)** — a per-process monotonically non-decreasing integer counter, *not* a physical wall clock.
2. **The Happens-Before Relation (`→`)** — a partial-order causal precedence defined on distributed events.
3. **A Total-Order Lexicographic Pair `(C_i, P_i)`** — the tie-breaker that converts the partial order into a total order across the entire system.

## 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — “The Airport Security Queue with Numbered Tickets”**
>
> Imagine **N** passengers standing in a lounge, all wanting to walk up to **one** security officer (the *Critical Section*). There is no central queue manager. Each passenger carries a **digital stopwatch** that only ticks when they hand a slip to someone or receive a slip from someone.
>
> 1. When passenger *i* wants the officer, he **increments his stopwatch**, writes the new time on a *Request slip* (his timestamp), and broadcasts it to **every other passenger**.
> 2. Every other passenger `j`, on receiving the slip, also **updates his stopwatch** to be at least one tick greater than the slip's time, and sends back a *Reply slip* (acknowledgement), *unless* `j` is himself waiting with an *older* (smaller) timestamp.
> 3. Passenger `i` may walk up to the officer **only when** he has collected a Reply slip from **all** other passengers **AND** his own slip is at the **top of every passenger's local queue** (i.e., his timestamp is the smallest).
> 4. After leaving the officer, `i` broadcasts a *Release slip* so everyone else can pull his request out of their local queues.
>
> **The physical time is irrelevant.** What matters is the *causal sequence* captured by the stopwatches. This is exactly how Lamport's algorithm achieves **distributed mutual exclusion** — by ordering requests on logical, not physical, time.

## 1.3 The Three Core Logical-Clock Rules (Lamport's Foundation)

These rules must be **memorized verbatim** for KTU 2024 questions:

| Rule # | Trigger Event | Clock Update Action | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | An *internal* event occurs in process $P_i$ | $C_i \leftarrow C_i + 1$ | Tick locally |
| **R2** | $P_i$ *sends* a message $m$ with timestamp $t$ | $C_i \leftarrow C_i + 1$; embed $t$ in $m$ | Stamp on send |
| **R3** | $P_i$ *receives* a message $m$ carrying timestamp $t_m$ | $C_i \leftarrow \max(C_i, t_m) + 1$ | Sync on receive |

> [!NOTE]
> The rule $C_i \leftarrow \max(C_i, t_m) + 1$ is the **clock-condition enforcement** that guarantees: *if* $a \rightarrow b$ (causally), *then* $C(a) < C(b)$. Without it, causality can be violated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Happens-Before Relation ($\rightarrow$)

The happens-before relation is a **strict partial order** on the set of all events in a distributed system. For any two events $a$ and $b$:

$$a \rightarrow b \;\; \text{(a happens-before b) if and only if one of the following holds:}$$

1. **Same Process (Local Precedence):** $a$ and $b$ are events in the *same* process and $a$ occurs *before* $b$ in that process's local execution.
2. **Message Communication:** $a$ is the *send* event of some message $m$, and $b$ is the *receive* event of that *same* message $m$.
3. **Transitivity:** $\exists\, c$ such that $a \rightarrow c$ and $c \rightarrow b$.

If **none** of the above holds, then $a$ and $b$ are said to be **concurrent**, written $a \,\|\, b$. They have no causal relationship.

## 2.2 The Strong Clock Condition

Lamport proved that his three rules R1–R3 are **sufficient** to enforce the *Clock Condition*:

$$\boxed{\; a \rightarrow b \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C(a) < C(b) \;}$$

The **converse is NOT necessarily true** — equal or ordered timestamps do *not* imply causal precedence (this is a famous KTU viva question).

## 2.3 Breaking Ties — The Total Order via Process ID

Logical timestamps alone are *not* unique*; two different events in different processes can hold identical clock values. To obtain a **total order** (a strict weak order compatible with $\rightarrow$), Lamport appended the **process identifier** as a tie-breaker:

$$\boxed{\; (C_i, P_i) \;\prec\; (C_j, P_j) \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \big[\,C_i < C_j\,\big] \;\lor\; \big[\,C_i = C_j \;\land\; P_i < P_j\,\big] \;}$$

This pair is called the **Lamport Logical Timestamp** and is written universally as:

$$\boxed{\; L_i \;=\; (C_i, P_i) \;}$$

## 2.4 The Distributed Mutual Exclusion Protocol

Each process $P_i$ maintains a local **request queue** $RQ_i$, initially empty. The protocol is event-driven:

### Algorithm Body (Pseudocode-form, language-agnostic)

**Entry Request by $P_i$:**
1. $C_i \leftarrow C_i + 1$
2. Insert the tuple $(C_i, P_i, REQ)$ into $RQ_i$.
3. Send the message $\langle REQ, C_i, P_i \rangle$ to **all** other $N-1$ processes.

**Receipt of $\langle REQ, t_s, P_s \rangle$ by $P_j$:**
1. $C_j \leftarrow \max(C_j, t_s) + 1$
2. Insert $(t_s, P_s, REQ)$ into $RQ_j$ (sorted by total order).
3. **Reply condition:** If $P_j$ is **not** currently requesting the CS, **or** its own queued request has a *strictly larger* timestamp than $t_s$, then send $\langle REPLY, C_j, P_j \rangle$ to $P_s$. Otherwise, **defer** the reply (it will be sent at release time).

**Entry into CS by $P_i$ — precondition is the conjunction:**

$$\boxed{\; \Big[\, (C_i, P_i) \text{ is at the head of } RQ_i \,\Big] \;\;\land\;\; \Big[\, P_i \text{ has received } \langle REPLY \rangle \text{ from every } P_j, \; j \neq i \,\Big] \;}$$

**Release of CS by $P_i$:**
1. Remove $(C_i, P_i, REQ)$ from the head of $RQ_i$.
2. Send $\langle RELEASE, C_i, P_i \rangle$ to **all** other $N-1$ processes.

**Receipt of $\langle RELEASE, t_s, P_s \rangle$ by $P_j$:**
1. $C_j \leftarrow \max(C_j, t_s) + 1$
2. Remove $(t_s, P_s, REQ)$ from $RQ_j$ (if present).
3. **Reply condition revisited:** If $P_j$'s own request is now the *oldest* in $RQ_j$ (i.e., $RQ_j$'s head), and $P_j$ had previously deferred a reply to $P_s$ or some other deferred process, then send the deferred $\langle REPLY, C_j, P_j \rangle$ now.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity / Property | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clock update on local event | $C_i \leftarrow C_i + 1$ | Rule R1 |
| 2 | Clock update on message **send** | $C_i \leftarrow C_i + 1$ | Rule R2 |
| 3 | Clock update on message **receive** | $C_i \leftarrow \max(C_i, t_m) + 1$ | Rule R3 — *memorize this* |
| 4 | Strong Clock Condition | $a \rightarrow b \Rightarrow C(a) < C(b)$ | Sufficient, **not necessary** |
| 5 | Total-order tie-breaker | $(C_i, P_i) \prec (C_j, P_j) \iff C_i < C_j \lor (C_i = C_j \land P_i < P_j)$ | Lexicographic |
| 6 | Messages per CS entry | $3(N-1)$ | $(N-1)$ REQ + $(N-1)$ REPLY + $(N-1)$ RELEASE |
| 7 | Synchronization delay | $T$ (one round-trip message delay) | Compared to Ricart–Agrawala |
| 8 | CS entries per synchronization delay | $1$ | |
| 9 | Number of messages (Ricart–Agrawala, *for comparison*) | $2(N-1)$ | Lower than Lamport |
| 10 | Symmetric / Asymmetric | **Symmetric** (all peers equal) | No master/slave |
| 11 | Assumption | FIFO channels, no failures | Failure-handling extensions exist |
| 12 | Type | Permission-based, non-token | Requests are *flooded* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Lamport's algorithm underpins modern implementations of **distributed lock managers (DLMs)**, **Apache ZooKeeper's Zab protocol ancestry**, **Google Chubby**, **consensus protocols' pre-history**, and is the theoretical ancestor of **vector clocks** (used today in DynamoDB, Cassandra for conflict resolution). The logical-clock abstraction is the basis for all *causal* event ordering in distributed databases and event-sourcing systems.

## 2.6 Correctness Properties (Required for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

| Property | Formal Statement | Why Lamport Satisfies It |
|---|---|---|
| **Safety (Mutual Exclusion)** | $\forall\, t$: at most one process is in the CS. | Two processes cannot both have their request at the head of *every* queue, because timestamps are totally ordered — one must be strictly older. |
| **Liveness (Deadlock-Freedom)** | If a process requests, it eventually enters. | Reply-deferral is finite; the *oldest* request in any queue is always replied to (FIFO + release). |
| **Fairness (Starvation-Freedom)** | Requests are served in *total order* of timestamps (FIFO). | The head-of-queue condition enforces a strict global FIFO ordering of CS entries. |
| **Ordering (Causality)** | If $P_i$'s request causally precedes $P_j$'s, $P_i$ enters first. | $C_i < C_j$ under Rule R3; total-order makes the queue respect this. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Logical Clock Calculation Across Three Processes

> **Problem statement [KTU University Exam – June 2022 style]:** Three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ execute concurrently. The following inter-process messages are sent (with logical-clock values embedded at send-time):
> - $P_1$ sends $m_1$ at $C_1 = 2$.
> - $P_2$ sends $m_2$ at $C_2 = 4$.
> - $P_1$ sends $m_3$ at $C_1 = 5$.
> - $P_2$ receives $m_1$ when $C_2 = 3$.
> - $P_3$ receives $m_2$ when $C_3 = 1$.
> - $P_1$ receives $m_2$ when $C_1 = 4$.
>
> **Required:** Compute the *final* logical clock of every process and state the **total-order** of all receive events.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Initial state:** $C_1 = 0, \; C_2 = 0, \; C_3 = 0$.

**Step 1 — $P_1$ sends $m_1$.**
Apply Rule R2 (send rule): $P_1$ increments before stamping.

$$C_1 \leftarrow C_1 + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1$$

Then embeds the timestamp. The problem says the *embedded* value is $2$, so $P_1$ must have performed an additional internal event between the increment and the send:

$$\text{Internal event at } P_1: C_1 \leftarrow 1 + 1 = 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{embed } 2 \text{ in } m_1.$$

**Step 2 — $P_2$ sends $m_2$ at $C_2 = 4$.**
By the same logic, $P_2$ ticked from 0 to 4 through internal events or earlier receives.

**Step 3 — $P_1$ sends $m_3$ at $C_1 = 5$.**
This means $P_1$ ticked from 2 to 5. We will see *why* below when $P_1$ receives $m_2$.

**Step 4 — $P_2$ receives $m_1$ when $C_2 = 3$.**
Apply Rule R3 (receive rule):

$$C_2 \leftarrow \max(C_2, t_{m_1}) + 1 = \max(3, 2) + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$$

After receipt, $C_2 = 4$. This explains why $P_2$'s next send $m_2$ carries timestamp 4.

**Step 5 — $P_3$ receives $m_2$ when $C_3 = 1$.**
Apply Rule R3:

$$C_3 \leftarrow \max(C_3, t_{m_2}) + 1 = \max(1, 4) + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5$$

After receipt, $C_3 = 5$.

**Step 6 — $P_1$ receives $m_2$ when $C_1 = 4$.**
Apply Rule R3:

$$C_1 \leftarrow \max(C_1, t_{m_2}) + 1 = \max(4, 4) + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5$$

After receipt, $C_1 = 5$. This confirms the $m_3$ send at $C_1 = 5$.

**Final clock state:**

$$\boxed{\; C_1 = 5, \quad C_2 = 4, \quad C_3 = 5 \;}$$

**Total-order of receive events (lexicographic on $(C, P)$):**

| Order | Event | $(C_{\text{after}}, P)$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_2$ receives $m_1$ | $(4, P_2)$ | $4 < 5$, so first |
| 2 (tie) | $P_1$ receives $m_2$ | $(5, P_1)$ | $C = 5, P_1 < P_3$ |
| 3 (tie) | $P_3$ receives $m_2$ | $(5, P_3)$ | $C = 5, P_3 > P_1$ |

$$\boxed{\; \text{Total order: } \; (4, P_2) \;\prec\; (5, P_1) \;\prec\; (5, P_3) \;}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Critical-Section Entry Trace

> **Problem statement [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]:** Three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ invoke mutual exclusion. All start with $C = 0$. Trace Lamport's algorithm assuming:
> - $P_1$ requests CS first at its local time $C_1 = 1$.
> - $P_2$ requests CS at $C_2 = 1$ (concurrently with $P_1$).
> - $P_3$ does not request.
>
> Show who enters first, the queue evolution, and the total message count.

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — $P_1$ requests.**

$$C_1 \leftarrow 0 + 1 = 1$$

Insert $(1, P_1, REQ)$ into $RQ_1$. Send $\langle REQ, 1, P_1 \rangle$ to $P_2$ and $P_3$. **Messages so far: 2 REQ.**

**Step 2 — $P_2$ requests (concurrently, so its $C_2$ ticks independently).**

$$C_2 \leftarrow 0 + 1 = 1$$

Insert $(1, P_2, REQ)$ into $RQ_2$. Send $\langle REQ, 1, P_2 \rangle$ to $P_1$ and $P_3$. **Messages so far: 4 REQ.**

**Step 3 — $P_2$ receives $P_1$'s REQ.**

$$C_2 \leftarrow \max(1, 1) + 1 = 2$$

Insert $(1, P_1, REQ)$ into $RQ_2$. Now $RQ_2 = [(1, P_1), (1, P_2)]$ — sorted lexicographically. The head is $(1, P_1)$ because $P_1 < P_2$ in the tie-breaker.

**Reply decision:** $P_2$ is *itself* requesting with timestamp $(1, P_2)$. Is $P_2$'s request *strictly older* than $P_1$'s? No — they are equal, and $P_1 < P_2$. Therefore $P_2$ **defers** the reply (the rule: reply only if your request is *strictly older*; otherwise defer). **No REPLY sent yet.**

**Step 4 — $P_1$ receives $P_2$'s REQ.**

$$C_1 \leftarrow \max(1, 1) + 1 = 2$$

Insert $(1, P_2, REQ)$ into $RQ_1$. Now $RQ_1 = [(1, P_1), (1, P_2)]$ — head is $(1, P_1)$.

**Reply decision:** $P_1$'s own request has the same timestamp $(1, P_1)$, and $P_1 < P_2$ in tie-breaker, so $P_1$'s own request is *not strictly older* than $P_2$'s. $P_1$ **defers** the reply. **No REPLY sent yet.**

**Step 5 — $P_3$ (idle) receives both REQs.**

On receiving $\langle REQ, 1, P_1 \rangle$:

$$C_3 \leftarrow \max(0, 1) + 1 = 2$$

Insert $(1, P_1, REQ)$ into $RQ_3$. $P_3$ is not requesting, so it **sends** $\langle REPLY, 2, P_3 \rangle$ to $P_1$. **Messages: 5.**

On receiving $\langle REQ, 1, P_2 \rangle$:

$$C_3 \leftarrow \max(2, 1) + 1 = 3$$

Insert $(1, P_2, REQ)$ into $RQ_3$. $P_3$ still not requesting, so it **sends** $\langle REPLY, 3, P_3 \rangle$ to $P_2$. **Messages: 6.**

**Step 6 — $P_1$ receives $P_3$'s REPLY.**

$$C_1 \leftarrow \max(2, 2) + 1 = 3$$

$P_1$ now holds: $RQ_1$ head is $(1, P_1)$ ✓, and REPLY received from $\{P_3\}$ only. Still missing $P_2$'s REPLY. **Not yet eligible.**

**Step 7 — $P_2$ receives $P_3$'s REPLY.**

$$C_2 \leftarrow \max(2, 3) + 1 = 4$$

$P_2$ holds: $RQ_2$ head is $(1, P_1)$ — **not its own** $(1, P_2)$! So $P_2$ is **not eligible** to enter the CS even though it has a REPLY from $P_3$. This is the **FIFO fairness** in action.

**Step 8 — The deadlock-resolution moment.**
The system now waits for $P_2$ to send the deferred REPLY to $P_1$. But $P_2$ is itself blocked! Resolution comes when either $P_1$ or $P_2$ finishes its CS. Neither has entered yet — so we have a **wait-state**, but **not a deadlock**, because:

- $P_1$ will eventually be at the head of $P_2$'s queue (it already is).
- The moment $P_1$ enters and releases, $P_2$'s reply-deferral is resolved by the *release handshake* (or, in some variants, $P_2$'s reply is sent once it sees $P_1$'s request is older — which it is, so $P_2$ *could* send the reply *now*; many textbook formulations permit this since the rule is "strictly older").

**Let us adopt the standard KTU textbook formulation:**

**Step 8 (corrected) — $P_2$ now sends the deferred REPLY to $P_1$.**

$P_2$'s request $(1, P_2)$ is *not strictly older* than $P_1$'s $(1, P_1)$ — they are equal, and $P_1 < P_2$. So $P_2$ should **defer** until it releases. But $P_2$ cannot release without entering, and it cannot enter without $P_1$'s REPLY... 

> [!WARNING]
> **The classical Lamport's algorithm avoids this pathological loop by a different rule:** $P_j$ sends REPLY to $P_s$ if $P_j$ is *not requesting*, *or* if $P_s$'s request has a timestamp *strictly less than* $P_j$'s. In our case $C_{P_1} = 1 = C_{P_2}$, so $P_2$ defers. The loop is broken because **at most one process holds the oldest timestamp** — and ties are broken by the process ID. The *truly* oldest process will eventually enter, and the release handshake clears the deferral.

**Resolution path:** Suppose $P_1$ had instead started at $C_1 = 0$ (one tick earlier) and $P_2$ at $C_2 = 1$. Then $P_2$ would have replied immediately to $P_1$, and $P_1$ would have entered first. The KTU answer is to **draw the queue evolution and identify the head process**.

**Step 9 — $P_1$ enters CS, then releases.**

$P_1$ eventually satisfies both preconditions. It executes the CS. On release:

- Remove $(1, P_1, REQ)$ from $RQ_1$ (now head of $RQ_1$ is $(1, P_2)$).
- Send $\langle RELEASE, 1, P_1 \rangle$ to $P_2$ and $P_3$. **Messages: 6 + 2 = 8.**

**Step 10 — $P_2$ receives $P_1$'s RELEASE.**

$$C_2 \leftarrow \max(C_2, 1) + 1$$

Remove $(1, P_1, REQ)$ from $RQ_2$. Now $RQ_2$ head is $(1, P_2)$ — $P_2$'s *own* request. The earlier-deferred REPLY is now sent to itself (no-op) and any *other* deferred replies are flushed. **Messages: 8 + 1 = 9.**

$P_2$ now has its own REPLY (from $P_3$) and a self-reply; $P_1$ has released and sent its own RELEASE that $P_2$ is processing; $P_2$ is now eligible. $P_2$ enters CS.

**Step 11 — $P_2$ releases.**

Send $\langle RELEASE, 1, P_2 \rangle$ to $P_1, P_3$. **Messages: 9 + 2 = 11.**

**Total message count for $N=3$, single CS entry by $P_1$, then $P_2$:**

| Type | Count |
|---|---|
| REQ | $2(N-1) = 4$ |
| REPLY | $N-1 = 2$ (only $P_3$ replied; $P_2$ replied after release) |
| RELEASE | $2(N-1) = 4$ |
| **Total** | $\boxed{\,3(N-1) = 6 \text{ per CS entry}\,}$, so for 2 CS entries: **12 messages.** |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Clock-Condition Sufficiency

> **Why does the max-plus-one update rule work?**

**Claim:** If every process follows R1–R3, then for any two events $a, b$ with $a \rightarrow b$, we have $C(a) < C(b)$.

**Proof by induction on the definition of $\rightarrow$:**

- **Base case 1 — Same process:** $a$ and $b$ are local events in $P_i$ with $a$ before $b$. By R1, every local event increments by at least 1, so $C_i$ is strictly increasing along the local timeline. Hence $C(a) < C(b)$.
- **Base case 2 — Message send-receive:** $a = \text{send}(m)$ in $P_i$, $b = \text{receive}(m)$ in $P_j$. By R2, $C(a) = C_i^{\text{at send}} = t_m$. By R3, the receive updates $C_j$ to $\max(C_j^{\text{old}}, t_m) + 1 \geq t_m + 1 > t_m = C(a)$. So $C(a) < C(b)$.
- **Inductive step — Transitivity:** Suppose $a \rightarrow c$ and $c \rightarrow b$ with $C(a) < C(c)$ and $C(c) < C(b)$ by the inductive hypothesis. By transitivity of $<$, $C(a) < C(b)$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This proof is a **favorite KTU 14-mark question**: *"Prove that Lamport's clock-update rules preserve the clock condition."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Lamport's Mutual Exclusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Process P_i wants Critical Section])
    nodeClockA["Clock Module: C_i ← C_i + 1"]
    nodeEnqReq["Local Request Queue RQ_i: enqueue (C_i, P_i, REQ)"]
    nodeBroadReq["Network Layer: BROADCAST REQ to all N-1 peers"]
    nodeWait1{"All REPLY received?"}
    nodeClockB["Clock Module: C_j ← max(C_j, t_msg) + 1 on every receive"]
    nodeEnqPeer["Peer RQ_j: enqueue (t_msg, P_msg, REQ)"]
    nodeReplyDecide{"Reply Decision: own request strictly older?"}
    nodeSendReply["Send REPLY to P_msg"]
    nodeDeferReply["DEFER reply - queue internally"]
    nodeHeadCheck{"(C_i, P_i) at head of RQ_i?"}
    nodeEnterCS["ENTER Critical Section"]
    nodeDeqSelf["Dequeue own request from RQ_i"]
    nodeBroadRel["BROADCAST RELEASE to all N-1 peers"]
    nodePeerProcRel["Peer P_j: receive RELEASE, update C_j, dequeue P_i from RQ_j"]
    nodeFlushDef["Flush any deferred REPLY if oldest in RQ_j"]
    nodeExit([Exit - back to idle])

    nodeStart --> nodeClockA
    nodeClockA --> nodeEnqReq
    nodeEnqReq --> nodeBroadReq
    nodeBroadReq --> nodeClockB
    nodeClockB --> nodeEnqPeer
    nodeEnqPeer --> nodeReplyDecide
    nodeReplyDecide -- YES, send --> nodeSendReply
    nodeReplyDecide -- NO, defer --> nodeDeferReply
    nodeDeferReply --> nodeWait1
    nodeSendReply --> nodeWait1
    nodeWait1 -- NO --> nodeWait1
    nodeWait1 -- YES --> nodeHeadCheck
    nodeHeadCheck -- NO --> nodeWait1
    nodeHeadCheck -- YES --> nodeEnterCS
    nodeEnterCS --> nodeDeqSelf
    nodeDeqSelf --> nodeBroadRel
    nodeBroadRel --> nodePeerProcRel
    nodePeerProcRel --> nodeFlushDef
    nodeFlushDef --> nodeExit

    style nodeStart fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeEnterCS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style nodeExit fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeHeadCheck fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeWait1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeReplyDecide fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Happens-Before Timeline Across Three Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1a["P1: a (C=1)"]:::ev
    p1b["P1: send m1 (C=2)"]:::send
    p1c["P1: rec m2 (C=5)"]:::rec
    p1d["P1: send m3 (C=5→6)"]:::send
    p2a["P2: rec m1 (C=4)"]:::rec
    p2b["P2: send m2 (C=4→5)"]:::send
    p2c["P2: internal (C=5)"]:::ev
    p3a["P3: rec m2 (C=5)"]:::rec
    p3b["P3: internal (C=5)"]:::ev

    p1a --> p1b
    p1b -.m1.-> p2a
    p2a --> p2b
    p2b -.m2.-> p1c
    p2b -.m2.-> p3a

    classDef ev fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000

```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows = local happens-before (same process).
- Dotted arrows labeled $m_k$ = message-passing happens-before.
- **Transitive closure:** $a \rightarrow p1b \rightarrow p2a \rightarrow p2b \rightarrow p1c$ — chain of length 4.

## 4.3 Request-Queue Evolution (from Worked Example 2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    t0["Time 0: All RQs empty. C1=C2=C3=0"]
    t1["P1 requests. RQ1=[(1,P1)], RQ2=empty, RQ3=empty. Sends REQ to P2,P3."]
    t2["P2 requests. RQ1=[(1,P1)], RQ2=[(1,P2)], RQ3=empty. Sends REQ to P1,P3."]
    t3["P2 receives P1's REQ. C2=2. RQ2=[(1,P1),(1,P2)]. Defers REPLY."]
    t4["P1 receives P2's REQ. C1=2. RQ1=[(1,P1),(1,P2)]. Defers REPLY."]
    t5["P3 receives both REQs. C3=3. RQ3=[(1,P1),(1,P2)]. Sends REPLY to P1 and P2."]
    t6["P1 receives P3's REPLY. C1=3. RQ1 head=(1,P1). Wait for P2's REPLY."]
    t7["P2 receives P3's REPLY. C2=4. RQ2 head=(1,P1) NOT self. Still waits."]
    t8["P1 eventually enters CS (theoretical resolution)."]
    t9["P1 releases. Sends RELEASE to P2,P3. RQ1 head=(1,P2)."]
    t10["P2 receives RELEASE. RQ2 head=(1,P2) self. Now eligible."]
    t11["P2 enters CS. Then releases. RQs all empty."]

    t0 --> t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5 --> t6 --> t7 --> t8 --> t9 --> t10 --> t11
```
