### Step 5: Assemble the Complete Model
$$T_p = \dfrac{T_1}{p} + k(t_0 + m \cdot t_s) + T_{idle} + \alpha \cdot p$$

### Step 6: Derive the Speedup
$$S(p) = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p} = \dfrac{T_1}{\dfrac{T_1}{p} + k(t_0 + m \cdot t_s) + T_{idle} + \alpha \cdot p}$$

### Step 7: Solve for Optimal Processor Count
To find the value of `p` that maximizes $S(p)$, we differentiate and set to zero (assuming $T_{idle} \approx 0$ for simplicity):

$$S(p) = \dfrac{T_1}{\dfrac{T_1}{p} + k \cdot t_0 + k \cdot m \cdot t_s + \alpha p}$$

Differentiate the denominator with respect to `p`:

$$\dfrac{d}{dp}\left[\dfrac{T_1}{p} + \alpha p\right] = -\dfrac{T_1}{p^2} + \alpha$$

Setting equal to zero:

$$-\dfrac{T_1}{p^2} + \alpha = 0$$

$$\alpha = \dfrac{T_1}{p^2}$$

$$p^2 = \dfrac{T_1}{\alpha}$$

$$p_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_1}{\alpha}}$$

### Step 8: Interpretation
The optimal number of processors scales as the **square root of the ratio of serial time to overhead coefficient**. Doubling the serial work allows ~41% more processors to be used efficiently. This is a powerful **KTU high-yield result** demonstrating why problem size must grow with processor count (the basis of **isoefficiency analysis**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Degree of Concurrency from Task Graph

Given a task graph with $n$ tasks and critical path length $T_\infty$, derive the **average degree of concurrency** $\overline{D}$.

### Step 1: Total Work
The total work of a parallel program is the sum of execution times of all tasks:
$$W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$$

### Step 2: Critical Path Lower Bound
The parallel execution time $T_p$ is bounded below by the critical path:
$$T_p \geq T_\infty = \max_{\text{path } P} \sum_{i \in P} t_i$$

### Step 3: Average Parallelism
By definition, the average degree of concurrency is the average number of tasks running at any time:
$$\overline{D} = \dfrac{\text{Total Work}}{\text{Critical Path Length}}$$

### Step 4: Substituting
$$\overline{D} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{T_\infty} = \dfrac{W}{T_\infty}$$

### Step 5: Example
Suppose we have **5 tasks** with execution times $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ seconds and a critical path of length **7 seconds**.
- Total work: $W = 1+2+3+4+5 = 15$ seconds.
- Average concurrency: $\overline{D} = 15/7 \approx 2.14$ tasks.

This means, on average, only 2.14 tasks run in parallel — a poor degree of concurrency for a 16-core machine.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Interaction-to-Computation Ratio and Scalability

### Step 1: Define the Ratio
$$r = \dfrac{t_{comm}}{t_{comp}}$$

### Step 2: Express $T_p$ in Terms of $r$
$$T_p = t_{comp} \cdot (1 + r) + T_{ovh}(p)$$

### Step 3: Express Speedup
$$S(p) = \dfrac{p \cdot t_{comp}}{t_{comp}(1+r) + T_{ovh}(p)} = \dfrac{p}{1 + r + \dfrac{T_{ovh}(p)}{t_{comp}}}$$

### Step 4: Limit Analysis
- If $r \to 0$ and $T_{ovh} \to 0$, then $S \to p$ (linear speedup).
- If $r \to \infty$ (interaction dominates), $S \to 0$ (parallelization is worse than serial).

### Step 5: Isoefficiency Derivation
For efficient scaling, we require $S(p) = \Theta(p)$, i.e., the parallel efficiency $E = S/p$ remains constant. From:
$$E = \dfrac{1}{1 + r + \dfrac{T_{ovh}(p)}{t_{comp}}}$$

Setting $E = c$ (a constant between 0 and 1):
$$1 + r + \dfrac{T_{ovh}(p)}{t_{comp}} = \dfrac{1}{c}$$

Therefore, the **total work $W$** must scale as:

$$W(p) = \Theta(T_{ovh}(p) \cdot p)$$

This is the **isoefficiency function** of the parallel algorithm — a critical concept in scalability analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If asked to compute the scalability of a parallel algorithm, derive the isoefficiency function $W = \Theta(K_1 \cdot p \cdot T_p)$ and identify its polynomial class. Linear isoefficiency (e.g., $W = \Theta(p)$) means **highly scalable**; quadratic isoefficiency (e.g., $W = \Theta(p^2)$) means **poorly scalable**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table

| **Property** | **Fine-Grained** | **Medium-Grained** | **Coarse-Grained** |
|:-------------|:----------------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| Task Count | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| Computation per Task | Small | Balanced | Large |
| Interaction Frequency | High | Moderate | Low |
| Best for Architecture | Shared Memory (Multi-core) | Hybrid (NUMA) | Distributed (Clusters) |
| Load Balancing | Easier | Moderate | Harder |
| Synchronization Overhead | High | Moderate | Low |
| Example | OpenMP loop-level | MPI+OpenMP hybrid | MPI process-per-node |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Task Interaction Graph (DAG Representation)

This diagram shows a typical **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** for a parallel computation. Each node is a task; each directed edge represents a data dependency/interaction.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Task T1: Input Read"] --> nodeB["Task T2: Preprocess A"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Task T3: Preprocess B"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Task T4: Preprocess C"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Task T5: Compute Stage 1"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF["Task T6: Compute Stage 2"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Task T7: Aggregate"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Task T8: Output Write"]

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeG fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeH fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Yellow node (T1):** Root task — no predecessors.
- **Blue nodes (T2, T3, T4):** Independent tasks — can run in **parallel** (degree of concurrency = 3 here).
- **Green nodes (T5, T6):** Tasks with multiple predecessors — must **synchronize** on inputs from blue nodes.
- **Orange node (T7):** Synchronization point — barrier-like aggregation.
- **Red node (T8):** Final task — on the critical path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Path in this DAG:** T1 → T2 → T5 → T7 → T8 (or T1 → T3 → T5 → T7 → T8) is the longest chain, determining the **lower bound on parallel runtime**.

---

## 4.2 Task Granularity vs. Interaction Cost Trade-off

This block diagram illustrates how the **balance between computation and communication** determines whether parallelization is beneficial.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA["Coarse-Grained Tasks"] --> procA["Low Interaction Cost"]
    inputA --> procB["High Computation per Task"]
    inputB["Medium-Grained Tasks"] --> procC["Balanced Cost"]
    inputC["Fine-Grained Tasks"] --> procD["High Interaction Cost"]
    inputC --> procE["Low Computation per Task"]

    procA --> outA["Good Scalability"]
    procB --> outA
    procC --> outB["Moderate Scalability"]
    procD --> outC["Poor Scalability on Distributed Memory"]
    procE --> outC
    procD --> outD["Good Scalability on Shared Memory"]

    style inputA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style inputB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style inputC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style outA fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style outB fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style outC fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style outD fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Communication Pattern Architecture

This diagram maps out the **four canonical communication patterns** in parallel computing.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Processor P1"] -- "Broadcast" --> P2
    P1 -- "Broadcast" --> P3
    P1 -- "Broadcast" --> P4
    P2["Processor P2"] -- "Point-to-Point" --> P3
    P3["Processor P3"] -- "Point-to-Point" --> P4
    P2 -- "Reduce" --> P1
    P3 -- "Reduce" --> P1
    P4 -- "Reduce" --> P1
    P2 -- "Barrier Sync" --> P3
    P3 -- "Barrier Sync" --> P4

    style P1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style P4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Pattern Mappings:**
- **Broadcast:** One-to-all (e.g., distributing input data).
- **Point-to-Point:** One-to-one (e.g., halo exchange in CFD).
- **Reduce:** All-to-one (e.g., computing global sum).
- **Barrier:** All-to-all synchronization (all tasks wait at a rendezvous point).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define task granularity. Differentiate between fine-grained and coarse-grained tasks with an example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Task granularity is the ratio of the amount of computation performed by a task to the amount of communication it performs. It is mathematically defined as $G = \dfrac{t_{comp}}{t_{comm}}$.

**Fine-Grained Tasks (1 mark):** These tasks perform small amounts of computation and require frequent communication/synchronization with other tasks. They are best suited for **shared memory architectures**. *Example:* In OpenMP, a `#pragma omp parallel for` dividing a loop into thousands of micro-tasks — each task computes a few iterations and synchronizes via implicit barriers.

**Coarse-Grained Tasks (1 mark):** These tasks perform large amounts of computation and require infrequent communication. They are ideal for **distributed memory architectures** like clusters. *Example:* In MPI, assigning an entire sub-matrix to each process — each task performs substantial computation before a single halo-exchange communication step.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a task interaction? List and briefly explain any two types of interactions in parallel programs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A task interaction is any communication, synchronization, or data-sharing operation between two or more concurrently executing tasks in a parallel program. Interactions introduce **latency, bandwidth consumption, and synchronization overhead**.

**Two Types (2 marks):**

1. **Synchronous Interaction:** The sending task blocks (waits) until the receiving task is ready to accept the data. This guarantees data delivery but introduces waiting time. *Example:* The MPI blocking call `MPI_Send`.

2. **Asynchronous Interaction:** The sending task continues execution immediately after initiating the communication, without waiting for the receiver. This allows overlap of computation and communication but requires careful buffer management. *Example:* The MPI non-blocking call `MPI_Isend`.

*(Other valid types: Point-to-Point vs. Collective, Tightly vs. Loosely Coupled, Shared Memory vs. Message Passing — any two well-explained earn full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often confuse **task granularity** with **task size** — granularity is a *ratio*, size is an *absolute quantity*.
> 2. When asked for the **optimal number of processors**, students forget to account for overhead — naïve answers using $T_p = T_1/p$ lose 3-4 marks.
> 3. In interaction analysis, students forget to mention **synchronization cost** separately from data-transfer cost — both must be itemized.
> 4. Isoefficiency derivations require **showing the differentiation step** — skipping the math costs 5 marks.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define **task characteristics** in parallel computing. Explain in detail any **four key characteristics** of tasks with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **various types of task interactions** in parallel programs. Compare **synchronous vs. asynchronous**, and **point-to-point vs. collective** communication with example MPI function calls. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Task Characteristics (7 marks):**

A **task** is an abstract unit of computational work scheduled for execution. Tasks possess the following key characteristics:

**1. Task Granularity (2 marks):**
The ratio of computation to communication performed by a task, $G = t_{comp}/t_{comm}$. Granularity determines the **scalability ceiling** of a parallel program.
- *Fine-grained* (G ≈ 1): OpenMP loop parallelization.
- *Coarse-grained* (G >> 1): MPI process-per-node.
- *Medium-grained* (G ≈ 10): Hybrid MPI+OpenMP.

**2. Task Concurrency (2 marks):**
The maximum number of tasks executable in parallel. For a task graph with total work $W$ and critical path $T_\infty$, the average concurrency is $\overline{D} = W / T_\infty$. High concurrency enables linear speedup; low concurrency bottlenecks performance.

**3. Task Dependency (1.5 marks):**
Tasks can be **independent** (run in any order) or **dependent** (must respect a precedence constraint, represented as a DAG edge). The **critical path** — the longest chain of dependent tasks — sets the **lower bound on parallel runtime**: $T_p \geq T_\infty$.

**4. Task Symmetry (1.5 marks):**
- **SPMD (Symmetric):** All processors run the same code on different data. Common in MPI and CUDA.
- **MPMD (Asymmetric):** Different processors run different code. Common in master-worker or pipeline parallel patterns.

---

**(b) Types of Task Interactions (7 marks):**

Task interactions are categorized as follows:

**1. Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Communication (3.5 marks):**

| **Property** | **Synchronous** | **Asynchronous** |
|:-------------|:----------------|:-----------------|
| Blocking | Sender blocks until receiver ready | Sender proceeds immediately |
| Latency Hiding | No | Yes (overlap with computation) |
| Data Safety | Guaranteed | Requires explicit wait (e.g., `MPI_Wait`) |
| MPI Example | `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv` | `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv` |
| Use Case | Strict ordering, small messages | Pipeline, latency hiding |

**2. Point-to-Point vs. Collective Communication (3.5 marks):**

- **Point-to-Point:** Communication between **two specific tasks**.
  - Examples: `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`, `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`.
  - Use case: Halo exchanges in stencil computations, pipeline stages.

- **Collective:** Communication involving **all tasks in a communicator**.
  - **Broadcast** (one-to-all): `MPI_Bcast` — distribute input data.
  - **Reduce** (all-to-one): `MPI_Reduce` — compute global sum.
  - **All-Reduce** (all-to-all result): `MPI_Allreduce` — used in deep learning.
  - **Barrier** (synchronization): `MPI_Barrier` — all tasks wait.
  - **All-to-All**: `MPI_Alltoall` — transpose, FFT.
  - Use case: Distributed aggregations, parallel I/O.

> **Engineering Insight (Bonus 1 mark):** Collective operations often have **optimized implementations** (e.g., tree-based reduction) that are significantly faster than naïve point-to-point equivalents. This is why MPI encourages collective calls for global operations.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the **fundamental parallel execution time model** $T_p = \dfrac{T_1}{p} + k(t_0 + m \cdot t_s) + T_{idle} + \alpha p$ starting from the basic definition of speedup. Clearly explain each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel program takes **T₁ = 100 seconds** to execute serially. When parallelized on **p = 10 processors**, the total communication time is **8 seconds**, idle time is **5 seconds**, and overhead is **2 seconds**. Calculate: **(i)** the parallel execution time $T_p$, **(ii)** the speedup $S$, **(iii)** the efficiency $E$, and **(iv)** the optimal number of processors $p_{opt}$ assuming $T_{idle} = 0$ and overhead coefficient $\alpha = 0.5$ seconds. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of the Parallel Execution Time Model (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** By definition, speedup is:
$$S = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** The parallel execution time $T_p$ consists of four components: parallel computation, communication, idle (waiting), and runtime overhead.
$$T_p = T_{comp}^{parallel} + T_{comm} + T_{idle} + T_{ovh}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Under **perfect load balancing**, computation is evenly distributed:
$$T_{comp}^{parallel} = \dfrac{T_1}{p}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Communication cost, using the **Hockney model** for `k` operations each of `m` bytes:
$$T_{comm} = k \cdot (t_0 + m \cdot t_s)$$
where $t_0$ is the **latency** (startup time) and $t_s$ is the **per-byte transfer cost**.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting and assuming overhead grows linearly with `p` ($\alpha$ is a constant):
$$\boxed{T_p = \dfrac{T_1}{p} + k(t_0 + m \cdot t_s) + T_{idle} + \alpha p}$$

**Term Explanations:**
- $T_1/p$ — useful parallel computation.
- $k(t_0 + m \cdot t_s)$ — communication cost (latency + transfer).
- $T_{idle}$ — time wasted on barriers or waiting for dependencies.
- $\alpha p$ — runtime/coordination overhead scaling with processor count.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 100$ s
- $p = 10$
- $T_{comm} = 8$ s
- $T_{idle} = 5$ s
- $T_{ovh} = 2$ s
- $\alpha = 0.5$ s, $T_{idle} = 0$ (for part iv)

**(i) Parallel Execution Time (2 marks):**
$$T_p = \dfrac{T_1}{p} + T_{comm} + T_{idle} + T_{ovh}$$
$$T_p = \dfrac{100}{10} + 8 + 5 + 2 = 10 + 8 + 5 + 2$$
$$\boxed{T_p = 25 \text{ seconds}}$$

**(ii) Speedup (2 marks):**
$$S = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p} = \dfrac{100}{25}$$
$$\boxed{S = 4}$$

**(iii) Efficiency (1.5 marks):**
$$E = \dfrac{S}{p} = \dfrac{4}{10} = 0.4$$
$$\boxed{E = 40\%}$$

**(iv) Optimal Number of Processors (1.5 marks):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:
$$p_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_1}{\alpha}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{0.5}} = \sqrt{200}$$
$$\boxed{p_{opt} \approx 14.14 \approx 14 \text{ processors}}$$

Since `p` must be an integer, we use `p = 14` (or 15, depending on the rounding convention).

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** At `p = 14` with $T_{idle} = 0$, $T_{ovh} = \alpha p = 7$ s, and $T_{comm} = 8$ s:
> $T_p = 100/14 + 8 + 7 \approx 7.14 + 15 = 22.14$ s.
> Speedup = $100/22.14 \approx 4.52$, which is **greater** than the speedup at `p = 10` (which was 4). ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Characteristics of Tasks and Interactions**

### A. Tasks
- A **task** is the **atomic unit of work** scheduled on a processor.
- Key characteristics: **Granularity, Concurrency, Dependency, Symmetry, Mapping**.
- **Granularity** $G = t_{comp}/t_{comm}$ — the dominant knob for scalability.
- **Average degree of concurrency** $\overline{D} = W / T_\infty$.
- **Critical path** $T_\infty$ = longest dependent task chain = **lower bound on $T_p$**.

### B. Interactions
- An **interaction** is communication, synchronization, or shared data access between tasks.
- Cost = **Latency $t_0$** + **Transfer cost $m \cdot t_s$**.
- **Synchronous** = blocks; **Asynchronous** = non-blocking (overlap allowed).
- **Point-to-Point** = 2 tasks; **Collective** = all tasks (Bcast, Reduce, Barrier, etc.).
- **Tightly coupled** = shared memory; **Loosely coupled** = distributed memory.

### C. The Fundamental Timing Model
$$T_p = \dfrac{T_1}{p} + k(t_0 + m \cdot t_s) + T_{idle} + \alpha p$$
$$S = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p}, \quad E = \dfrac{S}{p}$$
$$p_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_1}{\alpha}}$$

### D. KTU Exam-Specific Tips
- Always **itemize all four components** of $T_p$ in derivations.
- Mention **synchronization overhead** separately from communication cost.
- For scalability questions, compute the **isoefficiency function** $W(p)$.
- For graphs, **identify the critical path** and **maximum degree of concurrency**.
- Use the **Hockney model** $t_{comm} = t_0 + m \cdot t_s$ for any communication timing.
- Distinguish **speedup** (absolute) from **efficiency** (normalized) in every answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping Techniques for Load Balancing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mapping Techniques for Load Balancing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mapping** in parallel and distributed computing refers to the systematic assignment of computational tasks (or data elements) to a set of available processors in order to **minimize total execution time**, **balance the workload**, and **reduce inter-processor communication overhead**. Formally, mapping is the process of finding a function $M: T \rightarrow P$, where $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$ is the set of tasks and $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m\}$ is the set of processors, such that an objective function (typically makespan or total execution cost) is minimized.

**Load Balancing** is a runtime/compile-time strategy that ensures the workload is distributed **evenly** across all processors so that no processor remains idle while others are overloaded. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, mapping techniques are categorized as a **static** optimization problem (decisions made at compile time) distinct from dynamic load balancing (decisions made at runtime).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The PECCT411 syllabus treats mapping techniques as a precursor to dynamic load balancing. Static mapping assumes prior knowledge of task execution times and communication patterns, while dynamic load balancing reacts to runtime system state. Both are assessed in the ESE.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **busy airport check-in hall** with 6 counters and 300 passengers arriving simultaneously. If all passengers are queued at counter #1 while counters #2–#6 are empty, the total service time is enormous. A **load balancing mapping** is like the airport's queue management system:
- It looks at all **counters** (processors).
- It looks at all **passengers** (tasks) and their baggage sizes (task computation cost).
- It **routes** passengers to the least-busy counter.

If the system knows passenger details *in advance* (e.g., from a flight manifest), it can do a **static mapping** (pre-assigned counters). If passengers trickle in randomly, it must perform **dynamic load balancing** (re-routing at runtime).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The goal of mapping is NOT to make every processor 100% busy — it is to make the **slowest processor finish as early as possible** (minimize the *makespan*).

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standardized Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Task Graph** | A DAG $G = (V, E)$ where nodes $V$ represent tasks and edges $E$ represent inter-task dependencies. |
| **Computation Cost** $w_i$ | The CPU time required to execute task $t_i$ on any processor. |
| **Communication Cost** $c_{ij}$ | The time to send a message from task $t_i$ to task $t_j$. |
| **Makespan** | The wall-clock time at which the **last** processor finishes its assigned work. |
| **Speedup** $S = T_{\text{serial}} / T_{\text{parallel}}$ | Ratio of sequential to parallel execution time. |
| **Efficiency** $E = S / m$ | Speedup normalized to the number of processors $m$. |
| **Load Imbalance Factor (LIF)** | A scalar measuring deviation from perfect balance. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Mapping techniques are foundational in **HPC cluster schedulers** (SLURM, PBS), **cloud orchestration** (Kubernetes pod placement), **GPU kernel launches**, and **embedded multi-core SoCs**. Every modern parallel runtime (OpenMP, MPI, CUDA) uses some form of mapping under the hood.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Mapping Techniques

Mapping techniques are broadly classified along two axes:

### 2.1.1 By Decision Time
- **Static Mapping (Compile-Time):** All mapping decisions are made before program execution. Requires a-priori knowledge of:
  - Task execution costs $w_i$
  - Inter-task communication costs $c_{ij}$
  - Processor network topology
  - **Assumption:** Workload is **predictable** and **homogeneous**.
- **Dynamic Mapping (Runtime):** Decisions are made during execution using system state feedback.
  - Sub-categories: **Centralized** (one master node) vs **Distributed** (peer-to-peer).
  - Sub-categories: **Synchronous** (periodic state exchange) vs **Asynchronous** (event-driven).

### 2.1.2 By Algorithmic Approach
- **Deterministic (Algorithmic):** Greedy, graph-based, optimal — guarantees a specific output.
- **Stochastic (Randomized):** Simulated annealing, genetic algorithms — explore solution space probabilistically.
- **Hybrid:** Combine two or more approaches (e.g., initial greedy seed + simulated annealing refinement).

## 2.2 The Task Assignment Problem (TAP) — Formal Model

Given:
- A set of $n$ tasks $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$
- A set of $m$ processors $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m\}$
- An **$n \times n$ cost matrix** $E$ where $E[i][j]$ = cost of executing task $i$ on processor $j$
- An **$n \times n$ communication matrix** $C$ where $C[i][k]$ = inter-task communication cost

**Objective:** Find an assignment matrix $X \in \{0,1\}^{n \times m}$ that minimizes:

$$F(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} X_{ij} \cdot E_{ij} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \sum_{l=1}^{m} X_{ij} \cdot X_{kl} \cdot C_{ik}$$

subject to:
$$\sum_{j=1}^{m} X_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, n\} \quad \text{(each task assigned to exactly one processor)}$$

## 2.3 Static Mapping Algorithms (Detailed)

### 2.3.1 Block Mapping
Divides a data domain of size $N$ into $m$ equal contiguous blocks and assigns block $k$ to processor $k$.

$$\text{Block}_k = \left[ k \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{N}{m} \right\rfloor, \; (k+1) \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{N}{m} \right\rfloor \right)$$

- **Pros:** Simple, excellent cache locality, low communication.
- **Cons:** Severe load imbalance if work-per-element is non-uniform.

### 2.3.2 Cyclic Mapping
Assigns element $i$ to processor $(i \bmod m)$. Distributes data in round-robin fashion.
- **Pros:** Perfectly balances small data; tolerates non-uniform work.
- **Cons:** Destroys locality; increases communication.

### 2.3.3 Block-Cyclic Mapping (Hybrid)
Blocks of size $b$ are distributed cyclically across $m$ processors.
- Element $i$ is assigned to processor: $\lfloor (i / b) \rfloor \bmod m$
- Used in **ScaLAPACK**, **HPF**, and most parallel BLAS libraries.

### 2.3.4 Greedy Mapping
Repeatedly assigns the largest unassigned task to the processor with the **current minimum load**.

**Algorithm:**
1. Sort tasks in descending order of $w_i$.
2. Initialize $L_j = 0$ for all $j \in \{1, \ldots, m\}$.
3. For each task $t_i$ in sorted order, assign it to $p^* = \arg\min_j L_j$.
4. Update $L_{p^*} \leftarrow L_{p^*} + w_i$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n \log n + n \cdot m)$

### 2.3.5 Recursive Bisection (Graph Partitioning)
For task graphs $G = (V, E)$:
1. Recursively split the task graph into $k$ balanced subgraphs using **edge-cut minimization** (e.g., Kernighan-Lin, METIS).
2. Each subgraph is assigned to one processor.

**Edge Cut Cost:**
$$\text{Cut}(V_1, V_2) = \sum_{i \in V_1, \; j \in V_2} E[i][j]$$

### 2.3.6 Simulated Annealing (Stochastic)
Probabilistically accepts worse mappings to escape local optima.

**Acceptance Probability:**
$$P_{\text{accept}}(\Delta E, T) = e^{-\Delta E / T}$$

where $\Delta E$ is the change in cost and $T$ is the "temperature" parameter that decreases over time.

## 2.4 Load Balancing Metrics (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Examiners love asking about **Load Imbalance Factor** and **Speedup / Efficiency**. Memorize the formulas below.

| Metric | Formula | Optimal Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Imbalance Factor** | $\text{LIF} = \dfrac{\max_j L_j - \min_j L_j}{\max_j L_j}$ | $0$ |
| **Mean Load** | $\bar{L} = \dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} L_j$ | — |
| **Standard Deviation of Load** | $\sigma_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m}(L_j - \bar{L})^2}$ | $0$ |
| **Speedup** | $S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$ | $\leq m$ |
| **Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{S}{m}$ | $\leq 1$ |
| **Isoefficiency** | $W = K \cdot T_p$ | — |
| **Load Balance Ratio** | $\text{LBR} = \dfrac{\bar{L}}{\max_j L_j}$ | $1$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **HPC Cluster Schedulers:** SLURM uses combinatorial optimization for job-to-node mapping.
- **Cloud Computing (Kubernetes):** Pod-to-node scheduling uses bin-packing heuristics (greedy variants).
- **GPU Computing:** Block-cyclic distribution maps matrix tiles to CUDA thread blocks.
- **Embedded Systems:** Real-time OS schedulers (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks) map tasks to cores using deadline-aware algorithms.
- **Data Centers:** Hadoop YARN uses capacity-based load balancing to assign map/reduce tasks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lower Bound on Makespan

We want to prove that no mapping can achieve a makespan smaller than a deterministic lower bound $T^*$.

**Setup:**
- Total work to be done: $W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i$
- Number of processors: $m$
- The fastest processor can do at most $w_{\max} = \max_i w_i$ work

**Step 1: Lower bound from total work.**
Every processor can perform at most 1 unit of work per unit time (normalized), and total work must be redistributed across $m$ processors. Therefore, even with perfect parallelism:

$$T_p \geq \frac{W}{m} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}{m}$$

**Step 2: Lower bound from critical path.**
The longest dependency chain (critical path) in the task DAG defines a sequential time floor:

$$T_p \geq T_{\text{critical}} = \sum_{e \in \text{critical path}} (w_e + c_e)$$

**Step 3: Combined tight lower bound.**
The actual minimum makespan is bounded below by both:

$$T_p \geq \max \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}{m}, \; T_{\text{critical}} \right)$$

**Step 4: Optimality interpretation.**
A mapping is **optimal** iff:

$$T_p = \max \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}{m}, \; T_{\text{critical}} \right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Greedy Mapping's Worst-Case Cost

The greedy algorithm has a known approximation ratio for the **multiprocessor scheduling problem**.

**Setup:**
- $T_{\text{OPT}}$ = makespan of optimal mapping
- $T_{\text{GREEDY}}$ = makespan of greedy mapping

**Step 1: Bound on max load.**
At the end of greedy assignment, let processor $p^*$ be the one with maximum load. Task $t_{i^*}$ was the **last** task assigned to $p^*$.

**Step 2: Lower bound on $T_{\text{OPT}}$.**
Before $t_{i^*}$ was placed, the load on $p^*$ was $L_{p^*} - w_{i^*}$. At that moment, since greedy chose $p^*$ (the minimum), every other processor had load $\geq L_{p^*} - w_{i^*}$.

Total work already done before placing $t_{i^*}$:
$$W_{\text{prev}} \geq m \cdot (L_{p^*} - w_{i^*})$$

**Step 3: Optimal makespan relation.**
$$T_{\text{OPT}} \geq \frac{W_{\text{prev}} + w_{i^*}}{m} = L_{p^*} - w_{i^*} + \frac{w_{i^*}}{m}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $T_{\text{GREEDY}}$.**
Since $L_{p^*} = T_{\text{GREEDY}}$, we have:

$$T_{\text{GREEDY}} \leq T_{\text{OPT}} + w_{i^*} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right) \leq T_{\text{OPT}} + w_{\max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)$$

**Step 5: Approximation ratio.**

$$\frac{T_{\text{GREEDY}}}{T_{\text{OPT}}} \leq 1 + \frac{w_{\max}}{T_{\text{OPT}}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right) \leq 2 - \frac{1}{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The LPT (Longest Processing Time) Greedy variant is a **$\left(2 - \frac{1}{m}\right)$-approximation algorithm**. This is a classic KTU 2024 expected question.

## 3.3 Derivation: Block-Cyclic Mapping Communication Cost

**Setup:**
- $N$ elements, $m$ processors, block size $b$
- Each processor owns elements with indices $\{(k + jm) \cdot b \;|\; k = 0, \ldots, b-1, \; j = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$

**Step 1: Neighbors of processor $p$.**
Two adjacent elements in memory are owned by processors differing by $1$ (modulo $m$).

**Step 2: Number of cross-processor boundaries.**
For a 1D stencil of width $N$ with block size $b$, the number of ghost-cell exchanges per processor per step is:

$$C_{\text{comm}} = 2 \cdot \left(\left\lceil \frac{N}{m \cdot b} \right\rceil\right) \text{ messages per processor}$$

**Step 3: Total communication volume.**
$$V_{\text{comm}} = 2 \cdot b \cdot \left\lceil \frac{N}{m \cdot b} \right\rceil \text{ words transferred}$$

**Step 4: Special case $b = 1$ (pure cyclic).**
$$V_{\text{comm}} = 2 \cdot N \text{ words}$$

**Step 5: Special case $b = N/m$ (pure block).**
$$V_{\text{comm}} = 2 \cdot \frac{N}{m} \text{ words (only at domain edges)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Trade-off:** Block mapping minimizes communication, cyclic mapping maximizes parallelism for small tasks. Block-cyclic lets you tune the **compromise** via the block size parameter $b$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Map 7 tasks with execution times $\{w_1, \ldots, w_7\} = \{8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2\}$ to 3 processors using **LPT Greedy**. Compute makespan, speedup, and efficiency.

**Step 1: Sort tasks in descending order.**
$$8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2$$

**Step 2: Initialize loads** $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = 0$.

**Step 3: Iterate greedy assignments.**

| Step | Task $w$ | Min-load Processor | $L_1$ | $L_2$ | $L_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | $p_1$ | **8** | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 7 | $p_2$ | 8 | **7** | 0 |
| 3 | 6 | $p_3$ | 8 | 7 | **6** |
| 4 | 5 | $p_3$ | 8 | 7 | **11** |
| 5 | 4 | $p_2$ | 8 | **11** | 11 |
| 6 | 3 | $p_1$ | **11** | 11 | 11 |
| 7 | 2 | any (tie) | 11 | 11 | **13** |

**Step 4: Compute makespan.**
$$T_p = \max(L_1, L_2, L_3) = \max(11, 11, 13) = 13$$

**Step 5: Compute sequential time.**
$$T_s = 8 + 7 + 6 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 35$$

**Step 6: Speedup and efficiency.**

$$S = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{35}{13} \approx 2.69$$

$$E = \frac{S}{m} = \frac{2.69}{3} \approx 0.897 \text{ (or 89.7\%)}$$

**Step 7: Verify against lower bound.**
$$T_p \geq \frac{W}{m} = \frac{35}{3} \approx 11.67 \;\;\checkmark \; 13 \geq 11.67$$

**Step 8: Optimal makespan (by exhaustive search).**
A better mapping exists: assign $\{8, 5\}$ to $p_1$ (load 13), $\{7, 4\}$ to $p_2$ (load 11), $\{6, 3, 2\}$ to $p_3$ (load 11). Thus $T_{\text{OPT}} = 13$ — **Greedy achieved OPT in this case**.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Mapping Technique | Time Complexity | Approximation Ratio | Load Balance | Comm. Overhead | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Block** | $O(N)$ | $1 + (m-1)/m$ | Poor for non-uniform | Very Low | Uniform data, dense matrices |
| **Cyclic** | $O(N)$ | $1$ (perfect) | Perfect for small data | Very High | Sparse matrices, irregular work |
| **Block-Cyclic** | $O(N/b)$ | Tunable | Tunable | Tunable | ScaLAPACK, dense linear algebra |
| **LPT Greedy** | $O(n \log n + nm)$ | $2 - 1/m$ | Good | Low | Independent tasks |
| **Recursive Bisection (METIS)** | $O(n \log m)$ | Near-optimal | Good | Minimized | Task graphs, unstructured meshes |
| **Simulated Annealing** | $O(k \cdot n^2)$ | Tunable | Excellent | Tunable | Offline one-time mapping |
| **Genetic Algorithm** | $O(g \cdot p \cdot n)$ | Tunable | Excellent | Tunable | Large search spaces |
| **Min-Cost Max-Flow** | $O(n^2 m)$ | Optimal (LP) | Optimal | Optimal | Small problem instances |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.3 LPT Greedy Algorithm — Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Start]) --> S1[Read task set T with execution costs w1 to wn]
    S1 --> S2[Sort tasks in descending order of wi]
    S2 --> S3[Initialize load array Lj = 0 for j = 1 to m]
    S3 --> S4{Pick largest unassigned task ti}
    S4 --> S5[Find processor p with min Lj]
    S5 --> S6[Assign task ti to processor p]
    S6 --> S7[Update Lp = Lp + wi]
    S7 --> S8{All tasks assigned?}
    S8 -->|No| S4
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Compute makespan Tp = max of Lj]
    S9 --> S10[Compute speedup S = Ts / Tp]
    S10 --> S11[Compute efficiency E = S / m]
    S11 --> S12([Output final mapping and metrics])

    style S0 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style S12 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style S4 fill:#fff59d,color:#000000
    style S8 fill:#fff59d,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Simulated Annealing Mapping — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SA0([Start: Random initial mapping M0]) --> SA1[Compute current cost C0 of M0]
    SA1 --> SA2[Set temperature T = Tmax]
    SA2 --> SA3[Set iteration counter k = 0]

    SA3 --> SA4[Perturb mapping: generate neighbor M1 by swapping two tasks]
    SA4 --> SA5[Compute cost C1 of M1]
    SA5 --> SA6[Compute delta E = C1 - C0]
    SA6 --> SA7{delta E less than 0?}

    SA7 -->|Yes, better| SA8[Accept M1: C0 = C1, M0 = M1]
    SA7 -->|No, worse| SA9[Generate random r in 0,1]
    SA9 --> SA10{r less than exp of negative delta E over T?}
    SA10 -->|Yes| SA8
    SA10 -->|No| SA11[Reject: keep M0]

    SA8 --> SA12[Increment k]
    SA11 --> SA12
    SA12 --> SA13{k less than max iterations?}
    SA13 -->|Yes| SA14[Apply cooling schedule T = alpha times T]
    SA14 --> SA4
    SA13 -->|No| SA15([Output best mapping found])

    style SA0 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style SA15 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff

```
