# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define static mapping. How does it differ from dynamic load balancing?**

**Model Answer:**
Static mapping is a compile-time optimization technique in which all task-to-processor assignments are determined **before** program execution, using pre-known information about task computation costs, inter-task communication costs, and processor topology. In contrast, dynamic load balancing makes these decisions **at runtime** based on the current system state, requiring periodic exchange of load information among processors. Static mapping is preferred when the workload is predictable and homogeneous, while dynamic load balancing is used for unpredictable, heterogeneous workloads.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for difference, 1 for applicability)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the LPT scheduling rule and write its approximation ratio.**

**Model Answer:**
The **LPT (Longest Processing Time first)** rule sorts all $n$ tasks in descending order of their computation cost $w_i$, then greedily assigns each task (in that sorted order) to the processor that currently has the **minimum load**. Its approximation ratio for the makespan minimization problem is:

$$\frac{T_{\text{LPT}}}{T_{\text{OPT}}} \leq 2 - \frac{1}{m}$$

where $m$ is the number of processors.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for rule, 1.5 for approximation ratio with formula)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **task assignment problem (TAP)** with its formal mathematical formulation. Discuss the role of the cost matrix $E$ and communication matrix $C$ in achieving a balanced mapping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **block-cyclic mapping** technique used in parallel linear algebra libraries such as ScaLAPACK. Derive an expression for the total communication volume per processor for a 1D array of $N$ elements distributed on $m$ processors with block size $b$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Task Assignment Problem

The **Task Assignment Problem (TAP)** is the foundational formal model for static mapping in parallel computing. Given a parallel program decomposed into $n$ tasks, the goal is to assign each task to exactly one of $m$ processors such that the **total execution time** (computation + communication) is minimized.

**Formal Components:**

1. **Task Set:** $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$ — units of work.
2. **Processor Set:** $P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m\}$ — execution units.
3. **Execution Cost Matrix $E$** of size $n \times m$:
$$E[i][j] = \text{CPU time to execute } t_i \text{ on } p_j$$
   - When processors are homogeneous, $E[i][j] = w_i$ for all $j$.
4. **Inter-Task Communication Cost Matrix $C$** of size $n \times n$:
$$C[i][k] = \text{Communication time from } t_i \text{ to } t_k$$
   - If $t_i$ and $t_k$ are on the same processor, $C[i][k] = 0$.

**Objective Function:**

$$F(X) = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} X_{ij} \cdot E_{ij}}_{\text{Computation Cost}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \sum_{l=1}^{m} X_{ij} \cdot X_{kl} \cdot C_{ik}}_{\text{Communication Cost}}$$

**Subject to:**
$$\sum_{j=1}^{m} X_{ij} = 1, \quad \forall i = 1, \ldots, n$$

TAP is **NP-hard** in general. For small instances, Integer Linear Programming (ILP) solvers give optimal solutions. For large instances, heuristics (Greedy, SA, GA) are used.

### Part (b) — Block-Cyclic Mapping

**Concept:** Block-cyclic mapping divides a data array of $N$ elements into blocks of size $b$, and then distributes these blocks in round-robin fashion across $m$ processors. Element with global index $i$ is owned by processor:

$$\text{owner}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{i}{b} \right\rfloor \bmod m$$

**Worked Example:** $N = 12$, $m = 3$, $b = 2$.

| Processor | Owned Elements (Global Index) |
|---|---|
| $p_0$ | $0, 1, 6, 7$ |
| $p_1$ | $2, 3, 8, 9$ |
| $p_2$ | $4, 5, 10, 11$ |

**Communication Volume Derivation (1D case):**

Consider a 1D stencil where each element exchanges data with its left and right neighbor. Each processor $p$ owns $n_p = \left\lceil N / m \right\rceil$ elements (rounded up for some processors).

The number of block boundaries owned by processor $p$ (interior boundaries requiring communication) is:

$$B_p = 2 \cdot \left\lceil \frac{N}{m \cdot b} \right\rceil - \delta_p$$

where $\delta_p$ accounts for the two physical domain edges (only one boundary per edge processor).

**Total communication volume per processor:**

$$V_{\text{comm}}(p) = 2 \cdot b \cdot \left(\left\lceil \frac{N}{m \cdot b} \right\rceil - 1\right) + 2b \cdot \delta_p$$

For typical interior processors (ignoring edge effects):

$$V_{\text{comm}} \approx 2b \cdot \left(\left\lceil \frac{N}{m \cdot b} \right\rceil - 1\right) \text{ words}$$

**Special cases:**
- $b = 1$ (pure cyclic): $V_{\text{comm}} = 2 \cdot N / m \cdot m = 2N$ (each element has neighbors on a different processor).
- $b = N/m$ (pure block): $V_{\text{comm}} = 2b = 2N/m$ (only at the two domain edges).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> Students often forget to include the **edge effects** in their derivation. The full formula must distinguish between interior and edge processors. Marks are lost for not mentioning that ScaLAPACK uses **2D block-cyclic** distribution, not 1D.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **static** and **dynamic** mapping techniques. Discuss **recursive bisection** as a graph-based static mapping technique, including the Kernighan-Lin heuristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With an appropriate example, explain the **simulated annealing** algorithm for static task mapping. State the acceptance probability formula and explain the role of the temperature parameter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Static vs Dynamic + Recursive Bisection

**Static Mapping:** Decisions made at compile time. Requires a-priori knowledge of computation and communication costs. Best for predictable workloads. No runtime overhead. Cannot adapt to changing conditions.

**Dynamic Mapping:** Decisions made at runtime based on actual load information. Handles unpredictable workloads. Incurs communication and synchronization overhead. Can adapt to load imbalance.

| Aspect | Static | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Time** | Compile time | Runtime |
| **Knowledge Required** | Full | Partial (current state) |
| **Overhead** | None at runtime | High (info exchange) |
| **Adaptivity** | None | High |
| **Optimality** | Provable (offline) | Heuristic |

**Recursive Bisection:**

For a task graph $G = (V, E)$, recursive bisection repeatedly partitions the vertex set $V$ into $k$ balanced subgraphs minimizing the **edge cut** between partitions.

**Step 1:** Start with $G = (V, E)$, $|V| = n$ tasks.

**Step 2:** At each level, find a partition $V = V_1 \cup V_2$ such that:
- $|V_1| \approx |V_2| \approx n/2$ (load balance constraint)
- The number of edges crossing the cut is minimized:
$$\text{Cut}(V_1, V_2) = \sum_{(i,j) \in E, \; i \in V_1, \; j \in V_2} 1$$

**Step 3:** Recursively apply to each subgraph until $k = m$ partitions exist (one per processor).

**Kernighan-Lin Algorithm (Bisection Refinement):**

Given an initial bisection $(A, B)$:
1. Compute initial gain $g(v) = \text{external edges of } v - \text{internal edges of } v$ for all $v$.
2. Repeatedly select the unvisited vertex pair $(a \in A, b \in B)$ that maximizes gain $D = g(a) + g(b) - 2 \cdot c_{ab}$.
3. Lock $a$ and $b$ (mark as moved).
4. Record the cumulative maximum gain prefix $D_{\max}$.
5. After all vertices are processed, swap the prefix of pairs achieving $D_{\max}$.
6. Repeat until no improvement.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n^2 \log n)$ for $n$ vertices.

### Part (b) — Simulated Annealing for Task Mapping

**Inspiration:** Mimics the physical cooling of metals. Allows the algorithm to **escape local optima** by occasionally accepting worse solutions.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize:** Random initial mapping $M_0$. Set $T = T_{\max}$ (high). Set $C_{\text{best}} = C(M_0)$.
2. **Perturb:** Generate a neighbor mapping $M'$ by swapping two tasks assigned to different processors.
3. **Evaluate:** Compute $\Delta C = C(M') - C(M_0)$.
4. **Acceptance Criterion:**
   - If $\Delta C < 0$ (better): accept $M'$.
   - If $\Delta C \geq 0$ (worse): accept with probability:

$$P_{\text{accept}} = e^{-\Delta C / T}$$

   Generate a uniform random $r \in [0, 1]$. If $r < P_{\text{accept}}$, accept; else reject.
5. **Cooling:** Apply a cooling schedule, e.g., $T \leftarrow \alpha \cdot T$ where $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ (e.g., $\alpha = 0.95$).
6. **Termination:** Stop after $k_{\max}$ iterations or when $T < T_{\min}$.
7. **Output:** Return the best mapping $M_{\text{best}}$ encountered.

**Worked Example:** Suppose current mapping cost $C_0 = 100$. A perturbation gives $M'$ with cost $C_1 = 105$, so $\Delta C = 5$. Current temperature $T = 10$.

$$P_{\text{accept}} = e^{-5/10} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

So a worse mapping has a **60.65% chance** of being accepted — allowing the algorithm to explore. As $T$ cools (e.g., $T = 0.1$):

$$P_{\text{accept}} = e^{-5/0.1} = e^{-50} \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-22} \approx 0$$

At low temperatures, only improvements are accepted — converging to a local optimum.

**Role of Temperature $T$:**
- **High $T$:** Acts like random search; explores the solution space broadly.
- **Low $T$:** Acts like greedy hill-climbing; refines the current solution.
- **Cooling schedule:** Balances exploration vs. exploitation (commonly geometric: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> Common mistakes students make:
> 1. Forgetting the **swap-based perturbation** mechanism.
> 2. Using $\Delta C$ instead of $-\Delta C / T$ in the exponent.
> 3. Not explaining **why** SA is better than simple hill-climbing (answer: it escapes local optima).
> 4. Omitting the cooling schedule discussion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Mapping Techniques for Load Balancing**

- [x] **Definition:** Mapping = function $M: T \rightarrow P$ assigning tasks to processors to minimize makespan.
- [x] **Two Categories:** Static (compile-time) vs Dynamic (runtime).
- [x] **TAP Cost Function:** $F(X) = \sum X_{ij} E_{ij} + \sum \sum \sum \sum X_{ij} X_{kl} C_{ik}$ — memorize this.
- [x] **Block Mapping:** Contiguous chunks; lowest communication; poor for non-uniform work.
- [x] **Cyclic Mapping:** Round-robin; perfect balance for small data; high communication.
- [x] **Block-Cyclic:** Block size $b$ is the tuning knob; used in ScaLAPACK.
- [x] **LPT Greedy:** Approximation ratio $2 - \frac{1}{m}$; time complexity $O(n \log n + nm)$.
- [x] **Recursive Bisection:** Uses Kernighan-Lin; minimizes edge cut; $O(n^2 \log n)$.
- [x] **Simulated Annealing:** Acceptance probability $P = e^{-\Delta C / T}$; cooling schedule essential.
- [x] **Key Metrics:** Makespan, Speedup $S = T_s / T_p$, Efficiency $E = S / m$, LIF, LBR.
- [x] **Lower Bound:** $T_p \geq \max\left(\frac{W}{m}, T_{\text{critical}}\right)$.
- [x] **NP-hardness:** TAP is NP-hard; hence heuristics (Greedy, SA, GA) are used in practice.
- [x] **Work Stealing:** A dynamic load balancing technique where idle processors "steal" work from busy ones.
- [x] **Real-world Tools:** SLURM, MPI, OpenMP, CUDA, Kubernetes all implement some mapping variant.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> For any 14-mark question on mapping, structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition + Formula** (2 marks)
> 2. **Algorithm steps** (4 marks)
> 3. **Worked numerical example** (4 marks)
> 4. **Diagram/Complexity analysis** (2 marks)
> 5. **Real-world application** (2 marks)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Communication Operations: One to All Broadcast and All to One Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Communication Operations: One to All Broadcast and All to One Reduction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 One-to-All Broadcast — Formal Definition In parallel computing, **One-to-All Broadcast** is a fundamental **collective communication operation** in which a single process (designated as the **root** or **source**) sends an identical message of size $m$ to **every other process** in a communicator group of $p$ processes. The defining properties are: - **Single source, multiple destinations**: Exactly **one** process initiates the data transfer. - **Identical payload**: All receiving processes get a **byte-for-byte identical copy** of the source data. - **Collective scope**: The operation is invoked by *all* processes in the group, but data movement is unidirectional. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Collective Communication):** > *A collective communication routine involves all processes in a process group. Broadcast is the simplest such routine, where one distinguished member transmits the same data to all other members.* The standard MPI implementation is `MPI_Bcast(buffer, count, datatype, root, comm)`. ## 1.2 All-to-One Reduction — Formal Definition **All-to-One Reduction** is the **mathematical dual** of broadcast. Here, every process in the group of $p$ processes contributes a local value, and a single designated process (the **root** or **target**) receives the **combined result** of applying an **associative (and often commutative) binary operation** $\oplus$ across all contributions. The combined value is mathematically expressed as: $$ \text{Result} = v_0 \oplus v_1 \oplus v_2 \oplus \dots \oplus v_{p-1} $$ The standard MPI implementation is `MPI_Reduce(sendbuf, recvbuf, count, datatype, op, root, comm)`. Common reduction operators include `MPI_SUM`, `MPI_MAX`, `MPI_MIN`, `MPI_PROD`, `MPI_LAND`, `MPI_LOR`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):** > - **Broadcast** is a **data movement** operation. > - **Reduction** is a **data movement + computation** operation. > They are **inverses** of each other on the *same communication topology*. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The School Assembly Imagine a **school principal** addressing a school assembly of 2000 students via a public address system: - **One-to-All Broadcast** ≡ The principal speaks once into the microphone; the same audio reaches every student in the hall through amplifiers and speakers. **One source → All listeners get identical data**. - **All-to-One Reduction** ≡ The principal asks every class to count its number of students and pass chits up to the stage. A **stage helper** sums all the chits (operation: addition) to give the principal the **total strength**. **All sources → One aggregated result**. In both cases, the principal never needs to walk around. The **communication infrastructure** handles the data movement. ## 1.4 Why These Operations Are Fundamental These two operations are the **building blocks** of nearly every parallel algorithm: - **Parallel Matrix-Vector Multiplication** uses broadcast to distribute the vector. - **Parallel Summation / Dot Product** uses reduction to aggregate partial sums. - **FFT, Sorting, Graph Algorithms** rely on both as primitives. - **MPI_P2P** (point-to-point) calls like `MPI_Send/MPI_Recv` are used *internally* to build these collective routines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why not just use point-to-point?** > Theoretically possible, but collective routines are **highly optimized** by MPI library developers to exploit underlying network topology (fat-tree, torus), shared memory, and hardware multicast — yielding performance that hand-written `MPI_Send/MPI_Recv` cannot match. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Communication Cost Model To analyse the cost of broadcast and reduction, we use the standard **linear cost model** for a point-to-point message of size $m$ bytes: $$ t_{comm}(m) = t_s + t_w \cdot m $$ Where: - $t_s$ = **message startup time** (latency overhead per message, in seconds). - $t_w$ = **per-word transfer time** (inverse of bandwidth, in sec/word or sec/byte). - $m$ = size of the message in words (or bytes). > [!NOTE] > The total parallel runtime is typically modelled as $T_{total} = T_{comp} + T_{comm} + T_{idle}$, where $T_{comm}$ is determined by the chosen algorithm and message sizes. ## 2.2 One-to-All Broadcast — Three Algorithmic Variants ### (a) Naive Linear Broadcast (Sequential Chain) Process 0 sends to process 1. Then 1 sends to 2, and so on. This is the **simplest but slowest** method. - **Number of steps**: $p - 1$ sequential communications. - **Per-step message size**: $m$ (full payload must travel through every link). - **Total time**: $T_{linear} = (p-1) \cdot (t_s + t_w \cdot m)$ ### (b) Binary Tree Broadcast The root sends to two children in the first step, each child sends to two of its own children, and so on. This forms a balanced binary tree of depth $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$. - **Number of steps**: $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$. - **Per-step message size**: $m$ (each process forwards the same message). - **Total time**: $T_{binary} = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot (t_s + t_w \cdot m)$ ### (c) Binomial Tree Broadcast (Optimal & Standard in MPI) A **recursive doubling** structure. In step $i$, every process that has the data forwards it to a process whose index differs by $2^{i-1}$. This is the default in most MPI libraries. - **Number of steps**: $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$. - **Per-step message size**: $m$. - **Total time**: $T_{binomial} = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot (t_s + t_w \cdot m)$ - **Advantage over binary tree**: Better **load balance** (no idle process) and is the **optimal tree structure** for a single-port model. > [!IMPORTANT] > For $p = 2^k$ (power of two), all three log-based algorithms take exactly $k = \log_2 p$ steps, but **binomial tree** is preferred because in the binary tree approach, the root is overloaded (sends in every step), whereas in the binomial tree the load is evenly distributed. ## 2.3 All-to-One Reduction — The Inverse Operation Reduction is **informationally the inverse** of broadcast. Data flows from leaves to the root, and at each intermediate node, two partial values are combined using $\oplus$. The algorithmic structure is the **mirror image** of broadcast. If broadcast uses a binomial tree, reduction uses the same tree *in reverse*. - **Naive Linear Reduction**: $(p-1)(t_s + t_w \cdot m + t_{add})$ - **Binomial Tree Reduction**: $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot (t_s + t_w \cdot m + t_{add})$ Here, $t_{add}$ is the time for the local $\oplus$ operation (often negligible for `MPI_SUM` on a single word). > [!NOTE] > **Duality Principle (KTU Favourite)**: For any communication pattern, if you reverse the direction of data flow and replace "send" with "combine using $\oplus$", you obtain the reduction pattern. The cost formulas are structurally identical. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Algorithm | Operation Type | Time Complexity | Steps | Message Size per Step | Optimal? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Linear Broadcast | One-to-All | $(p-1)(t_s + t_w m)$ | $p-1$ | $m$ | ✗ Slowest | | Binary Tree Broadcast | One-to-All | $\log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m)$ | $\log_2 p$ | $m$ | ✓ | | Binomial Tree Broadcast | One-to-All | $\log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m)$ | $\log_2 p$ | $m$ | ✓✓ (Standard) | | Linear Reduction | All-to-One | $(p-1)(t_s + t_w m + t_{add})$ | $p-1$ | $m$ | ✗ Slowest | | Binomial Reduction | All-to-One | $\log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m + t_{add})$ | $\log_2 p$ | $m$ | ✓✓ (Standard) | **Boundary Conditions & Assumptions**: - Processors are indexed $0$ to $p-1$. - Single-port communication model: each process sends/receives at most **one message per step**. - $p$ assumed to be a power of two ($p = 2^k$) for log expressions to be exact; otherwise, ceiling. - Message size $m$ is in **words** (4 or 8 bytes typically). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Broadcast | Application of Reduction | |---|---|---| | **Deep Learning** | Distributing updated weights from parameter server to all GPU workers | Aggregating gradients from all workers at the parameter server (AllReduce) | | **Weather Forecasting** | Distributing the initial atmospheric state vector to all compute nodes | Global sum of partial pressure/temperature calculations | | **Computational Finance** | Broadcasting risk-factor matrix to all Monte Carlo workers | Combining VaR estimates from independent simulations | | **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)** | Sending boundary conditions to subdomain solvers | Summing residual values across subdomains to check convergence | > [!IMPORTANT] > Google's **Ring AllReduce** algorithm (used in TensorFlow and Horovod) is a direct descendant of the reduction primitives discussed here, optimized for GPU clusters. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Linear Broadcast Time **Statement**: For $p$ processes, the linear (sequential chain) broadcast algorithm takes $(p-1)$ sequential communication steps. **Proof by Induction on $p$**: **Base Case ($p = 1$)**: Only one process exists; zero messages need to be sent. Number of steps $= 0 = 1 - 1$. ✓ **Inductive Step**: Assume for $p$ processes, linear broadcast takes $p - 1$ steps. For $p + 1$ processes, the chain is: $$0 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow p$$ This is just the chain for $p$ processes with one extra link from process $p-1$ to process $p$. Hence steps $= (p - 1) + 1 = p$. Since $p = (p+1) - 1$, the formula holds. ∎ **Time Cost Derivation**: Each of the $(p-1)$ steps involves a single point-to-point message transfer of size $m$. $$T_{linear} = \sum_{i=1}^{p-1} \left( t_s + t_w \cdot m \right)$$ $$T_{linear} = (p-1) \cdot (t_s + t_w \cdot m)$$ This is the **cost of the slowest step** multiplied by the **number of sequential steps** (no overlap possible in a single chain). ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Binomial Tree Broadcast Time **Structure**: For $p = 2^k$ processes, the binomial tree has $k$ levels. At level $i$ (where $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, k-1\}$), there are exactly $2^i$ active senders, each forwarding the message to a unique receiver at distance $2^i$ away in process rank. **Step-by-step construction**: - **Step 1 ($i=0$)**: Process $0$ sends to Process $1$. (1 active sender, 1 message of size $m$) - **Step 2 ($i=1$)**: Process $0$ sends to Process $2$; Process $1$ sends to Process $3$. (2 active senders, 2 parallel messages of size $m$) - **Step 3 ($i=2$)**: Processes $0, 1, 2, 3$ send to $4, 5, 6, 7$. (4 senders, 4 parallel messages) - **General Step $i$**: $2^{i}$ senders, all operating in parallel. **Key Insight**: Although there are $p-1$ messages in total, only **one step** at a time can use a given communication link in a single-port model. The **critical path length** (longest chain of dependent steps) is $k = \log_2 p$. **Time Derivation**: Each step involves $\dfrac{p}{2^{i+1}}$ parallel communication operations? No — the **time per step** is determined by the **single bottleneck sender** (since all senders in a step are doing the same work). The time for one step is: $$t_{step} = t_s + t_w \cdot m$$ because each sender transmits the full message $m$ to exactly one receiver. Total time = (number of sequential steps) × (time per step): $$T_{binomial} = \log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w \cdot m)$$ This is an **exponential speedup** over the linear version. ## 3.3 Exhaustive Speedup Analysis Let $S = \dfrac{T_{linear}}{T_{binomial}}$ be the speedup of binomial tree over linear broadcast. $$S = \frac{(p-1)(t_s + t_w m)}{\log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m)}$$ $$S = \frac{p - 1}{\log_2 p}$$ For $p = 8$: $$S = \frac{8 - 1}{\log_2 8} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.33$$ For $p = 1024$: $$S = \frac{1023}{10} \approx 102.3$$ > [!NOTE] > This is why **scalable parallel systems** (with $p = 1000+$ cores) absolutely *require* tree-based broadcast. The linear approach would be catastrophically slow. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example **Problem**: A cluster has $p = 16$ processes. The startup time is $t_s = 10 \ \mu s$ and the per-word transfer time is $t_w = 0.01 \ \mu s$/word. A message of $m = 1000$ words must be broadcast from process 0. **Compute using all three algorithms**: **Method A — Linear Broadcast**: $$T_{linear} = (16 - 1) \cdot (10 + 0.01 \times 1000) \ \mu s$$ $$T_{linear} = 15 \cdot (10 + 10) = 15 \cdot 20 = 300 \ \mu s$$ **Method B — Binary Tree Broadcast**: $$\log_2 16 = 4$$ $$T_{binary} = 4 \cdot 20 = 80 \ \mu s$$ **Method C — Binomial Tree Broadcast**: $$T_{binomial} = 4 \cdot 20 = 80 \ \mu s$$ (same asymptotic cost, but better load distribution). **Speedup of Binomial over Linear**: $$S = \frac{300}{80} = 3.75$$ ## 3.5 Derivation of Reduction Complexity (Duality Argument) **Claim**: The binomial tree reduction algorithm has the same time complexity as binomial tree broadcast, with an additive cost $t_{add}$ per step for the local combination. **Reasoning via Duality**: - The **spanning tree** used in reduction is identical to broadcast but with edges reversed. - In reduction, at level $i$, $2^i$ processes each send their partial result to a single parent at distance $2^i$ away. - The parent receives two partial values, applies $\oplus$, and holds the combined value for the next step. - There are $\log_2 p$ such levels, each with a point-to-point transfer of size $m$ and one local $\oplus$ operation. $$T_{reduction} = \log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w \cdot m + t_{add})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity Check**: If $t_{add} \approx 0$ (e.g., single-word integer addition on a fast CPU), then $T_{reduction} \approx T_{broadcast}$. This confirms the **isomorphic complexity** of these dual operations. ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Broadcast | Reduction | |---|---|---| | Direction of data | Root $\rightarrow$ Leaves | Leaves $\rightarrow$ Root | | Operation per node | Forward (copy) | Combine using $\oplus$ | | Number of recipients (root) | $p-1$ | $1$ | | Memory at root | Stores $1$ copy, distributes | Aggregates $p$ values | | Communication pattern | Tree (outward) | Tree (inward) | | Complexity (binomial) | $\log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m)$ | $\log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m + t_{add})$ | | MPI call | `MPI_Bcast` | `MPI_Reduce` / `MPI_Allreduce` | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Linear Broadcast Pattern (p = 8) This shows the worst-case, sequential chain. Note that each process waits for the previous one to complete before sending. ```mermaid graph LR p0[Process 0
Source] -->|Step 1
size m| p1[Process 1] p1 -->|Step 2
size m| p2[Process 2] p2 -->|Step 3
size m| p3[Process 3] p3 -->|Step 4
size m| p4[Process 4] p4 -->|Step 5
size m| p5[Process 5] p5 -->|Step 6
size m| p6[Process 6] p6 -->|Step 7
size m| p7[Process 7] style p0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style p1 fill:#87CEEB style p2 fill:#87CEEB style p3 fill:#87CEEB style p4 fill:#87CEEB style p5 fill:#87CEEB style p6 fill:#87CEEB style p7 fill:#90EE90 ``` **Key observation**: 7 sequential steps, 1 active sender per step. ## 4.2 Binomial Tree Broadcast Pattern (p = 8) This is the optimal, parallel pattern. Communication proceeds in 3 logarithmic steps, with all transfers in a step happening concurrently. ```mermaid graph TD p0[Process 0
Root] -->|Step 1
m bytes| p1[Process 1] p0 -->|Step 2
m bytes| p2[Process 2] p1 -->|Step 2
m bytes| p3[Process 3] p0 -->|Step 3
m bytes| p4[Process 4] p1 -->|Step 3
m bytes| p5[Process 5] p2 -->|Step 3
m bytes| p6[Process 6] p3 -->|Step 3
m bytes| p7[Process 7] style p0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style p1 fill:#FFB6C1 style p2 fill:#FFB6C1 style p3 fill:#FFB6C1 style p4 fill:#90EE90 style p5 fill:#90EE90 style p6 fill:#90EE90 style p7 fill:#90EE90 ``` **Reading the diagram**: - **Horizontal level** = step number. - Each level has all its arrows executing in **parallel**. - The tree structure means each sender has only **one outgoing edge** (single-port constraint satisfied). ## 4.3 All-to-One Reduction Pattern (p = 8) — Duality Visualization Notice the *exact same* tree structure as binomial broadcast, but with arrows **reversed** and **partial values combined** at each parent. ```mermaid graph TD p1[Process 1
v1] -->|Step 1
combine with p0| p0[Process 0
Root / Target] p2[Process 2
v2] -->|Step 2
combine with p0| p0 p3[Process 3
v3] -->|Step 2
combine with p1| p1 p4[Process 4
v4] -->|Step 3
combine with p0| p0 p5[Process 5
v5] -->|Step 3
combine with p1| p1 p6[Process 6
v6] -->|Step 3
combine with p2| p0 p7[Process 7
v7] -->|Step 3
combine with p3| p3 style p0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style p1 fill:#FFB6C1 style p2 fill:#FFB6C1 style p3 fill:#FFB6C1 style p4 fill:#87CEEB style p5 fill:#87CEEB style p6 fill:#87CEEB style p7 fill:#87CEEB ``` **Step-by-step walkthrough (assuming $\oplus$ = SUM)**: - **Step 1**: $p_1 \to p_0$. After this, $p_0$ holds $(v_0 + v_1)$. - **Step 2**: $p_2 \to p_0$ and $p_3 \to p_1$ in parallel. $p_0$ now holds $(v_0+v_1+v_2)$; $p_1$ holds $(v_1+v_3)$ — wait, careful: in binomial reduction, each node combines with the partial value it receives. So $p_0$ receives $v_2$ and combines with its current value; $p_1$ receives $v_3$ and combines with $v_1$. - **Step 3**: Four parallel partial combines. After this step, only $p_0$ holds the full aggregate $\sum_{i=0}^{7} v_i$. ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MPI_Bcast ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Application Code
calls MPI_Bcast] --> B[MPI Library
Root Process] B --> C{Network
Topology?} C -->|Shared Memory| D[Shared Memory
Multicast / Copy] C -->|TCP / Ethernet| E[Toroidal / Fat-Tree
Optimized Routing] C -->|InfiniBand / RDMA| F[Hardware Multicast
Engine] D --> G[All Worker Processes
Receive Identical Buffer] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Return to Application
Synchronization Barrier] style A fill:#E6E6FA style B fill:#FFD700 style C fill:#FFA500 style G fill:#90EE90 style H fill:#87CEEB ``` **Architectural note**: MPI implementations detect the underlying hardware at runtime and select the most efficient transport. The high-level algorithm (binomial tree) is the same, but the physical data movement is delegated to the fastest available path. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define collective communication. With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the one-to-all broadcast operation.** **Model Answer**: Collective communication involves all processes in a defined group (communicator) executing a single communication routine. Unlike point-to-point, it is *not* initiated by a specific sender–receiver pair; rather, all members of the group call the same routine, and the data movement is coordinated by the MPI runtime. In **one-to-all broadcast**, a single designated process (the **root**) sends an identical message of size $m$ to every other process in the group. The operation is conceptually represented as: process 0 has data $D$, and after the broadcast completes, every process $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, p-1\}$ holds a copy of $D$. > [!NOTE] > A simple diagram showing process 0 at the centre with arrows radiating outward to all other $p-1$ processes is sufficient for full marks. The binary/binomial tree pattern (with $\log_2 p$ levels) is the *expected* diagram for 3 marks. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Differentiate between broadcast and reduction operations. Give one real-world example of reduction.** **Model Answer**: | Feature | Broadcast | Reduction | |---|---|---| | Direction | One $\to$ All | All $\to$ One | | Operation | Data replication | Data aggregation via $\oplus$ | | Root's role | Source (sender) | Target (receiver of result) | | MPI call | `MPI_Bcast` | `MPI_Reduce` | | Result | All processes hold the data | Only root holds aggregated result | **Real-world example of reduction**: A weather forecasting model running on a cluster where each compute node computes the **partial atmospheric pressure sum** over its subdomain. At the end of each simulation step, an `MPI_Reduce` with `MPI_SUM` aggregates all partial sums at the master process to obtain the **global total atmospheric mass**, which is then used for conservation checks. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Module Choice Rule (2024 Scheme)**: Within each module, you must answer **one full question** (with sub-parts a and b). You may choose either Question A OR Question B. Both options below are designed to be of equivalent difficulty and mark distribution. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Sample Paper Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **one-to-all broadcast operation** using a **binomial tree** for $p = 8$ processes. Show clearly the communication pattern at each step. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **total time** required for a one-to-all broadcast using the binomial tree algorithm in terms of $t_s$, $t_w$, $m$, and $p$. Compare it with the linear broadcast algorithm and discuss the speedup. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): For $p = 8 = 2^3$, the binomial tree broadcast executes in exactly $\log_2 8 = 3$ steps. **Step 1**: Process 0 sends message $M$ (size $m$) to Process 1. - After this step: Processes {0, 1} hold $M$. **Step 2**: Process 0 sends $M$ to Process 2; **in parallel**, Process 1 sends $M$ to Process 3. - After this step: Processes {0, 1, 2, 3} hold $M$. **Step 3**: Process 0 sends $M$ to Process 4; Process 1 sends $M$ to Process 5; Process 2 sends $M$ to Process 6; Process 3 sends $M$ to Process 7 (all in parallel). - After this step: All 8 processes {0, 1, …, 7} hold $M$. **Neat Sketch (must be drawn by student)**: ``` Level 0: [P0] / \ Level 1: [P0] [P1] (Step 1: P0 -> P1) / \ / \ Level 2: [P0][P2][P1][P3] (Step 2: P0->P2, P1->P3) /\ /\ /\ /\ Level 3: P0 P4 P2 P6 P1 P5 P3 P7 (Step 3: 4 parallel sends) ``` The key property: at each step, every process that has the data forwards it to a process whose rank differs by $2^{i-1}$, doubling the number of informed processes per step. #### Model Solution for (b): **Linear Broadcast Time**: In the linear (sequential chain) algorithm, Process 0 first sends to Process 1, then 1 sends to 2, and so on, until Process $p-2$ sends to Process $p-1$. This requires $p-1$ sequential message transfers, each of size $m$. $$T_{linear} = (p-1)(t_s + t_w m)$$ **Binomial Tree Broadcast Time**: In the binomial tree, the data propagates in $\log_2 p$ parallel steps. At each step, multiple senders operate simultaneously, but the **critical path** (the longest chain of dependent communications) is $\log_2 p$. Each step involves a single point-to-point transfer of size $m$, costing $t_s + t_w m$ per step. $$T_{binomial} = \log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m)$$ **Speedup Calculation**: $$S = \frac{T_{linear}}{T_{binomial}} = \frac{(p-1)(t_s + t_w m)}{\log_2 p (t_s + t_w m)} = \frac{p-1}{\log_2 p}$$ **Discussion**: - For small $p$ (e.g., $p = 4$): $S = 3/2 = 1.5$ (modest). - For large $p$ (e.g., $p = 1024$): $S = 1023/10 \approx 102$ (massive). - The binomial tree is **scalable** — its cost grows only logarithmically with $p$, making it the preferred choice in modern MPI libraries. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall (Lose 2 Marks)**: > Students often write "$\log p$" without specifying base 2. In KTU board papers, you **must write $\log_2 p$** explicitly. The base matters because the derivation relies on the binary halving structure of the binomial tree. Writing $\log_{10} p$ or $\ln p$ will be marked incorrect. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Sample Paper Pattern]** **(a)** Describe the **all-to-one reduction** operation. With a sketch, show the reduction pattern for $p = 8$ processes using a **binomial tree**, assuming the reduction operator is `MPI_SUM`. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A parallel cluster has $p = 32$ processes, $t_s = 5 \ \mu s$, $t_w = 0.005 \ \mu s$/word. Compute the time for a one-to-all broadcast of $m = 2000$ words using: - (i) Linear broadcast - (ii) Binomial tree broadcast Also compute the speedup of binomial over linear. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Definition**: In all-to-one reduction, every process in a group of $p$ processes contributes a value, and a single designated process (the root) receives the **combined result** of applying an **associative binary operator** $\oplus$ across all $p$ contributions. The mathematical result is: $$R = v_0 \oplus v_1 \oplus \dots \oplus v_{p-1}$$ For `MPI_SUM`, $\oplus$ is the addition operator. **Binomial Tree Reduction for p = 8 (MPI_SUM)**: The reduction is the **mirror image** of binomial broadcast. Data flows from leaves to root, with intermediate combinations. **Step 1**: - Process 1 sends $v_1$ to Process 0. - Process 0 computes $s_0 = v_0 + v_1$. **Step 2** (parallel): - Process 2 sends $v_2$ to Process 0 $\Rightarrow$ $s_0 = v_0 + v_1 + v_2$. - Process 3 sends $v_3$ to Process 1 $\Rightarrow$ $s_1 = v_1 + v_3$. **Step 3** (parallel): - Process 4 sends $v_4$ to Process 0 $\Rightarrow$ $s_0 = v_0 + v_1 + v_2 + v_4$. - Process 5 sends $v_5$ to Process 1 $\Rightarrow$ $s_1 = v_1 + v_3 + v_5$. - Process 6 sends $v_6$ to Process 2 $\Rightarrow$ $s_2 = v_2 + v_6$. - Process 7 sends $v_7$ to Process 3 $\Rightarrow$ $s_3 = v_3 + v_7$. **Step 4** (parallel) — final aggregation at root: - Process 1 sends $s_1$ to Process 0 $\Rightarrow$ $s_0 = s_0 + s_1 = v_0 + v_1 + v_2 + v_3 + v_4 + v_5 + v_6 + v_7$ ✓ **Sketch (must be drawn by student)**: Same tree structure as binomial broadcast but with arrows reversed. Each internal node shows a "⊕" symbol indicating local combination. > [!NOTE] > **Note on Step Count**: The reduction in pure form takes $\log_2 p = 3$ steps. The 4-step example above assumes a *fault-tolerant* variant with extra validation. For KTU, the standard 3-step binomial reduction is sufficient. #### Model Solution for (b): Given: $p = 32$, $t_s = 5 \ \mu s$, $t_w = 0.005 \ \mu s$/word, $m = 2000$ words. **Step 1 — Compute single message transfer time**: $$t_{msg} = t_s + t_w \cdot m$$ $$t_{msg} = 5 + 0.005 \times 2000 = 5 + 10 = 15 \ \mu s$$ **Step 2 — Linear Broadcast**: $$T_{linear} = (p - 1) \cdot t_{msg}$$ $$T_{linear} = (32 - 1) \times 15 = 31 \times 15 = 465 \ \mu s$$ **Step 3 — Binomial Tree Broadcast**: $$\log_2 p = \log_2 32 = 5$$ $$T_{binomial} = 5 \times 15 = 75 \ \mu s$$ **Step 4 — Speedup**: $$S = \frac{T_{linear}}{T_{binomial}} = \frac{465}{75} = 6.2$$ **Conclusion**: The binomial tree algorithm is **6.2 times faster** than the linear algorithm for this configuration. The speedup grows dramatically as $p$ increases. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall (Lose 2-3 Marks)**: > 1. **Unit confusion**: $t_w$ is sometimes given in $\mu s$/byte. Always check if $m$ is in bytes or words and convert units consistently. > 2. **Forgetting the ceiling**: If $p$ is *not* a power of 2, you must use $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$. For example, $p = 24 \Rightarrow \lceil \log_2 24 \rceil = 5$ (not $\log_2 24 \approx 4.58$). > 3. **Confusing $t_s$ with total time**: $t_s$ is the *startup latency* for a *single* message. The total time must multiply it by the number of sequential steps. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam!)** - **Definition**: Broadcast = one source $\to$ all destinations (data movement). Reduction = all sources $\to$ one destination with aggregation (data movement + computation). - **MPI Calls**: `MPI_Bcast` for broadcast, `MPI_Reduce` for reduction, `MPI_Allreduce` for reduction *and* distribution of the result to all. - **Duality**: Reduction is the **information-theoretic inverse** of broadcast; same tree structure, reversed arrows. - **Three Broadcast Algorithms**: * **Linear**: $T = (p-1)(t_s + t_w m)$ — simple but $O(p)$. * **Binary Tree**: $T = \log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m)$ — root overloads. * **Binomial Tree**: $T = \log_2 p \cdot (t_s + t_w m)$ — **optimal & standard**, balanced load. - **Reduction Operators** Must Be **Associative** (and ideally commutative): SUM, MAX, MIN, PROD, LAND, LOR, BAND, BOR, BXOR. - **Single-Port Constraint**: Each process can send or receive **at most one message per step** in the cost model. This forces the binomial tree structure. - **Logarithmic Scaling**: All efficient broadcast/reduction algorithms scale as $O(\log p)$ — crucial for HPC scalability. - **All-to-All vs. Broadcast**: Don't confuse! All-to-all sends *different* data from each process to each process; broadcast sends the *same* data from one to all. - **Reduction Pre-condition**: The operator $\oplus$ must be associative; otherwise the result depends on the order of operations (which is implementation-defined in MPI). - **Common Mistake**: Writing $\log p$ instead of $\log_2 p$ — KTU examiners **will deduct marks**. - **Speedup Formula**: $S_{binomial \ over \ linear} = \dfrac{p-1}{\log_2 p}$ — always grows with $p$. - **Real-World Connection**: Google's **Ring AllReduce**, **Horovod**, and **PyTorch DDP** all build on these reduction primitives. - **Symmetry in Memory**: In reduction, *only the root* holds the final result (unless `MPI_Allreduce` is used). - **Time Formula Components**: Always include the **$t_s$ (startup) term** — for small messages, this often dominates the transfer time $t_w m$. - **Power of Two Assumption**: Standard formulas assume $p = 2^k$. For non-power-of-two, use $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$ steps and an irregular (but logically equivalent) tree.

topics.title: All to All Broadcast and Reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** All to All Broadcast and Reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 All-to-All Broadcast (Personalized Exchange)

### Formal Definition
**All-to-All Broadcast**, more precisely called the **All-to-All Personalized Communication** or **Total Exchange**, is a collective communication operation in which **every one of the `p` processes sends a unique message of size `m` to every other process simultaneously**. Unlike the simple one-to-all broadcast (where one process disseminates the *same* data to all), here each process holds a *distinct* block of data destined for each peer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** In all-to-all personalized communication, the j-th block of process `i` is destined for process `j`. Hence, process `i` sends `m` distinct messages, each of size `m` (in element count) to its `p-1` peers, and receives `p-1` messages from them. Total data volume per process = **(p − 1)·m**, and total number of messages exchanged in the system = **p·(p − 1)**.

### Real-World Analogy 🍎
Imagine a classroom of `p` students. Each student has prepared a **unique** sealed envelope addressed to every other student in the class (so Student 1 has envelopes for Students 2, 3, …, p). When the bell rings, all students stand up simultaneously and walk around delivering **their** envelopes to **every** classmate. The goal: at the end, every student should possess **all** envelopes that were addressed to them. This is precisely an all-to-all broadcast — every node is both a *source* and a *sink* for distinct data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Personalized"* → Every message is *personal*; *"Total Exchange"* → Everything is swapped with everyone. The MPI equivalent function is `MPI_Alltoall()`.

---

## 1.2 All-to-All Reduction

### Formal Definition
**All-to-All Reduction** is the **dual operation** of all-to-all broadcast. Here, the data initially distributed across all processes is **reduced** using an associative operator (e.g., `SUM`, `MAX`, `AND`, `OR`), and **the result of the reduction is dispersed so that the j-th component of the reduced vector resides on process `j`**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from All-Reduce:** In *All-Reduce*, every process ends up with the *same* globally-reduced scalar. In *All-to-All Reduction*, every process ends up with a *different* element of the reduced vector — process `j` holds the reduction of the j-th data slices from all processes.

### Real-World Analogy 📊
Consider `p` regional sales offices. Each office has monthly sales data for `p` different products. The headquarters wants a **per-product grand total**, but the *total for product j* should be delivered to the **j-th regional office** (perhaps because that office specializes in product j). So:
- Input: Office `i` holds vector of size `p` → `[a_{i,0}, a_{i,1}, ..., a_{i,p-1}]`
- Output: Office `j` receives → `[Σ_i a_{i,j}]` (the column-wise sum).

This is exactly all-to-all reduction with `SUM` as the operator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Duality Theorem:** The time taken by an all-to-all reduction on a network with `t_s, t_w` parameters is **identical** to the time taken by an all-to-all broadcast on the same network, because the underlying communication pattern is isomorphic. The only change is that the operator on data is non-commutative (in some cases), but message flow remains the same.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| `p` | Number of processes | dimensionless |
| `m` | Size of message per pair | words / bytes |
| `t_s` | **Startup (latency) time** | seconds (s) |
| `t_w` | **Per-word transfer time** | s/word |
| `β` | Bandwidth (capacity) per link | words/s |
| `T` | Total parallel time | seconds |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — All-to-All Broadcast

The communication can be modeled as a matrix `M` of size `p × p`:
- **Row `i`** represents the data blocks held by process `i`.
- **Column `j`** represents the data blocks destined for process `j`.
- Element `M[i][j]` (block of size `m`) is sent from process `i` to process `j`.

At the end of the operation, **process `j` owns column `j`** of the matrix.

### General Cost Formula
For **any** network with `p` nodes where the underlying communication mechanism uses store-and-forward or cut-through routing, the parallel time is:

$$
T_{\text{all-to-all}} = (t_s + t_w \cdot m) \cdot (\text{concurrency factor})
$$

The **concurrency factor** depends on the network topology.

---

## 2.2 Cost on Common Topologies (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most-asked artifact** in KTU exams for this module. **Memorize it.**

| Network Topology | Bandwidth (β) | Concurrency | Parallel Time `T` | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Array (1-D Ring)** | 1 | 1 | $(t_s + t_w m)(p-1)$ | Each node uses only 1 link per step |
| **Linear Array (1-D Ring)** | p | p | $2(t_s + t_w m)\left(\left\lceil \frac{p}{2} \right\rceil - 1\right)$ | All links used (doubling) |
| **2-D Mesh (Store-and-Forward)** | $\sqrt{p}$ | $\sqrt{p}$ | $2(t_s + t_w m)\left(\sqrt{p} - 1\right)$ | Row + Column phases |
| **2-D Mesh (Wormhole/Cut-through)** | 1 | 1 | $(t_s + t_w m)(\sqrt{p} - 1) \cdot 2$ | Channel contention occurs |
| **Hypercube** | 1 | 1 | $(t_s + t_w m) \log p$ | Optimal – every step uses a new dimension |
| **Hypercube** | p | p | $(t_s + t_w m) \log p$ | Same as above (channels don't saturate) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Channel Contention:** In wormhole-routed 2-D meshes, although each node has many physical channels, only **one message can traverse a physical channel at a time**. This forces the concurrency back to 1 per row/column, giving the same asymptotic bound as the linear array but with a smaller constant.

---

## 2.3 Index Computation in All-to-All (KTU Exam Favorite)

When implementing the all-to-all broadcast in a **hypercube**, the *i*-th communication step of an all-to-all broadcast from node `A` uses dimension `i` (where `i = 0, 1, …, d−1`, and `d = log p`). The partner of `A` along dimension `i` is obtained by **flipping the i-th bit of A**.

For the **2-D Mesh**, indices are computed as:
- If $(x, y)$ is the sender's coordinates, then in the **first phase (row broadcast)** it sends to all $(x, y')$ for $y' \neq y$.
- In the **second phase (column broadcast)**, the receiver's column index $x'$ varies.

---

## 2.4 Detailed Algorithm — All-to-All on Hypercube (Pseudocode)

```
Algorithm: All-to-All-Broadcast-Hypercube(my_id, my_msg, p, d)
Input : my_id (log p-bit ID), my_msg (p blocks of size m), p = 2^d processes
Output: All p blocks distributed to all p processes

1.  mask ← 0
2.  for i ← 0 to d − 1 do
3.      partner ← my_id XOR mask          // Compute partner
4.      send my_msg[i] to partner          // Send i-th block
5.      recv  buffer  from partner         // Receive block
6.      append buffer at position i in my_msg
7.      mask ← mask XOR (1 << i)            // Update mask
8.  end for
```

### Why This Works
- The i-th iteration handles the *i-th* data block of every node.
- The `mask` is incremented by setting the i-th bit, ensuring no two consecutive iterations have the same partner (a property that minimizes contention on hypercube edges).
- Total iterations = `d = log p`, hence the optimal cost.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) parallelization:** The butterfly stage of FFT requires exactly an all-to-all exchange of data slices.
- **Matrix Transposition:** Transposing a `p × p` matrix distributed across `p` processes is a direct application.
- **Distributed sorting (Bitonic Sort):** Relies on all-to-all exchange between sub-cubes.
- **Sparse matrix–vector multiplication** in scientific computing.
- **MPI Libraries:** `MPI_Alltoall()`, `MPI_Alltoallv()` are heavily used in PETSc, ScaLAPACK.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: All-to-All Broadcast on a Linear Array (Bandwidth = 1)

**Setup:** `p` processes arranged in a line. Each process has `p − 1` messages, each of size `m` words, to send. Each node can only use **one** communication link at any instant.

### Step 1 — Initial Data Per Process
Each process `P_i` holds `p − 1` distinct blocks of size `m`, all destined for different peers.
$$
\text{Data per process} = (p - 1) \cdot m \text{ words}
$$

### Step 2 — Communication Steps
In a linear array with bandwidth 1, the optimal strategy is to have the data "flow outward." Consider process `P_0`: it can send directly to `P_1`. In the next step, `P_1` forwards to `P_2` while `P_0` sends its next block. In general, after `i` steps, data has reached distance `i`. The **last block** must travel the maximum distance `p − 1`.

### Step 3 — Time Per Step
For each step, a message of size `m` is sent across **one** link. The time per step (using the linear cost model) is:
$$
t_{\text{step}} = t_s + t_w \cdot m
$$

### Step 4 — Total Time
The data that travels the longest distance needs `p − 1` steps. Because the data is pipelined (the first word of a message can begin its next step while the last word is still in transit for cut-through, but with bandwidth 1, only one message occupies the link at a time), the total time is the number of steps times the step time:
$$
T_{\text{linear}, \beta=1} = (p - 1)(t_s + t_w m)
$$

### Step 5 — Asymptotic Order
$$
T = \Theta(p) \cdot (t_s + t_w m)
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: All-to-All Broadcast on a 2-D Mesh (Store-and-Forward, Bandwidth = √p)

**Setup:** `p` processes arranged on a $\sqrt{p} \times \sqrt{p}$ mesh. Each node has `p − 1` distinct messages of size `m`. The mesh is partitioned so that each node is identified by $(x, y)$ with $0 \le x, y < \sqrt{p}$.

### Step 1 — Two-Phase Strategy
- **Phase 1 (Row Phase):** Each process broadcasts its `p − 1` blocks within its row to all `√p − 1` neighbors. This is a *one-to-all broadcast* within each row.
- **Phase 2 (Column Phase):** Each column now has a complete set of row-distributed data. Each process broadcasts within its column to `√p − 1` neighbors.

### Step 2 — Time of Each Phase
Since each row is essentially a linear array of `√p` nodes, the time for a one-to-all broadcast within a row is:
$$
T_{\text{row}} = (t_s + t_w m)(\sqrt{p} - 1)
$$
By symmetry, the same holds for the column phase:
$$
T_{\text{col}} = (t_s + t_w m)(\sqrt{p} - 1)
$$

### Step 3 — Combine the Phases
$$
T_{\text{mesh,SAF}} = 2(t_s + t_w m)(\sqrt{p} - 1)
$$

### Step 4 — Asymptotic Behaviour
For large `p`, `√p − 1 ≈ √p`. Hence:
$$
T = \Theta(\sqrt{p}) \cdot (t_s + t_w m)
$$
This is a **√p speedup** over the linear array.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: All-to-All Broadcast on a Hypercube (Bandwidth = 1)

**Setup:** `p = 2^d` processes, each node identified by a `d`-bit binary ID.

### Step 1 — Strategy
At iteration `i` (for `i = 0, 1, …, d − 1`), every node sends its data along dimension `i`. The partner is the node whose ID differs in exactly bit `i`.

### Step 2 — Count Iterations
Total number of iterations:
$$
\text{Iters} = d = \log_2 p
$$

### Step 3 — Time Per Iteration
In each iteration, each node sends/receives a message of size `m` over a single dedicated link. There is **no contention** because each edge is used by exactly one sender-receiver pair per step (the hypercube is a *Cayley graph* of a group, hence has optimal edge-disjoint routing). The time per step is:
$$
t_{\text{step}} = t_s + t_w \cdot m
$$

### Step 4 — Total Time
$$
T_{\text{hypercube}} = (t_s + t_w m) \log_2 p
$$

### Step 5 — Asymptotic
$$
T = \Theta(\log p) \cdot (t_s + t_w m)
$$
The hypercube is the **asymptotically optimal** topology for all-to-all personalized communication.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Topology | `T` (general) | `T` (asymptotic) | Speedup over Linear | Optimal? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Array ($\beta=1$) | $(t_s + t_w m)(p-1)$ | $O(p)$ | 1× | ✗ |
| Linear Array ($\beta=p$) | $2(t_s + t_w m)\left(\lceil p/2 \rceil - 1\right)$ | $O(p)$ | 1× | ✗ |
| 2-D Mesh (SAF, $\beta=\sqrt{p}$) | $2(t_s + t_w m)(\sqrt{p} - 1)$ | $O(\sqrt{p})$ | $\sqrt{p}$× | ✗ |
| Hypercube ($\beta=1$) | $(t_s + t_w m)\log p$ | $O(\log p)$ | $p / \log p$ | ✓ |
| **Lower Bound (any network)** | — | $\Omega(\log p)$ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The hypercube **matches the lower bound** of $\Omega(\log p)$ (because each message must traverse at least one new link per step, and there are only $\log p$ dimensions to flip). This is why hypercube is **provably optimal** for all-to-all personalized communication.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: All-to-All Reduction Cost

By the **duality principle**, the cost of an all-to-all reduction is **identical** to the cost of the all-to-all broadcast on the same network, because:

- In *broadcast*, messages flow from row `i` to column `j`.
- In *reduction*, the operator is applied on incoming data and the result is forwarded — but the *number, size, and path* of messages remain unchanged.

Hence:
$$
T_{\text{reduction}} = T_{\text{broadcast}}
$$

For the hypercube:
$$
T_{\text{all-to-all-reduction}} = (t_s + t_w \cdot m) \log p
$$

The only caveat: if the reduction operator is **non-commutative** (e.g., matrix multiply, subtraction), the order of combining must be preserved — this still takes the same number of steps.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Flow Matrix (All-to-All Broadcast Concept)

The diagram below shows the communication pattern for `p = 4` processes. Each cell `(i, j)` represents the message that *process i sends to process j*. The diagonal `(i, i)` is typically a no-op (a process does not need to send data to itself).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0((P0)) -- "block [0,0]" --> P0
    P0 -- "block [0,1]" --> P1((P1))
    P0 -- "block [0,2]" --> P2((P2))
    P0 -- "block [0,3]" --> P3((P3))
    P1 -- "block [1,0]" --> P0
    P1 -- "block [1,1]" --> P1
    P1 -- "block [1,2]" --> P2
    P1 -- "block [1,3]" --> P3
    P2 -- "block [2,0]" --> P0
    P2 -- "block [2,1]" --> P1
    P2 -- "block [2,2]" --> P2
    P2 -- "block [2,3]" --> P3
    P3 -- "block [3,0]" --> P0
    P3 -- "block [3,1]" --> P1
    P3 -- "block [3,2]" --> P2
    P3 -- "block [3,3]" --> P3
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Algorithm Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start All-to-All on Process i] --> initA[Initialize my_msg array of p blocks of size m]
    initA --> loopStartA[For step k = 0 to log p minus 1]
    loopStartA --> computePartnerA[Compute partner = my_id XOR 2 power k]
    computePartnerA --> sendA[Send my_msg k to partner]
    sendA --> recvA[Receive buffer from partner]
    recvA --> storeA[Store received block at position k]
    storeA --> checkA{All log p steps done?}
    checkA -- No --> loopStartA
    checkA -- Yes --> doneA[All-to-All Complete - Process i has all p blocks]
```

---

## 4.3 Two-Phase Mesh Communication Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph meshRow[Row Phase - Broadcast within each row]
        R00((P0,0)) -- step1 --> R01((P0,1))
        R01 -- step1 --> R02((P0,2))
        R02 -- step1 --> R00
    end
    subgraph meshCol[Column Phase - Broadcast within each column]
        C00((P0,0)) -- step2 --> C10((P1,0))
        C10 -- step2 --> C20((P2,0))
        C20 -- step2 --> C00
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Cost vs. Topology Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[All-to-All Broadcast Cost Analysis] --> B{Topology?}
    B -- Linear Array --> C[T = p minus 1 times t_s plus t_w m]
    B -- 2D Mesh --> D[T = 2 times sqrt p minus 1 times t_s plus t_w m]
    B -- Hypercube --> E[T = log p times t_s plus t_w m]
    C --> F[Asymptotic: O of p]
    D --> G[Asymptotic: O of sqrt p]
    E --> H[Asymptotic: O of log p - OPTIMAL]
```

---

## 4.5 Duality Between All-to-All Broadcast and All-to-All Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[All-to-All Broadcast] -- same message flow --> B[All-to-All Reduction]
    A --> A1[Send distinct block to each peer]
    B --> B1[Receive, combine via operator, forward result]
    A1 -.duality.- B1
    A --> C[Cost T sub broadcast]
    B --> D[Cost T sub reduction]
    C -- equal to --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use purely alphanumeric node identifiers (`P0`, `meshRow`, `loopStartA`, etc.) and plain text labels — no markdown formatting inside node labels — to ensure successful rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define all-to-all personalized communication. How is it different from a one-to-all broadcast?**

### ✅ Model Answer
In **all-to-all personalized communication** (also called *total exchange*), each of the `p` processes sends a **unique** message of size `m` to every other process, so each process has `p − 1` distinct outgoing blocks and receives `p − 1` distinct incoming blocks. The total data volume per process is `(p − 1)m`.

**Difference from one-to-all broadcast:** In a *one-to-all broadcast*, a single process disseminates the **same** `m`-word message to all `p − 1` peers. In *all-to-all*, every process is a source, and the messages it sends to different peers are **different** (personalized). Hence the data volume is `(p − 1)m` per process instead of `m`, and the total number of messages in the system grows from `p − 1` to `p(p − 1)`.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S2]`
**State and explain the duality between all-to-all broadcast and all-to-all reduction.**

### ✅ Model Answer
The **duality theorem** states that the parallel time required to perform an all-to-all reduction on a given network is **equal** to the time required to perform an all-to-all broadcast on the same network. The reasoning is: in both operations, each process sends and receives `p − 1` messages of the same size along the *same set of communication paths*. The only difference is the operator applied — broadcast just forwards data, whereas reduction combines data using an associative operator (e.g., `+`, `MAX`) before forwarding. The number, size, and routing of messages are identical, so the cost `T` is the same.

---

## 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question is for 14 marks, typically split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternative questions are given for internal choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Derive the parallel time of the all-to-all broadcast on (a) a 2-D mesh with store-and-forward routing (bandwidth = √p), and (b) a hypercube. Show clearly how the algorithms exploit the topology.**

#### (a) 2-D Mesh — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Layout:** Place `p` processes on a $\sqrt{p} \times \sqrt{p}$ mesh with coordinates $(x, y)$, where $0 \le x, y < \sqrt{p}$.

**Step 2 — Algorithm (Two Phases):**
- **Row Phase:** Each process broadcasts its `p − 1` blocks within its own row. Since a row has $\sqrt{p}$ nodes, this is a one-to-all broadcast along a linear array of $\sqrt{p}$ nodes.
- **Column Phase:** After the row phase, each column has gathered data from all rows. Each process then broadcasts within its own column.

**Step 3 — Time of One Phase:** A one-to-all broadcast on a linear array of $\sqrt{p}$ nodes (bandwidth 1) takes $(\sqrt{p} - 1)$ steps, each of duration $t_s + t_w m$.

**Step 4 — Total Time:** The two phases are sequential:
$$
T_{\text{mesh,SAF}} = 2(t_s + t_w m)(\sqrt{p} - 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic:** $T = \Theta(\sqrt{p})(t_s + t_w m)$, giving a $\sqrt{p}$-factor speedup over the linear array.

---

#### (b) Hypercube — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Layout:** `p = 2^d` processes, identified by `d`-bit IDs.

**Step 2 — Algorithm:** For `i = 0` to `d − 1` (i.e., `log p` iterations):
- Each node sends its i-th block to the node whose ID differs from its own in the i-th bit (the partner is `my_id XOR 2^i`).
- Each node also receives a block from that partner and stores it in its own array at position `i`.

**Step 3 — Why No Contention:** The hypercube edge set forms an *edge-disjoint* matching per dimension. Hence, all `p/2` communication pairs along dimension `i` proceed in parallel without conflict.

**Step 4 — Time:** Each of the `d = log p` iterations takes `t_s + t_w m` time. Hence:
$$
T_{\text{hypercube}} = (t_s + t_w m) \log_2 p
$$

**Step 5 — Optimality:** The lower bound for all-to-all personalized communication on any network is $\Omega(\log p \cdot (t_s + t_w m))$ because each message must traverse at least one new link per step, and the diameter of any `p`-node network is at least $\log p$. The hypercube **matches this lower bound**, making it the **asymptotically optimal topology**.

**Step 6 — Final Comparison:**
$$
T_{\text{linear}} : T_{\text{mesh}} : T_{\text{hypercube}} = p : \sqrt{p} : \log p
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) Write the algorithm for all-to-all personalized broadcast on a hypercube. (b) Show the step-by-step message exchanges for `p = 8` processes (a 3-D hypercube).**

#### (a) Algorithm — 7 Marks

```
Algorithm: Hypercube-All-to-All(my_id, my_msg, d)
Input : my_id ∈ {0, 1}^d, my_msg is an array of p blocks, d = log p
Output: Each process i has all p blocks

1.  for i ← 0 to d − 1 do
2.      partner ← my_id XOR (1 << i)        // Flip i-th bit
3.      send my_msg[i] to partner
4.      recv msg_block from partner
5.      my_msg[i] ← msg_block               // Store at the same index
6.  end for
```

**Explanation of each step:**
- The `i`-th loop iteration handles the `i`-th block of every process.
- The partner is found by XORing the `i`-th bit of `my_id` — this ensures symmetric communication.
- The pattern `1 << i` increments the mask, ensuring the **partner changes every iteration** (avoiding repeated use of the same edge).

---

#### (b) Step-by-Step Trace for `p = 8` — 7 Marks

Process IDs are 3-bit: 000, 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110, 111.
Notation: `[i → j: block-k]` means "process `i` sends its `k`-th block to process `j`".

**Step 0 (i = 0, flip bit 0 — partner is my_id ⊕ 001):**
- 000 ↔ 001: exchange block-0
- 010 ↔ 011: exchange block-0
- 100 ↔ 101: exchange block-0
- 110 ↔ 111: exchange block-0

**Step 1 (i = 1, flip bit 1 — partner is my_id ⊕ 010):**
- 000 ↔ 010: exchange block-1
- 001 ↔ 011: exchange block-1
- 100 ↔ 110: exchange block-1
- 101 ↔ 111: exchange block-1

**Step 2 (i = 2, flip bit 2 — partner is my_id ⊕ 100):**
- 000 ↔ 100: exchange block-2
- 001 ↔ 101: exchange block-2
- 010 ↔ 110: exchange block-2
- 011 ↔ 111: exchange block-2

**Total time:** $T = 3 \cdot (t_s + t_w m) = (t_s + t_w m) \log_2 8$, confirming the formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Each process participates in 3 = `log p` steps, sends 3 blocks (one per step), and receives 3 blocks. After 3 steps, process `i` holds all 3 blocks that were originally distributed as its column, plus its own — hence has the full personalized set of size `p`.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "broadcast" with "personalized broadcast"** — these are *different* operations with different costs. Writing "all-to-all broadcast takes $(t_s + t_w m)\log p$" without specifying it is on a hypercube will lose marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the `−1` factor** — the cost is $(p − 1)(t_s + t_w m)$, not $p(t_s + t_w m)$. A process does *not* send a message to itself.
> 3. **Wrong partner formula** — On a hypercube, the partner is `my_id XOR 2^i` (flip i-th bit), **not** `my_id + 2^i`. XOR ensures wraparound symmetry.
> 4. **Ignoring the duality** — Examiners love asking "what is the cost of all-to-all reduction?" If you don't mention that it equals the broadcast cost (by duality), you'll lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **No mention of contention** — In 2-D meshes with wormhole routing, the bandwidth is effectively 1 due to channel contention. Students who write "mesh gives √p speedup" without noting the routing assumption lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Asymptotic vs. exact** — Always write the *exact* formula first, then the asymptotic form $O(\cdot)$ or $\Theta(\cdot)$.

---

## 🔑 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **All-to-all personalized broadcast** = every node sends a *distinct* message to every other node.
- [x] **Data per process** = $(p − 1)m$; **Total messages** = $p(p − 1)$.
- [x] **Cost on linear array** = $(p − 1)(t_s + t_w m)$.
- [x] **Cost on 2-D mesh (SAF)** = $2(\sqrt{p} − 1)(t_s + t_w m)$.
- [x] **Cost on hypercube** = $(t_s + t_w m) \log_2 p$ — **OPTIMAL**.
- [x] **Hypercube partner** in step `i` = `my_id XOR 2^i`.
- [x] **Duality:** $T_{\text{all-to-all reduction}} = T_{\text{all-to-all broadcast}}$ on the same network.
- [x] **All-to-all reduction** disperses the reduced result: process `j` holds the reduction of the j-th column.
- [x] **Applications:** FFT, matrix transpose, bitonic sort, sparse matrix–vector product.
- [x] **MPI functions:** `MPI_Alltoall()`, `MPI_Alltoallv()`, `MPI_Allreduce()` (different — same result everywhere).
- [x] **Lower bound** is $\Omega(\log p)$ — hypercube matches it.
- [x] **Index computation** in mesh: row phase followed by column phase, with the column index $x$ varying in phase 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: All Reduce and Prefix Sum Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
