<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# All Reduce and Prefix Sum Operations — Module 3 Master Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is a cornerstone of **Collective Communication & Data-Parallel Operations** under Module 3 of PECCT411. It directly maps to **CO3 (Apply collective communication primitives to solve parallel problems)** and is a frequent 14-mark question in End-Semester Evaluations (ESE).

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Reduce Operation?

A **Reduce** is a collective communication operation in which data distributed across multiple processes is combined using an associative operator (such as sum, max, min, XOR, or a user-defined function), and the final reduced value is delivered to **exactly one designated process** (typically the *root*).

**Formal Definition (HPC Terminology):**
If $P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{p-1}$ are $p$ processes, each holding a value $x_i$, then a **Reduce** with operator $\bigoplus$ returns:

$$\text{Reduce}(x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{p-1}, \bigoplus) = \bigoplus_{i=0}^{p-1} x_i \quad \text{delivered to } P_{\text{root}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim syllabus tone):** *Reduce* is a collective operation in which a binary associative operator is applied across the values supplied by each process in a group, and the result is placed at a specified *root* process.

### 1.2 What is an All Reduce Operation?

**All Reduce** is the natural extension of Reduce in which the final combined result is **delivered to ALL processes**, not just one root. Every process in the group finishes with the identical reduced value.

**Formal Definition:**
$$\text{AllReduce}(x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{p-1}, \bigoplus) = \left[\bigoplus_{i=0}^{p-1} x_i\right]_{j=0}^{p-1} \text{ on } P_j$$

That is, every process $P_j$ for $j = 0, 1, \ldots, p-1$ receives the same final value.

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Class Test Tally":** Imagine 60 students in 6 rows. Each row has a *row monitor* who tallies marks of students in their row. The *class monitor* (root) collects one total from each row monitor → this is **Reduce** (result at one place). Now, if **every** row monitor also gets a copy of the grand total so they can all announce it → this is **All Reduce**. The work is the same; only the **distribution of the result** differs.

### 1.3 What is a Prefix Sum (Scan) Operation?

The **Prefix Sum** (also called **Scan**) is a fundamental data-parallel operation in which, given a sequence of $n$ elements $[a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_{n-1}]$ and an associative binary operator $\bigoplus$, the output is the sequence of all partial reductions:

$$\text{Scan}([a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_{n-1}], \bigoplus) = [y_0, y_1, \ldots, y_{n-1}]$$

where $y_i = a_0 \oplus a_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus a_i$.

#### Two Variants of Scan

| Variant | Definition | Output Equation |
|---------|------------|-----------------|
| **Inclusive Scan** | $y_i$ includes $a_i$ | $y_i = \bigoplus_{k=0}^{i} a_k$ |
| **Exclusive Scan** | $y_i$ excludes $a_i$ (uses identity element) | $y_i = \bigoplus_{k=0}^{i-1} a_k$, with $y_0 = e$ (identity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When $\bigoplus = +$ and the sequence is $[3, 1, 7, 0, 4, 1, 6, 3]$:
> - **Inclusive Scan:** $[3, 4, 11, 11, 15, 16, 22, 25]$
> - **Exclusive Scan:** $[0, 3, 4, 11, 11, 15, 16, 22]$
> The total sum (25) is **not** part of the inclusive scan output, but is the natural by-product of All Reduce.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy — The Assembly Line

> [!TIP]
> Think of a car assembly line where each worker adds a specific part. At any point in the line, the **prefix sum** tells you: "How much total work has been done up to this station?" At the end of the line, the **All Reduce** tells every worker: "Here is the total number of cars produced today." Both use the same underlying accumulation, but answer different *who-needs-the-answer* questions.

### 1.5 Why These Operations Are Central in HPC

- **MPI/MPI-DAOS:** `MPI_Allreduce` is one of the most-called collective primitives in scientific codes (e.g., CG solver, molecular dynamics).
- **GPU Computing:** NVIDIA's NCCL and CUDA `thrust::inclusive_scan` are heavily optimized scan kernels forming the backbone of radix sort, compaction, and BFS.
- **Big Data:** Spark's `reduceByKey` is conceptually an All Reduce; `aggregateByKey` extends it.
- **Numerical Methods:** Parallel prefix is the engine of work-efficient sparse matrix-vector multiplication, polynomial evaluation, and segment scans.

### 1.6 KTU-Standard Notation Conventions

The following notation is used throughout the rest of these notes and is the **same notation that will appear in your question paper**:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $p$ | Number of processes / processors |
| $n$ | Number of data elements |
| $n/p$ | Elements per process (assumed balanced) |
| $T_s$ | **Latency / startup time** per message (**in seconds**) |
| $T_w$ | **Bandwidth reciprocal** (time per byte/word transmitted) |
| $\alpha$ | Startup cost (often $T_s = \alpha$ in HPC literature) |
| $\beta$ | Per-word transfer cost (often $T_w = \beta$) |
| $\gamma$ | Per-element compute cost |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Primitive Operations

In their purest form, every All Reduce can be decomposed into two logical phases, and every Scan is built from a pair of sweeps. Understanding these primitives unlocks every higher-level algorithm.

### 2.1.1 Reduce + Broadcast ≡ All Reduce (Conceptual View)

**Phase 1 — Reduce:** Aggregate all data to one root process.
**Phase 2 — Broadcast:** The root redistributes the result to everyone.

This conceptual model yields an All Reduce but is **inefficient** because the root becomes a bottleneck. All practical implementations (Ring, Tree, Recursive Halving) avoid the bottleneck.

### 2.1.2 The Two Realizations of All Reduce

| Realization | Topology | Best Use Case |
|-------------|----------|---------------|
| **Ring All Reduce** | Each process sends to next, $p-1$ steps | Large messages, bandwidth-bound |
| **Tree / Butterfly All Reduce** | Hypercube / binomial tree | Small messages, latency-bound |
| **Recursive Halving & Doubling** | Power-of-2 $p$, log-depth | Balanced mid-size messages |

### 2.1.3 Scan Decomposition — Up-Sweep + Down-Sweep

The **Blelloch Scan** (work-efficient, O(n) work) is universally used. It operates on a balanced binary tree with $2^k = n$ leaves:

**Step 1 — Up-Sweep (Reduce Phase):** The tree accumulates partial sums upward from leaves to root. After this phase, the **root holds the total sum**.

**Step 2 — Down-Sweep (Distribute Phase):** The root passes the identity (or the total) downward, propagating prefix values to every node.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two sweeps?** The total sum is at the root after Up-Sweep. To compute **all** prefix sums (not just the total), we need a Down-Sweep that walks the tree the other way, distributing prefix values as it goes. This is the *exact same trick* as All Reduce — gather, then distribute.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize the four boxed rows. They are worth **at least 7 marks** in any 14-mark KTU question on this topic.

| Operation | Communication Model | Total Time $T$ (per process) | Cost Metric |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------------------|-------------|
| **Naive Reduce (tree, $p$ procs)** | $T_s + T_w \cdot m \cdot \log p$ | $T_s + T_w \cdot m \cdot \log p$ | Latency optimal |
| **Naive All Reduce (Reduce+Bcast)** | $2(T_s + T_w \cdot m \cdot \log p)$ | $2T_s + 2T_w \cdot m \cdot \log p$ | Sub-optimal (2×) |
| **Ring All Reduce** (each proc holds $n/p$ data) | $2 \cdot (p-1) \cdot (T_s + T_w \cdot n/p)$ | $2 T_s (p-1) + 2 T_w n (p-1)/p$ | **Bandwidth optimal** |
| **Recursive Halving All Reduce** | $T_s \cdot \log p + T_w \cdot n \cdot \frac{\log p}{p}$ | $T_s \log p + T_w n \log p / p$ | **Latency optimal** |
| **Sequential Scan** (single proc) | $T_{\text{comp}} = n \cdot \gamma$ | $n \gamma$ | Work: $O(n)$ |
| **Parallel Scan (Blelloch, $p$ procs, $n$ elements)** | $O\!\left(\frac{n}{p} + \log p\right)$ work-per-proc | $O(n/p) + O(\log p)$ | **Work-efficient: $O(n)$** |
| **Parallel Scan (Hillis-Steele, naive)** | $O(n \log n / p)$ work-per-proc | $O(n \log n / p)$ | **Not work-efficient** |
| **Speedup Blelloch (ideal)** | $S = p \cdot \frac{n/p}{n/p + \log p}$ | $\to p$ as $n \gg p \log p$ | Scales linearly for large $n$ |
| **Isoefficiency (Blelloch)** | $\Theta(p \log p)$ | Work grows $\Theta(p \log p)$ to keep efficiency constant | Very scalable |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX in Tables:** In the table above, the absolute-value-like expressions (e.g. \vert x \vert) are intentionally written as \frac or \log to preserve the markdown table pipe `|` boundary. **Never** write `$|x|$` inside a table cell — it breaks the table.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of All Reduce / Prefix Sum |
|-------|----------------------------------------|
| **Deep Learning (Distributed Training)** | Gradient synchronization across GPUs in data-parallel SGD (Ring All Reduce in NCCL, Horovod) |
| **Computational Fluid Dynamics** | Global residual norm computation in iterative solvers (CG, GMRES) |
| **Bioinformatics** | Smith-Waterman dynamic programming scoring on GPU clusters |
| **Graphics & Rendering** | Stream compaction, BVH construction, prefix-sum based particle systems |
| **Cryptography** | Merkle tree construction, distributed randomness generation |
| **Smart Grid / IoT** | Consensus-based energy aggregation across distributed sensors |
| **Database Query Engines** | Parallel aggregations in Spark, MapReduce, and RAPIDS cuDF |

## 2.4 Theoretical Properties of Scan and Reduce

Both operations are characterized by the **algebraic closure** of the operator $\bigoplus$:

- **Associativity:** $(a \oplus b) \oplus c = a \oplus (b \oplus c)$ — required for correctness across reorderings.
- **Identity element $e$:** $e \oplus a = a \oplus e = a$ — required for **exclusive** scan.
- **Commutativity** (optional for Reduce/All Reduce, **not** required for Scan).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU trick question:** *Is Scan defined for non-associative operators?* **No.** Without associativity, the result depends on the tree-shape used to evaluate partial reductions, breaking determinism. This is why `MPI_Scan` mandates an associative op.

## 2.5 The Relationship: All Reduce = Total Sum after Scan

For a vector $[a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_{n-1}]$ partitioned across $p$ processes:

$$\text{AllReduce}([a_0, \ldots, a_{n-1}], +) = \text{last\_element}(\text{InclusiveScan}([a_0, \ldots, a_{n-1}], +))$$

This identity is the bridge between the two operations and is often the basis of a 7-mark exam question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivations are exhaustive — every algebraic step is explicitly shown, as required by KTU 14-mark valuation keys.**

## 3.1 Cost Model Assumptions (Hockney / LogP)

We adopt the **Hockney channel model** for point-to-point message of size $m$ bytes:

$$T_{\text{msg}}(m) = T_s + T_w \cdot m$$

For $p$ processes, each holding $m$ bytes, the **aggregate bandwidth** (sum of data moved) is the canonical lower bound. An algorithm is **bandwidth-optimal** if it matches this bound.

**Lower bound on All Reduce cost (per process):**
$$T_{\text{AllReduce}}^{\min} \geq \frac{(p-1) \cdot m}{p} \cdot T_w$$

This corresponds to each process receiving the full result of size $m$ (data from $p-1$ other processes), distributed across $p$ communication steps.

## 3.2 Derivation 1 — Cost of Ring All Reduce (Bandwidth-Optimal)

The Ring All Reduce algorithm executes in two phases, each of $p-1$ communication steps on a logical ring of $p$ processes.

### Phase A: Reduce-Scatter

**Step 1:** Partition each process's local block of $n$ elements into $p$ chunks of size $n/p$.
**Step 2:** In step $i$ (for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, p-2$), process $P_k$ sends chunk $k$ to process $P_{(k+1) \bmod p}$ and receives chunk $_{(k+i+1) \bmod p}$ from process $P_{(k-1) \bmod p}$.
**Step 3:** Each received chunk is locally reduced with the existing accumulated value.

**Per-step communication volume** (each step transfers $n/p$ elements):

$$V_{\text{step}} = \frac{n}{p}$$

**Number of communication steps** in Reduce-Scatter:

$$N_{\text{RS}} = p - 1$$

**Time for Reduce-Scatter** (summing the per-step costs, each $T_s + T_w \cdot V_{\text{step}}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{RS}} &= \sum_{i=0}^{p-2} \left( T_s + T_w \cdot \frac{n}{p} \right) \\
&= (p-1) \cdot T_s + (p-1) \cdot T_w \cdot \frac{n}{p} \\
&= (p-1) \cdot T_s + T_w \cdot n \cdot \frac{p-1}{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Phase B: All-Gather

**Step 1:** Each process now holds $p$ reduced chunks, one of which (the *aggregate of its own block*) is the *final* chunk for the global result.
**Step 2:** In step $i$ (for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, p-2$), the *final* chunks are circulated around the ring, so every process receives the chunks of all others.

**Time for All-Gather** (same per-step size, same number of steps):

$$T_{\text{AG}} = (p-1) \cdot T_s + T_w \cdot n \cdot \frac{p-1}{p}$$

### Total Ring All Reduce Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{RingAR}} &= T_{\text{RS}} + T_{\text{AG}} \\
&= \left[(p-1) T_s + T_w n \frac{p-1}{p}\right] + \left[(p-1) T_s + T_w n \frac{p-1}{p}\right] \\
&= 2 (p-1) T_s + 2 T_w n \cdot \frac{p-1}{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Asymptotic Form):** As $p \to \infty$, $T_{\text{RingAR}} \to 2(p-1) T_s + 2 T_w n$. The dominant term is the $2 T_w n$ **bandwidth cost**, which is **independent of $p$**. This is why Ring All Reduce scales beautifully with thousands of GPUs (e.g., Facebook's 256-GPU ImageNet training in 1 hour).

### Optimality Verification

Compare $T_{\text{RingAR}}$ to the lower bound $T_{\text{AllReduce}}^{\min} = T_w n (p-1)/p \approx T_w n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{RingAR}} &= 2(p-1) T_s + 2 T_w n \frac{p-1}{p} \\
                  &= 2 T_s (p-1) + 2 \cdot T_{\text{AllReduce}}^{\min} \cdot \frac{p}{p-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The bandwidth term approaches $2 T_w n$, exactly twice the per-process lower bound. Since each process sends and receives $n(p-1)/p$ bytes, the algorithm is **bandwidth-optimal to within a constant factor of 2**, which is the theoretical floor for any bidirectional ring exchange.

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Cost of Recursive Halving All Reduce (Latency-Optimal)

For power-of-2 $p$, the **Recursive Halving** algorithm proceeds in $\log_2 p$ rounds.

### Algorithm Sketch

**Round $r$** (for $r = 0, 1, \ldots, \log_2 p - 1$): Each process pairs with a partner at *Hamming distance* $2^r$ (bit $r$ differs). The two exchange their accumulated halves and reduce locally.

### Communication Volume per Round

In round $r$, the partner exchange carries half of the remaining data. Let $V_r$ be the data volume sent in round $r$:

$$V_r = \frac{n}{2^{r+1}}$$

The factor is $1/2^{r+1}$ because (a) we are exchanging half the data, and (b) at round $r$ we have $2^r$ sub-blocks and we exchange a sub-block of size $n/2^r$, but each partner does so simultaneously, halving to $n/2^{r+1}$ per direction.

### Total Data Volume per Process

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{r=0}^{\log_2 p - 1} V_r = \sum_{r=0}^{\log_2 p - 1} \frac{n}{2^{r+1}} \\
&= \frac{n}{2} \sum_{r=0}^{\log_2 p - 1} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^r \\
&= \frac{n}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^{\log_2 p}}{1 - 1/2} \\
&= \frac{n}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - 1/p}{1/2} = n \cdot (1 - 1/p) \approx n
\end{aligned}
$$

> Each process sends total volume $\approx n$, matching the bandwidth lower bound.

### Total Startup Cost (Latency Term)

Each of the $\log_2 p$ rounds incurs one startup. With concurrent startups (since pairs work in parallel):

$$T_{\text{startup}} = T_s \cdot \log_2 p$$

### Total Recursive Halving All Reduce Time

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{RHAR}} = T_s \cdot \log_2 p + T_w \cdot n \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Compare Ring vs Recursive Halving:**
> - **Ring:** $2(p-1) T_s + 2 T_w n (1 - 1/p)$ — bad latency, good bandwidth.
> - **Recursive Halving:** $T_s \log p + T_w n (1 - 1/p)$ — good latency, same bandwidth.
> - **For $n$ small** (latency-bound): use Recursive Halving.
> - **For $n$ large** (bandwidth-bound): use Ring All Reduce (Horovod/NCCL default).

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Work & Span of Blelloch's Parallel Prefix Sum

Let $T_1$ = work (sequential time on 1 processor), $T_\infty$ = span (parallel time on $\infty$ processors). Brent's theorem: parallel time on $p$ processors is $T_p \leq T_1/p + T_\infty$.

### Up-Sweep (Reduce) Phase

The Up-Sweep operates on a binary tree of depth $d = \log_2 n$.

- **Work per level:** Each level performs $n/2$ additions (assuming $n$ elements, $n/2$ independent pair-additions).
- **Number of levels:** $\log_2 n$.
- **Total work of Up-Sweep:**

$$T_1^{\text{up}} = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} \frac{n}{2} = \frac{n}{2} \cdot \log_2 n$$

- **Span of Up-Sweep:** At each level, all $n/2$ additions are independent, so depth per level is $1$ (or one $\gamma$):

$$T_\infty^{\text{up}} = \log_2 n \cdot \gamma$$

### Down-Sweep Phase

The Down-Sweep has identical structure but propagates prefix values downward.

- **Total work of Down-Sweep:**

$$T_1^{\text{down}} = \frac{n}{2} \cdot \log_2 n$$

- **Span of Down-Sweep:**

$$T_\infty^{\text{down}} = \log_2 n \cdot \gamma$$

### Total Blelloch Scan (Both Phases)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1^{\text{scan}} &= T_1^{\text{up}} + T_1^{\text{down}} = n \log_2 n \\
T_\infty^{\text{scan}} &= 2 \log_2 n \cdot \gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

### Corrected Work-Efficient Form (Pairwise + Final Sweep)

The recurrence for the **pairwise scan** (sometimes called Hillis-Steele corrected form) yields the true work-efficient bound:

$$
T_1 = 2n \quad \text{(linear work, not } n \log n\text{)}
$$

This is achieved by the **Blelloch 1990 algorithm** using two passes — one for partial sums, one for prefix propagation. The recurrence is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W(n) &= 2 W(n/2) + O(n) \\
     &= O(n) \quad \text{(by Master Theorem, } a=2, b=2, f(n)=n \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n)\text{)} \\
D(n) &= D(n/2) + O(1) \\
     &= O(\log n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Is the Blelloch Scan work-efficient?", answer: **Yes, it is $O(n)$ work and $O(\log n)$ span, giving parallel time $O(n/p + \log n)$ on $p$ processors** — optimal by Brent's bound.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Speedup and Isoefficiency of Parallel Scan

For $p$ processors, the Brent-bound parallel time is:

$$T_p = \frac{T_1}{p} + T_\infty = \frac{n}{p} + 2 \gamma \log_2 n$$

### Speedup

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{n}{\frac{n}{p} + 2\gamma \log_2 n} \\
    &= \frac{p}{1 + \frac{2 p \gamma \log_2 n}{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

As $n \to \infty$ with $p$ fixed:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S_p = p \quad \text{(linear speedup)}$$

### Efficiency

$$
E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2 p \gamma \log_2 n}{n}}
$$

### Isoefficiency (the *n* needed to keep $E_p$ constant)

Setting $E_p = c$ (a constant), solve for $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2 p \gamma \log_2 n}{n}} \\
1 + \frac{2 p \gamma \log_2 n}{n} &= \frac{1}{c} \\
\frac{2 p \gamma \log_2 n}{n} &= \frac{1-c}{c} \\
n &= \frac{2 c p \gamma \log_2 n}{1-c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\log_2 n$ grows slowly, the isoefficiency is approximately:

$$\boxed{n = \Theta(p \log p)}$$

This is the **iso-efficiency function of the parallel prefix-sum algorithm** — to maintain fixed efficiency, the problem size must grow as $p \log p$. This is one of the **best (smallest) isoefficiencies** known in parallel computing, making parallel scan extremely scalable.

## 3.6 Worked Example — 8-Element Inclusive Scan via Blelloch

**Input:** $[3, 1, 7, 0, 4, 1, 6, 3]$, operator $+$

**Step 1 — Up-Sweep Tree (visualized):**

| Level | Pairs (left, right) → operation | Resulting Array |
|-------|----------------------------------|-----------------|
| 0 | $(3,1), (7,0), (4,1), (6,3)$ | $[4, -, 7, -, 5, -, 9, -]$ |
| 1 | $(4,7), (5,9)$ | $[11, -, -, -, 14, -, -, -]$ |
| 2 | $(11,14)$ | $[25, -, -, -, -, -, -, -]$ |

After Up-Sweep, the root holds the total sum = **25**.

**Step 2 — Down-Sweep Tree (passing prefix values down):**

| Level | Action | Resulting Array |
|-------|--------|-----------------|
| 2 → 1 | Set left child of root = 0 (identity), right child = 11 (root) | $[25, 0, 11, -]$ |
| 1 → 0 | Set left=0, right=4 for first internal; left=11, right=14 for second | $[0, 4, 11, 14]$ |
| 0 (final) | Add parent prefix to each leaf: $y_i = a_i + \text{parent}$ | $[3, 4, 11, 11, 15, 16, 22, 25]$ |

**Verification by direct computation:**
- $y_0 = 3$ ✓
- $y_1 = 3+1 = 4$ ✓
- $y_2 = 3+1+7 = 11$ ✓
- $y_3 = 3+1+7+0 = 11$ ✓
- $y_4 = 3+1+7+0+4 = 15$ ✓
- $y_5 = 3+1+7+0+4+1 = 16$ ✓
- $y_6 = 3+1+7+0+4+1+6 = 22$ ✓
- $y_7 = 3+1+7+0+4+1+6+3 = 25$ ✓

**Number of operations:** $7 + 7 = 14 = 2(n-1) = O(n)$ — work-efficient confirmed.
**Number of parallel steps (span):** $3 + 3 = 6 = 2 \log_2 n$ — $O(\log n)$ confirmed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Reduce vs Broadcast vs All Reduce Family

This flowchart clarifies the *delivery* of the result, which is the single most-tested concept in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Process P0: a0"] --> R{"Reduce Op +"}
    A1["Process P1: a1"] --> R
    A2["Process P2: a2"] --> R
    A3["Process P3: a3"] --> R
    R --> S["Result S = a0+a1+a2+a3"]
    S --> Q1{"Distribution?"}
    Q1 -- "One root only" --> ROOT["P0 holds S only"]
    Q1 -- "All processes" --> ALL["Bcast to P0,P1,P2,P3"]
    ROOT --> NOTE1["Plain Reduce"]
    ALL --> NOTE2["AllReduce = Reduce + Bcast"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Same computation, different *result placement policy*. The work (the tree reduction) is identical.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Ring All Reduce: Reduce-Scatter and All-Gather Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase1["PHASE A: Reduce-Scatter  p-1 = 3 steps"]
        direction LR
        R0["P0: a0,b0,c0,d0"] --step 0: send a0--> R1["P1: a1,b1,c1,d1"]
        R1 --step 0: send a1--> R2["P2: a2,b2,c2,d2"]
        R2 --step 0: send a2--> R3["P3: a3,b3,c3,d3"]
        R3 --step 0: send a3--> R0
    end
    subgraph Phase2["PHASE B: All-Gather  p-1 = 3 steps"]
        direction LR
        G0["P0: holds agg_a0"] --step 3: send agg_a0--> G1["P1: holds agg_a1"]
        G1 --step 3: send agg_a1--> G2["P2: holds agg_a2"]
        G2 --step 3: send agg_a2--> G3["P3: holds agg_a3"]
        G3 --step 3: send agg_a3--> G0
    end
    Phase1 --> Phase2
    Phase2 --> FINAL["Each Pi now has the FULL AllReduce result S"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Two logical rings, each of $p-1$ steps. Total = $2(p-1)$ communication steps, each transferring $n/p$ data.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Blelloch Up-Sweep + Down-Sweep on 8 Leaves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Leaves["Leaves (Level 0)"]
        L0["3"]
        L1["1"]
        L2["7"]
        L3["0"]
        L4["4"]
        L5["1"]
        L6["6"]
        L7["3"]
    end
    subgraph L1Level["Level 1 (4 internal nodes)"]
        I0["4"]
        I1["7"]
        I2["5"]
        I3["9"]
    end
    subgraph L2Level["Level 2 (2 internal nodes)"]
        J0["11"]
        J1["14"]
    end
    subgraph RootNode["Level 3 (Root)"]
        K0["25"]
    end
    L0 --> I0
    L1 --> I0
    L2 --> I1
    L3 --> I1
    L4 --> I2
    L5 --> I2
    L6 --> I3
    L7 --> I3
    I0 --> J0
    I1 --> J0
    I2 --> J1
    I3 --> J1
    J0 --> K0
    J1 --> K0
```

**Reading the diagram:** Up-Sweep reads bottom-up. The value **25** at the root is the *total sum* — the output of a Reduce. The Down-Sweep (not drawn to avoid clutter) walks top-down, distributing the prefix values $[0, 3, 4, 11, 11, 15, 16, 22]$ to every leaf's parent context.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block Architecture: From Local Data to Global Answer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LocalData["Per-Process Local Memory"]
        D0["P0: a0 ... a n/p-1"]
        D1["P1: a n/p ... a 2n/p-1"]
        D2["P2: a 2n/p ... a 3n/p-1"]
        D3["P3: a 3n/p ... a n-1"]
    end
    subgraph NetLayer["Collective Communication Layer"]
        N1["Step 1: Local Reduce within process"]
        N2["Step 2: Pairwise Exchange Hypercube"]
        N3["Step 3: Recursive Doubling"]
    end
    subgraph ResultLayer["Per-Process Result Buffer"]
        R0["P0: S_total"]
        R1["P1: S_total"]
        R2["P2: S_total"]
        R3["P3: S_total"]
    end
    D0 --> N1
    D1 --> N1
    D2 --> N1
    D3 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> R0
    N3 --> R1
    N3 --> R2
    N3 --> R3
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each processor starts with a *local* slice. The communication layer implements a $\log_2 p$-step reduction (tree shown for 4 procs = 2 rounds). Every processor ends with the *same* global scalar $S_{\text{total}}$.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Inclusive vs Exclusive Scan Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Array"]
        IN0["a0 = 3"]
        IN1["a1 = 1"]
        IN2["a2 = 7"]
        IN3["a3 = 0"]
    end
    subgraph Inc["Inclusive Scan"]
        IC0["y0 = 3"]
        IC1["y1 = 4"]
        IC2["y2 = 11"]
        IC3["y3 = 11"]
    end
    subgraph Exc["Exclusive Scan"]
        EX0["y0 = 0 identity"]
        EX1["y1 = 3"]
        EX2["y2 = 4"]
        EX3["y3 = 11"]
    end
    IN0 --> IC0
    IN1 --> IC1
    IN2 --> IC2
    IN3 --> IC3
    IN0 --> EX0
    IN1 --> EX1
    IN2 --> EX2
    IN3 --> EX3
```

**Reading the diagram:** The exclusive scan *shifts right by one* and inserts the identity element at index 0. The two scans differ by exactly one such shift.

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Time-Complexity Comparison Radar

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Naive Reduce-Bcast AllReduce: 2 T_s log p + 2 T_w m log p"] -- "Bandwidth better" --> B["Ring AllReduce: 2 T_s(p-1) + 2 T_w n (1-1/p)"]
    B -- "Latency better" --> C["Recursive Halving AR: T_s log p + T_w n (1-1/p)"]
    C -- "Small message" --> C
    A -- "Large message" --> B
    A -- "Latency critical" --> C
    A -- "Bandwidth critical" --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule for KTU answers:** Use **Recursive Halving** when message size $m$ is small (latency-bound regime: $T_s \gg T_w m$). Use **Ring** when $m$ is large (bandwidth-bound regime: $T_w m \gg T_s$). Use **Naive Tree** only for didactic purposes — it is dominated by both in their respective regimes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern, PECCT411):**
> - Part A: 3 questions × 3 marks = 9 marks (answer any 2)
> - Part B: 2 questions × 14 marks = 28 marks (answer any 1, with internal choice)
> - **Total from this topic in a typical paper: 14 marks (one full Part B question)**

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the All Reduce collective operation. With a suitable example, illustrate how it differs from a simple Reduce operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**All Reduce** is a collective communication operation in which data from all processes in a group are combined using an associative operator $\bigoplus$, and the resulting reduced value is delivered to **every** process in the group, not just one root.

**Example:** Let 4 processes hold values $[5, 3, 8, 4]$ and the operator be $+$. The total sum is $20$. A *Reduce* operation delivers $20$ to (say) $P_0$ only. An *All Reduce* operation delivers $20$ to all four processes $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3$.

**Difference:** Reduce → result at one process; All Reduce → result at all processes. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for distinction.)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a prefix-sum (scan) operation? Differentiate between inclusive and exclusive scan with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **prefix sum** (or **scan**) applies a binary associative operator $\bigoplus$ across a sequence, producing for each position $i$ the reduction of all elements up to and including position $i$.

**Inclusive scan** includes the element at position $i$ in the partial sum; **exclusive scan** excludes it and uses the identity element $e$ for position 0.

**Example** for input $[3, 1, 7, 0]$ with $+$:
- Inclusive scan: $[3, 4, 11, 11]$
- Exclusive scan: $[0, 3, 4, 11]$

The exclusive scan is the inclusive scan shifted right by one position, with the identity 0 prepended. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for inclusive example, 1 mark for exclusive example + distinction.)

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice Pattern (Mandatory in KTU 2024 ESE):** You must attempt **one** of the two questions below. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

#### (a) **Explain the Ring All Reduce algorithm in detail. Derive its total time complexity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps:** Ring All Reduce executes in two phases, each of $(p-1)$ communication steps on a logical ring of $p$ processes, where each process initially holds $n/p$ data elements.

**Phase 1 — Reduce-Scatter (Steps 0 to $p-2$):**
1. Partition each process's local data into $p$ chunks of size $n/p$ each.
2. In step $i$, process $P_k$ sends chunk $k$ to $P_{(k+1) \bmod p}$ and receives chunk $_{(k+i+1) \bmod p}$ from $P_{(k-1) \bmod p}$.
3. Locally reduce (using $\bigoplus$) the received chunk with the chunk already held in slot $_{(k+i+1) \bmod p}$.

**Phase 2 — All-Gather (Steps 0 to $p-2$):**
1. After Phase 1, each process holds $p$ reduced chunks, but only the chunk indexed by its own rank is *final*.
2. In step $i$, processes circulate these *final* chunks around the ring, so every process eventually holds all $p$ final chunks.

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

Let $T_s$ = startup latency, $T_w$ = per-word transfer time. Each communication step transfers $n/p$ words, costing $T_s + T_w \cdot n/p$.

**Phase 1 time:**
$$T_{\text{RS}} = (p-1) T_s + (p-1) T_w \frac{n}{p} = (p-1) T_s + T_w n \frac{p-1}{p}$$

**Phase 2 time** (identical):
$$T_{\text{AG}} = (p-1) T_s + T_w n \frac{p-1}{p}$$

**Total:**
$$
\boxed{T_{\text{RingAR}} = 2(p-1) T_s + 2 T_w n \cdot \frac{p-1}{p} = 2(p-1) T_s + 2 T_w n \left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right)}
$$

**Optimality:** The bandwidth term $2 T_w n (1 - 1/p) \to 2 T_w n$ as $p \to \infty$, which is **bandwidth-optimal** to within a factor of 2. The Ring algorithm is the algorithm of choice for **bandwidth-bound** All Reduce operations. (1 mark algorithm, 2 marks derivation, 1 mark optimality — total 4 marks, the remaining 3 marks for diagram + clarity.)

#### (b) **Describe the Blelloch parallel prefix-sum algorithm. Show that it is work-efficient with $O(n)$ work and $O(\log n)$ span. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Blelloch algorithm (1990) computes the prefix sum of $n = 2^k$ elements on a binary tree of depth $k = \log_2 n$ in two phases.

**Phase 1 — Up-Sweep (Reduce):**
The tree is built bottom-up. At each level, every pair of sibling nodes is summed, and the result is stored in the right sibling. After this phase, the root holds the **total sum** of all leaves.

**Phase 2 — Down-Sweep (Distribute):**
A value of 0 (the identity for $+$) is placed at the root's left child. The root's value is copied to the right child. The process recurses: at each internal node, the left child receives the prefix from the parent, and the right child receives the sum of the parent's left-child value and the local accumulated value. After this phase, every leaf holds its **inclusive prefix sum**.

**Work Analysis:**
- Up-Sweep work: $\frac{n}{2} + \frac{n}{4} + \cdots + 1 = n - 1$ additions.
- Down-Sweep work: $n - 1$ additions.
- **Total work:** $T_1 = 2(n-1) = O(n)$. (Work-efficient — linear in input size.)

**Span Analysis:**
- Up-Sweep depth: $k = \log_2 n$ sequential additions on the critical path.
- Down-Sweep depth: $k = \log_2 n$ sequential additions.
- **Total span:** $T_\infty = 2 \log_2 n = O(\log n)$. (Logarithmic — near-optimal for prefix computation.)

**Parallel Time on $p$ Processors (Brent's Theorem):**
$$T_p = \frac{T_1}{p} + T_\infty = O\!\left(\frac{n}{p}\right) + O(\log n) = O\!\left(\frac{n}{p} + \log n\right)$$

This is **optimal** because a prefix sum cannot be computed in less than $n/p$ work-time and $\log n$ communication depth. (3 marks for algorithm, 2 marks for work derivation, 2 marks for span derivation and optimality.)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### (a) **Compare the Recursive Halving and Recursive Doubling algorithms for All Reduce. Derive the time complexity of Recursive Halving and discuss when it is preferred over the Ring algorithm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Recursive Halving and Doubling Algorithm:**
For $p$ processes (assumed power of 2), the algorithm proceeds in $\log_2 p$ rounds. In each round $r$ (for $r = 0, 1, \ldots, \log_2 p - 1$), processes are paired by Hamming distance $2^r$ (i.e., differ in bit $r$ of the rank).

- **Halving phase:** Each pair exchanges their data, and the receiver *reduces* (combines via $\bigoplus$) the received data with its own.
- **Doubling phase:** Each pair exchanges their *reduced* data, and the receiver *replaces* (overwrites) with the received data (the "doubling" reflects the doubling of contributing processes).

The net effect: after $\log_2 p$ rounds, every process has received contributions from all other processes.

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

Let $V_r$ = data volume sent in round $r$ (per process):
$$V_r = \frac{n}{2^{r+1}}$$

**Total data volume per process:**
$$V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{r=0}^{\log_2 p - 1} V_r = \frac{n}{2} \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^{\log_2 p}}{1 - 1/2} = n \left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right) \approx n$$

**Total time:**
$$
\boxed{T_{\text{RHAR}} = T_s \log_2 p + T_w n \left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right)}
$$

**Comparison Table (for full marks, present in tabular form in exam):**

| Property | Ring All Reduce | Recursive Halving All Reduce |
|----------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Latency term | $2 T_s (p-1)$ | $T_s \log_2 p$ |
| Bandwidth term | $2 T_w n (1 - 1/p)$ | $T_w n (1 - 1/p)$ |
| Number of steps | $2(p-1)$ | $\log_2 p$ |
| Power-of-2 $p$ required? | No | Yes |
| Optimal regime | Large $m$ (bandwidth-bound) | Small $m$ (latency-bound) |
| Used by | NCCL, Horovod, Baidu Ring | Early MPI implementations |

**When Recursive Halving is preferred:** When message size $n$ is small such that $T_w n \ll T_s$ (i.e., latency-bound regime), the $\log_2 p$ startup cost of Recursive Halving vastly outperforms the $2(p-1)$ startup cost of Ring. Conversely, Ring is preferred when $n$ is large. (3 marks derivation, 2 marks table, 2 marks selection rule.)

#### (b) **Illustrate the Hillis-Steele inclusive scan algorithm on the input sequence $[5, 2, 4, 1, 7, 3, 6, 2]$ step by step. Why is it not work-efficient? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Hillis-Steele algorithm** performs a scan in $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ iterations. In iteration $d$ (1-indexed), each element at index $i$ adds the element that was at index $i - 2^{d-1}$ in the previous iteration (if $i - 2^{d-1} \geq 0$).

**Step-by-step execution on input $[5, 2, 4, 1, 7, 3, 6, 2]$:**

**Iteration 0 (initial — d=0):** $[5, 2, 4, 1, 7, 3, 6, 2]$
No operation, this is the input.

**Iteration 1 (d=1, shift = $2^0 = 1$):** Each $y_i \mathrel{+}= y_{i-1}$ (if $i \geq 1$):

| $i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| New $y_i$ | 5 | $2+5$ | $4+2$ | $1+4$ | $7+1$ | $3+7$ | $6+3$ | $2+6$ |
| Result | 5 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 8 | 10 | 9 | 8 |

**Iteration 2 (d=2, shift = $2^1 = 2$):** Each $y_i \mathrel{+}= y_{i-2}$ (if $i \geq 2$):

| $i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| New $y_i$ | 5 | 7 | $6+5$ | $5+7$ | $8+6$ | $10+5$ | $9+8$ | $8+10$ |
| Result | 5 | 7 | 11 | 12 | 14 | 15 | 17 | 18 |

**Iteration 3 (d=3, shift = $2^2 = 4$):** Each $y_i \mathrel{+}= y_{i-4}$ (if $i \geq 4$):

| $i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| New $y_i$ | 5 | 7 | 11 | 12 | $14+5$ | $15+7$ | $17+11$ | $18+12$ |
| Result | 5 | 7 | 11 | 12 | **19** | **22** | **28** | **30** |

**Final result (inclusive scan):** $[5, 7, 11, 12, 19, 22, 28, 30]$

**Verification by direct prefix sums:** $5, 5+2=7, 7+4=11, 11+1=12, 12+7=19, 19+3=22, 22+6=28, 28+2=30$. ✓

**Why is Hillis-Steele not work-efficient?**

- **Number of iterations:** $\log_2 n = 3$ for $n = 8$.
- **Number of additions per iteration:** $n = 8$ (each element performs one addition in parallel).
- **Total work:** $T_1 = n \log_2 n = 8 \times 3 = 24$ additions.

Compare to the sequential scan: $T_{\text{seq}} = n - 1 = 7$ additions.

Since $T_1 = n \log n \gg n$, the algorithm performs **$\log n$ times more work** than the sequential version. The extra work comes from recomputing intermediate values that the sequential algorithm only computes once.

**Span:** $T_\infty = \log_2 n = 3$ additions (all additions within an iteration are independent), which is excellent.

The Hillis-Steele algorithm is **step-optimal but not work-efficient**, with parallel time $O(\log n)$ but total work $O(n \log n)$. For a work-efficient alternative, use **Blelloch's algorithm** (covered in Q-A part b). (2 marks setup, 2 marks iteration 1-2, 2 marks iteration 3 + verification, 1 mark work-inefficiency discussion.)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–4 marks in KTU valuation keys:**

1. **Confusing Reduce with All Reduce in notation.** Examiners will deduct 1 mark for stating "All Reduce returns the result to the root process" — it returns to **all** processes. The result is the *same scalar*, but the *placement* differs.

2. **Writing $T_{\text{AllReduce}} = T_s + T_w \cdot m$ without the $\log p$ factor.** The naive All Reduce is $2(T_s + T_w m \log p)$, not $T_s + T_w m$. This is a classic 1-mark deduction.

3. **Forgetting to include BOTH phases of Ring All Reduce.** Many students derive only the Reduce-Scatter phase and forget the All-Gather, getting half the time and losing 2 marks.

4. **Stating that Blelloch Scan has span $O(n)$.** It is $O(\log n)$ (the tree depth), not $O(n)$. Mistaking span for work is a frequent 2-mark error.

5. **Confusing the operator's associativity requirement.** Reduce/All Reduce/Scan **require** associativity. Commutativity is *nice-to-have* for Reduce/All Reduce but **not required** for Scan. Many students write the wrong direction.

6. **Hillis-Steele: stating it is work-efficient.** It is **not** work-efficient ($O(n \log n)$ work). Only Blelloch is work-efficient ($O(n)$ work).

7. **In the recursive halving derivation, skipping the geometric-series step.** The sum $\sum_{r=0}^{\log_2 p - 1} 2^{-(r+1)} = 1 - 1/p$ must be explicitly shown; students often write the result without derivation, losing 1 mark.

8. **Mixing inclusive and exclusive scan notations.** Always state explicitly whether $y_i$ includes $a_i$ or not. The KTU convention: *inclusive* scan includes, *exclusive* scan excludes (uses identity at $y_0$).

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Month KTU Revision Card — Memorize the contents of this box.**

- [x] **All Reduce** = Reduce + Bcast; the only difference is the *placement* of the result (one vs all).
- [x] **Scan (Prefix Sum)** = sequence of partial reductions; **Inclusive** includes $a_i$, **Exclusive** uses identity.
- [x] **Operator requirements:** Associativity (mandatory); Commutativity (optional for Reduce/AR, not required for Scan).
- [x] **Ring All Reduce time:** $T = 2(p-1) T_s + 2 T_w n (1 - 1/p)$ — **bandwidth-optimal**.
- [x] **Recursive Halving AR time:** $T = T_s \log_2 p + T_w n (1 - 1/p)$ — **latency-optimal** for power-of-2 $p$.
- [x] **Naive Reduce-Bcast AR time:** $T = 2 T_s \log p + 2 T_w m \log p$ — sub-optimal.
- [x] **Hillis-Steele Scan:** Span $O(\log n)$, Work $O(n \log n)$ — **NOT work-efficient**.
- [x] **Blelloch Scan:** Span $O(\log n)$, Work $O(n)$ — **work-efficient**; uses Up-Sweep + Down-Sweep.
- [x] **Isoefficiency of parallel scan:** $\Theta(p \log p)$ — one of the best in parallel computing.
- [x] **Selection rule:** Small message → Recursive Halving; Large message → Ring.
- [x] **Final-element identity:** $\text{AllReduce}(\vec{x}, +) = \text{last}(\text{InclusiveScan}(\vec{x}, +))$.
- [x] **Real-world usage:** Distributed DL gradient sync (Horovod/NCCL Ring All Reduce), stream compaction in GPU rendering (Scan), iterative solver convergence checks (All Reduce for residual norm).
- [x] **MPI primitives:** `MPI_Reduce`, `MPI_Allreduce`, `MPI_Scan`, `MPI_Exscan`.
- [x] **CUDA primitives:** `thrust::inclusive_scan`, `thrust::exclusive_scan`, `ncclAllReduce`.
- [x] **Memory layout note:** Ring All Reduce needs only **$O(n/p)$** additional memory per process (in-place aggregation); Recursive Halving typically needs $O(n)$ extra buffer.
- [x] **Numerical stability:** Use **Kahan summation** or **double precision** when summing millions of floating-point values via All Reduce, to avoid catastrophic cancellation.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — ALL REDUCE & PREFIX SUM OPERATIONS**

topics.title: Scatter and Gather
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scatter and Gather

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of parallel programming using the **Message Passing Interface (MPI)** standard, **Scatter** and **Gather** are two fundamental **collective communication operations** that govern one-to-many and many-to-one data movement across a set of processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scatter (MPI_Scatter):** A collective communication operation in which a designated **root process** partitions a send buffer into **N** equal (or near-equal) disjoint segments and dispatches the **i-th** segment exclusively to the process with rank **i** in the communicator. The operation is synchronized — all processes must call it for the communication to proceed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Gather (MPI_Gather):** The dual inverse of Scatter. Every process in the communicator (including the root) contributes a segment of its local send buffer, and the **root process** concatenates these segments in **rank order** into a single contiguous receive buffer. Again, it is a synchronized collective call.

Where:
- **N** = number of processes in the MPI communicator (commonly **MPI_COMM_WORLD**)
- **rank** = the unique integer identifier of a process, ranging from **0** to **N − 1**
- **root** = the process initiating and terminating the data assembly/disassembly

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Scatter like a Head Chef distributing ingredients:**
> Imagine the Head Chef (root process, rank 0) has a giant sack of vegetables. She must send **exactly one bunch** of vegetables to each of the 4 Sous Chefs (processes of rank 0, 1, 2, 3). She does not duplicate — Sous Chef 1 gets carrots, Sous Chef 2 gets potatoes, Sous Chef 3 gets onions, Sous Chef 4 gets tomatoes. Each chef receives **only their own portion**. This is **Scatter** — a one-to-many *division* of data.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Gather like a Judge collecting score sheets:**
> Now reverse the scenario. Each of the 4 Sous Chefs has prepared a dish and written a score on their personal notepad. The Judge (root process) walks to each chef in turn — Chef 0 first, then Chef 1, then Chef 2, then Chef 3 — and **pastes all the scores onto one master clipboard in rank order**. The original notepads stay with the chefs. This is **Gather** — a many-to-one *assembly* of data.

## 1.3 Why Scatter and Gather Matter in KTU Syllabus

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework for PECCT411, these operations map directly to:

- **CO3:** *Design and implement parallel algorithms using collective communication primitives.*
- High weightage in **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** Module 3 questions.
- Foundation for advanced topics: **MPI_Allgather**, **MPI_Alltoall**, **MPI_Reduce**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Scatter and Gather form the backbone of the **Single Program Multiple Data (SPMD)** model. Mastery of these two operations is mandatory before tackling advanced collective operations and parallel sorting/prefix-sum algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of MPI_Scatter

The **MPI_Scatter** routine executes the following logical steps across all participating processes:

1. **Root Process Initialization:** The process with rank `root` holds the entire data array `sendbuf` of size **N × sendcount** in its local memory.
2. **Logical Partitioning:** The send buffer is virtually split into **N** contiguous chunks, each of length `sendcount`. Chunk *i* is logically destined for process *i*.
3. **Synchronization Barrier:** All N processes must invoke `MPI_Scatter` concurrently. MPI runtime blocks until this condition is met.
4. **Point-to-Point Routing:** MPI internally executes **N − 1** point-to-message transfers (or uses optimized tree-based algorithms) to deliver the chunks.
5. **Local Reception:** Each non-root process receives exactly `sendcount` elements into `recvbuf`. The root process also receives its own chunk.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of MPI_Gather

The **MPI_Gather** routine executes the inverse logical flow:

1. **All-Process Preparation:** Every process (including root) prepares a local `sendbuf` containing `sendcount` elements.
2. **Synchronization Barrier:** All N processes call `MPI_Gather` simultaneously.
3. **Rank-Ordered Dispatch:** Each process sends its data to the root process. MPI internally guarantees rank-ordered assembly.
4. **Concatenation at Root:** The root process populates `recvbuf` such that data from process *i* is placed at offset `i × recvcount`.
5. **Non-Root Inaction:** Non-root processes do not require a valid `recvbuf`; they pass `MPI_IN_PLACE` or a dummy buffer.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

| **Parameter** | **MPI_Scatter Role** | **MPI_Gather Role** | **Type / Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `sendbuf` | Source array at root | Local source array at every process | `void*` pointer |
| `sendcount` | Elements per chunk | Elements each process sends | `int` (count) |
| `sendtype` | Datatype of send elements | Datatype of send elements | `MPI_Datatype` |
| `recvbuf` | Local receive buffer | Gathered array at root | `void*` pointer |
| `recvcount` | Elements to receive locally | Elements received from each process | `int` (count) |
| `recvtype` | Datatype of receive | Datatype of receive | `MPI_Datatype` |
| `root` | Rank of distributing process | Rank of collecting process | `int` (rank id) |
| `comm` | Communicator handle | Communicator handle | `MPI_Comm` |
| `Total Data Volume` | $N \times \text{sendcount}$ | $N \times \text{sendcount}$ | elements |

### 2.4 Key Equations for Problem Solving

**Total Data Transferred in Scatter (bytes):**

$$
D_{\text{scatter}} = N \times \text{sendcount} \times \text{sizeof(datatype)}
$$

**Total Data Transferred in Gather (bytes):**

$$
D_{\text{gather}} = N \times \text{sendcount} \times \text{sizeof(datatype)}
$$

**Communication Time (Linear Model):**

$$
T_{\text{comm}} = T_{\text{startup}} + (D / R)
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{startup}}$ = **latency** (fixed overhead per message, typically **10–100 μs**)
- $D$ = total data transferred
- $R$ = **bandwidth** of the interconnect (e.g., InfiniBand: ~**10 Gbps**)

**Memory Required at Root (Gather):**

$$
M_{\text{root}} = N \times \text{recvcount} \times \text{sizeof(datatype)} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Memory Per Process (Scatter receive):**

$$
M_{\text{local}} = \text{sendcount} \times \text{sizeof(datatype)} \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:** Numerical problems in ESE often ask: *"Given 8 processes and a root array of 64 integers, how many elements does each process receive in MPI_Scatter?"* The answer is trivially **64 / 8 = 8 elements**, but you must show the formula $N \times \text{sendcount} = \text{Total}$ and rearrange it.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Use of Scatter** | **Use of Gather** |
|---|---|---|
| **Climate Modeling** | Distribute grid cells to worker nodes | Collect partial simulation results |
| **Deep Learning (Data Parallel)** | Distribute mini-batches across GPUs | Aggregate gradients at parameter server |
| **Database Sharding** | Send query partitions to DB nodes | Collect and merge result sets |
| **Image Processing** | Distribute image tiles to cores | Stitch processed tiles back together |
| **Monte Carlo Simulations** | Distribute random seed ranges | Aggregate statistical outcomes |

## 2.6 Scatterv and Gatherv — Variable Count Variants

> [!TIP]
> When chunks are **unequal in size** (e.g., process 0 gets 5 elements, process 1 gets 3), use:
> - `MPI_Scatterv` — uses `sendcounts[]` array and `displs[]` (displacements)
> - `MPI_Gatherv` — uses `recvcounts[]` array and `displs[]`

This is critical for **load balancing** in irregular parallel algorithms.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: MPI_Scatter with N = 4 Processes

Let us derive the exact memory state, message routing, and data flow for `MPI_Scatter` when **N = 4** processes share a communicator.

### 3.1.1 Initial Setup

Let the **root process (rank 0)** contain the array:

$$
A = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80]
$$

We invoke:

$$
\text{MPI\_Scatter}(A, 2, \text{MPI\_INT}, \text{recvbuf}, 2, \text{MPI\_INT}, 0, \text{MPI\_COMM\_WORLD})
$$

**Given:** `sendcount` = 2, `root` = 0, `N` = 4.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logical Partitioning

**Step 1: Compute the chunk size per process.**

$$
\text{chunk\_size} = \frac{\text{total\_elements}}{N} = \frac{8}{4} = 2
$$

This confirms each process receives exactly **2 integers**.

**Step 2: Define the partitioning offsets.**

For process with rank $i$, the starting offset in the root's send buffer is:

$$
\text{offset}(i) = i \times \text{chunk\_size}
$$

**Step 3: Compute each process's logical chunk.**

$$
\text{Chunk}(i) = A[\text{offset}(i) \,:\, \text{offset}(i) + \text{chunk\_size} - 1]
$$

| **Process Rank (i)** | **Offset $i \times 2$** | **Logical Chunk (2 elements)** | **Receive Buffer** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | $[10, 20]$ | $B_0 = [10, 20]$ |
| 1 | 2 | $[30, 40]$ | $B_1 = [30, 40]$ |
| 2 | 4 | $[50, 60]$ | $B_2 = [50, 60]$ |
| 3 | 6 | $[70, 80]$ | $B_3 = [70, 80]$ |

**Step 4: Verify the conservation law.**

Total elements after Scatter = Sum of all received chunks:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} \text{recvcount}(i) = \sum_{i=0}^{3} 2 = 8
$$

$$
\text{Conservation: } \text{Total}_{\text{root}} = \text{Total}_{\text{received}} = 8 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Total bytes transferred across the network.**

$$
D_{\text{scatter}} = N \times \text{sendcount} \times \text{sizeof}(\text{int}) = 4 \times 2 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 6: Estimated communication time (Hypothetical InfiniBand).**

Given $T_{\text{startup}} = 10\,\mu s$ and $R = 10\,\text{Gbps} = 1.25\,\text{GB/s}$:

$$
T_{\text{comm}} = 10 \times 10^{-6} + \frac{32}{1.25 \times 10^{9}} \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{comm}} = 10\,\mu s + 25.6\,\text{ns} \approx 10.0256\,\mu s
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough: MPI_Gather (Inverse Operation)

Starting from the state after Scatter, we now invoke:

$$
\text{MPI\_Gather}(B_i, 2, \text{MPI\_INT}, A, 2, \text{MPI\_INT}, 0, \text{MPI\_COMM\_WORLD})
$$

**Step 1: Each process contributes its local 2-element array.**

| **Rank** | **Contribution $B_i$** |
|---|---|
| 0 | $[10, 20]$ |
| 1 | $[30, 40]$ |
| 2 | $[50, 60]$ |
| 3 | $[70, 80]$ |

**Step 2: Root process concatenates in rank order.**

The root computes the placement offset for each incoming chunk:

$$
\text{placement}(i) = i \times \text{recvcount} = i \times 2
$$

**Step 3: Construct the final gathered array $A'$.**

$$
A'[i \times 2] = B_i[0], \quad A'[i \times 2 + 1] = B_i[1]
$$

$$
A' = [10, 20, \; 30, 40, \; 50, 60, \; 70, 80]
$$

**Step 4: Verify identity.**

$$
A' = A \;\checkmark \text{ (Scatter followed by Gather recovers the original)}
$$

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Property** | **MPI_Scatter** | **MPI_Gather** |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of data flow | One → Many (root to all) | Many → One (all to root) |
| Data buffer at root | Send buffer (input) | Receive buffer (output) |
| Data buffer at non-root | Receive buffer (output) | Send buffer (input) |
| Memory at non-root after call | Holds $1/N$ fraction | Holds its own original data |
| Memory at root after call | Holds its own $1/N$ fraction | Holds full $N$ fraction array |
| Symmetry | Asymmetric (root special) | Asymmetric (root special) |
| Reversibility | `MPI_Scatter` $\circ$ `MPI_Gather` = identity (if same parameters) | `MPI_Gather` $\circ$ `MPI_Scatter` $\neq$ identity (data destroyed) |
| Underlying MPI message count | $\geq N-1$ (often $\log_2 N$ in optimized impl) | $\geq N-1$ (often $\log_2 N$ in optimized impl) |
| Load balancing | Even (with Scatter) or custom (with Scatterv) | Even (with Gather) or custom (with Gatherv) |

## 3.4 Reference MPI C Program — Scatter and Gather

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <mpi.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);

    int rank, size;
    MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);
    MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);

    int sendbuf[8] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80};
    int recvbuf[2];

    /* Each process receives 2 elements */
    MPI_Scatter(sendbuf, 2, MPI_INT,
                recvbuf, 2, MPI_INT,
                0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);

    printf("Rank %d received: [%d, %d]\n", rank, recvbuf[0], recvbuf[1]);

    /* Root gathers 2 elements from each of the 4 processes */
    int gathered[8];
    MPI_Gather(recvbuf, 2, MPI_INT,
               gathered, 2, MPI_INT,
               0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);

    if (rank == 0) {
        printf("Root gathered array: ");
        for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) printf("%d ", gathered[i]);
        printf("\n");
    }

    MPI_Finalize();
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output (excerpt):**
- `Rank 0 received: [10, 20]`
- `Rank 1 received: [30, 40]`
- `Rank 2 received: [50, 60]`
- `Rank 3 received: [70, 80]`
- `Root gathered array: 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 80`

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between MPI_Scatter and MPI_Bcast. Mention one scenario where Scatter is preferred over Broadcast.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **MPI_Scatter** | **MPI_Bcast** |
|---|---|---|
| Data distribution | Distinct chunks per process | Identical copy to all processes |
| Memory per process after call | $1/N$ of total data | Full copy of the data |
| Use case | Distributing partitions of an array | Sending same configuration/instruction |

**Scenario where Scatter is preferred:** When implementing a parallel **vector–matrix multiplication**, the matrix rows are partitioned into disjoint slabs and distributed one-per-process. Since each process needs **different** data, Scatter is used; Bcast would waste memory by duplicating the entire matrix on every process. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*In MPI_Gather, what is the role of the `recvcounts` array when using MPI_Gatherv? Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer:**
The `recvcounts` array in `MPI_Gatherv` specifies **how many elements each contributing process sends** to the root. It is necessary because the standard `MPI_Gather` enforces that **all processes send the same number of elements** (`sendcount` is a scalar). When different processes contribute variable-sized data (e.g., 2 elements from rank 0, 5 from rank 1, 3 from rank 2), the root needs an explicit per-process count to correctly index the placement of each chunk in its receive buffer. The corresponding `displs[]` array further specifies the **offset** at which each chunk must be placed. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the working of `MPI_Scatter` with its complete function signature and parameters. Illustrate with a diagram showing data distribution across 4 processes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an MPI program in C where the root process (rank 0) holds an array of 16 integers `{1, 2, ..., 16}` and uses `MPI_Scatter` to distribute 4 integers to each of the 4 processes. Each non-root process should compute the sum of its 4 received elements and print it with its rank. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) MPI_Scatter Explanation (7 marks):**

**Function Signature:**

```c
int MPI_Scatter(
    const void *sendbuf,    /* IN:  source buffer at root */
    int sendcount,          /* IN:  elements sent to each process */
    MPI_Datatype sendtype,  /* IN:  datatype of send elements */
    void *recvbuf,          /* OUT: receive buffer at each process */
    int recvcount,          /* IN:  elements this process receives */
    MPI_Datatype recvtype,  /* IN:  datatype of receive elements */
    int root,               /* IN:  rank of distributing process */
    MPI_Comm comm           /* IN:  communicator */
);
```

**Working Mechanism:**

1. Only the `root` process supplies a meaningful `sendbuf`. The total size of this buffer is **N × sendcount** elements, where **N** is the number of processes in `comm`.
2. The MPI runtime virtually partitions the send buffer into **N** equal consecutive chunks.
3. Chunk $i$ is routed to process $i$ via internal point-to-point messages.
4. All processes (including root) must invoke the call concurrently; otherwise the program **deadlocks**.
5. Each non-root process receives its chunk into `recvbuf`; root receives its own chunk.

**Data Distribution Diagram (textual representation for 4 processes):**

| **Root Send Buffer** | [A1][A2][A3][A4] | [B1][B2][B3][B4] | [C1][C2][C3][C4] | [D1][D2][D3][D4] |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Destination Rank** | Rank 0 | Rank 1 | Rank 2 | Rank 3 |
| **Receive Buffer** | [A1..A4] | [B1..B4] | [C1..C4] | [D1..D4] |

**(b) MPI C Program (7 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <mpi.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);

    int rank, size;
    MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);
    MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);

    int sendbuf[16];
    int recvbuf[4];
    int local_sum = 0;

    if (rank == 0) {
        for (int i = 0; i < 16; i++)
            sendbuf[i] = i + 1;  /* {1, 2, ..., 16} */
    }

    MPI_Scatter(sendbuf, 4, MPI_INT,
                recvbuf, 4, MPI_INT,
                0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);

    for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++)
        local_sum += recvbuf[i];

    printf("Process rank %d received [", rank);
    for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) printf(" %d", recvbuf[i]);
    printf(" ] --> Local sum = %d\n", rank, local_sum);

    MPI_Finalize();
    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**
- `Process rank 0 received [ 1 2 3 4 ] --> Local sum = 10`
- `Process rank 1 received [ 5 6 7 8 ] --> Local sum = 26`
- `Process rank 2 received [ 9 10 11 12 ] --> Local sum = 42`
- `Process rank 3 received [ 13 14 15 16 ] --> Local sum = 60`

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain `MPI_Gather` with its function signature. How does it differ from `MPI_Allgather`? Provide a real-world use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider 8 processes in `MPI_COMM_WORLD`. Each process holds an array of 4 floats representing a row of a matrix. The root process needs to reconstruct the full 8×4 matrix using `MPI_Gather`. Write the MPI code and compute the total bytes transferred if `sizeof(float) = 4` bytes. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) MPI_Gather Explanation (7 marks):**

**Function Signature:**

```c
int MPI_Gather(
    const void *sendbuf,    /* IN:  local buffer at every process */
    int sendcount,          /* IN:  elements each process sends */
    MPI_Datatype sendtype,  /* IN:  datatype of send */
    void *recvbuf,          /* OUT: gathered array at root */
    int recvcount,          /* IN:  elements received from each process */
    MPI_Datatype recvtype,  /* IN:  datatype of receive */
    int root,               /* IN:  rank of collecting process */
    MPI_Comm comm           /* IN:  communicator */
);
```

**Differences from MPI_Allgather:**

| **Aspect** | **MPI_Gather** | **MPI_Allgather** |
|---|---|---|
| Result location | Only at root | At **every** process |
| Symmetry | Asymmetric | Symmetric |
| Memory usage | Root holds $N \times$ data | All processes hold $N \times$ data |
| Network cost | Lower (only root receives) | Higher (broadcast phase added) |

**Real-World Use Case:** In a **distributed log aggregation system**, multiple worker processes parse different shards of log files and extract error counts. The aggregator process (root) uses `MPI_Gather` to collect these counts into a single summary report, while workers do not need a copy of the aggregated data, conserving memory.

**(b) MPI Code and Byte Calculation (7 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <mpi.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);

    int rank, size;
    MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);
    MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);

    float local_row[4] = {1.0f, 2.0f, 3.0f, 4.0f};
    float full_matrix[32];   /* Only meaningful at root */

    /* Modify so each process has a unique row for demonstration */
    for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++)
        local_row[i] = (float)(rank * 4 + i + 1);

    MPI_Gather(local_row, 4, MPI_FLOAT,
               full_matrix, 4, MPI_FLOAT,
               0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);

    if (rank == 0) {
        printf("Reconstructed 8x4 matrix at root:\n");
        for (int r = 0; r < 8; r++) {
            for (int c = 0; c < 4; c++)
                printf("%6.1f ", full_matrix[r * 4 + c]);
            printf("\n");
        }
    }

    MPI_Finalize();
    return 0;
}
```

**Byte Calculation:**

Given $N = 8$ processes, `sendcount` = 4, `sizeof(float)` = 4 bytes:

$$
D_{\text{gather}} = N \times \text{sendcount} \times \text{sizeof(float)}
$$

$$
D_{\text{gather}} = 8 \times 4 \times 4 = 128 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing `sendcount` with `recvcount`:** In Gather, `recvcount` is the number of elements received **per process** (not the total). Mixing these leads to a -2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting MPI_Init / MPI_Finalize:** KTU evaluators often deduct 1 mark for missing MPI initialization in code questions.
> 3. **Assuming all processes receive the gathered data:** In plain `MPI_Gather`, only the root process gets the assembled array. Use `MPI_Allgather` if every process needs it. A wrong statement loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Order of arguments in MPI_Scatter:** The signature has `sendbuf` BEFORE `recvbuf`. Students frequently swap them and the code fails to compile. Examiners award **0 marks** for non-compiling code.
> 5. **Mismatched `sendcount` across processes:** In `MPI_Gather`, the root must pass `recvcount` equal to the `sendcount` used by the contributing processes. A mismatch causes `MPI_ERR_COUNT`.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Scatter** = One-to-Many *distinct* distribution; **Gather** = Many-to-One *rank-ordered* assembly.
- Both are **collective** operations — all processes in the communicator **must** call the routine or the program **deadlocks**.
- Total data volume conserved: $D_{\text{scatter}} = D_{\text{gather}} = N \times \text{count} \times \text{sizeof(datatype)}$.
- Memory at non-root after Scatter = $\text{count} \times \text{sizeof(datatype)}$ bytes.
- Memory at root after Gather = $N \times \text{count} \times \text{sizeof(datatype)}$ bytes.
- Use `MPI_Scatterv` / `MPI_Gatherv` for **variable-sized** chunks (irregular parallelism).
- Use `MPI_Allgather` when **every** process needs the assembled array (symmetric case).
- Underlying implementation is optimized via **binomial tree** or **pipelined** algorithms — not naive $N-1$ sequential messages.
- Function signature order in MPI_Scatter: `sendbuf, sendcount, sendtype, recvbuf, recvcount, recvtype, root, comm`.
- Function signature order in MPI_Gather: `sendbuf, sendcount, sendtype, recvbuf, recvcount, recvtype, root, comm`.
- Always use `MPI_Comm_rank` and `MPI_Comm_size` to identify process identity and total count.
- KTU Module 3 also expects awareness of related collectives: `MPI_Alltoall`, `MPI_Reduce`, `MPI_Scan`.
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting that in `MPI_Gather`, non-root processes pass a *valid send buffer* but their `recvbuf` is ignored.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: All to All Personalized Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** All to All Personalized Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**All-to-All Personalized Communication** (also called *Personalized All-to-All* or *Total Exchange*) is a fundamental **collective communication primitive** in parallel and distributed computing in which each of the **p** processes in a parallel system sends a **distinct, unique message** of (generally) size **m** to **every other process** in the system.

In this pattern:
- Every process **Pi** holds **p − 1** outgoing messages, one destined for each of the other processes.
- Every process **Pi** will ultimately **receive p − 1** incoming messages, one originating from each of the other processes.
- The total number of point-to-point messages generated is **p × (p − 1)**, and the total volume of data exchanged across the network is **p × (p − 1) × m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The terms *personalized*, *tailored*, *individually distinct*, and *one-to-one per destination* are all key identifiers of this pattern. The opposite operation is **All-to-All Broadcast**, where a single message from each process is delivered to *all* processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Communication Model Used in KTU:**
> The widely-accepted analytical model for parallel communication is:
> **t = Ts + Tw × m**
> where **Ts** is the *startup/handshake latency* (in seconds) and **Tw** is the *per-word transfer time* (in seconds per word). For **m** = 0, t = Ts (latency bound); for large **m**, t ≈ Tw × m (bandwidth bound).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Classroom Story

Imagine a classroom of **p = 5** students seated in a row (call them S1, S2, S3, S4, S5). At the teacher's command, **each student must hand out a different, personalized chit to every other student**.

- S1 has 4 unique chits (one for S2, one for S3, one for S4, one for S5).
- S2 has 4 unique chits, and so on.
- Total chits distributed = 5 × 4 = **20 chits**.

Now, the chits cannot be teleported — students must exchange them *physically*, walking in the aisle. The challenge of All-to-All Personalized Communication is to design a **schedule of movements** such that:
1. Every chit reaches its intended recipient.
2. The total time taken is **minimized**.
3. **No two students collide** in the aisle (no link congestion).

This is *exactly* what parallel algorithms must do on a network — the classroom row is a **linear/ring network**, and the students are processes. The "time taken" is the **makespan** of the communication.

## 1.3 Real-World Engineering Context

All-to-All Personalized Communication is the workhorse behind:
- **Matrix Transposition** of an m × m matrix distributed over a p-process grid.
- **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** — the bit-reversal step.
- **Parallel Sorting algorithms** (e.g., Sample Sort, Bitonic Sort).
- **Dense Linear Algebra kernels** — e.g., Cannon's algorithm, SUMMA.
- **Database Shuffle / Redistribution** operations in MapReduce-style frameworks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Broken Down

The generic All-to-All Personalized Communication proceeds in **d communication steps**, where **d** is the **diameter of the underlying network topology** (i.e., the maximum shortest-path distance between any two nodes).

**Step 0 — Initialization:**
- Every process **Pi** partitions its outgoing message buffer of size **(p − 1) × m** into **(p − 1)** chunks of size **m**, with chunk **j** destined for process **Pj**.

**Step k (for k = 0, 1, …, d − 1):**
- Each process sends its **k-th sub-message** to the appropriate neighbor in the topology.
- In a hypercube, this corresponds to sending across the **k-th dimension**.
- In a linear array / ring, this corresponds to sending **left** or **right** by one hop, with the message re-routed by intermediate nodes.

**Step d — Termination:**
- Every process has now received all its **(p − 1)** incoming messages, which it concatenates to form the final buffer of size **(p − 1) × m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why d steps and not (p − 1) steps?**
> The magic of collective communication is that intermediate nodes act as **relays**. A single message can be forwarded through multiple hops in a pipeline, so the total number of *synchronous rounds* is bounded by the **diameter**, not the number of messages.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why partition into (p − 1) chunks?** Because each process has a *personalized* message for every other. Without partitioning, you would be doing broadcast, not personalized exchange.
- **How does congestion arise?** Two processes may simultaneously try to send across the same physical link — this is called **link contention**, and it **inflates effective Tw**.
- **How is congestion avoided?** Through **edge-coloring** of the communication graph or **dimension-order routing** in regular topologies like hypercubes and meshes.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **S.No.** | **Topology / Algorithm** | **Time Complexity (t_all-to-all)** | **Key Assumption** |
|:---------:|:------------------------|:------------------------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | One-port, store-and-forward, ring / linear array | $t = (p-1) \cdot (Ts + Tw \cdot m)$ | Sequential per-node send |
| 2 | Hypercube (with congestion-free routing) | $t = (p-1) \cdot Ts + \tfrac{p-1}{2} \cdot Tw \cdot m$ | d = log p, message doubling at each step |
| 3 | 2D Mesh (no wrap-around) | $t = 2(\sqrt{p}-1) \cdot Ts + \tfrac{2(p-1)\cdot m}{1} \cdot Tw$ (approx.) | d = 2(√p − 1) |
| 4 | All-port, full-bisection bandwidth mesh | $t \approx Ts + Tw \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ | Idealized upper bound |
| 5 | Total data volume per process | $(p-1) \cdot m$ | Incoming + outgoing |
| 6 | Total messages in system | $p \cdot (p-1)$ | Bidirectional count |
| 7 | Optimal lower bound (p processes) | $t_{opt} \geq Ts \cdot (p-1)$ | Each process must initiate ≥ p − 1 sends |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For a **ring**, students frequently forget the **doubling** effect. When p = 8 and message size m, process P0 must finally receive a chunk of size 4m from P4. The total *transferred* bytes per step grows, but the number of *synchronization* rounds is still p − 1.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of This Primitive

| **Field** | **Application** |
|:----------|:----------------|
| High-Performance Computing (HPC) | MPI_Alltoall — used in linear algebra, FFT |
| Big Data | Shuffle phase of MapReduce / Spark reshuffles |
| Graph Processing | Distributed adjacency redistribution |
| Network Design | Optical cross-connect reconfigurations |
| ML/Distributed Training | All-reduce style parameter exchanges (variant) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time Complexity on a Ring / Linear Array

Consider **p** processes arranged in a **ring** (P0 ↔ P1 ↔ … ↔ Pp-1 ↔ P0). Each process must send **(p − 1)** unique messages of size **m** each. Communication is **one-port** (a process can only send OR receive on a single link at a time) and **store-and-forward**.

**Step 1: Communication Rounds Required**

In the worst case, two processes that are diametrically opposite (e.g., P0 and P⌊p/2⌋) are **⌊p/2⌋** hops apart. However, with pipelining across the ring, we can schedule sends in **p − 1** synchronous rounds — one "rotation phase" per round, where each Pi hands a chunk to Pi+1 mod p.

> Therefore, **number of rounds = p − 1**.

**Step 2: Time per Round**

In a one-port model, per round:
- Startup latency: **Ts** (handshake for one message).
- Per-word transfer: **Tw × m** for a single message of size m.

So time per round = **Ts + Tw × m**.

**Step 3: Total Time**

$$
t_{ring} = (p-1) \cdot (Ts + Tw \cdot m)
$$

This can be expanded as:

$$
t_{ring} = (p-1) \cdot Ts \;+\; (p-1) \cdot Tw \cdot m
$$

> **Textual Interpretation:** The first term represents the **synchronization overhead** (must be paid p − 1 times due to one-port constraint). The second term represents the **per-word data transfer cost** (each of the p − 1 messages of size m must traverse one link).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time Complexity on a Hypercube (d = log₂ p)

A **p-process hypercube** has dimension **d = log₂ p**. We assume **store-and-forward**, **one-port**, and **congestion-free** dimension-order routing (each link carries at most one message per round).

**Step 1: Phases**
Communication is staged in **d phases** (one per dimension). In phase **k** (k = 0, 1, …, d − 1), every process sends and receives **2^{d−1} − 1** messages across the **k-th dimension** neighbors — *but the total data doubles as we go deeper into the routing tree*.

> A cleaner way: total **synchronization steps** = p − 1 (one per message to be originated).

**Step 2: Average Message Length at the Wire**
Because the message is split into chunks of size **m** and forwarded along the dimension-order path, the *expected* distance a chunk of size **m** travels is the **average distance** in a hypercube, which equals **d/2 = (log₂ p)/2**.

**Step 3: Total Time**

$$
t_{hypercube} = (p-1) \cdot Ts \;+\; \tfrac{p-1}{2} \cdot Tw \cdot m
$$

**Step 4: Why /2 in the Tw term?**

Each message of size m traverses on average **(log p)/2** hops. Total hops across all messages = p(p − 1) × (log p)/2. Dividing by the **p** available **bisection links** (per round) gives the bandwidth-bound term.

**Detailed algebraic walk-through:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total hops in system} &= p(p-1) \cdot \frac{\log_2 p}{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Bisection bandwidth (links/round)} &= \frac{p}{2} \\[4pt]
\text{Rounds for data} &= \frac{p(p-1) \cdot \frac{\log_2 p}{2}}{\frac{p}{2}} = (p-1) \cdot \log_2 p \\[4pt]
\text{Wait — this is for store-and-forward, so data per round} &= m \\[4pt]
\text{Data rounds} &= (p-1) \cdot \log_2 p / \log_2 p = (p-1) \\[4pt]
\text{Average data per link per round} &= \frac{m}{2} \\[4pt]
\therefore t_{hypercube} &= (p-1) \cdot Ts \;+\; (p-1) \cdot \frac{m}{2} \cdot Tw \\[4pt]
&= (p-1) \cdot Ts \;+\; \frac{p-1}{2} \cdot Tw \cdot m
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Interpretation:** The **Ts** term remains (p − 1)·Ts because every one of the p − 1 sends still requires a handshake. The **Tw** term is *halved* compared to a ring because the hypercube's higher bisection bandwidth doubles effective throughput.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time Complexity on a 2D Mesh (p = q² processes)

A **q × q mesh** with **q = √p** has diameter **d = 2(q − 1)** (row traversal + column traversal).

**Step 1: Row + Column Decomposition**
A 2D mesh all-to-all can be decomposed into **q independent row-wise all-to-alls** + **q independent column-wise all-to-alls**. Each row (or column) has **q** processes connected as a linear array.

**Step 2: Time for a single q-process linear array**

$$
t_{row} = (q-1) \cdot (Ts + Tw \cdot m)
$$

**Step 3: Total Time (row phase + column phase)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{mesh} &= 2 \cdot t_{row} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{concurrency}} \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot (q-1) \cdot (Ts + Tw \cdot m) \\[4pt]
&= 2(\sqrt{p}-1) \cdot Ts + 2(\sqrt{p}-1) \cdot Tw \cdot m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison**

$$
\frac{t_{hypercube}}{t_{mesh}} \approx \frac{(p-1) \cdot Ts + \tfrac{p-1}{2} Tw \cdot m}{2(\sqrt{p}-1)(Ts + Tw \cdot m)} \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{\sqrt{p}}{4} \quad \text{as } p \to \infty
$$

This shows the **hypercube is asymptotically 4×/√p faster** in the Tw-dominated regime.

## 3.4 The Lower Bound — Why We Cannot Do Better

Any algorithm must satisfy:

$$
t_{opt} \geq \max \left\{ (p-1) \cdot Ts, \; \frac{(p-1) \cdot m}{B_{bisection}} \cdot Tw \right\}
$$

where **B_bisection** is the **bisection bandwidth** of the network. For a hypercube, B_bisection = p/2, so:

$$
t_{opt}^{HC} \geq \max\left\{ (p-1) \cdot Ts, \; \frac{2(p-1) \cdot m}{p} \cdot Tw \right\} \approx (p-1) \cdot Ts + \frac{2m}{Tw^{-1}} \cdot \frac{1}{p/2}
$$

This sets the **asymptotic floor** below which no algorithm on that topology can fall.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A parallel program with **p = 8** processes running on a ring topology performs an All-to-All Personalized Communication. Each message has size **m = 1024 words**. Given **Ts = 50 μs** and **Tw = 1 ns/word**, find the total communication time.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= 8, \quad m = 1024, \quad Ts = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}, \quad Tw = 1 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s/word} \\[4pt]
t_{ring} &= (p-1) \cdot (Ts + Tw \cdot m) \\[4pt]
&= 7 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-6} + 1 \times 10^{-9} \times 1024) \\[4pt]
&= 7 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-6} + 1.024 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 7 \cdot 51.024 \times 10^{-6} \\[4pt]
&= 357.168 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**For hypercube** (d = 3, B_bisection = 4):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{HC} &= (p-1) \cdot Ts + \frac{p-1}{2} \cdot Tw \cdot m \\[4pt]
&= 7 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6} + 3.5 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 1024 \\[4pt]
&= 350 \; \mu s + 3.584 \; \mu s \\[4pt]
&\approx 353.58 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Observation:** The hypercube is slightly faster (≈ 1% gain) because the Tw term is halved, but Ts dominates here. For **larger m**, the hypercube advantage grows.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Data Flow (4 Processes, Ring Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["P0 sends m01 to P1 and m02 to P2 and m03 to P3"]
    P1["P1 sends m10 to P0 and m12 to P2 and m13 to P3"]
    P2["P2 sends m20 to P0 and m21 to P1 and m23 to P3"]
    P3["P3 sends m30 to P0 and m31 to P1 and m32 to P2"]

    P0 -- "m01" --> P1
    P0 -- "m02" --> P2
    P0 -- "m03" --> P3
    P1 -- "m10" --> P0
    P1 -- "m12" --> P2
    P1 -- "m13" --> P3
    P2 -- "m20" --> P0
    P2 -- "m21" --> P1
    P2 -- "m23" --> P3
    P3 -- "m30" --> P0
    P3 -- "m31" --> P1
```
