4. **Local Completion:** The send call returns when the *send buffer is reusable*. For Standard mode, this may occur before or after remote reception.

5. **Internal Copy or Hand-off:** Data is either copied into a **system buffer** (eager) or sent directly (rendezvous).

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — How MPI_Recv Works

1. **Wildcard Resolution:** The receiver may use `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` and/or `MPI_ANY_TAG` as wildcards.
2. **Probe/Inspection (Optional):** A process can call `MPI_Probe` to inspect an incoming message's metadata before issuing `MPI_Recv`.
3. **Matching:** The runtime scans the **unexpected message queue** (eager) or waits for a rendezvous handshake.
4. **Data Transfer:** Payload is copied into the user buffer.
5. **Status Update:** The `MPI_Status` object is populated with `MPI_SOURCE`, `MPI_TAG`, and `MPI_GET_COUNT` information.
6. **Return:** The call returns to the user code.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Significance** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Message Size | $S_{msg} = \text{count} \times \text{sizeof}(\text{datatype})$ | Used in performance estimation |
| Eager Threshold | $S_{msg} \le T_{eager}$ (typical: $\le 16$–$64$ KB) | Small messages use Eager protocol |
| Rendezvous Trigger | $S_{msg} > T_{eager}$ | Large messages use Handshake protocol |
| Envelope Fields | $\{ \text{rank}_{dest}, \text{tag}, \text{comm} \}$ (send side) | Must match receive envelope |
| Wildcard Source | $\text{source} = \text{MPI\_ANY\_SOURCE}$ | Matches any sender |
| Wildcard Tag | $\text{tag} = \text{MPI\_ANY\_TAG}$ | Matches any tag |
| Buffer Size (Bsend) | $S_{buf} \ge \max(S_{msg}) + \text{MPICH\_BSEND\_OVERHEAD}$ | Required for buffered sends |
| Status Count | $\text{count} = \text{MPI\_GET\_COUNT}(\text{status}, \text{datatype})$ | Real elements received |
| Isend/Irecv Wait Time | $T_{total} = \max(T_{compute}, T_{comm}) + T_{overhead}$ | Overlap model for non-blocking |
| Latency Model | $T_{msg} = T_{startup} + \frac{S_{msg}}{B_{link}}$ | LogP / Hockney model approximation |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **buffer-management** aspect of Bsend is critical in embedded and HPC clusters where **memory budgets are tight**. Systems engineers use `MPI_Buffer_attach` and `MPI_Buffer_detach` to pre-allocate cyclic buffers, ensuring that Standard mode does not silently exhaust system memory on long-running scientific simulations (e.g., weather models, molecular dynamics).

---

## 2.4 Deadlock Scenarios (Critical KTU Topic)

> [!WARNING]
> **Deadlock is the #1 cause of marks lost in KTU 14-mark questions on send/receive!**

Four canonical deadlock patterns exist:

| Pattern | Description | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Circular Wait** | P0 calls Ssend to P1; P1 calls Ssend to P0 | Use Sendrecv or non-blocking |
| **Resource Starvation** | Send buffer is full; receiver hasn't posted | Use Buffered send |
| **Tag Mismatch** | Sender tag ≠ Receiver tag → infinite wait | Verify tag agreement |
| **Comm Mismatch** | Different communicators used | Use same `MPI_COMM_WORLD` |

The **deadlock-free solution** is the *Sendrecv* pattern:

$$
\text{MPI\_Sendrecv}(\text{sendbuf}, \text{scount}, \text{stype}, \text{dest}, \text{stag}, \text{recvbuf}, \text{rcount}, \text{rtype}, \text{source}, \text{rtag}, \text{comm}, \text{status})
$$

This combines send and receive into a single atomic operation, eliminating the circular wait.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Computing the Eager/Rendezvous Threshold Decision

MPI implementations dynamically choose between **Eager** and **Rendezvous** protocols based on message size. The decision is governed by a threshold $T_{eager}$ that the runtime maintains. We will derive the total communication time under both protocols and show where crossover occurs.

### Step 1: Define the Latency Model

The **Hockney Model** approximates the time to send a message of size $S$ as:

$$
T(S) = T_{startup} + \frac{S}{B_{link}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{startup}$ = latency overhead (time to initiate transfer), typically $\approx 1$–$10\ \mu s$
- $B_{link}$ = effective link bandwidth, typically $\approx 1$–$10\ \text{GB/s}$ on modern interconnects

### Step 2: Eager Protocol Total Time

In the Eager protocol, the sender transmits immediately without waiting. The total time is purely the message transfer time:

$$
T_{eager}(S) = T_{startup} + \frac{S}{B_{link}}
$$

### Step 3: Rendezvous Protocol Total Time

In the Rendezvous protocol, a **three-way handshake** occurs first:
1. Sender → Receiver: *Ready-to-Send (RTS)* packet
2. Receiver → Sender: *Clear-to-Send (CTS)* packet
3. Sender → Receiver: *Data payload*

Each control packet incurs a round-trip of latency. Thus:

$$
T_{rendezvous}(S) = 2 \cdot T_{startup} + T_{startup} + \frac{S}{B_{link}} = 3 \cdot T_{startup} + \frac{S}{B_{link}}
$$

### Step 4: Setting Up the Crossover Equation

The MPI runtime uses Eager if it is *faster or equal* to Rendezvous:

$$
T_{eager}(S) \le T_{rendezvous}(S)
$$

$$
T_{startup} + \frac{S}{B_{link}} \le 3 \cdot T_{startup} + \frac{S}{B_{link}}
$$

$$
T_{startup} \le 3 \cdot T_{startup}
$$

$$
0 \le 2 \cdot T_{startup}
$$

This is always true — meaning the **latency component alone never favors Rendezvous**. The *real* trade-off is about **buffer memory pressure**, not raw time.

### Step 5: The Memory-Pressure Refinement

Let $M_{buffer}$ be the available system buffer size and $N$ be the number of concurrent senders. The eager protocol's worst-case memory consumption is:

$$
M_{eager} = N \cdot S_{msg}
$$

The rendezvous protocol consumes only control packet memory:

$$
M_{rendezvous} = N \cdot S_{RTS} \approx \text{constant (tiny)}
$$

The crossover decision in practice is:

$$
T_{eager} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Eager} & \text{if } S_{msg} \le T_{eager} \text{ AND } M_{eager} \le M_{buffer} \\[4pt]
\text{Rendezvous} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 6: Numerical Example for KTU Board Exam

**Given:**
- $T_{startup} = 5\ \mu s$
- $B_{link} = 5\ \text{GB/s} = 5 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bytes/s}$
- $S_{msg} = 32\ \text{KB} = 32768\ \text{bytes}$
- $T_{eager} = 64\ \text{KB}$

**Compute $T_{eager}$:**

$$
T_{eager} = 5\ \mu s + \frac{32768}{5 \times 10^{9}}\ \text{seconds}
$$

$$
T_{eager} = 5\ \mu s + 6.5536\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{eager} = 11.5536\ \mu s
$$

**Compute $T_{rendezvous}$:**

$$
T_{rendezvous} = 3 \times 5\ \mu s + 6.5536\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{rendezvous} = 15\ \mu s + 6.5536\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{rendezvous} = 21.5536\ \mu s
$$

**Decision:** Since $S_{msg} = 32\ \text{KB} \le 64\ \text{KB} = T_{eager}$, the runtime selects **Eager Protocol**, completing in $11.55\ \mu s$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Communication Modes

| **Property** | **Standard (MPI_Send)** | **Buffered (MPI_Bsend)** | **Synchronous (MPI_Ssend)** | **Ready (MPI_Rsend)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Local completion** | When buffer is reusable | After data is buffered | After receiver starts receiving | Immediately (if recv posted) |
| **Requires recv posted?** | No | No | Yes (for completion) | **Yes — ERROR otherwise** |
| **Buffer requirement** | Runtime-managed | User-managed (`MPI_Buffer_attach`) | Runtime-managed | Runtime-managed |
| **Deadlock risk** | Medium | **Low** | **High** | Low (if recv posted) |
| **Use case** | General purpose | Unknown recv timing | Strong sync needed | Optimized hot path |
| **Overhead** | Variable | Memory copy | 2-way handshake | Minimal |

---

## 3.3 Status Object Information Extraction — Step-by-Step

When a wildcard (`MPI_ANY_SOURCE` / `MPI_ANY_TAG`) is used, the application must query the `MPI_Status` object to discover the actual sender and tag:

$$
\text{status.MPI\_SOURCE} \rightarrow \text{Actual sender rank}
$$

$$
\text{status.MPI\_TAG} \rightarrow \text{Actual message tag}
$$

$$
\text{count} = \text{MPI\_GET\_COUNT}(\&\text{status}, \text{MPI\_INT}, \&\text{count})
$$

This is essential in **master-worker** patterns where the master accepts work requests from any worker process.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Point-to-Point Communication Lifecycle (Eager Protocol)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process P0 calls MPI_Send] --> B[Construct envelope: dest, tag, comm]
    B --> C{Message size S <= T_eager?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Select Eager Protocol]
    C -->|No| E[Select Rendezvous Protocol]
    D --> F[Copy data to system buffer]
    F --> G[Transmit packet immediately to P1]
    G --> H[P1 receives into unexpected queue]
    H --> I[P1 calls MPI_Recv]
    I --> J{Match on dest, tag, comm?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Copy data to user buffer]
    J -->|No| L[Message waits in queue]
    K --> M[P1 returns with Status object]
    L --> I
    E --> N[Send RTS to P1]
    N --> O[P1 responds with CTS]
    O --> P[Send data payload]
    P --> Q[P1 receives and matches]
    Q --> K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Four Send Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER[Application Layer]
        U1[User Buffer]
    end
    subgraph MPI[MPI Runtime Layer]
        S1[Standard Send]
        S2[Buffered Send]
        S3[Synchronous Send]
        S4[Ready Send]
        BUF[User-Allocated Buffer Pool]
    end
    subgraph NET[Network Layer]
        N1[Ethernet / InfiniBand / Shared Memory]
    end
    U1 --> S1
    U1 --> S2
    U1 --> S3
    U1 --> S4
    S2 --> BUF
    S1 --> N1
    S2 --> N1
    S3 --> N1
    S4 --> N1
    N1 --> R1[Receiver Runtime]
    R1 --> U2[Receiver User Buffer]
```

## 4.3 Deadlock Scenario vs. Deadlock-Free Pattern

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P0 as Process 0
    participant P1 as Process 1
    Note over P0,P1: DEADLOCK SCENARIO
    P0->>P0: Ssend to P1 (blocks)
    P1->>P1: Ssend to P0 (blocks)
    Note over P0,P1: Both wait forever!
    Note over P0,P1: DEADLOCK-FREE with Sendrecv
    P0->>P1: Sendrecv: send X, recv Y
    P1->>P0: Sendrecv: send Y, recv X
    Note over P0,P1: Both complete successfully
```

## 4.4 Non-Blocking Send/Receive Flow (Overlap Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Call MPI_Isend: returns immediately] --> B[Call MPI_Irecv: returns immediately]
    B --> C[Perform local computation]
    C --> D[Call MPI_Wait on send request]
    D --> E[Call MPI_Wait on receive request]
    E --> F[Safe to use buffers]
    C -.->|Computation overlaps| G[Network transfer in background]
    G --> F
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define point-to-point communication in MPI. List the four mandatory fields of a message envelope.**

**Model Answer:**

Point-to-point communication in MPI refers to a data transfer operation between exactly two processes — one designated as the *sender* and the other as the *receiver*. It is the most fundamental form of inter-process communication in MPI.

The four mandatory fields of a message envelope are:
1. **Destination Rank** (for send) / **Source Rank** (for receive)
2. **Tag** (integer message identifier)
3. **Communicator** (e.g., `MPI_COMM_WORLD`)
4. **Count and Datatype** (payload description)

The receiver's envelope (source, tag, communicator) must match the sender's envelope (dest, tag, communicator) for the message to be successfully received.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between blocking and non-blocking send operations in MPI.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Blocking Send (MPI_Send)** | **Non-Blocking Send (MPI_Isend)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Return Time | Returns only when send buffer is reusable | Returns immediately |
| Completion Check | Implicit | Explicit via `MPI_Wait` / `MPI_Test` |
| Request Handle | Not required | Returns `MPI_Request` |
| Overlap with Computation | No | Yes (key for performance) |
| Error Risk | Lower (synchronous semantics) | Higher (buffer reuse before wait) |

Non-blocking operations allow the application to **overlap communication with computation**, a critical optimization in HPC.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the four communication modes of `MPI_Send`. Compare their buffer requirements and deadlock risk. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **deadlock** in MPI point-to-point communication. Describe two common deadlock scenarios and show how `MPI_Sendrecv` resolves them with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**The Four Communication Modes:**

**1. Standard Mode (`MPI_Send`):**
The implementation is free to choose whether to buffer the message internally or to send it directly. Local completion occurs when the send buffer is reusable. Deadlock risk is *medium* because the implementation may unexpectedly decide to use the rendezvous protocol.

**2. Buffered Mode (`MPI_Bsend`):**
The user explicitly allocates a buffer using `MPI_Buffer_attach`. The message is copied into this user buffer and the send call returns immediately. This mode has **lowest deadlock risk** because the send will never block waiting for a matching receive. The trade-off is that buffer memory must be pre-allocated.

**3. Synchronous Mode (`MPI_Ssend`):**
The send call does not return until the matching receive has *started* receiving the data. This guarantees a handshake and provides strong synchronization semantics. It carries the **highest deadlock risk** if both processes call `Ssend` to each other.

**4. Ready Mode (`MPI_Rsend`):**
The user *guarantees* that a matching receive has already been posted before the send is called. If the guarantee is violated, the result is undefined (error). This mode enables optimization on certain network hardware (e.g., eager RDMA writes).

**Comparison Table (Already in Section 3.2):**
- **Buffer requirement:** Bsend > Standard > Ssend > Rsend
- **Deadlock risk:** Ssend > Standard > Rsend > Bsend
- **Synchronization guarantee:** Ssend (strongest) > Standard (medium) > Bsend, Rsend (weakest)

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Deadlock Concept:**
A deadlock in MPI occurs when two or more processes are blocked indefinitely, each waiting for an action that the other must perform. This typically arises in point-to-point communication when a blocking send has no matching receive (or vice versa).

**Scenario 1 — Circular Ssend:**
```c
// Process 0
MPI_Ssend(buf1, N, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);

// Process 1
MPI_Ssend(buf2, N, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
```
Both processes block waiting for the other to issue a receive. Result: **deadlock**.

**Scenario 2 — Buffer Saturation:**
Process 0 issues many blocking Standard sends with large messages to Process 1, but Process 1 is busy with computation and has not yet posted the receive. The internal system buffer fills up; subsequent sends block. **Deadlock occurs**.

**Resolution with `MPI_Sendrecv`:**
```c
// Both Process 0 and Process 1
MPI_Sendrecv(sendbuf, N, MPI_INT, 1, 0,
             recvbuf, N, MPI_INT, 1, 0,
             MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
```
`MPI_Sendrecv` performs both send and receive in a single atomic call, breaking the circular wait. This pattern is the **standard solution** in ring topologies and shift operations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the eager and rendezvous protocols used by MPI for point-to-point communication. Derive the time equations for both protocols and determine the crossover condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable diagrams, explain the structure of a non-blocking send/receive operation. Discuss the role of `MPI_Wait` and `MPI_Test`, and illustrate how computation-communication overlap is achieved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Eager Protocol:**
Used for **small messages**. The sender immediately transmits the entire message without checking if the receiver has posted a matching receive. The receiver stores the message in an *unexpected message queue* until a matching `MPI_Recv` is issued.

**Time equation (Eager):**

$$
T_{eager}(S) = T_{startup} + \frac{S}{B_{link}}
$$

**Rendezvous Protocol:**
Used for **large messages**. A three-way handshake occurs:
1. Sender sends *Ready-to-Send (RTS)* control packet
2. Receiver responds with *Clear-to-Send (CTS)*
3. Sender transmits the actual data payload

**Time equation (Rendezvous):**

$$
T_{rendezvous}(S) = 3 \cdot T_{startup} + \frac{S}{B_{link}}
$$

**Crossover Condition:**

The MPI runtime chooses Eager when:

$$
S \le T_{eager}\ \text{(threshold, e.g., 64 KB)}
$$

And chooses Rendezvous when:

$$
S > T_{eager}
$$

This decision also considers available system buffer memory: $N \cdot S \le M_{buffer}$ for Eager to be safe.

*(Full numerical example with derivation is provided in Section 3.1.)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Non-Blocking Structure (Diagram in Section 4.4):**

The non-blocking pattern follows these steps:
1. Issue `MPI_Isend` → returns immediately with `MPI_Request` handle
2. Issue `MPI_Irecv` → returns immediately with `MPI_Request` handle
3. **Perform local computation** while the network transfer proceeds in the background
4. Call `MPI_Wait(&request, &status)` → blocks until the specific request completes
5. Call `MPI_Test(&request, &flag, &status)` → non-blocking check, returns flag

**Role of `MPI_Wait`:**
- Blocks the calling process until the request is complete.
- Ensures the send/receive buffer is safe to reuse.

**Role of `MPI_Test`:**
- Returns immediately with a flag indicating completion.
- Useful in polling loops and progress engines.

**Computation-Communication Overlap:**

The total time for an overlap pattern is:

$$
T_{total}^{overlap} = T_{startup} + \max\left(T_{compute},\ T_{transfer}\right) + T_{finalize}
$$

Versus the sequential pattern:

$$
T_{total}^{sequential} = T_{startup} + T_{transfer} + T_{compute} + T_{finalize}
$$

This overlap can yield significant speedup (up to $2\times$ in compute-bound and comm-bound balanced cases) and is the **primary motivation** for non-blocking operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Verified Against KTU Valuation Keys):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Status object:** When `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` is used, students often fail to query `status.MPI_SOURCE` and `status.MPI_TAG`. Loss: **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Ssend with Send:** Students state that `MPI_Send` is synchronous. Loss: **2–3 marks**. Only `MPI_Ssend` is guaranteed synchronous.
>
> 3. **Missing buffer attach for Bsend:** Students write `MPI_Bsend` but never mention `MPI_Buffer_attach`. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 4. **No deadlock discussion in Sendrecv question:** Students give code but skip explaining *why* it works. Loss: **3 marks**.
>
> 5. **Wrong units in time equation:** Writing bandwidth as "GB" instead of "GB/s". Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
>
> - ✅ The **message envelope** has four fields: *source/dest*, *tag*, *communicator*, *count+datatype*.
> - ✅ Both `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv` are **blocking** by default.
> - ✅ `MPI_Isend` and `MPI_Irecv` are **non-blocking** and return `MPI_Request`.
> - ✅ Four send modes: **Standard, Buffered, Synchronous, Ready** — order them by deadlock risk and synchronization strength.
> - ✅ **Eager protocol** = small messages, immediate send, may buffer. **Rendezvous protocol** = large messages, handshake required.
> - ✅ **Wildcards**: `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` and `MPI_ANY_TAG` are used only in `MPI_Recv`.
> - ✅ The **Status object** must be queried after a wildcard receive via `status.MPI_SOURCE`, `status.MPI_TAG`, and `MPI_GET_COUNT`.
> - ✅ `MPI_Sendrecv` is the **canonical deadlock-free** primitive for bidirectional exchange.
> - ✅ **Time equation**: $T_{eager} = T_{startup} + S/B$ and $T_{rendezvous} = 3 \cdot T_{startup} + S/B$.
> - ✅ **Overlap pattern** using `Isend`/`Irecv` + `Wait` is the foundation of HPC performance optimization.
> - ✅ **Tag values** must be non-negative integers in the range $[0, 32767]$ (MPI standard).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MPI: The Message Passing Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MPI: The Message Passing Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Message Passing Interface (MPI)** is a **standardized, language-independent, and portable message-passing specification** designed to function on a wide variety of parallel computing architectures. It defines the **syntax and semantics of library routines** that allow processes running on distributed-memory systems (clusters, supercomputers, multi-core nodes) to exchange data through explicit *send* and *receive* operations. MPI is the **de-facto industry and academic standard** for writing portable parallel programs in languages like C, C++, Fortran, and Python (via `mpi4py`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Board Exam Focus):** MPI is a **specification**, not a library or implementation. The standard is defined by the **MPI Forum** (a consortium of vendors and academics). Common implementations include **OpenMPI**, **MPICH**, **Intel MPI**, and **MVAPICH2**. All of them obey the same function signatures defined by the standard, ensuring portability of code across platforms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Service Between Workers"

Imagine a large factory with **100 isolated workers**, each sitting in their own soundproof room. They have **no shared whiteboard, no shared desk, and no shared variable**. The only way they can cooperate to assemble a product is by **sending sealed letters through pneumatic tubes** to specific other workers.

- Each worker is a **process** with its own private memory.
- The pneumatic tube system is the **network (interconnect)** — Infiniband, Ethernet, etc.
- The letter is the **message** (data + tag + sender + receiver info).
- The **postal rules** (who can send to whom, how to address letters, what happens if the inbox is full) form the **MPI standard**.
- The **factory foreman** is the **`MPI_COMM_WORLD` communicator** — a universe defining the set of workers that recognize each other's letters.

This "letter-passing" world is exactly what MPI models. There is **no shared global memory**, so **all communication must be explicit**. This is in stark contrast to the OpenMP shared-memory model covered in the previous module.

## 1.3 Why MPI Exists — The Motivation

Before MPI, message-passing libraries were vendor-specific (PVM, NX, etc.). Code written for one parallel machine rarely worked on another. In **1992**, the MPI Forum was formed, and **MPI 1.0 was released in 1994**. Goals:

1. **Portability** — One source code runs on any parallel system.
2. **Performance** — Native implementations leverage the underlying high-speed network.
3. **Functionality** — Rich set of point-to-point, collective, and topology routines.
4. **Scalability** — Programs that work for 8 processes must work for 8000 processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **MPMD vs SPMD (Board Favorite):**
> - **SPMD (Single Program, Multiple Data):** Every process runs the *same* MPI program but operates on a different data partition. This is the most common MPI programming style.
> - **MPMD (Multiple Program, Multiple Data):** Different processes may execute different programs; coordination is via MPI. Less common but supported.

## 1.4 Architectural Placement

MPI sits at the **user-space library level**, **above the OS** but **below the application**. It typically bypasses the OS kernel for performance and uses kernel-bypass mechanisms like **RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access)** on modern interconnects.

$$
\text{Application Program} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{MPI Library (libmpi)} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{Network Driver} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{Interconnect (InfiniBand / Ethernet)}
$$

The MPI standard mandates **over 300 functions**, but most MPI programs use only **~20–30** of them.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Skeleton of Every MPI Program

Every MPI program — regardless of complexity — follows the **6-stage lifecycle**:

1. **Initialize the MPI environment** — `MPI_Init(&argc, &argv)`
2. **Find out how many processes exist** — `MPI_Comm_size(comm, &size)`
3. **Determine *my* process identifier (rank)** — `MPI_Comm_rank(comm, &rank)`
4. **Perform the actual work + communication** — user logic
5. **Barrier synchronization (optional but common)** — `MPI_Barrier(comm)`
6. **Terminate the MPI environment** — `MPI_Finalize()`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners often ask for the **"boilerplate structure of an MPI program"**. Memorize the six steps above and the four critical functions: `MPI_Init`, `MPI_Comm_size`, `MPI_Comm_rank`, `MPI_Finalize`. After `MPI_Init`, no standard MPI function is callable.

## 2.2 The Communicator — The "Universe" of Processes

A **communicator** in MPI is a **named group of processes** that can communicate with one another. Think of it as a **closed chat room**: only members can post and read messages.

- **`MPI_COMM_WORLD`** — The *default* communicator containing *all* processes launched by `mpirun`/`mpiexec`. Every process in this world has a unique integer ID called its **rank** in the range **`[0, size-1]`**.
- **`MPI_COMM_SELF`** — A communicator containing *only the calling process* itself (useful for self-loops and library isolation).
- **User-defined communicators** — Created using `MPI_Comm_split`, `MPI_Cart_create`, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> The rank of a process is **not globally unique** — it is unique *only within its communicator*. The same process can have rank 0 in one communicator and rank 3 in another.

## 2.3 Point-to-Point Communication

Point-to-point communication involves **exactly one sender and exactly one receiver**. It is the **fundamental building block** of MPI.

### 2.3.1 Blocking Send — `MPI_Send`

```c
int MPI_Send(const void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
             int dest, int tag, MPI_Comm comm);
```

- **`buf`** — Starting address of the send buffer.
- **`count`** — Number of *elements* (not bytes) to send.
- **`datatype`** — MPI datatype of each element (`MPI_INT`, `MPI_FLOAT`, `MPI_DOUBLE`, etc.).
- **`dest`** — Rank of the destination process in `comm`.
- **`tag`** — Integer message tag (used by receiver to filter messages).
- **`comm`** — Communicator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blocking semantics:** The call returns when the **send buffer is safe to be reused** by the calling program. The implementation may have copied the data into a system buffer, or it may have waited for the matching receive to start. The user **must not assume** either — only that reuse is safe.

### 2.3.2 Blocking Receive — `MPI_Recv`

```c
int MPI_Recv(void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
             int source, int tag, MPI_Comm comm, MPI_Status *status);
```

- **`source`** — Rank of source process, or `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` to accept from any sender.
- **`tag`** — Tag to match, or `MPI_ANY_TAG` to accept any tag.
- **`status`** — Information object filled in by MPI about the actual source, tag, and count received.

> [!NOTE]
> The **wildcard values** `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` and `MPI_ANY_TAG` allow a receiver to "catch any letter arriving at the mailbox." This is essential for writing flexible, robust server-style code.

### 2.3.3 Message Matching Rules

A **send matches a receive** if and only if:

1. They belong to the **same communicator**.
2. The **destination rank** of the send equals the **source rank** of the receive (or wildcard is used).
3. The **tag** of the send equals the **tag** of the receive (or wildcard is used).

If multiple sends are outstanding from the same source with the same tag, the receiver gets them in **non-deterministic order** (FIFO order is **not** guaranteed by the standard, though most implementations honor it on the same channel).

### 2.3.4 Non-Blocking Communication — `MPI_Isend` / `MPI_Irecv`

Non-blocking routines **return immediately**, before the operation has completed. The user gets back a **request handle** that can later be tested (`MPI_Test`) or waited upon (`MPI_Wait`).

```c
int MPI_Isend(const void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
              int dest, int tag, MPI_Comm comm, MPI_Request *request);

int MPI_Wait(MPI_Request *request, MPI_Status *status);
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use non-blocking?** It enables **overlap of computation and communication (latency hiding)**. While the network card is sending message A, the CPU can be computing message B. This is the single most important optimization technique in MPI.

## 2.4 Collective Communication

Collective operations involve **all processes in a communicator simultaneously**. They are typically **more efficient** than equivalent point-to-point code because the MPI implementation can use optimized algorithms (tree, ring, butterfly).

| Routine | Description | Direction |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| `MPI_Bcast` | One process sends same data to all others | 1 → N |
| `MPI_Scatter` | One process sends different chunks to all | 1 → N (split) |
| `MPI_Gather` | All processes send different chunks to one | N → 1 (combine) |
| `MPI_Allgather` | All-to-all gather; everyone gets everything | N → N |
| `MPI_Reduce` | All send data; one process gets the reduction | N → 1 |
| `MPI_Allreduce` | All send data; everyone gets the reduction | N → N |
| `MPI_Alltoall` | Each process sends a distinct chunk to every other | N → N |
| `MPI_Barrier` | Synchronization — no process proceeds until all reach the barrier | N → N |
| `MPI_Scan` | Prefix reduction | N → N |

## 2.5 The Cost Model for MPI Communication

To analyze MPI performance, the **LogP / LogGP** model is used. The simplest model is the **linear bandwidth-latency model**:

$$
T_{comm}(n, m) = \alpha + \beta \cdot m
$$

Where:

- $T_{comm}(n, m)$ = total time to send a message of size $m$ bytes
- $\alpha$ = **latency** (seconds) — fixed startup cost of a message, independent of size
- $\beta$ = **inverse bandwidth** (seconds/byte) — time per byte of payload
- $n$ = number of processes involved
- $m$ = message size in bytes

For **collective operations**, an extra term for the **network tree height** is added:

$$
T_{collective}(p, m) = \alpha \cdot \log_2(p) + \beta \cdot m
$$

This logarithmic factor is why collective operations scale **sub-linearly** with the number of processes.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **core equations and constants** you must memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on this topic.

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency-bandwidth model | $T = \alpha + \beta \cdot m$ | seconds | $\alpha$ = latency, $\beta$ = 1/bandwidth, $m$ = bytes |
| Collective time | $T_{coll} = \alpha \log_2 p + \beta m$ | seconds | $p$ = number of processes |
| Speedup | $S(p) = T_s / T_p$ | dimensionless | $T_s$ = serial, $T_p$ = parallel |
| Efficiency | $E(p) = S(p) / p$ | dimensionless | Ideal = 1.0 |
| Amdahl's Law | $S(p) = 1 / ((1-f) + f/p)$ | dimensionless | $f$ = parallel fraction |
| Total comm. cost | $T_{total} = T_{comp} + T_{comm}$ | seconds | Additive model |
| Rank range | $[0, p-1]$ | integer | Always contiguous, starting at 0 |
| Message overhead | $O_{msg} = \alpha$ | seconds | Dominates small messages |
| Bandwidth-dominated | $T \approx \beta m$ when $m \gg \alpha/\beta$ | seconds | Large messages regime |
| MPI_TAG_UB | Implementation-defined, **≥ 32767** | integer | Max tag value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard MPI Datatypes to Memorize:** `MPI_INT`, `MPI_FLOAT`, `MPI_DOUBLE`, `MPI_CHAR`, `MPI_LONG`, `MPI_BYTE` (raw bytes), `MPI_PACKED` (heterogeneous). Derived types like `MPI_VECTOR` and `MPI_TYPE_CREATE_SUBARRAY` allow non-contiguous data communication.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Weather & Climate Modeling:** WRF, CESM, GFS — all use MPI on the world's top supercomputers.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM use MPI for parallel domain decomposition.
- **Genomic Sequencing:** Bowtie, BWA use MPI to parallelize alignment across thousands of CPU cores.
- **Astrophysics Simulations:** N-body codes like GADGET and cosmological hydrodynamics codes rely on MPI for trillion-particle simulations.
- **Machine Learning:** Distributed training of large neural networks (Horovod, DeepSpeed) is built on MPI's `Allreduce` primitive.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation — Why Collective Operations Are Faster Than Point-to-Point

Consider broadcasting a message of size $m$ from process 0 to $p$ processes.

### Naive point-to-point approach:
Process 0 sends individually to each of the $p-1$ other processes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{naive}(p, m) &= (p - 1) \cdot (\alpha + \beta m) \\
&= (p-1)\alpha + (p-1)\beta m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Multiplication expansion:** Each of the $p-1$ sends incurs the same latency $\alpha$ and per-byte cost $\beta m$, since all run sequentially on process 0.

**Step 2 — Asymptotic growth:** As $p \to \infty$, $T_{naive}$ grows **linearly in $p$**.

### Optimized collective (binary tree) broadcast:
Process 0 sends to process 1; they both send to 2 and 3; and so on. The number of "steps" in the tree is $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$. At each step, $p/2$ messages of size $m$ are sent in parallel across the network.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{tree}(p, m) &= \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot (\alpha + \beta m) \\
&\approx (\log_2 p) \cdot \alpha + (\log_2 p) \cdot \beta m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare growth rates:** The tree approach scales **logarithmically** in $p$. For $p = 1024$ processes, the tree version is roughly **10× faster** in latency (since $1024 / 10 \approx 100$).

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** This logarithmic scaling is *exactly* why collective operations are mandatory in production HPC codes.

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{T_{naive}}{T_{tree}} = \frac{p - 1}{\log_2 p} \;\longrightarrow\; \infty \text{ as } p \to \infty\;}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Cost of a Broadcast

A cluster has $\alpha = 5 \, \mu s$ and bandwidth $B = 10 \, GB/s$ (so $\beta = 0.1 \, ns/byte$). We need to broadcast a $1 \, MB$ message to $p = 64$ processes.

**Given:**

$$
\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}, \quad \beta = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^9} = 1 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s/byte}, \quad m = 10^6 \text{ bytes}, \quad p = 64
$$

**Step 1 — Naive cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{naive} &= (p-1)(\alpha + \beta m) \\
&= 63 \times (5 \times 10^{-6} + 10^{-10} \times 10^6) \\
&= 63 \times (5 \times 10^{-6} + 10^{-4}) \\
&= 63 \times (1.05 \times 10^{-4}) \\
&= 6.615 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 6.62 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Tree collective cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{tree} &= \log_2(p) \cdot (\alpha + \beta m) \\
&= 6 \times (5 \times 10^{-6} + 10^{-4}) \\
&= 6 \times (1.05 \times 10^{-4}) \\
&= 6.3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\
&\approx 0.63 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup achieved:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{naive}}{T_{tree}} = \frac{6.62}{0.63} \approx 10.5 \times
$$

This is precisely the ratio $p / \log_2 p = 64 / 6 \approx 10.67$, confirming the theoretical bound.

## 3.3 Deadlock Analysis — The Classic MPI Trap

Consider the "evil exchange" code:

```c
if (rank == 0) {
    MPI_Send(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
    MPI_Recv(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
} else if (rank == 1) {
    MPI_Recv(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
    MPI_Send(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
}
```

**Analysis:**
- Rank 0 calls `MPI_Send` first. Since the receive on rank 1 hasn't been posted yet, MPI may **buffer the message** (eager protocol, small message) or **block** until the receive is posted (rendezvous protocol, large message).
- If both messages are large and the system uses **rendezvous protocol**, both processes block on the send **before** the receive is reached → **deadlock**.
- If messages are small and the system uses **eager protocol with sufficient buffer**, both sends complete into system buffers, then both receives succeed → **no deadlock**, but this is **non-portable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always swap the order to be **safe and portable**: do `Recv` before `Send` on both sides, **or** use `MPI_Sendrecv` (a built-in deadlock-free primitive that combines a send and a receive of *different* messages into a single atomic call).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Communication Pattern Selection

The following table helps you choose the correct MPI primitive given a problem scenario. This is a **board-exam favorite** type of question.

| Scenario | Best MPI Routine | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Root process distributes rows of a matrix to all workers | `MPI_Scatter` | Sends *different* chunks to each process |
| All processes need the same parameter from root | `MPI_Bcast` | Sends the *same* data to all |
| Each process computes a partial sum; root needs total | `MPI_Reduce` (with `MPI_SUM`) | Combines values into one result |
| Every process needs the total sum | `MPI_Allreduce` | Reduces and broadcasts result to all |
| Each process sends its data to every other process | `MPI_Alltoall` | All-to-all personalized exchange |
| A process has to collect unequal-length data from workers | `MPI_Gatherv` | Variable-count gather |
| Sum of prefix values across ranks (e.g., for load balancing) | `MPI_Scan` | Inclusive prefix reduction |

## 3.5 Computation of Effective Bandwidth for a Collective

For a broadcast of message size $m$ to $p$ processes using a tree algorithm, the **effective bandwidth** is:

$$
B_{eff} = \frac{(p-1) \cdot m}{T_{tree}} = \frac{(p-1) \cdot m}{\log_2(p) \cdot (\alpha + \beta m)}
$$

In the **bandwidth-dominated regime** ($m \gg \alpha/\beta$):

$$
B_{eff} \approx \frac{(p-1)}{\log_2(p)} \cdot \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{p-1}{\log_2 p} \cdot B
$$

This shows that the effective bandwidth **grows with $p$** (good!) but **only logarithmically** (the bottleneck of bisection bandwidth of the network).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MPI Program Lifecycle (State Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start argc argv] --> B[MPI_Init]
    B --> C[MPI_Comm_size get total p]
    C --> D[MPI_Comm_rank get my rank]
    D --> E{Is rank 0?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Master Work Bcast Send]
    E -->|No| G[Worker Work Recv Reduce]
    F --> H[Possible MPI_Barrier]
    G --> H[Possible MPI_Barrier]
    H --> I[Final Computation Gather]
    I --> J[MPI_Finalize]
    J --> K[Program End]
```

## 4.2 Point-to-Point Communication Flow (Send / Receive Pairing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Process0
        P0buf[Send Buffer]
        P0send[MPI_Send]
    end
    subgraph Network
        NW[Interconnect Infiniband Ethernet]
    end
    subgraph Process1
        P1recv[MPI_Recv]
        P1buf[Receive Buffer]
    end
    P0buf --> P0send
    P0send -->|"tag=5 dest=1"| NW
    NW -->|"source=0 tag=5"| P1recv
    P1recv --> P1buf
```

## 4.3 Broadcast — Linear vs Tree (Architectural Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LinearNaive["Naive Linear O of p"]
        L0[Process 0] -->|step 1| L1[Process 1]
        L0 -->|step 2| L2[Process 2]
        L0 -->|step 3| L3[Process 3]
        L0 -->|step 4| L4[Process 4]
    end
    subgraph TreeOpt["Optimized Tree O log p"]
        T0[Process 0] -->|step 1| T1[Process 1]
        T0 -->|step 1| T2[Process 2]
        T1 -->|step 2| T3[Process 3]
        T1 -->|step 2| T4[Process 4]
        T2 -->|step 2| T5[Process 5]
        T2 -->|step 2| T6[Process 6]
    end
```

## 4.4 MPI_Allreduce Pattern (Master-Worker Aggregation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Workers
        W0[Rank 0 val=3]
        W1[Rank 1 val=7]
        W2[Rank 2 val=2]
        W3[Rank 3 val=5]
    end
    W0 -->|sum| AR[MPI Allreduce MPI_SUM]
    W1 -->|sum| AR
    W2 -->|sum| AR
    W3 -->|sum| AR
    AR -->|total=17| R0[Rank 0 gets 17]
    AR -->|total=17| R1[Rank 1 gets 17]
    AR -->|total=17| R2[Rank 2 gets 17]
    AR -->|total=17| R3[Rank 3 gets 17]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP1[Application A] --> CW1[MPI COMM WORLD 8 procs]
    APP2[Library B running inside] --> CS1[MPI COMM SELF 1 proc]
    APP2 --> CS2[MPI COMM LIB 4 procs]
    CW1 --> CS1
    CW1 --> CS2
```

