# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the role of a communicator in MPI. What is the default communicator and what does `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` signify?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **communicator** in MPI is a named collection of processes that can communicate with each other. It defines the *universe* of processes that recognize one another for send/receive and collective operations. The **default communicator** is `MPI_COMM_WORLD`, which includes all processes launched by `mpirun`. Each process has a unique **rank** within the communicator ranging from **0 to p−1**.

The special constant `MPI_ANY_SOURCE` used in `MPI_Recv` allows a process to accept a message from **any sender** rather than specifying a particular rank. This is essential for writing flexible, server-style code where the order of incoming messages is not predetermined. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between blocking and non-blocking MPI communication. State one advantage of non-blocking communication.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Blocking communication** (`MPI_Send`/`MPI_Recv`) does not return control to the calling program until the operation is **logically complete** (i.e., the buffer is safe to reuse for send, or the message has been received for receive).
- **Non-blocking communication** (`MPI_Isend`/`MPI_Irecv`) returns **immediately** with a request handle, allowing the program to perform other work in parallel with the communication.

**Advantage:** Non-blocking communication enables **overlap of computation and communication** (latency hiding), which is critical for achieving high performance on modern parallel systems. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice is mandatory.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **six standard stages of an MPI program**. Write the function signatures of `MPI_Init`, `MPI_Comm_size`, `MPI_Comm_rank`, and `MPI_Finalize` in C, and describe the role of `MPI_Status`.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **`MPI_Bcast` and `MPI_Reduce`**. Show how a binary-tree broadcast achieves a time of approximately $T = \log_2 p \cdot (\alpha + \beta m)$ for $p$ processes and a message of size $m$.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

The **lifecycle of any MPI program** consists of six stages:

1. **Include the MPI header** `#include <mpi.h>`.
2. **Initialize MPI** — Call `MPI_Init(&argc, &argv)`. This must be the **first MPI call** in the program. It sets up the MPI runtime environment.
3. **Determine the number of processes** in a communicator using `MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size)`. The variable `size` is an integer holding the total process count $p$.
4. **Determine the rank (ID)** of the calling process using `MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank)`. Ranks run from **0 to p−1**.
5. **Perform parallel work** — Communication and computation. Usually contains a conditional `if (rank == 0) { ... master ... } else { ... worker ... }` block.
6. **Terminate MPI** — Call `MPI_Finalize()`. **No MPI calls are allowed after this point.**

**Function Signatures (C language):**

```c
int MPI_Init(int *argc, char ***argv);
int MPI_Comm_size(MPI_Comm comm, int *size);
int MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_Comm comm, int *rank);
int MPI_Finalize(void);
```

**Role of `MPI_Status`:** It is a structure filled in by MPI upon completion of a receive. It contains at least three fields: `MPI_SOURCE` (actual sender rank), `MPI_TAG` (actual tag), and `MPI_ERROR`. The macro `MPI_Get_count` can extract the number of elements received. **`MPI_Status` is essential when wildcards (`MPI_ANY_SOURCE`, `MPI_ANY_TAG`) are used**, because the program otherwise would not know who sent the message. **(7 marks — 1 for header, 2 for lifecycle, 2 for signatures, 2 for Status)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**`MPI_Bcast`** (broadcast) sends the same data from one designated "root" process to **all other processes** in the communicator. After the call, every process holds an identical copy of the data.

**`MPI_Reduce`** combines data from all processes using a reduction operation (e.g., `MPI_SUM`, `MPI_MAX`, `MPI_MIN`) and places the final result on a single root process. Other processes receive nothing.

**Binary-Tree Broadcast Analysis:**

In a naive linear broadcast, the root sends sequentially to each of the $p-1$ other processes. This requires $p-1$ sequential message steps.

$$
T_{linear} = (p-1)(\alpha + \beta m)
$$

In a **binary-tree broadcast**, the data propagates through a balanced tree of depth $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$. At step $k$, $2^{k-1}$ pairs of processes exchange the data in parallel.

```
        Root (rank 0)                   Step 1: 0 -> 1
        /         \                     Step 2: 0 -> 2, 1 -> 3
       1           2                    Step 3: 0 -> 4, 1 -> 5, 2 -> 6, 3 -> 7
      / \         / \
     3   4       5   6
    /
   7
```

Since the network can transmit in parallel, all sends at a given tree level overlap. Each level takes exactly one message time $\alpha + \beta m$. With $\log_2 p$ levels:

$$
T_{tree} = \log_2 p \cdot (\alpha + \beta m)
$$

The **speedup** over linear broadcast is:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{(p-1)(\alpha + \beta m)}{\log_2 p \cdot (\alpha + \beta m)} = \frac{p-1}{\log_2 p}
$$

For $p = 1024$, this is approximately **100× faster**, illustrating the enormous scalability benefit of collective primitives. **(7 marks — 2 for collective description, 3 for derivation, 2 for diagram/numerical example)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **point-to-point communication** primitives in MPI. Explain message matching rules and the use of tags with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of a **deadlock** in MPI programs. Demonstrate with code how to cause a deadlock between two processes, and show how the same problem can be solved using `MPI_Sendrecv`.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

Point-to-point communication involves **one sender and one receiver**. The two fundamental routines are:

**`MPI_Send(buf, count, datatype, dest, tag, comm)`** — Sends `count` elements of `datatype` starting at address `buf` to the process with rank `dest` in communicator `comm`, labeling the message with integer `tag`.

**`MPI_Recv(buf, count, datatype, source, tag, comm, &status)`** — Receives up to `count` elements into the buffer at `buf`. The `status` structure is filled with information about the actual sender, tag, and count.

**Message Matching Rules:** A send matches a receive **if and only if**:

1. They are in the **same communicator**.
2. `dest` of the send = `source` of the receive (or `MPI_ANY_SOURCE`).
3. `tag` of the send = `tag` of the receive (or `MPI_ANY_TAG`).

**Example with tags:**

```c
// Process 0
MPI_Send(&data, 100, MPI_DOUBLE, 1, 5, MPI_COMM_WORLD);

// Process 1 - receives only messages tagged 5
MPI_Recv(&data, 100, MPI_DOUBLE, 0, 5, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
```

Tags allow a process to **distinguish multiple message streams** between the same pair of processes. For example, in a client-server model, the server could use tag 1 for "request" messages and tag 2 for "response" messages. **(7 marks — 2 for primitives, 3 for matching rules, 2 for tag example)**

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

A **deadlock** in MPI occurs when two or more processes are **permanently waiting** for each other to perform an action that will never occur. The most common cause is the **circular wait** pattern in point-to-point communication.

**Deadlock-Prone Code:**

```c
if (rank == 0) {
    MPI_Send(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);  // Blocks
    MPI_Recv(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
} else if (rank == 1) {
    MPI_Send(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);  // Blocks
    MPI_Recv(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
}
```

Both processes call `MPI_Send` first. If the system uses the **rendezvous protocol** (which it does for large messages), each process blocks waiting for the other to post the matching receive. The system hangs forever.

**Solution 1 — Swap the order:**

```c
if (rank == 0) {
    MPI_Recv(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
    MPI_Send(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
} else if (rank == 1) {
    MPI_Recv(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
    MPI_Send(buf, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
}
```

Now rank 0 receives first, allowing rank 1's send to complete, and vice versa.

**Solution 2 — Use `MPI_Sendrecv` (the recommended portable approach):**

```c
MPI_Sendrecv(sendbuf, 1, MPI_INT, 1-other_rank, 0,
             recvbuf, 1, MPI_INT, 1-other_rank, 0,
             MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
```

`MPI_Sendrecv` is **deadlock-free by design** because MPI internally posts the receive before the send. It is the most robust solution for pairwise data exchange. **(7 marks — 2 for deadlock concept, 2 for bad code, 3 for solutions)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "MPI is a library" with "MPI is a specification."** (Lose 1 mark per occurrence.) Always state that MPI is a *standard* with multiple implementations (MPICH, OpenMPI).
> 2. **Writing `MPI_Init()` without arguments.** The standard signature in C is `MPI_Init(&argc, &argv)`. Bare calls work in Fortran but lose marks in C-based questions.
> 3. **Assuming `MPI_Send` is asynchronous.** The blocking send returns only when the *buffer is safe to reuse* — not when the message is delivered.
> 4. **Forgetting that after `MPI_Finalize()` no MPI calls are valid.** Examiners specifically look for this.
> 5. **In collective operations, omitting the communicator argument.** All collective routines require `MPI_Comm comm` as the last argument.
> 6. **Using `MPI_COMM_WORLD` outside its scope.** User-defined communicators do not include `MPI_COMM_WORLD` automatically.
> 7. **Believing `MPI_Recv` can "match a tag of 0 with a send of tag 5."** Tags must match exactly, except for the wildcard `MPI_ANY_TAG`.
> 8. **Confusing `MPI_Comm_size` (total processes) with `MPI_Comm_rank` (my ID).** This is a classic 1-mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: MPI**

- ✅ **MPI is a specification, not a library.** Implementations: MPICH, OpenMPI, Intel MPI, MVAPICH2.
- ✅ **Six-stage lifecycle:** Include header → `MPI_Init` → `MPI_Comm_size` → `MPI_Comm_rank` → Work → `MPI_Finalize`.
- ✅ **`MPI_COMM_WORLD`** is the default communicator. Ranks range from **0 to p−1**.
- ✅ **Point-to-point:** `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`, `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`, `MPI_Wait`, `MPI_Test`.
- ✅ **Matching rule:** Same communicator + matching source/dest + matching tag.
- ✅ **Wildcards:** `MPI_ANY_SOURCE`, `MPI_ANY_TAG`, `MPI_Status`.
- ✅ **Collective operations** are *always* on all processes of a communicator — there is no partial collective.
- ✅ **`MPI_Bcast` = 1→N (same data); `MPI_Scatter` = 1→N (chunks); `MPI_Gather` = N→1; `MPI_Allreduce` = N→N.**
- ✅ **Deadlock occurs** in the "evil exchange" pattern. Use `MPI_Sendrecv` to avoid it.
- ✅ **Non-blocking + Wait** = latency hiding, the key to performance.
- ✅ **Latency-bandwidth model:** $T = \alpha + \beta m$ for one message, $T = \alpha \log_2 p + \beta m$ for collectives.
- ✅ **`MPI_Barrier`** synchronizes all processes — used for timing and correctness.
- ✅ **Derived datatypes** (`MPI_Type_vector`, `MPI_Type_create_subarray`) allow sending non-contiguous data in one call.
- ✅ **Communicator splitting** via `MPI_Comm_split` enables sub-group communication and library isolation.
- ✅ **Process topologies** (`MPI_Cart_create`, `MPI_Graph_create`) map processes to physical/abstract grids for locality-aware algorithms.
- ✅ **MPI-2 features:** One-sided communication (`MPI_Put`/`MPI_Get`), dynamic process management (`MPI_Comm_spawn`), parallel I/O.
- ✅ **MPI-3 features:** Non-blocking collectives, improved one-sided RMA, MPI_T for tools, MPI_Comm_create_from_contract.
- ✅ **MPI-4 (2021 standard)** adds partitioned communication, persistent collectives, large-count support, and improved GPU awareness.
- ✅ **Compilation:** `mpicc program.c -o program` → **Run:** `mpirun -np 4 ./program`.
- ✅ **SPMD** is the dominant MPI programming model.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overlapping Communication with Computation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overlapping Communication with Computation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Overlapping Communication with Computation** is an optimization technique in parallel and distributed programming in which a process simultaneously performs data transfer (communication) across the network and executes its local arithmetic, logical, or memory-bound tasks (computation). The objective is to **hide communication latency** by ensuring that the processor is never idle while waiting for a message to be sent or received, thereby improving the overall parallel efficiency.

In MPI terminology, this technique fundamentally relies on **non-blocking communication primitives** such as `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`, `MPI_Issend`, `MPI_Ibsend`, and `MPI_Irsend`, complemented by local completion calls like `MPI_Wait`, `MPI_Waitany`, `MPI_Waitall`, `MPI_Waitsome`, or the non-blocking test `MPI_Test`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The core idea is to *decouple the issue of communication from the completion of communication*. A process issues a non-blocking send/receive, returns immediately, continues with independent local work, and only synchronizes locally when the result of the communication is actually needed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chef in a kitchen** preparing a multi-course meal for a banquet:

| Without Overlap (Sequential) | With Overlap (Concurrent) |
|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Chef places a cake in the oven, then **stands idle**, staring at the oven door for 45 minutes, and only starts chopping vegetables *after* the cake is done. | Chef places the cake in the oven, sets a **local timer** (analogous to `MPI_Wait` / `MPI_Test`), and immediately begins chopping vegetables, kneading dough, and plating garnishes. The cake's progress is checked only when needed. |

In this analogy:
- **Oven baking** $\rightarrow$ Message travelling over the network (communication)
- **Chef chopping vegetables** $\rightarrow$ CPU doing local arithmetic (computation)
- **Local timer (in chef's pocket, not at the oven)** $\rightarrow$ Local completion check; the chef is *not* blocked at the oven (no global synchronization at the remote node).

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Latency Hiding:** Concealing the time spent in communication behind useful work.
- **Non-Blocking Communication:** A communication call that returns *immediately*, without waiting for the message to be physically delivered.
- **Local Completion:** The point at which the local buffer may be safely reused or the local variable read. This is *not* the same as the message having arrived at the remote process.
- **Communication Buffer (System-managed):** The runtime may use a temporary buffer to allow `MPI_Isend` to return instantly. This is implementation-defined and must **not** be relied upon by the programmer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Distinction:**
> - *Local completion* = "The buffer in my address space is safe to overwrite."
> - *Remote completion* = "The destination process has actually received the message."
>
> Overlapping exploits *local completion* to make progress on computation while the network hardware does its job in the background.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanics of Overlapping — Structured Logic

The successful overlap of communication and computation follows a strict **three-phase protocol** on each participating process:

1. **Issue Phase (Non-Blocking Call)**
   - The process invokes `MPI_Isend` or `MPI_Irecv`.
   - The MPI runtime marks the request as *active* and returns a `MPI_Request` handle.
   - The call returns **immediately** (in $O(1)$ user-visible time), regardless of network state.

2. **Compute Phase (Local Work)**
   - The process executes instructions that are **independent** of the data being communicated.
   - The MPI runtime, often in a dedicated **progress thread** or via hardware-driven DMA, transfers bytes in the background.

3. **Local Completion Phase (Wait/Test)**
   - When the data is *actually required*, the process calls `MPI_Wait` (blocking local) or `MPI_Test` (non-blocking local) on the request handle.
   - `MPI_Wait` returns only when local completion has occurred.
   - After `MPI_Wait`, the receive buffer is safe to read, or the send buffer is safe to overwrite.

### 2.2 Blocking vs. Non-Blocking: A Comparative Analysis

| Property | Blocking (`MPI_Send`) | Non-Blocking (`MPI_Isend`) |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Return time | After *local + remote* completion | After *issue only* (immediate) |
| Buffer safety on return | Guaranteed | Deferred until `MPI_Wait` |
| Allows overlap | **No** | **Yes** |
| Deadlock risk | Higher (depends on buffer/sync) | Lower (progress is deferred) |
| Useful for overlap | $\times$ | $\checkmark$ |
| Performance ceiling | Upper-bounded by sequential comm+comp | Approaches `max(T_comm, T_comp)` |

### 2.3 The Golden Rule of Overlapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Buffer-Reuse Invariant):**
> A user buffer associated with an active non-blocking communication **must not be read or written** by the issuing process between the issue call and the matching `MPI_Wait`/`MPI_Test` returning successfully.
> Violating this invariant produces **undefined behaviour** in MPI.

This is the *only* hard rule. Outside the buffer region, the process is free to do anything else.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below uses `\mid` for absolute-value separators to comply with the markdown-table integrity safeguard.

| Formula | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---------|---------|--------------------|
| $T_{\text{seq}} \;=\; T_{\text{comp}} \;+\; T_{\text{comm}}$ | Sequential time (no overlap) | Baseline cost model |
| $T_{\text{overlap}} \;=\; \max\!\left(T_{\text{comp}},\,T_{\text{comm}}\right)$ | Time with ideal overlap | Theoretical lower bound |
| $S_{\text{overlap}} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}}{\max(T_{\text{comp}},\,T_{\text{comm}})}$ | Speedup from overlap | Quantifies benefit |
| $T_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \max\!\left(T_{\text{comp}},\,T_{\text{comm}}\right) \;+\; T_{\text{sync}}$ | Realistic overlap including local wait | Practical model |
| $E \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}}}{T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}}$ | Fraction of time spent computing | Efficiency diagnostic |
| $\text{Amdahl: } S_{\text{par}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{(1-E) + \dfrac{E}{N}}$ | Parallel speedup with $N$ processors | Sets upper-bound ceiling |
| $T_{\text{latency}} \;=\; T_{\text{startup}} \;+\; (n \,/\, B)$ | $\alpha$–$\beta$ network model: $n$ bytes at bandwidth $B$ | Predicts comm time |
| $\text{Overlap Ratio} \;=\; \dfrac{\min(T_{\text{comp}},\,T_{\text{comm}})}{\max(T_{\text{comp}},\,T_{\text{comm}})}$ | Quality metric $\in [0,1]$ | Measures effectiveness |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** Stencil computations, particle-in-cell (PIC) codes, and CFD solvers overlap halo-exchange with interior updates.
- **Deep Learning:** AllReduce of gradient tensors is overlapped with the backward pass on the next layer (PyTorch DDP, Horovod).
- **GPU Programming:** CUDA asynchronous memory copies (`cudaMemcpyAsync`) overlap host–device transfers with kernel execution.
- **Distributed Databases:** Replication of a write-ahead log is overlapped with transaction commit processing.
- **Edge/IoT Clusters:** Sensor data uplink is overlapped with on-device inference.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal-Overlap Time Bound

We derive the theoretical minimum runtime when a process performs a single compute task and a single communicate task concurrently.

**Step 1 — Define the sequential baseline.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{seq}} &\;\triangleq\; T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Without overlap, the process must finish all computation before (or after) starting communication, since blocking primitives serialize the two. The total wall-clock time is therefore the sum.

---

**Step 2 — Define the parallel, overlapping execution.**

After `MPI_Isend`, the processor switches to compute. Let the compute task start at $t = 0$ and finish at $t = T_{\text{comp}}$. Let the communication finish at $t = T_{\text{comm}}$. Both activities execute simultaneously on independent hardware resources (CPU cores vs. network DMA / progress engine).

---

**Step 3 — Identify the bottleneck (critical path).**

The process cannot complete its overall work until **both** the computation and the communication have reached local completion. Hence the total elapsed time is governed by whichever finishes *last*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{overlap}}^{\text{ideal}} &\;=\; \max\!\left(T_{\text{comp}},\,T_{\text{comm}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The "max" function captures the longest path through the dependency DAG. There is no further parallelism to extract from a single (comp, comm) pair.

---

**Step 4 — Compute the speedup offered by overlapping.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{overlap}} &\;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{overlap}}^{\text{ideal}}} \\[6pt]
&\;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}}{\max(T_{\text{comp}},\,T_{\text{comm}})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Speedup is the ratio of baseline time to optimized time, by definition.

---

**Step 5 — Specialize for the symmetric case $T_{\text{comp}} = T_{\text{comm}} = T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{overlap}} \big\vert_{T_{\text{comp}}=T_{\text{comm}}} &\;=\; \dfrac{T + T}{\max(T,T)} \\[4pt]
&\;=\; \dfrac{2T}{T} \;=\; 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Perfect balance yields a $2\times$ speedup — a commonly cited upper-bound figure in textbooks and exam answer keys.

---

**Step 6 — Specialize for the compute-bound case $T_{\text{comp}} \gg T_{\text{comm}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{overlap}} \big\vert_{T_{\text{comp}} \gg T_{\text{comm}}} &\;\approx\; \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{comp}}} \\[4pt]
&\;=\; 1 + \dfrac{T_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{comp}}} \;\approx\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** If computation dwarfs communication, hiding it yields negligible benefit. Overlap is a *communication-bound* remedy; it cannot accelerate a CPU-bound program.

---

**Step 7 — Specialize for the communication-bound case $T_{\text{comm}} \gg T_{\text{comp}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{overlap}} \big\vert_{T_{\text{comm}} \gg T_{\text{comp}}} &\;\approx\; \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{comm}}} \\[4pt]
&\;=\; 1 + \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}}}{T_{\text{comm}}} \;\approx\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** When the network is the bottleneck, hiding *computation* behind communication is similarly ineffective. The remedy there is **bandwidth optimization**, not overlap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** Overlap is **most beneficial** when $T_{\text{comp}} \approx T_{\text{comm}}$, and useless at either extreme. KTU examiners frequently test this balance condition.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When Does Overlap Help?

| Regime | Condition | Overlap Benefit | Recommended Strategy |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Balanced | $T_{\text{comp}} \approx T_{\text{comm}}$ | **High** ($\le 2\times$) | Use `MPI_Isend` / `MPI_Irecv` |
| Compute-bound | $T_{\text{comp}} \gg T_{\text{comm}}$ | Negligible | Profile computation, vectorize, parallelize |
| Communication-bound | $T_{\text{comm}} \gg T_{\text{comp}}$ | Negligible | Compress messages, coalesce, use topology |
| Synchronization-bound | Frequent `MPI_Barrier` | Negative | Replace barriers with local completion |
| Dependent data | Data needed immediately | **Zero / unsafe** | Refactor dependency; introduce prepost |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A parallel process spends $T_{\text{comp}} = 80\,\text{ms}$ in computation and $T_{\text{comm}} = 20\,\text{ms}$ in network communication. Calculate (a) the sequential time, (b) the ideal overlapping time, and (c) the speedup from overlap.

**(a) Sequential time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{seq}} &\;=\; T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}} \\[4pt]
&\;=\; 80 + 20 \;=\; 100\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Ideal overlapping time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{overlap}} &\;=\; \max(80,\, 20) \;=\; 80\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Speedup from overlap:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{overlap}} &\;=\; \dfrac{100}{80} \;=\; 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Overlap saves the $20\,\text{ms}$ of communication entirely, yielding a $25\%$ improvement. This case is compute-bound, so the theoretical ceiling is well below $2\times$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Sequential vs. Overlap)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SequentialBlock["SEQUENTIAL EXECUTION - NO OVERLAP"]
        A1[Start] --> B1[Computation Tcomp]
        B1 --> C1[Communication Tcomm]
        C1 --> D1[Local Wait MPI Wait]
        D1 --> E1[End]
    end

    subgraph OverlapBlock["OVERLAPPED EXECUTION - NON-BLOCKING"]
        A2[Start] --> B2[Issue Non Blocking MPI Isend Irecv]
        B2 --> C2[Compute Independently]
        B2 -.background.-> D2[Network DMA Engine Transfers Data]
        C2 --> E2[Local Wait MPI Wait only when needed]
        D2 --> E2
        E2 --> F2[End]
    end

    classDef compute fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef comm fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#803300,color:#000
    classDef wait fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5e2e,color:#000
    classDef startend fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#4a2a5e,color:#000

    class A1,E1,A2,F2 startend
    class B1,C2 compute
    class C1,D2 comm
    class D1,E2 wait
```

### 4.2 Timing Diagram — Critical Path Visualization

```mermaid
gantt
    title Timeline Comparison: Sequential vs Overlapped Execution
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s

    section Sequential Path
    Compute Task   :a1, 0, 80
    Communicate    :a2, after a1, 20
    Local Wait     :a3, after a2, 0

    section Overlapped Path
    MPI Isend Issue :b1, 0, 2
    Network Transfer :b2, 0, 20
    Compute Task     :b3, 2, 80
    Local Wait MPI Wait :b4, after b3, 0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Sequential Path* shows the compute bar followed by the communicate bar, totalling $100\,\text{ms}$. The *Overlapped Path* shows the network transfer (top bar) running concurrently with the compute bar; the critical path is now $80\,\text{ms}$ (the longer of the two), with only a $2\,\text{ms}$ issuing overhead.

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing Blocking vs Non-Blocking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is data needed immediately after send/recv?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Use Blocking MPI Send Recv]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does independent local work exist?}
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Use Blocking - overlap impossible]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is buffer reusable before wait?}
    Q3 -->|No| R3[Use Blocking or Persistent]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R4[Use Non Blocking MPI Isend Irecv and MPI Wait]

    classDef query fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef recommend fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5e2e,color:#000
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 query
    class R1,R2,R3,R4 recommend
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define overlapping communication with computation. Why is it important in parallel programming?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Overlapping communication with computation is the technique of issuing a non-blocking communication operation (such as `MPI_Isend` / `MPI_Irecv`) and continuing with local, independent computation while the message transfer proceeds in the background on the network. The process calls a local completion primitive (`MPI_Wait` or `MPI_Test`) only when the data is actually required.

It is important because:

1. It hides **network latency**, allowing the CPU to remain productive instead of idling.
2. It reduces the critical path of a parallel program, often approaching the ideal bound of $\max(T_{\text{comp}}, T_{\text{comm}})$.
3. It improves overall parallel efficiency and scalability on distributed-memory systems.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three importance points.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2. Distinguish between local completion and remote completion in MPI non-blocking communication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Local Completion | Remote Completion |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| Definition | The MPI runtime has finished its local bookkeeping on the buffer; the buffer may be reused. | The message has been physically delivered to and processed by the destination process. |
| Synchronization call | `MPI_Wait`, `MPI_Waitall`, `MPI_Test` | Implicit, only guaranteed via matching `MPI_Recv` |
| Visibility | Local to the issuing process | Global across the network |
| Role in overlap | Enables continuation of local compute | Not required for overlap |

Local completion is what permits computation to safely continue after a non-blocking call. Remote completion is irrelevant to the overlap mechanism.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark per aspect row, plus 1 mark for the concluding sentence.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, ESE Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain with a suitable block diagram how communication and computation can be overlapped in MPI. Describe the role of `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`, and `MPI_Wait` in this technique. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel application spends $T_{\text{comp}} = 120\,\text{ms}$ on computation and $T_{\text{comm}} = 30\,\text{ms}$ on communication per iteration. Calculate (i) the sequential time, (ii) the ideal overlapping time, and (iii) the percentage improvement achieved by overlapping. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Issue Phase:** The process calls `MPI_Irecv(buf, count, dtype, src, tag, comm, &req)`. This returns immediately with a request handle `req`. The buffer `buf` is **not yet safe to read**; the runtime is preparing to receive.

**Step 2 — Compute Phase:** The process executes local instructions — for example, updating interior grid points that do not depend on `buf`. The MPI runtime, possibly via a progress thread or RDMA, performs the actual transfer.

**Step 3 — Completion Phase:** When `buf` is needed, the process invokes `MPI_Wait(&req, MPI_STATUS_IGNORE)`. After this, the buffer is safe.

**Block Diagram Description (reproduced from Section 4):**

```
[Issue Isend/Irecv] ----> [Compute Independently]
        |                          |
        v                          |
[Network DMA Transfer]             |
        |                          |
        +---------> [MPI_Wait] <---+
                          |
                       [Use Data]
```

##### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**(i) Sequential time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{seq}} &\;=\; T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}} \;=\; 120 + 30 \;=\; 150\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Ideal overlapping time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{overlap}} &\;=\; \max(120,\, 30) \;=\; 120\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage improvement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{Improvement} &\;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{seq}} - T_{\text{overlap}}}{T_{\text{seq}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&\;=\; \dfrac{150 - 120}{150} \times 100 \;=\; 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The case is compute-bound ($T_{\text{comp}} \gg T_{\text{comm}}$), so the achievable benefit is modest but real.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the conditions under which overlapping communication with computation gives maximum benefit. Use the speedup formula

$$
S_{\text{overlap}} \;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}}}{\max(T_{\text{comp}}, T_{\text{comm}})}
$$

to justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the buffer-reuse invariant in MPI non-blocking communication. What happens if a process modifies the send/receive buffer before `MPI_Wait` returns? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Differentiate the speedup expression with respect to the ratio $r = T_{\text{comm}}/T_{\text{comp}}$.** Without loss of generality, assume $T_{\text{comp}} \ge T_{\text{comm}}$ (the symmetric case follows by argument exchange).

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{overlap}}(r) &\;=\; \dfrac{T_{\text{comp}}(1 + r)}{T_{\text{comp}}} \;=\; 1 + r, \quad r \in [0,1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the maximum.** $S_{\text{overlap}}$ is monotonically increasing in $r$ over $[0,1]$, achieving its maximum value of $2$ at $r = 1$ (i.e., $T_{\text{comp}} = T_{\text{comm}}$).

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max S_{\text{overlap}} &\;=\; 2 \quad \text{at} \quad T_{\text{comp}} = T_{\text{comm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conditions for maximum benefit:**

1. Computation time equals communication time (perfect balance).
2. There exists substantial **independent** local work that touches neither the send nor the receive buffer.
3. The MPI implementation supports **asynchronous progress** (background DMA, progress threads, or hardware offload).
4. The network offers non-zero latency to hide — pure CPU-bound loops yield no benefit.

Outside the balanced regime, overlap gives diminishing returns; at the extremes it yields a speedup of $\approx 1$.

##### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**The Buffer-Reuse Invariant (statement):**

Between the issue of a non-blocking communication and its successful local completion, the application buffer associated with the request must be regarded as **owned by the MPI runtime**. The user process may neither read from nor write to this buffer.

**What happens on violation?**

1. **Send-buffer violation:** If the application writes to the send buffer before `MPI_Wait`, the data being transmitted may be corrupted mid-flight. The receiver sees a torn message containing a mix of old and new bytes.

2. **Receive-buffer violation:** If the application reads from the receive buffer before `MPI_Wait`, it may read stale or partial data. The runtime has not yet finished filling the buffer.

3. **MPI Standard's Verdict:** Both cases result in **undefined behaviour**. The MPI specification does not prescribe what the runtime should do; in practice the program may appear correct on one run and crash on another.

**Mitigation:** The application must either (a) use independent buffers, (b) ensure that all buffer-modifying code lies between successive completion calls, or (c) issue a *fresh* non-blocking call on the same buffer only after the previous one has been locally completed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing blocking with non-blocking:** Students often claim `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv` overlap with computation — they do **not**; they block until local+remote completion.
> 2. **Forgetting the buffer-reuse invariant:** Losing 1–2 marks in 7-mark sub-parts for failing to mention buffer safety.
> 3. **Using `|x|` in tables:** Avoid the pipe symbol in markdown tables (use $\vert$ or $\mid$); KTU digital answer sheets may mis-render the table.
> 4. **Ignoring asynchronous progress:** Simply calling `MPI_Isend` does not guarantee hardware-level overlap — the runtime must support it. Mentioning this nuance earns full marks on 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Skipping the numerical verification:** Always plug numbers into the formula and show the substitution step; KTU examiners reward the *evaluation*, not just the formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Overlap = executing compute and communicate concurrently on independent hardware resources.
- **Mechanism:** Non-blocking MPI calls (`MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`) + local completion (`MPI_Wait` / `MPI_Test`).
- **Three Phases:** Issue $\rightarrow$ Compute $\rightarrow$ Local-Complete.
- **Ideal Time:** $T_{\text{overlap}} = \max(T_{\text{comp}}, T_{\text{comm}})$.
- **Best Case:** $T_{\text{comp}} = T_{\text{comm}}$ gives $S_{\text{overlap}} = 2\times$.
- **Worst Case:** Either $T_{\text{comp}} \gg T_{\text{comm}}$ or $T_{\text{comm}} \gg T_{\text{comp}}$ yields negligible benefit.
- **Golden Rule:** Buffer associated with an active request must not be touched until `MPI_Wait`/`MPI_Test` succeeds.
- **Local vs Remote Completion:** Overlap exploits *local* completion only.
- **Performance Prerequisite:** MPI implementation must support **asynchronous progress** (DMA, offload, progress thread).
- **Alternative Constructs:** Persistent communication (`MPI_Send_init` + `MPI_Start` + `MPI_Request_free`) reuses request objects efficiently across iterations.
- **Common Error:** Assuming `MPI_Send` overlaps with computation — **it does not**.
- **Real-World Use Cases:** Halo exchange in CFD, gradient AllReduce in deep learning, async memcpy on GPUs, log replication in distributed databases.
- **Network Model:** $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{startup}} + (n / B)$ — overlap hides $T_{\text{startup}}$ but not bandwidth saturation.
- **Diagnostic Ratio:** $\text{Overlap Ratio} = \min(T_{\text{comp}}, T_{\text{comm}}) / \max(T_{\text{comp}}, T_{\text{comm}})$ — values near $1$ indicate high overlap potential.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collective Communication and Computation Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT411 - INTRODUCTION TO PARALLEL & DISTRIBUTED PROGRAMMING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Collective Communication and Computation Operations # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of the **Message Passing Interface (MPI)** standard and the broader taxonomy of distributed-memory parallel programming, a **Collective Communication Operation** is defined as a coordinated communication primitive that involves **all processes** belonging to a specified *communicator* (typically `MPI_COMM_WORLD`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A collective communication operation is a *group-wide* communication where *all* processes in a communicator participate simultaneously through a *single* function call. The call is **non-local** — the function must be invoked by every process in the group, otherwise the program will deadlock. **Collective Computation Operations** extend this definition by combining communication with a *reduction* operator (such as `MPI_SUM`, `MPI_MAX`, `MPI_MIN`, `MPI_PROD`, `MPI_LAND`, etc.) on the data being exchanged. The classical taxonomy of MPI collective operations is summarised below: | Category | Operations | Direction of Data Flow | |---|---|---| | **Synchronization** | `MPI_Barrier` | None (control flow only) | | **Data Movement** | `MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Scatter`, `MPI_Gather`, `MPI_Allgather`, `MPI_Alltoall`, `MPI_Gatherv`, `MPI_Scatterv` | One↔All, All↔All | | **Reduction** | `MPI_Reduce`, `MPI_Allreduce`, `MPI_Reduce_scatter` | One↔All, All↔All | | **Scan / Prefix** | `MPI_Scan`, `MPI_Exscan` | All processes receive partial aggregates | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — "The Conference Call Scenario"** > > Imagine **8 regional managers** (processes) joined on a single conference call (the *communicator*). > > - **Broadcast** = The *CEO* (root process) shares the quarterly report and **everyone** on the call hears it. The CEO speaks once, but all 7 other managers receive the data. > - **Scatter** = The CEO tears the report into **8 different sections** and gives one section to each manager. Each manager gets a *unique* piece. > - **Gather** = The *opposite* of scatter — every manager sends their section back, and the CEO assembles the full report. > - **Allgather** = Everyone gathers everyone's piece — at the end, **every** manager holds the full assembled report. > - **Reduce** = Every manager sends a number (e.g., their sales figure). The CEO applies an operator (e.g., *sum*) and announces the company total. Only the CEO gets the result. > - **Allreduce** = Same as Reduce, but **every** manager now knows the company total. > - **Barrier** = The CEO says, *"Let's all pause until everyone is ready, then continue."* It is pure synchronization — no data is moved. > - **Scan (Prefix)** = The CEO walks through the managers in order: P0 reports its number, P0+P1 reports, P0+P1+P2 reports, and so on. Each manager receives a running aggregate. The critical insight is that **all collective calls are synchronising** to some degree — processes must reach the same logical point in execution for the operation to complete. ## 1.3 Why Collective Operations Matter — The Engineering Motivation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Writing collective operations **manually** using point-to-point `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv` is: > - **Bug-prone** (deadlocks on irregular message sizes or orderings) > - **Non-portable** (ordering semantics are implementation-dependent) > - **Sub-optimal** (vendor MPI libraries use highly-tuned, hardware-aware implementations — e.g., shared-memory smp transports, RDMA, on-load engines) > > Hence, MPI mandates that well-formed parallel programs *should* use collective primitives for any operation involving the entire process group. ## 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics (Bolded) The following are the **standard performance metrics** used to evaluate collective operations in KTU 2024 assessment: - **Latency ($\alpha$ or $t_s$):** fixed startup cost of a message, measured in **microseconds ($\mu s$)**. - **Per-word transfer time ($\beta$ or $t_w$):** inverse bandwidth, measured in **nanoseconds per word (ns/word)** or **seconds per byte (s/byte)**. - **Message size ($m$):** number of data words (or bytes). - **Number of processes ($p$):** the size of the communicator. - **Asymptotic cost:** typically expressed as $O(\log_2 p)$ for tree-based implementations. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The MPI Collective Function Template All MPI collective routines follow the same C signature template: ```c int MPI_CollectiveFunction( void *sendbuf, /* address of send buffer */ int sendcount, /* number of elements sent */ MPI_Datatype sendtype,/* type of each element */ void *recvbuf, /* address of receive buffer */ int recvcount, /* number of elements received */ MPI_Datatype recvtype,/* type of each element */ MPI_Op op, /* reduction operator (Reduce/Allreduce only) */ int root, /* rank of the root process (Bcast/Scatter/Gather/Reduce) */ MPI_Comm comm /* communicator */ ); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Signature Variants to Memorise for KTU ESE:** > - `MPI_Bcast` — **only** `sendbuf`, `sendcount`, `sendtype`, `root`, `comm` (no separate receive parameters). > - `MPI_Reduce` — no `recvcount`/`recvtype` (output is always a single scalar per process). > - `MPI_Allreduce` — same as Reduce, but no `root` argument (all processes get the result). ## 2.2 Detailed Operation Breakdown ### 2.2.1 `MPI_Bcast` — One-to-All Broadcast **Logic:** 1. Root process holds an $m$-word buffer. 2. After the call, **every** process in the communicator holds an identical copy of that buffer. 3. Equivalent to a *group multicast*. **Naïve Implementation Cost:** $t_{\text{bcast-naive}} = t_s \cdot (p-1) + t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ **Tree-Based (Binomial) Cost:** $t_{\text{bcast-tree}} = t_s \cdot \lceil \log_2 p \rceil + t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ ### 2.2.2 `MPI_Scatter` — One-to-All Personalized Distribution **Logic:** 1. Root holds an array of $p$ chunks, each of size $m$. 2. Process $i$ receives the $i$-th chunk. 3. Distinct content per process — *not* duplicated. **Cost:** $t_{\text{scatter}} = t_s \cdot \log_2 p + t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ ### 2.2.3 `MPI_Gather` — All-to-One Collection **Logic:** 1. Each process contributes an $m$-word buffer. 2. The root process collects all $p$ buffers into a $p \times m$ array, in rank order. **Cost:** Symmetric to scatter — $t_{\text{gather}} = t_s \cdot \log_2 p + t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ ### 2.2.4 `MPI_Allgather` — All-to-All Broadcast of Segments **Logic:** 1. Each process contributes an $m$-word buffer. 2. After the call, **every** process holds the *complete* $p \times m$ array. **Cost:** $t_{\text{allgather}} = t_s \cdot \log_2 p + t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ *(one gather, then one broadcast of the assembled data — implementation may fuse them)* ### 2.2.5 `MPI_Reduce` — All-to-One Reduction **Logic:** 1. Each process contributes a scalar (or short array) of $m$ words. 2. The root process applies the operator $\oplus$ (e.g., `MPI_SUM`) element-wise across all $p$ contributions. 3. Only the root receives the result. **Cost:** $t_{\text{reduce}} = t_s \cdot \log_2 p + t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ ### 2.2.6 `MPI_Allreduce` — All-to-All Reduction **Logic:** Same as `MPI_Reduce`, but **every** process receives the result. **Cost:** $t_{\text{allreduce}} = t_s \cdot 2 \log_2 p + t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ *(reduce + bcast)* > [!NOTE] > **Modern MPI note:** The factor of 2 has been broken in tuned libraries (e.g., Bruck's algorithm, Rabenseifner's algorithm) to give $O(\log_2 p)$ latency with a slightly higher $t_w$ term. ### 2.2.7 `MPI_Scan` / `MPI_Exscan` — Prefix Reduction **Logic:** Process $i$ receives the reduction of elements from processes $0, 1, \ldots, i$ (inclusive for Scan, exclusive for Exscan). - *Example (Scan with SUM, p=4, inputs 1,2,3,4):* P0→1, P1→3, P2→6, P3→10. **Cost:** $O(\log_2 p \cdot t_s + m \cdot t_w \cdot (p-1)/\text{step})$ ### 2.2.8 `MPI_Barrier` — Synchronization Primitive **Logic:** Blocks the calling process until **all** processes in the communicator have entered the barrier. **Cost:** $t_{\text{barrier}} = t_s \cdot \log_2 p$ (using a dissemination barrier). ### 2.2.9 `MPI_Alltoall` — Complete Exchange **Logic:** Every process sends a distinct $m$-word message to every other process. After the call, every process has received $p$ messages totalling $p \cdot m$ words. **Cost:** $t_{\text{alltoall}} = t_s \cdot \log_2 p + t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ *(Brucek/Jeong methods)* ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following **canonical cost formulas** (Grama, Gupta, Karypis–Kumar model) are examinable. All use the logP/Hockney model parameters: > - $t_s$ = **latency** (startup time, in $\mu s$) > - $t_w$ = **per-word transfer cost** (inverse bandwidth, in $\mu s$/word) > - $m$ = **message size** (words) > - $p$ = **number of processes** | Operation | Naïve Cost | Tree-Based Cost | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Barrier | $t_s (p-1)$ | $t_s \log_2 p$ | No data movement | | Broadcast (1→All) | $t_s(p-1) + t_w m (p-1)$ | $t_s \log_2 p + t_w m (p-1)$ | Root = 0; duplication is acceptable | | Scatter (1→All distinct) | $t_s(p-1) + t_w m (p-1)$ | $t_s \log_2 p + t_w m (p-1)$ | Personalised segments | | Gather (All→1) | $t_s(p-1) + t_w m (p-1)$ | $t_s \log_2 p + t_w m (p-1)$ | Result only at root | | Allgather (All→All) | $2 t_s (p-1) + t_w m (p-1)$ | $t_s \log_2 p + t_w m (p-1)$ | Each process gets full array | | Reduce (All→1) | $t_s(p-1) + t_w m (p-1)$ | $t_s \log_2 p + t_w m (p-1)$ | Single scalar at root | | Allreduce (All→All) | $2 t_s(p-1) + 2 t_w m (p-1)$ | $2 t_s \log_2 p + 2 t_w m (p-1)$ | Tuned: $O(\log p)$ only | | Scan (Prefix) | $t_s (p-1) + t_w m (p-1)$ | $t_s \log_2 p + t_w m (p-1)$ | Inclusive prefix sum | | Alltoall | $t_s(p-1) + t_w m (p-1)$ | $t_s \log_2 p + t_w m (p-1)$ | Complete exchange | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pitfall — Formula Recall:** A common KTU error is forgetting that the **$t_w \cdot m \cdot (p-1)$ bandwidth term is the same** for one-to-all and all-to-one collectives — the data still has to traverse the network $(p-1)$ times. Only the *latency* term drops logarithmically under tree-based implementations. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Domain | Use Case | Collective Used | |---|---|---| | **Numerical Linear Algebra** | Distributed matrix-vector multiplication | `MPI_Allreduce` (dot product), `MPI_Allgather` (row sharing) | | **Deep Learning** | Gradient averaging in data-parallel SGD | `MPI_Allreduce` (the dominant cost in Horovod, NCCL) | | **Computational Fluid Dynamics** | Halo-exchange in domain decomposition | `MPI_Sendrecv` or collective neighbourhood ops | | **N-Body Simulations** | Force computation in Barnes-Hut tree | `MPI_Allgatherv` for tree reconstruction | | **Monte Carlo Methods** | Reduction of partial estimates | `MPI_Reduce` to root, then summary | | **PageRank / Graph Algorithms** | Aggregating vertex contributions | `MPI_Allreduce` per superstep | # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Naïve Linear-Loop Broadcast > [!NOTE] > **Setup.** Root = process 0. Each of the $p-1$ non-root processes issues a blocking `MPI_Recv` while the root issues $p-1$ blocking `MPI_Send` calls in a loop. Each send/recv transmits the full $m$-word message. **Step 1 — Cost of one send or receive:** A single message transfer of $m$ words takes: $$t_{\text{msg}} = t_s + t_w \cdot m$$ **Step 2 — Cost of all $p-1$ transfers:** The root must send to $p-1$ distinct receivers sequentially (or non-sequentially; the bandwidth term is the same): $$T_{\text{naive}} = (p-1) \cdot t_{\text{msg}}$$ **Step 3 — Substituting:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{naive}} &= (p-1)(t_s + t_w \cdot m) \\ &= (p-1)\, t_s + (p-1)\, t_w \cdot m \end{aligned} $$ This gives us the **O(p) latency** lower-bound for naïve point-to-point emulation. ## 3.2 Derivation — Tree-Based (Recursive Doubling) Broadcast > [!IMPORTANT] > **Algorithm.** Use a binomial tree of depth $\lceil \log_2 p \rceil$. At each of the $d = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil$ levels, the set of processes that already hold the message doubles. Each doubling involves a single send–receive pair per doubling node, costing $t_s + t_w m$ per level. **Step 1 — Number of levels:** If $p$ is a power of 2: $$d = \log_2 p$$ If $p$ is not a power of 2, we round up: $$d = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil$$ **Step 2 — Cost per level:** At each level, every message hop carries the full $m$-word buffer: $$t_{\text{level}} = t_s + t_w \cdot m$$ **Step 3 — Total cost:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{tree}} &= d \cdot t_{\text{level}} \\ &= \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \cdot (t_s + t_w \cdot m) \\ &= \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \, t_s + \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \, t_w \cdot m \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Wait — why is the $t_w$ term sometimes written as $(p-1) \cdot t_w \cdot m$?** > The "$\log_2 p$" term is the **latency** cost. The **bandwidth** cost counts how many *total words* cross the network. In a binomial broadcast of $m$ words to $p-1$ recipients, the root's $m$ words are forwarded $(p-1)$ times in aggregate (each recipient receives the data once). So the bandwidth term is $(p-1) t_w m$ — a **constant independent of the tree depth**, because the data still has to reach everyone once. **Refined formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{tree}} = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \, t_s + (p-1) \, t_w \cdot m \end{aligned} $$ This is the **canonical cost model** used in standard texts (Quinn, Grama). ## 3.3 Derivation — Allreduce Cost (Reduce + Broadcast) **Step 1 — Cost of reduce phase (rooted):** $$ T_{\text{reduce}} = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \, t_s + (p-1) \, t_w \cdot m $$ **Step 2 — Cost of broadcast phase (rooted):** $$ T_{\text{bcast}} = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \, t_s + (p-1) \, t_w \cdot m $$ **Step 3 — Naïve sequential composition:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{allreduce-naive}} &= T_{\text{reduce}} + T_{\text{bcast}} \\ &= 2 \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \, t_s + 2 (p-1) \, t_w \cdot m \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Tuned (Bruck's algorithm) — observe that bandwidth is halved because we never copy the *whole* array unnecessarily:** $$ T_{\text{allreduce-tuned}} = \lceil \log_2 p \rceil \, t_s + \frac{p \cdot \log_2 p}{2} \, t_w \cdot m \quad \text{(for large } p, m \text{)} $$ ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **Question.** Given $p = 8$ processes, $t_s = 10 \, \mu s$, $t_w = 0.1 \, \mu s/\text{word}$, $m = 1000$ words. Compute the cost of (a) naïve broadcast, (b) tree broadcast, (c) naïve allreduce. **Step 1 — Naïve broadcast:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{naive-bcast}} &= (p-1)(t_s + t_w m) \\ &= 7 \cdot (10 + 0.1 \cdot 1000) \\ &= 7 \cdot (10 + 100) \\ &= 7 \cdot 110 \\ &= 770 \, \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Tree broadcast ($d = \log_2 8 = 3$):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{tree-bcast}} &= \log_2 8 \cdot t_s + (p-1) \cdot t_w \cdot m \\ &= 3 \cdot 10 + 7 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 1000 \\ &= 30 + 700 \\ &= 730 \, \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Naïve allreduce (reduce + bcast):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{allreduce-naive}} &= 2 \cdot (3 \cdot 10) + 2 \cdot (7 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 1000) \\ &= 60 + 1400 \\ &= 1460 \, \mu s \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compare speedups:** - Tree bcast vs naïve bcast: $770 / 730 \approx 1.05 \times$ (latency saved: $4 \cdot t_s = 40\,\mu s$). - For *small* messages ($m$ small), the saving is dominated by the latency term — the speedup approaches $\frac{p-1}{\log_2 p}$. - For *large* messages, both versions are bandwidth-bound and converge. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Collective Should I Use?" | If your parallel algorithm needs… | Use this MPI routine | |---|---| | One process to send same data to everyone | `MPI_Bcast` | | One process to send *different* data to each process | `MPI_Scatter` | | All processes to contribute data to one process | `MPI_Gather` | | All processes to share their data with everyone | `MPI_Allgather` | | Combine data from all into a single value (one process only) | `MPI_Reduce` | | Combine data from all and *everyone* gets the result | `MPI_Allreduce` | | Compute partial prefix sums | `MPI_Scan` (inclusive) / `MPI_Exscan` (exclusive) | | Synchronize all processes at a barrier | `MPI_Barrier` | | Every process sends distinct data to every other process | `MPI_Alltoall` | # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the data-flow topology of each major collective operation.
